item.title: [SCMP] Nobel laureate James Heckman on the value of risk-taking, and China’s ‘common goal’
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 4164

AI Summary:

* Nobel laureate James Heckman assesses that the global and Chinese economic outlook is under pressure due to geopolitical uncertainty (like the conflict in Middle East) and high oil prices, leading to a slowdown where forecasts (e.g., China's 4.5-5% growth) may be overly optimistic. * He characterizes China's demographic shift—a declining and aging population with falling fertility rates—as a "mixed blessing," putting social benefits systems under threat and ending the previous "population dividend." * Heckman emphasizes that navigating this period of uncertainty requires risk-taking and that China and the world share the "common goal" of fostering human development and skills formation, areas of his extensive research.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
James Heckman is the Henry Schultz distinguished service professor of economics and public policy and director of the Centre for the Economics of Human Development at the University of Chicago.

Heckman has devoted his professional life to understanding the origins of major social and economic questions related to inequality, social mobility, discrimination and the formation of skills and regulation in labour markets. He has also done extensive research in China’s labour market and early childhood development.

He was awarded a Nobel Prize in Economic Sciences in 2000 for developing methods to solve statistical sample-selection problems.

This interview first appeared in SCMP Plus. For other interviews in the Open Questions series, click here.

What is your assessment of China’s economic outlook, especially as it grapples with geopolitical tensions in different parts of the world?

The whole world’s economic outlook isn’t good right now because of the fighting in the Middle East and the rise in oil prices and the uncertainty that all of this has created. Right now, in a period of uncertainty, China and the rest of the world is a little bit on pause.

And I say “a little bit” because we are in the initial phases of a great bout of uncertainty before things are really resolved. The typical reaction of most trading partners and most human beings is to hold off and to wait until things get resolved.

Not only is there a shortage of oil, which is affecting the whole world and especially Asia, there is an additional factor which has to do with the slowdown. Things are slowing down right now, partly because it’s a matter of waiting to get more information.

However, the war in Iran continues and it looks like the Iranians are a pretty tough bunch of people and want to hold on in a way that I think most Americans did not expect, at least [US President Donald] Trump did not expect.

This is going to slow the whole world economy down. And you can see it – China’s growth rate forecast, 4.5 to 5 per cent per annum in the next year, is probably overly optimistic given the uncertainty that plagues the current environment.

And oil is still a very important ingredient for the Chinese economy and for the world economy. With oil and natural gas being limited and expensive, I think the slowdown will be real. I am not expecting even a 5 per cent growth rate but in the long term I am very optimistic.

And what about the impact of demographic changes in China – how will the disappearing population dividend affect the economic outlook?

The demographic dividend as you describe it is kind of a mixed blessing. Twenty years ago, the Chinese population pyramid was such that more young people were being born than old people were dying.

Now it’s the opposite; the population is declining, and it’s declining fairly substantially. The birth rate is also declining. So not only are people living longer, but fertility has fallen.

And so, social benefits are under threat; the so-called pay-as-you-go system where younger workers pay for the retirement and social benefits of older, less healthy workers.

But that can be addressed. It could be that retirement ages are expanded, which makes sense under any event, because the Chinese population is much healthier than it was 20 years ago.

Workers at 65 are much more able to work, full-time and even in strenuous jobs, in a way that was not possible before. This is a consequence of the improved Chinese healthcare system, nutrition and health practices. It’s a blessing that health has improved.

There is another avenue of progress where China has a flexibility that other ageing countries like Japan don’t have.

The population replacement rate is roughly 2.1. In other words, a family should have 2.1 children to replace itself and keep the population stable. South Korea and Japan are stuck far below that level. The South Korean decline in fertility is far worse than in China.

The current fertility rate in China is around 1.4 or 1.5, but in South Korea it’s 0.7 so you are getting a tremendous decline in South Korean families.

But there is a margin of response that China has and other countries do not and that is its large rural population. There is substantial migration already. However, the large rural population including migrant workers have traditionally not received good educations.

Per capita education expenditure has been much less in rural areas and western China than in the east and in urban areas. The possibility of schooling means that more trained workers can come on board if China essentially centralises its educational policy in a way that it currently does not.

There are moves in that direction. China has now started to redistribute resources, especially to minority areas like Tibet and Xinjiang, but it could do so more generally, and that brings on a supply of educated workers who are actually able to help and will provide a kind of demographic dividend that has not been realised yet.

And there are millions of Chinese children living in these areas who could become productive members of society. And that would give the same thing as a demographic and education dividend.

Why do I say that? Well, maybe the body count is lower but the quality count could be much higher, and so you need to think not only of just replacing bodies or getting more bodies.

The whole quality of training and skill requirements in the Chinese economy have increased tremendously, which means that China is going to be much better able to produce goods and to basically sustain and achieve a prosperous way of life.

So what I am saying is you can replace bodies with skills. Fewer bodies but more skills will benefit China a great deal because there will be fewer mouths to feed but more abilities and skills out there. This will make China a much more skilled environment than it was, say, 50 or 100 years ago.

Beijing makes clear in its new five-year plan that technology will be the main economic engine and that it wants to cultivate more talent to develop new technologies and innovate. How can this goal be achieved?

I was at the China Development Forum in Beijing when Premier Li Qiang gave the announcement of the 15th five-year plan and I listened with great interest, for several reasons.

As an American, I was very impressed with the quality of the Chinese leadership, at least the language used and the analysis they presented was very high quality. It was good economics. It was good social science actually, and it really reflected the basic principles of comparative advantage and the principles of trying to understand how a functioning economy would work.

So I was very impressed by the fact that the planning seemed to be very thoughtful and there were even a lot of elements of discussion about making the market factors in China work. It wasn’t a matter of planning from the centre without recognising market realities.

I thought there should have been more focus on building domestic consumption as a major component of Chinese development. The Chinese savings rate is still extraordinarily high by world standards.

This is not a new point. People have talked about the enormously high savings rate. The savings rate is a very great benefit for China in the sense that it allows you to make investments and improve society, which it has done tremendously.

Infrastructure is at a very high level, but the fact is that promoting domestic consumption is probably a wise approach for economic growth going forward. In other countries, savings rates are often lower, maybe 5 or 10 per cent of the Chinese savings rate.

The domestic market here is huge and it’s growing and that’s going to be a stimulus for economic growth for Chinese companies. Many organisations, like the IMF, the World Bank – and Chinese economists themselves of course – have always talked about it.

The premier said the government wanted to spend more on human capital to boost domestic consumption. What’s your view on that and what are the pitfalls?

The main thing that China should do, as I mentioned already, is alleviate the big gap between the west and east, between rural and urban. Increasing investment in the education of rural Chinese will be a very great buy – it is already helping to bolster the economy.

But I still think the gap can be closed a great deal more. And I also think the quality of education should probably be enhanced.

China’s got a lot of room for improvement in its education system.

For example, test scores are only a partial indicator of what a student can do. They’re not good measures of creativity or good measures of the ability to pioneer and engage in entrepreneurship. The Chinese system should be made to be more focused on creativity, productivity, entrepreneurship and risk-taking than it currently is.

I do believe that the Chinese have a great deal of gain potential here by being able to go and look closely at – and refocus – the education system.

I was a little dismayed when I heard the minister of education say earlier that the major focus in education was health.

Health is important, but I think the education minister didn’t appreciate the fact that certain social, emotional and cognitive skills promote health.

It’s not just a matter of having more health; not just more exercise, although exercise is very valuable, and that medical treatment is very valuable and vaccination is very valuable. All that’s valuable. But I think it was too narrow a perspective about how to promote health.

We promote health by promoting education. It’s a mistake to think that education should be just about health or only health. What we want to think of is producing more general skill sets of Chinese to make them more risk-taking, more creative and so forth. This is true not just of China but around the world.

So I am not picking on China here in particular, but I think that a lot of so-called educationists – the ones who work in schools of education – focus too narrowly on things like test scores and PISA [Programme for International Student Assessment] scores.

Even PISA itself is shifting its focus away, towards social and emotional skills as well. But those turn out to have a very big influence on health and the ability to regulate one’s life, to control one’s passions and appetite.

The ability to regulate oneself is really a very important ingredient, and schools and families can teach that. So I do think the educational system should probably recognise a little more that human capital production is not just a consequence of schools, but it’s also a consequence of what family life is.

And that starting early is a very important way to make schools more productive, and to engage the family in the life of the child.

So all those are possible moves that China could take to essentially bolster its human capital production.

You mentioned the importance of creativity and risk taking, but the Chinese system and Chinese culture are quite rigid in the sense that people are taught to align with the central values and the central agenda. How can that gap be bridged to enable the Chinese education system to encourage risk taking?

Well, I think the most important thing is a message that I heard from Jack Ma actually, although I don’t know if he wants to be quoted this way. He came to Chicago many years ago, and he opened his remarks to a group of entrepreneurs and business leaders and academics by saying that he had a university at Alibaba, and he wanted to teach people how to fail.

I think it’s clearly understood what he means is to take risks, try new things.

And I think there is nothing inconsistent with being obedient and respecting the law, and at the same time trying out new ideas, especially ideas that have to do with products, with science, with mathematics, with art.

So I think that the idea of conformity to values – certain values – is very good.

And I think that idea of having a common purpose and a desire to have a China that is uniform and consistent and fair to all is a good idea. But at the same time, I think you’ve got to tolerate the capacity of individuals to go off and make big mistakes.

Make mistakes not just deliberately, but in an effort to try new things. A lot of investigation – whether it’s in literature or in science or in business or in journalism or in any dimension – inevitably involves trying new things.

And if they’re new, you’re not sure they’re going to work. Sometimes they work really well, sometimes they fail. And so understanding that failure is part of just trying is really important – and encouraging that.

So, I don’t think it’s at odds with Chinese cultural values. I was very pleased to listen to the leaders talk in Beijing about a common goal. There was a notion that China was unified, that there was a national purpose, and the national purpose was to excel. And that division was not to be encouraged, but it was actually discouraged.

The leaders were seeking a common goal – they sought to be better, stronger, more prosperous and harmonious. But at the same time, there is a lot of room in that framework for thinking big about new ideas and disagreeing about the best way forward.

One thing that should be done is more teaching of creative acts, and basically breaking rules that get established solely by tradition, or not by logic or not by comparative advantage.

You’ve done a lot of research on early childhood intervention and education. What’s your advice for parents?

Many people think of early childhood programmes as kind of extending the school system to younger ages, and so instead of six-year-olds going to school, three-year-olds going to school or even two-year-olds. That’s a big mistake. I think it’s really about understanding the role of the family in producing these skills of children and in the interaction that parents have with their children, the interaction patterns, parent with teacher, parent with child, and child with teacher.

Those interaction patterns are very strong and very powerful. Strong in the sense that if they’re well maintained and developed, children can learn a lot and children will benefit. If they’re weak, then children won’t.

So the one thing that we’ve learned is how valuable attachment and interaction patterns are for the development of the self and of abilities. Successful early childhood programmes engage the family as much as they do the child, and certainly more than a school. So this is not another schooling programme; this is a programme which is teaching the parents how to teach the child to learn.

And also – and this is really important – protecting the child and at the same time encouraging the child to take risks. So this is what is sometimes called scaffolding. It’s a notion in psychology of finding where the child is and taking the child to the next step. It’s a very intense job, and parents are better at that in many ways than teachers.

We should have more parents as teachers – especially teaching young children – through example, through basically working with them, encouraging them, listening to them, engaging them in a variety of different activities.

The lessons that children learn from those engagements are powerful, and that’s important. Where the real learning takes place and where the real encouragement takes place is with the individual parent-teacher engaging with a child and getting that child to go off and engage with the world.

That interaction is the key. It’s not like you want to teach Chinese or you want to teach algebra or you want to teach any particular subject as much as you want to build a relationship.

These kinds of lessons have a huge spillover. They produce social and emotional strengths that people don’t anticipate and are very powerful.

Unemployment is a serious problem among young people in China. What’s your view on this and what can be done about it?

Well, the unemployment problem in China is partly related to skills again. It’s also related to the fact that industry and skills haven’t matched as well.

At the conference in Beijing, Tim Cook of Apple talked about a large donation he had made to the China Development Research Foundation and to promote vocational education.

And I think that’s something missing in China on a large scale. I know there is a formal schooling called vocational training, but the difference between successful vocational training and what’s being offered now will come from integrating training at the workplace along with training and education.

So young people will learn at the workplace, they will learn at the job, and they will also take academic education.

An example would be something like the German apprenticeship system or the Austrian or Swiss apprenticeship systems. They just integrate school and work in a way that makes it easier for young people to learn a job and to find a job and to learn the skills that are needed in the job.

China still has a lot of foreign direct investment – the FDI is huge – but what’s needed more is a skilled workforce to match it.

There are examples of how to do this. The US state of South Carolina at one time had mostly unskilled textile workers, but those textile industry jobs disappeared and the state was in crisis because so many were unemployed.

South Carolina attracted FDI in part by offering workplace training – so BMW and BASF and other large firms went there, and people were trained in the BMW system, trained in the BASF system and trained in other systems at other firms. So that training facilitated employment.

There’s a lot of discussion about how AI is affecting the job market, but also education. How should early education change, and what should we do to help children cope with the change?

Some people say AI can take over jobs. So it’s going to make simple accounting jobs no longer valuable because it can add faster – we can add numbers more quickly with AI.

However, this kind of logic is based on the idea that somehow AI does a set of tasks and it automatically replaces people. That’s not true.

The reason it’s not true is the following: sure, the job may be done partly by AI, but if the AI makes the output of that job, the task that they perform, cheaper, then it becomes much more in demand.

So even though it may require fewer workers per output item, there could be more workers employed if the demand for that task expands.

One thing more generally though is that the AI does have to be carefully used in the education system.

If people use AI as a substitute for thinking, that’s deadly. That’s going to prevent people from thinking on their own, and the act of thinking and creating is extremely important.

If we have too many students who are having DeepSeek or ChatGPT or Claude or some other program writing papers for them, they are not really thinking as young people.

We have to be aware of the danger of being overreliant on AI, and if students are using AI to write their papers instead of thinking through them, that’s destructive.

The thinking process itself literally builds neurons and builds intelligence. You have to literally understand the way that people solve problems and the way they build brain power is by solving problems directly, not by having somebody solve the problem for them.

It’s a mistake to essentially think that AI is going to be a substitute unless we just become, you know, a nation of drug addicts and dependent people. But I don’t think we want that.

We want children to be able to function, and by that I mean master tasks and not rely excessively on AI. Not play too many games, not spend too much time idle, but also spend more time solving new problems. AI can help do that. You can play chess with AI, you can play Go with AI, you can also use AI in a very constructive way.

AI can also be a very valuable check on yourself – it can help you correct your text or give you some other ideas. So we need to find the balance between total reliance and total avoidance of AI. I would say that we will find a happy medium but we’re still searching for that.

In your papers you’ve talked about the importance of children’s non-cognitive development, virtues and self-control. Why are these elements so important, and why should economists pay attention?

Well I think that these are skills. Traditionally when we think about technology, we think of a Solow model of economic growth done 60, 70 years ago. Now the emphasis is on the role of capital machines and labour.

What we’ve come to understand is that a lot of the modern economy consists of interactions among partners, trade relationships, personal interactions.

So these social skills of interacting with others are extremely vital, and it’s something we typically neglect. So it’s not surprising economists have always talked about this, but I think what we’ve come to understand is that it is much deeper than just IQ or cognition. We think that the way the human brain works, emotions play a very powerful role: emotion and self-regulation and virtue.

You are most famous for the Heckman correction model to correct statistical bias. How robust is Chinese data?

There are a number of social experimental data sets that are not made available publicly, but are used for private decision-making. So the Chinese data is rich.

There’s a real question, though – which is not completely resolved – as to how accurate some of the very large aggregate figures are for things like, you know, production and pollution and manufacturing.

I hesitate to make strong statements because I haven’t studied those in great detail. I remember many years ago [late American economist] Lawrence Klein was actively engaged in the question about whether the macro data – the data on the economy in China – was accurate.

I think the standards of micro data that individual organisations are collecting are generally very good, but I hate to make a general statement about all data collection in China because I simply haven’t studied it.

What do you think of the suggestion that China may overtake the US in a few years in terms of scientific or technological development?

The current administration in the United States has been very unwise in trying to block and failing to support science in a broad way. It has taken political actions in a way that are very unconstructive. So it’s possible that the rate of progress in the United States will slow because support from the government is low.

Nonetheless, the United States still has a large private sector and places like Silicon Valley and other centres like MIT and Harvard where there is a big private sector supporting research.

But the government support for the NIH [National Institutes of Health] and others has been politicised and reduced, and that means you are slowing down basic science. In certain quarters, parts of what they’ve reduced though is fine.

I personally am in agreement with some of the cuts in the sense that some of this research being funded before was purely political, and it was clearly done to kind of inspire people’s beliefs which were more of a political basis than serious social science research. So cuts of this type of research are good.

I do believe that China is really engaged very strongly.

It can only lead to output that’s very, very good. And the quality of the workers I’ve met with and interacted with has been extremely high. And the work quality is good.

I hope to collaborate with some of these people, and we are actively engaged in discussing those collaborations and even implementing them.

So I think it’s a matter of time, and I think that as China puts in more resources – and [puts] in high-quality resources – and maintains high standards, it will possibly overtake the US.

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

深层结构分析
消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] If anyone deserves a red card it’s Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1112

AI Summary:

- US President Donald Trump personally called FIFA President Gianni Infantino to request that a red card suspension for US striker Folarin Balogun be overturned for the World Cup quarter-final. - FIFA subsequently suspended Balogun's one-year ban, allowing him to play, a decision that stunned the football community as it broke standard disciplinary procedures. - The red card was issued by referee Raphael Claus for serious foul play during the US team's victory over Bosnia-Herzegovina; Balogun was the team's top goalscorer. - Trump admitted he did not know the rules but believed the call was unfair, and he publicly described the referee as "a little bit suspect." - The intervention highlighted the close relationship between Trump and Infantino, with FIFA stating the decision was made by an independent disciplinary committee despite the presidential request.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
The US had high hopes for success in the World Cup both as co-hosts and with the performance of its national team, promising to deliver the best tournament ever.

All appeared to be going well, especially when the home nation beat Bosnia-Herzegovina in the first knockout round to progress to the last 16.

Then President Donald Trump stepped in. It was an intervention waiting to happen. The scandal has gravely undermined the integrity of the sport, its governing body and the World Cup itself.

It all began with a red card, waved by Brazilian referee Raphael Claus at US star striker and top goalscorer Folarin Balogun. After viewing video replays, the ref sent the player off, deeming his treading on a Bosnian opponent’s ankle to be serious foul play. This did not prevent the US team from winning 2-0.

The red card, according to well-established and widely understood rules, meant that Balogun would automatically be suspended for the next game, a quarter-final clash with Belgium. A longer ban would not have been surprising.

This is the usual practice, no matter how controversial the issuing of the red card might be. Anyone who follows football knows this. But that, it seems, does not include the US government.

Officials professed to be astonished and outraged that a key US player would miss the big game. Andrew Giuliani, executive director of the White House World Cup task force and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick discussed appealing to the sport’s governing body, Fifa, to lift the ban. Lawyers were made available.

But Trump preferred the direct approach. He picked up the phone and called his good friend Gianni Infantino, Fifa’s president, asking for the suspension to be reviewed – even though the rules do not allow an appeal.

This highly improper personal, political intervention should have been instantly rebuffed. But on the eve of the match, Fifa stunned the global football community by announcing that the implementation of Balogun’s ban would be suspended for a year, clearing him to play.

It was an outrageous decision but one that will not surprise anyone aware of the close relationship between Trump and Infantino. The football chief’s fawning even went as far as awarding the president a hastily created “peace prize” last year.

Trump spoke openly about his flagrant and apparently successful bid to influence the tournament. He was characteristically shameless.

The president admitted he had not known “what the hell a red card was”. But this did not stop him forming the opinion, as someone who “understands sports really well” that Balogun’s challenge “wasn’t a foul.”

He branded the suspension unfair and said: “We have to have the best players.”

Trump even sought to unfairly tarnish the reputation of the referee, describing him as “a little bit suspect.”

Infantino admitted receiving a call from Trump but insisted he had nothing to do with the decision to suspend the player’s ban, stating it was made by an independent disciplinary committee.

A statement issued by that committee’s chairman was at pains to point out that the referee’s decision and the consequent ban had not been overturned. Viewed in legal terms, it had given him a suspended sentence.

The chairman said the decision was made under Article 27 of the disciplinary code which gives the committee the discretion to suspend the implementation of any disciplinary measure. This was done, the statement added, considering “all of the specific circumstances surrounding the incident and evidence available”.

More telling, however, is what it did not say. No explanation was provided. Fifa must, for the good of the game, make it clear precisely what circumstances and evidence persuaded the committee to take this extraordinary step.

Until it does, the decision appears arbitrary and the perception is that the only “specific circumstance” that mattered was the pressure applied by Trump. It will continue to be regarded as the bestowing of preferential treatment on the US team at the behest of the nation’s leader. That is a dangerous precedent.

Article 27 has been used before. Fifa invoked it, also controversially, to allow one of the giants of the game, Cristiano Ronaldo, to play in Portugal’s opening two matches of the World Cup even though he was supposed to be serving a ban.

But the decision to allow Balogun to play is the first time the provision has been used to suspend an automatic ban mid-tournament.

Balogun’s red card was a little harsh. His contact with the opposing player was accidental. But it is not unusual for such challenges to result in a sending off. There were no reasonable grounds to revoke it or suspend the match ban that followed. Some commentators in the US did not seem to understand that.

US officials have suggested slow motion replays should not have been used in the video review that led to the red card. That is incorrect. The protocol for video assistant referees expressly permits slow motion footage to be viewed to establish facts such as the “point of contact for physical offences.”

Fifa’s decision, unsurprisingly and rightly, prompted a furious backlash from prominent figures and football authorities around the world.

Europe’s governing body for football, Uefa, which has a history of conflicts with Fifa, slammed the move as “unprecedented, incomprehensible and unjustifiable”, declaring that it had crossed a red line.

The decision raises serious questions. Are other team’s now able to challenge red cards? Can their nation’s leaders make representations to Fifa? Why were suspensions for the other 12 World Cup red cards not also suspended under Article 27?

Belgium, facing a US team with Balogun restored to the line-up, was understandably furious. They suddenly and unexpectedly had to prepare for his presence.

Justice was ultimately done, but on the field rather than thanks to Fifa. The US put in a woeful performance, seemingly unsettled by the controversy, and Balogun was a shadow of his former self. Belgium won 4-1, with some of their players apparently mimicking the famous Trump dance when they scored. They went on to lose to Spain in the quarter-final.

The World Cup, which reaches its conclusion next Sunday, has been a success on many levels. There have been exciting matches, less-fancied teams from small nations have exceeded expectations and fans from different parts of the world have enjoyed the action together in good spirits. Football has shown, once again, that it can be a force for good.

But if this World Cup is to be remembered as the best ever, it will be in spite of the best efforts of Trump and Infantino, not because of them.

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

深层结构分析
消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] How traditional Chinese medicine found its way onto fine-dining and bar menus in Hong Kong
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1385

AI Summary:

- The "guochao" trend, which rebrands Chinese heritage as fashionable, has expanded into Hong Kong's culinary scene, integrating traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) into fine-dining and bar menus with promises of wellness and cultural pride. - Chefs like Jayson Tang at Man Ho restaurant make TCM's complex philosophy accessible by using its "Five Elements" system (mapping colours to organs and elements) to visually guide ingredient selection and modern cooking techniques. - This approach transforms abstract TCM concepts into tangible dishes, such as a yellow-themed pumpkin and banana fish maw soup designed to benefit the spleen and digestion. - The movement responds to younger diners by overcoming the perceived density of TCM knowledge, transforming it from an "arcane maze" into a desirable and digestible cultural experience.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
President Xi Jinping met North Korean Premier Pak Thae‑song in Beijing on Friday, urging a strengthening of the “combat friendship forged in blood” and expanded strategic coordination on the eve of the 65th anniversary of their mutual defence treaty.

Pak’s three-day trip comes just weeks after Xi’s state visit to North Korea last month, the Chinese leader’s first overseas journey this year and his first to Pyongyang in seven years. The trip was widely seen as signalling a warming of relations between the socialist neighbours.

Xi told Pak that his summit with North Korean leader Kim Jong-un had provided “strategic guidance” for developing bilateral ties, injecting them with “new meaning in the current era”, according to state broadcaster CCTV.

“Friendship across generations, shared destiny and mutual support have always been defining features of [our] bilateral ties,” Xi added.

He said the Treaty of Friendship, Cooperation and Mutual Assistance – signed on July 11, 1961, and China’s only formal mutual defence pact – laid an important political and legal foundation for consolidating the “combat friendship forged in blood” by the two peoples.

Xi recalled his consensus with Kim that the anniversary should serve as an opportunity to carry forward the legacy of earlier leaders and ensure relations “continue to thrive in the new historical period”.

“At a time of complex and volatile international circumstances, China and North Korea should maintain strategic resolve, strengthen confidence, and accelerate implementation of the consensus,” he said, adding that closer ties would support both countries’ socialist development and modernisation.

Xi called for steady progress in practical cooperation, strengthening public support through “shared revolutionary memories” – including the contributions of the Chinese People’s Volunteers who fought in the Korean war – and ensuring younger generations carried forward the friendship.

He also emphasised stronger strategic coordination to safeguard sovereignty, security and development interests, “creating a favourable external environment for both countries to pursue socialist paths suited to their national conditions”.

Ties have improved markedly since late 2025, following Beijing’s obvious unease over North Korea’s deepening military cooperation with Russia, which saw Pyongyang send troops to aid Moscow in its war against Ukraine.

During his summit with Kim last month, Xi described Beijing’s commitment to North Korea and Kim as “unwavering”.

The two sides agreed to expand exchanges at all levels and deepen cooperation across politics, economy, culture and other fields, while strengthening strategic coordination.

Pak was quoted in the Chinese read-out as hailing the Pyongyang meeting as a “new milestone” in relations that strengthened public support for bilateral ties, adding that the treaty provided “strong legal guarantees” for long-term cooperation.

Pak told Xi that North Korea would implement agreements reached by the two leaders, deepen unity and “develop bilateral relations into the strongest strategic partnership”, while reaffirming Pyongyang’s support for Beijing on its “core interests”, including Taiwan.

He also vowed that the two countries would “jointly promote the socialist cause and work together to defend regional and global peace”.

Pak, a member of the Politburo Presidium of the ruling Workers’ Party of Korea, landed at Beijing Capital International Airport on Friday morning accompanied by a joint party and government delegation.

In a rare move early Friday, the People’s Daily, a Communist Party mouthpiece, published an article praising important North Korean construction projects for “infusing socialist construction with robust vitality”.

The piece highlighted Pyongyang’s progress in tackling housing issues for both urban and rural residents, noting the completion of 50,000 housing units in the capital over the past five years and some 32,000 newly built homes in rural areas in 2025 alone.

It also commended North Korea’s progress in rural food production and large-scale construction of industrial factories, hospitals and service facilities across the country.

Pyongyang has struggled to revive its economy in recent years following strict border closures during the coronavirus pandemic, but is now seeing unexpected boosts driven by arms sales and troop deployments to Russia, alongside supplies and financing from China.

Its economic downturn began to reverse following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, with growth expanding to 3.7 per cent in 2024, according to estimates from central bank observers. Pyongyang has openly supported Russia in the Ukraine war.

Last year, Kim travelled to Beijing for commemorations marking the 80th anniversary of the end of World War II.

The ceremony saw Xi, Kim and their Russian counterpart, Vladimir Putin, standing side by side in Tiananmen Square, a rare display of solidarity among the three countries amid mounting pressure from the United States and its allies.

In October last year, Chinese Premier Li Qiang visited North Korea, joining celebrations for the 80th anniversary of North Korea’s ruling party.

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

item.title: [SCMP] Why did only 2 Hong Kong developers put up their hands for Northern Metropolis project?
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 2025

AI Summary:

- The first land tender for Hong Kong's Northern Metropolis project attracted two submissions: one solo bid and one joint venture, defying low expectations amid a property downturn. - The pilot site in Hung Shui Kiu is approximately 11 hectares and is designed for mixed-use development, combining housing supply with innovation and technology industries. - Analysts consider the turnout a success, noting that large consortiums involving local and mainland Chinese firms may become the norm for the project due to the scale and complexity of the multi-use plots. - The result sets a precedent for future mega-tenders, raising questions about whether the government will adapt future tender terms to sustain momentum and attract more bidders.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Stretches of green farmland and shimmering ponds, winding village roads and sprawling brownfield logistics yards all lend Hung Shui Kiu in the New Territories a patchwork look of rustic Hong Kong standing in stark contrast to its glistening skyscrapers.

This rural landscape is slated for radical transformation under the government’s ambitious Northern Metropolis blueprint and is primed to be reinvented as a modernised logistics hub in the near future.

However, the premise of this transformation relies heavily on market buy-in. The project faced its first litmus test with the closing of the site tender last Friday, which was also the government’s first new large-scale land disposal tender.

The tender has defied conservative expectations by drawing two submissions, comprising one solo bid and one massive joint venture.

“Even with only one bid, the government will treat it as a success,” said Hannah Jeong, head of valuation and advisory services at CBRE Hong Kong.

Others have hailed the turnout as a success given the current property downturn and noted that large consortiums made up of local and mainland Chinese firms would become the norm for Northern Metropolis developments due to the sheer scale and complexity of these multi-use plots.

This outcome, they said, had also set a reassuring precedent for the government’s coming mega-tenders, while providing a vital blueprint that would bolster investor confidence as future development parcels were rolled out in San Tin and Fanling North.

The results raise questions about whether momentum can be sustained for future tenders and what permutations may work well to optimise development. Will more players be encouraged to take on the risk or will they continue to hold back? Will the government adapt the terms of future tenders based on the recent outcome? Analysts say these are issues that will decide the development of the megaproject.

The Hung Shui Kiu pilot site marked the first land sale under the government’s new large-scale land disposal model for the Northern Metropolis. The authorities’ blueprint is to transform 30,000 hectares (74,130 acres) of land near the mainland border into an economic and residential hub.

As part of the first batch of the most construction-ready land within the megaproject, the roughly 11-hectare pilot site is designed for double duty, boosting the city’s housing supply while anchoring innovation and technology (I&T) industries right at the border.

Beyond this specific tender, the wider Hung Shui Kiu area is being strategically positioned as a high-end professional services and logistics hub. The master blueprint features dedicated zones for commerce, recreation and conservation, alongside a newly announced roughly 22-hectare university town area.

The cluster as a whole is designed to cultivate a mature, self-sustaining community by seamlessly connecting different sites within the megaproject.

The Northern Metropolis has been touted repeatedly as a top priority for the current administration, capped most recently by a visit by Xia Baolong, director of the Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office (HKMAO), who underscored Beijing’s call to accelerate construction.

The initiative serves as a critical strategic test of whether Hong Kong can successfully reinvent its role within the country’s broader I&T landscape. City leader John Lee Ka-chiu is in the midst of a large-scale consultation exercise to draft Hong Kong’s own five-year plan that will align with national development, specifically Beijing’s blueprint covering 2026 to 2030.

Hence, last week’s tender was closely watched as a test of whether the government can persuade property companies to take on a broader role in delivering its economic development agenda.

“The central government was watching how the developers play their part,” an establishment source told the South China Morning Post, without elaborating.

The 10.5-hectare site under the tender comprises three residential plots and three enterprise and technology park sites, with capacity for about 3,120 homes and 280,000 square metres (3.01 million sq ft) of industrial floor space.

Unlike traditional land sales, bids are assessed under a two-envelope system, with 30 per cent weighting given to the land premium offered and 70 per cent to non-price factors, including proposals for technology park development and plans to attract strategic industries, investment and jobs.

Henderson Land Development submitted a stand-alone bid, while Sino Land partnered with five companies – China Merchants Land, China Resources Land (Overseas), China Overseas Land & Investment, CTG Investment and Chinese e-commerce firm JD.com.

Other major developers, including Sun Hung Kai Properties, New World Development and CK Asset Holdings, did not take part.

Against the backdrop of a sluggish economic recovery, industry observers had low expectations for the tender before the closing deadline, the SCMP learned.

Jeong, of CBRE Hong Kong, said the firm had expected all along a maximum of two bidders for the plot of land, adding that limited commercial feasibility and operational difficulties acted as the key deterrents for potential bidders.

“The three land sites in Hung Shui Kiu are either triangular or irregularly shaped,” she said, noting that these irregular layouts posed development challenges for firms.

She said that a major “operator gap” existed in meeting the enterprise and technology (E&T) requirements, noting that residential developers were struggling to identify suitable partners with a proven track record and the financial strength to meet the government’s stringent criteria, especially since the tender required the development of a site spanning about 10,190 square metres.

She added that overall returns became marginal once potential E&T losses were factored in, meaning the risk-adjusted return simply did not justify the massive capital and time commitment required.

Industry analysts and government officials viewed the final turnout of only two bidders as a clear success.

“Two bids for a project of this complexity and scale should be viewed as a qualified success, not a disappointment,” Vincent Cheung, managing director at Vincorn Consulting and Appraisal, said, noting that the pilot area was the first of its kind under an entirely new large-scale land disposal framework.

“There is no market precedent, no comparable benchmark transaction.”

He said the formation of a multiparty consortium bid showed that “the pairing of mainland state-owned enterprise capital, local developer execution capability and industrial anchors has now been validated as a workable structure”.

“It [the consortium bid] reflects a calibrated, rather than fearful, risk posture … in an environment of elevated interest rates and subdued residential sentiment, aggregating complementary competencies across housing, technology and logistics is a highly sophisticated response,” he said.

He added that rather than a sign of corporate weakness, multiparty risk distribution was a rational way to meet the government’s complex scoring criteria.

Conversely, he said, Henderson Land’s decision to bid unilaterally was strategically consistent with its position as one of the largest private landowners in the New Territories.

“This allows the developer to maximise the government’s premium offset mechanisms by leveraging its massive local land bank,” he said.

Cheung said that the broader outcome provided a constructive, if cautious, signal that the Northern Metropolis framework could successfully attract well-resourced consortiums.

Commenting a day after the tender closed, Secretary for Development Bernadette Linn Hon-ho said that unlike previous stand-alone residential or commercial land tenders, the new Northern Metropolis model had achieved the government’s goal of pairing traditional developers with industrial operators.

She said she was particularly pleased that the bids differed from past tenders for residential or commercial sites, which often attracted “homogeneous” bidders.

However, asked whether the tender results had met Beijing’s expectations, a source said that the business sector had not faced much pressure as it was a free market and companies simply bid after calculating costs to ensure profitability.

The source added that the government was likely to have faced far more pressure than the private sector and authorities had been acting as matchmakers behind the scenes.

“They provided developers with lists of mainland artificial intelligence companies that have expressed interest in partnering on the project,” he said.

However, he said that because many of these tech firms were not household names and remained unfamiliar to the local commercial property sector, developers needed more time to assess them.

“The developers simply need more time to vet and get comfortable with these prospective tech partners,” the insider said.

He said that several major developers had conducted site visits and expressed initial interest in bidding, but many hit a bottleneck during discussions to create joint ventures.

He said that joint ventures with unfamiliar AI firms involved massive capital distribution, complex profit-sharing models and intricate legal agreements, processes that some consortiums simply could not finalise before the tight deadline.

He noted that some mainland AI firms were equally unfamiliar with Hong Kong’s large-scale land disposal frameworks, so many had adopted a “wait-and-see” approach, choosing to observe how this initial tender performed before committing to future tenders in the region.

Economist Simon Lee Siu-po said it was difficult for traditional developers to bid alone, adding that forming consortiums helped share the risk, especially for megaprojects.

He noted that back in the 2000s, several property developers tried to diversify and transform their businesses but ultimately failed.

“It is very difficult to get developers to pivot into industrial sectors because they are so accustomed to the traditional model of buying land, building properties and selling them,” he said, noting that developers were only now beginning to explore new business models.

Looking ahead to Fanling North and San Tin, the other two pilot areas earmarked for large-scale land disposal, the market expected the government to roll out the former first, given its more mature ancillary infrastructure.

“The objective conditions in San Tin are simply not mature enough yet,” another insider said, pointing out that critical transport infrastructure, such as some of the nearby proposed Northern Link railway stations, would not be ready until at least 2032.

He added that this prolonged infrastructure lag was expected to temporarily dampen developers’ appetite for bidding on San Tin plots.

The source said he expected the government would closely monitor the market’s response to the Hung Shui Kiu results and work to modify future tendering terms to better align with developer interest and financial realities.

“It was a highly, highly technical bid. Small players won’t have the appetite not just for risk but complexity; so it’s up to the big players to do their part,” the source said.

Kathy Lee, head of research and retail consultancy at Colliers, said the tender had successfully forced a structural evolution, drawing out cross-border joint ventures that were capable of executing long-term ecosystem building rather than conventional land banking.

Another source also warned that the market should brace for relatively few bidders, citing a total of fewer than five in the coming Northern Metropolis land sales, noting that the new tendering model represented a radical shift from traditional practices.

He added that historically, developers were only required to bid for land to build and sell flats. However, the current framework introduced a new, industry-driven style that bundles residential plots with information technology and industrial land, requiring property firms to partner directly with industrial operators.

“The industry needs time to adapt to this new model,” the source said.

The insider added that the sheer scale of these multi-use sites made it incredibly difficult for small to medium-sized developers to participate.

“These developers typically lack the immense capital required for such long-term, large-scale projects,” the source said, noting that the payback period for advanced industrial hubs was significantly longer compared with traditional residential developments, where profits were realised much faster through flat sales.

While forming consortiums offered a potential avenue for smaller developers to enter the Northern Metropolis market, the source noted they were likely to have very little say in the decision-making process.

As a result, smaller players were expected to pivot back to traditional investments such as urban redevelopment projects, where the development cycle was familiar and they could maintain full control over the asset.

“Only the major conglomerates can afford to enter the game now,” he said.

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

深层结构分析
消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Meet CXMT’s Zhu Yiming: the engineer building China’s answer to global memory-chip giants
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1042

AI Summary:

• **CXMT IPO:** ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT) launched a major IPO in Shanghai aiming to raise approximately US$4.4 billion, with some analysts projecting a future market value of 3 trillion yuan. • **Founder's Commitment:** Founder Zhu Yiming pledged in 2018 to forgo a salary until CXMT became profitable, a milestone the company achieved last year. • **Long-term Vision:** In the early 2000s, while in Silicon Valley, Zhu predicted the global memory chip industry would shift to China and set the goal of becoming the country's largest memory designer and manufacturer. • **Early Beginnings:** Zhu co-founded GigaDevice Semiconductor (a predecessor in his entrepreneurial journey) in 2004, securing initial funding after a successful pitch in a Silicon Valley Starbucks, with early work conducted in a rented garage.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
When ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT) – China’s attempt to build a home-grown rival to the world’s top makers of memory chips – kicked off one of the country’s most anticipated initial public offerings (IPOs) of 2026 last week, the spotlight fell on its founder Zhu Yiming.

The Shanghai offering, set to start taking subscriptions on Thursday, aims to raise 29.5 billion yuan (US$4.4 billion). And some analysts predict that CXMT could eventually command a market value of 3 trillion yuan, placing it among China’s most valuable listed technology companies.

The listing will be a milestone for Zhu, who reportedly pledged in 2018 not to draw a salary from CXMT until the memory-chip project became profitable – which it did last year.

Yet Zhu’s wager on Chinese memory chips began long before CXMT. More than two decades ago, when he was a Silicon Valley engineer with a chip design but little money, Zhu predicted that the centre of the global memory industry would eventually shift towards mainland China.

In emails retained by Li Jun, one of his earliest investors, and later reproduced in a Tsinghua University alumni publication, Zhu observed that the industry had migrated from the US to Japan, and then to South Korea.

“It is time for China to play [a] role in this industry,” he wrote.

His long-term ambition was even bolder: “to be the biggest memory designer and maker in China”.

Zhu’s interest in technology had already paid off. While studying physics at Tsinghua, he took programming jobs rather than the tutoring work common among students, making 300,000 yuan a year by the late 1990s when the average annual income was about 10,000 yuan, according to Tsinghua’s alumni magazine. As the software he wrote moved closer to the underlying hardware, he became fascinated by the integrated circuits that China largely relied on foreign companies to supply.

He later studied electronic engineering at Stony Brook University in New York and worked for technology companies in the United States, including memory-chip developer Monolithic System Technologies.

By the summer of 2004, Zhu had decided to start his own company.

At a Starbucks in Silicon Valley, he made a second pitch to Li, a fellow Tsinghua alumnus and technology investor. Their first meeting had produced little interest. This time, Zhu had quit his job, registered GigaDevice Semiconductor and prepared a design for a memory chip that promised lower costs and power consumption.

The pitch persuaded Li to introduce Zhu to another investor, who provided US$100,000 and rented his garage to the fledgling company for cheap. Zhu and fellow engineer Shu Qingming turned their design into a working sample there and applied for a patent.

While the garage origins followed a familiar Silicon Valley script, what came next was more characteristically Zhu: move quickly, even when the money was not quite enough.

In 2005, a group of investors, many of them Tsinghua alumni, assembled US$920,000 for GigaDevice’s first major fundraising round. The target had been US$1 million, but Zhu decided it was close enough and returned to Beijing to start the company.

The firm’s first technology struggled to win over large electronics makers, which were reluctant to rely on an unknown Chinese supplier. GigaDevice changed course and secured its first domestic order from chip designer Rockchip. The contract, worth only 100,000 yuan, put its memory technology into chips for MP3 players.

GigaDevice eventually grew into one of China’s leading chip-design companies and listed in Shanghai in August 2016.

For many founders, that would have been the capstone of a successful entrepreneurial career. For Zhu, it coincided with the start of a more ambitious second act.

That year, he joined forces with the Hefei government to launch the project that became CXMT. While GigaDevice could design chips and outsource their production, CXMT would have to build multibillion-dollar factories, master manufacturing processes and compete with companies that had decades of expertise.

The most important thing in entrepreneurship is to stay alive

In 2018, Zhu stepped back from GigaDevice to focus on CXMT – and made his no-salary pledge.

CXMT began producing its first self-designed mainstream memory chip in 2019, giving mainland China a foothold in a market that was almost entirely supplied by foreign manufacturers.

One of Zhu’s important early moves was to secure licences to patents originally developed by Qimonda, a failed German memory maker spun out of Infineon Technologies. This gave CXMT an established intellectual-property base and reduced the risk of disputes with overseas rivals.

CXMT nevertheless remained loss-making for years as it built factories, recruited engineers and strived to improve its production yield.

Its fortunes changed dramatically as artificial intelligence data centres drove demand for memory to record levels. The boom also attracted Apple, which has reportedly tested CXMT chips for use in devices sold in China.

The IPO has also cast an earlier investment by Alibaba Group Holding in a new light. Its Hong Kong-listed shares jumped as much as 17.1 per cent last week amid a broader rally in Chinese AI stocks, just as its exposure to CXMT drew renewed attention. Subsidiaries of Alibaba own just under 5 per cent of the chipmaker after investing about 7.6 billion yuan since 2021, making Alibaba its largest industry investor.

Despite CXMT’s success, Zhu has not yet won his original wager. South Korea’s Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix, along with US firm Micron Technology, still control more than 90 per cent of the global DRAM market and remain ahead in scale and advanced products. CXMT must continue spending heavily on manufacturing technology while navigating cyclical prices and tightening restrictions on China’s access to semiconductor equipment.

The engineer who once handled technology, fundraising and early customer development now chairs a manufacturer that employed nearly 19,300 people at the end of 2025.

In an interview published by Tsinghua’s alumni magazine after GigaDevice’s listing, Zhu attributed the company’s success to choosing the right market and the hard work of his employees. He also expressed a dogged determinism that is likely to serve him well as he continues working on his prediction about the industry’s migration to China.

“The most important thing in entrepreneurship is to stay alive,” he said.

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

item.title: [SCMP] Fuchsia Dunlop tells how she fell for China’s cuisine and names her perfect dish
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 3752

AI Summary:

• Fuchsia Dunlop is a British author and chef, widely considered the Western world's leading expert on Chinese regional cuisines, especially Sichuanese food, having written seven books on the subject. • Her interest began in 1994 when, as a student in Chengdu, she sought out cooking lessons from local restaurants and the Sichuan Higher Institute of Cuisine, later joining a formal chef's training course. • She wrote her first Sichuan cookbook, published in 2001, after noting the absence of English-language resources on the cuisine, aiming to introduce the region's diverse food culture to the West. • Dunlop describes Chinese cuisine as vast and diverse, seeing the exploration of its food as a fascinating way to understand China's culture, with her enthusiasm growing from the depth of the subject.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Fuchsia Dunlop is a British author and chef specialising in Chinese cuisine, particularly Sichuanese food. She has written seven popular books and is arguably the Western world’s most knowledgeable person about the diverse food cultures and cooking techniques of China’s various regions. In this interview, Dunlop talks about the comparisons that are often made between Chinese and Western cuisines, as well as her passion for China’s food culture and views on how it is changing.

What are your thoughts on mooncakes? Do you have a favourite flavour?

I enjoy the ritual of giving and sharing mooncakes around the Mid-Autumn Festival, especially when I’m at home in London, because it feels like another connection with China. I like the rich Cantonese mooncakes in small doses: a slice of a lotus seed paste mooncake with some green tea, for example. But my favourites are the savoury minced pork mooncakes made in Shanghai, and various Yunnan mooncakes. One classic Yunnan version is made with flaky pastry and stuffed with chopped Yunnan ham and sugar. I also love the Weishan mooncakes, which are larger and more like a regular cake, made with flour and eggs and sugar, with beautiful stuffings that might include red bean paste or rose petal jam.

How did your interest and research in Chinese cuisine begin, and has your enthusiasm changed over the years?

I went to China in 1994 as a British Council student. I attended Sichuan University as a postgraduate student in the history department.

But I had loved cooking more than anything since childhood. Finding myself in Chengdu, where the food was so exciting and so fresh and so different from any Chinese food I’d had before, I started learning to cook.

I persuaded the owners of little local restaurants near the university to let me study in their kitchens. And then this famous cooking school, the Sichuan Higher Institute of Cuisine, agreed to give me and a German friend some private cooking classes.

After I finished university, the teachers at the cooking school invited me to join a chef’s training course and I spent the following three months with 50 young people from Sichuan learning the basic skills of Sichuanese cooking.

When I went back to England I was struck by the fact that there really weren’t any Sichuan cookbooks in print that were in English.

There was this incredible Chinese region that was the size of a European country, but people in Britain knew virtually nothing about it, which is hard to believe now that Sichuan food has become so popular.

So I had this idea to write a Sichuan cookbook and over the next few years I made several trips back to Sichuan and did more research.

My first book came out in English in 2001. I never expected researching and writing about Chinese food to become my life’s work. But the more I learn – and China is so huge and the food is so diverse – the more I realise that writing about it can be a fascinating way to explore the country and Chinese culture.

People in China care so much about food: at a deep cultural level this seems to be how the Chinese have always expressed themselves.

How did your interest in Sichuan food extend to other regional cuisines?

My first book was quite successful, so afterwards I thought it would be interesting to look at another region. I decided to research Hunan cookery – and discovered that there was almost no material in English about Hunan food either.

I thought it would be interesting because it was another famously spicy region and clearly readers love to read spicy recipes and cook spicy Chinese food, which is very different from the Cantonese food that almost all the Chinese restaurants in the West were serving at the time.

In 2003 I went to Hunan for a couple of months and that was the start of my second book, Revolutionary Chinese Cookbook: Recipes from Hunan Province, which came out in 2006.

But in the meantime I had also visited Shanghai for the first time and that planted a little seed in my mind of interest in the cuisine of Jiangnan [the geographic region south of the Yangtze River].

Then in 2008 I started thinking that I’d really like to write a book about the region, and that eventually led to Land of Fish and Rice: Recipes from the Culinary Heart of China.

When I was studying in London more than five years ago, most of the Chinese restaurants were serving Sichuan food. Did that happen after your first book Sichuan Cookery?

When my Sichuan cookbook came out in 2001, there were no Sichuanese restaurants in central London. And all the Chinese supermarkets were Cantonese, so you couldn’t get authentic Sichuanese ingredients such as Pi Xian broad bean paste, for example. You could not get good, zingy Sichuan peppercorns because all the ingredients available were intended to be used for Cantonese cooking.

When my book came out, a lot of readers were amazed: at that time hardly anyone in Britain, even food writers, chefs and food experts, had tasted real Sichuan peppercorns.

People had no acquaintance with the mala – numbing and spicy – flavour of Sichuanese cuisine, which combines chilli heat with the tingling sensation on the lips that you get from Sichuan pepper. It’s hard to imagine now because mapo tofu, for example, is so well known, but at that time it wasn’t.

In 2006, there was a game changer – the first Sichuan restaurant in central London, which caused a lot of excitement and got fantastic reviews in all the newspapers.

That was the beginning of this big change that ended up as you described, by the time you were in London, with Sichuan food being a real feature of the London dining scene and everyone being familiar with it, not just in London, but in Manchester, Birmingham, Nottingham, Edinburgh.

In the past, there weren’t so many Chinese people living in Britain and the vast majority of Chinese restaurants catered to people who weren’t Chinese.

And I think the hugely significant change is that now we have so many Chinese students and other Chinese residents and visitors in Britain, so there’s a whole new generation of Chinese restaurants that are designed purely to cater for Chinese tastes. They don’t have to adapt their flavours to Western tastes.

So we now have the opportunity to try the kind of Chinese food that people in China want to eat themselves. And it also reflects changing culinary fashions in China.

Sichuan spice is not a problem. Sichuan pepper, if you haven’t had it before, is quite strange and disconcerting. But one of the things I always tried to do was to explain it to people and prepare them for it. So for most people, when they try Sichuan pepper in a cool environment where they feel relaxed, it’s strange at first, but then it’s wonderful.

I mean, why wouldn’t you love Sichuan food?

According to your research, what is the biggest misconception about Chinese food in the West, and how did it come about?

There are several misconceptions that have come out of a particular history. Early Chinese food was almost exclusively Cantonese, based on a single region, but also adapted to Western tastes.

And in Britain, the most familiar kind of Chinese food was the takeaway, which was extremely simplified – no bones, no shells, not so many vegetables, lots of fried foods. It was not designed for Chinese people to eat themselves. But this style of food really shaped perceptions of Chinese cuisine, both in Britain and in a similar way in the US.

The worst stereotype of Chinese food has been that it should be cheap. People will pay a lot of money for Japanese sushi, but while they may think Chinese food is tasty, they don’t necessarily see it as the kind of sophisticated, elite cuisine that you would pay serious money for.

Another misunderstanding is the idea that it’s unhealthy – again, because popular takeaway food involved a lot of fried food, few vegetables, few soups and so on. There is also a certain amount of Western prejudice against monosodium glutamate, which is perfectly normal in China, but Westerners don’t use it in home cooking or quality cooking, and so it’s very much stigmatised.

And then the other side of that coin is the idea that the Chinese eat a lot of weird, scary stuff like chicken feet and snakes.

The Chinese certainly do eat many ingredients that are seen as unusual or strange in the West, but this has always been seen through the lens of a notion that the Chinese eat such things because they were very poor and desperate, which is a terrible simplification, because obviously very rich Chinese people also like to eat duck tongues and goose feet.

There are other reasons Chinese people eat a lot of foods that Westerners reject. And they include this pleasure of texture, the pleasure of the sensation of food in the mouth, and also the Chinese idea of unusual and exotic food – the idea that since, for example, duck tongue is very small and there is only one of them in each duck, it’s a great privilege to have that particular part.

I think for Westerners it’s been a very strange picture because on the one hand, Chinese food has been hugely successful, everybody loves it. There’s no other foreign cuisine that’s been more popular globally than Chinese in a way, but it’s also been saddled with these rather negative prejudices that are very outdated.

As someone who is Chinese, you will know very well that these stereotypes are ridiculous. Because in China, yes, you do have cheap peasant food and street food, but you also have extremely extravagant, time-consuming gongfu food or banquet cooking, which is another aspect of Chinese food culture.

And then in terms of eating “weird” things, as I said, there are gastronomic reasons for this as well as economic reasons.

Finally in terms of health, I think no other culture is as insistent on the link between diet and health as the Chinese. In China, food has been seen as medicine for more than 2,000 years. And Chinese people talk about food and health all the time.

When I learned to cook Chinese food, I learned how to make vegetables taste delicious and how to eat food that is balanced and healthy as well as delicious. I think that is one of the most impressive things about Chinese cuisine.

What misconceptions do Chinese people have about Western food?

Just as Westerners can be very dismissive of Chinese food, Chinese people can be very dismissive of Western food.

All over China, people have told me that Western food is simple and boring, and a lot of people seem to think that it just consists of hamburgers and sandwiches.

And then recently there has been all this talk about “white people food”, which is just seen as completely uncouth and boring and depressing. And then there’s a very weird stereotype about British food – “dark cuisine”.

There’s an old-fashioned British dish from Cornwall called a stargazy pie and it’s a pie with fish heads poking up through the pastry. It’s a very obscure regional dish that I’ve never tried and most British people have never tried. But an English woman wrote a blog post about it that went viral in China, so it’s now almost certainly better known in China than it is at home.

I would say that a lot of Chinese people who have not had the chance to travel abroad make these huge generalisations about Western food that are just as silly as those some Westerners make about all Chinese food.

I’ve also found it very surprising that a lot of Chinese people have told me over the years that they think Western food is healthier because there’s this sort of tradition of scientific nutrition where you’re told how many calories and how much protein and that sort of thing.

But I find that very strange because it seems to me that people in China, certainly the older generation, have a knowledge of healthy eating that is incredibly deep and incredibly useful, whereas I think a lot of Westerners are actually quite confused about how to eat healthily.

There is also this idea that Chinese food can be quite oily and Western food is not. But again, I think that’s really strange because if you think about traditional Chinese food, it involves a lot of soups and steamed dishes and boiled dishes, and traditional Chinese home cooking does not use a lot of oil.

I think it’s quite sad to see that people in China are now making a lot of the same mistakes that we’ve already made in the West of cooking less and eating less fresh food. It is one of the unfortunate side effects of modernisation and industrialisation, isn’t it?

What do you think of Westernised Chinese food, such as the fast food restaurants popular in the US, like Panda Express?

You have to remember that Chinese food is not just the food of the country of China. It’s also the food of a huge Chinese diaspora.

So Chinese food is already part of global culture and it’s a presence in every city practically all over the world. Not only Chinese people living in the West want to eat Chinese food.

Chinese food doesn’t have to be one thing. I think people are always inventing and adapting and borrowing ideas from each other. For example, I recently wrote an article about Shanghainese Western food, which is a bit like Hong Kong-style Western food.

So in China you have versions of Western food that are completely separate from what Westerners actually want to eat.
