While we currently have some conference platforms, because they haven’t fully aligned with international standards to a certain extent, their appeal and influence on the international community remain insufficient.

At conferences hosted by the Philippines and Vietnam, I can completely articulate Chinese views and even directly criticise the Philippines or Vietnam. Our own platforms should also possess such an open and inclusive attitude. Everyone can engage in equal exchanges and even openly debate.

International recognition of a scholar requires a lengthy process. Therefore, we must be patient, providing support through policies and platforms, encouraging them to gain more international experience.

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

深层结构分析
消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] A cloud above the clouds: US, China race to make space a computing platform
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1852

AI Summary:

- The US and China are engaged in a new "space race" focused on establishing space as a computing and digital infrastructure platform, marking a shift from the Cold War-era competition for symbolic "firsts." - This competition involves building orbital data centres, satellite networks, and computing systems to perform data processing directly in space, moving beyond the traditional model of sensing in space and computing on Earth. - SpaceX is a major US player, with plans for orbital data centres driving demand for materials and components, and an imminent expected IPO to be the world's largest. - China, viewing space as a critical technological battlefield, is actively developing its commercial space sector. Key initiatives include Zhejiang Lab's "Three-Body Computing Constellation" project, aiming to deploy a thousand-satellite network for space computing.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
In 1957, the Soviet Union kicked off the Cold War’s space race, shocking the world by sending Sputnik, the world’s first artificial satellite, into orbit.

Four years later, it did the same to cosmonaut Yuri Gagarin, making him the first human in space. In 1969, the US provided another image that defined the era: Neil Armstrong leaving the Eagle lunar lander and planting the American flag on the moon, declaring it “one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.”

For the two superpowers, the space race was an ideological contest measured in firsts – with each milestone setting down a marker for technological know-how, national pride and a renewed commitment to push beyond the bounds of Earth.

Half a century later, a new space race is taking shape, with China emerging as one of the leading actors.

However, this competition is about more than symbolic firsts. Countries are seeking to make the technological achievements necessary to occupy, operate and utilise space, creating a new layer of digital infrastructure through satellites, networks, sensors and computing systems.

SpaceX, the US tech giant gearing up for what is expected to be the world’s biggest initial public offering as early as next month, has become a major player in this contest, releasing plans for orbital data centres, an experimental technology that is nonetheless sending ripples through global supply chains by driving demand for base metals, chips, satellites and rockets.

China, nurturing its own commercial space sector, is racing to keep up. Industry insiders and analysts based in the country said space was the new battlefield for technological competition, and Beijing could not afford a strategic absence.

“Talking too much about ‘the first’ is meaningless in a truly innovative context,” said Wang Jian, an academician at the Chinese Academy of Engineering, adding he wanted the country to “send computing power and AI into space”.

Wang, a founder of Alibaba Cloud, helped build China’s cloud computing industry. He now leads a research institute backed by Zhejiang’s provincial government, known as Zhejiang Lab. Its flagship Three-Body Computing Constellation project aims to build a thousand-satellite network for space computing.

“Space computing refers to shifting computing capability into orbit,” said Lin Guangrong, constellation communications systems architect at Chinese satellite maker GalaxySpace. “[It lets] satellites and spacecraft perform sensing, processing and data transmission directly in space.”

The ultimate vision is “space-based primary computing” – data centres that float in a layer above the actual clouds – according to China’s Academy of Information and Communications Technology, a think tank affiliated with the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology.

For decades, the aerospace industry operated under a standard procedure: sense in space, compute on Earth. Satellites were merely orbital cameras and mirrors, capturing raw data and waiting for a designated “downlink window” to beam it to a terrestrial ground station.

But a paradigm shift is under way, accelerated by rapid changes in the terrestrial economy and their attendant challenges.

On Earth, there are fears the boom in artificial intelligence data centres – described by Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang as “the largest infrastructure buildout in human history” – could run into crippling physical constraints such as land shortages, strained power grids and access to the water required for cooling.

“Current advances in AI are dependent on large terrestrial data centres,” wrote SpaceX CEO Elon Musk in a February memo announcing SpaceX’s acquisition of xAI. “In the long term, space-based AI is obviously the only way to scale.”

Tech leaders have begun to make the case for moving heavy AI workloads into orbit – where solar energy is abundant and the vacuum of space, in theory, would provide unlimited coolant – arguing it is the only sustainable way to ease the compounding infrastructure pressures on Earth’s ecosystems.

But more immediately, space computing would serve as a boon for data preservation – a wide-ranging benefit.

“As the number of satellites in orbit continues to grow alongside mission density, the volume of space data is rising rapidly,” Lin said.

Wang said about 90 per cent of remote-sensing satellite data was discarded in space because Earth-based networks could not handle the sheer volume of information.

That could result in delays and, sometimes, missed signals. Information about floods, wildfires, ship movements or cosmic ray bursts lasting only seconds could lose much of its value by requiring a downlink window before being analysed on Earth.

China entered the space race relatively late. Its first satellite, Dongfanghong 1, lifted off from Jiuquan in 1970 and broadcast the revolutionary song “The East Is Red” from orbit.

“From the perspective of technological competition, China cannot afford a strategic absence,” said Mi Lei, founder of CAS Star, a venture capital firm backed by the Chinese Academy of Sciences whose portfolio spans low-Earth-orbit communications, launch companies and satellite makers.

“Space is too important. In the new round of technological competition, a country has to be good at everything.”

While China does not face the same severe electricity and land constraints limiting the US capacity for data centre construction, it wants a central role in the next race.

Its advantage lies in its ability to fully mobilise a centralised industrial system – uniting components, satellites, rockets, launch sites, measurement systems, applications and a manufacturing base – under state command, according to a recent report by a research institute affiliated with the information technology ministry.

That holistic ecosystem allows China to bypass some of the fragmented commercial inefficiencies that can plague Western tech deployment.

Chinese authorities have voiced their support for space-based computing, sending a clear signal for capital and industry players. At a news conference last month, an official from the ministry said China would “support forward-looking research into space computing technologies and promote the orderly development of the space-computing industry”.

In the blueprint for the latest five-year plan, which runs through 2030, the commercial space sector was listed as one of 10 new industries and growth areas China would nurture and develop, as Beijing for the first time included the goal of becoming a “space power” among its national “strong country” objectives.

Against that backdrop, new research labs and companies are emerging.

The Three-Body Computing Constellation, designed to solve space’s data problem in space with AI, successfully launched an initial batch of 12 computing satellites in May last year. It is aiming for 100 next year and is equipping each spacecraft with an onboard computing unit capable of delivering up to 744 trillion operations a second.

Zhejiang Lab said in February it had deployed 10 AI models in orbit, including a remote-sensing model, an astronomical time-domain model, and a Qwen large language model from Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the South China Morning Post. Several of the models can be updated while in orbit.

In one recent experiment, satellites equipped with cosmic ray detectors used a space-based astronomical model to rapidly identify and classify gamma-ray bursts.

Zhejiang Lab also established in-orbit network links between six satellites this year, marking a significant step towards inter-satellite networking.

“The field is still in an early phase of exploration and technical validation,” said Lin of GalaxySpace.

The most immediate hurdle facing China’s ambition is the infrastructure required to get orbiting computers into the sky efficiently – an area where the US holds a long-standing advantage.

Driven by tech titans with deep pockets, the American aerospace industry has made strides in high-frequency, low-cost reusable rockets.

SpaceX completed roughly 165 orbital launches last year, with that rapid-fire capability enabling the construction of Starlink – a fully functioning global broadband low-Earth-orbit network with over 10,000 satellites.

Musk’s company is now leveraging that dominance, seeking US regulatory approval for a constellation of up to 1 million solar-powered satellites engineered specifically as orbital data centres. Such a massive fleet would solidify America’s architectural dominance over the lower atmosphere.

China, however, has yet to master high-frequency, SpaceX-style reusable rocket technology.

While its Qianfan and Guowang megaconstellation plans are ambitious, each has only launched a few hundred satellites so far.

Compounding the hardware gap is the nature of the competition itself. Beijing is primarily relying on state-directed labs, targeted pilot programmes, and a still-maturing commercial space industry, while Washington’s space computing push is being propelled by an aggressive, cash-rich alliance of private AI and chip giants.

Google is advancing its Project Suncatcher, a moon-shot effort to link solar-powered satellites equipped with its proprietary Tensor Processing Units in an orbital AI cloud.

AI start-up Anthropic struck a deal with SpaceX this month to use the latter’s Colossus 1 data centre, while signalling interest in a broader partnership to develop gigawatt-scale AI computational processing power in orbit.

Nvidia, meanwhile, unveiled a Space-1 Vera Rubin module at its GTC conference in March, describing it as part of a new accelerated computing platform for space applications. The company has already placed an H100 graphics processing unit in orbit on a satellite launched by Starcloud, a start-up it backs.

Yet both sides must confront the laws of physics, which favour no superpower.

“To make space computing work at scale, onboard computing, inter-satellite links, space-ground integration and thermal management all have to move forward together,” Lin said.

High-power computing units generate massive amounts of heat, but cooling is an issue in such a brutal environment. While space is cold, it is also a vacuum – meaning there is no air for convection.

Instead, heat must be laboriously conducted through the structural body of the spacecraft and radiated away through large thermal panels, making high-power space computing a heavy, complicated and expensive thermodynamic challenge.

Then comes the power paradox. Running trillions of operations per second requires immense energy, forcing engineers to develop lightweight, foldable, radiation-resistant solar arrays that must constantly balance thermal design with spacecraft orientation.

It is a systems-level conundrum, and no current solar cell has presented a clear solution. Every material choice involves severe trade-offs between weight, flexibility, lifespan and radiation tolerance.

To combat these challenges, industry players like GalaxySpace have not only poured capital into mass production of synthetic aperture radar satellites, but also into complex pumped fluid-loop thermal management systems and advanced flexible solar arrays.

According to a paper written by Google researchers, the engineering hurdles also encompass high-bandwidth inter-satellite laser communications, radiation-hardened components and long-term hardware reliability in an environment hostile to delicate silicon.

Google’s researchers were not as positive as Musk, who frames orbital data centres as a near-term possibility. They said the cost of launching and operating a space-based data centre would not become economically comparable to terrestrial infrastructure until the mid-2030s, assuming launch prices fall below US$200 per kg.

To put that into perspective, SpaceX says its Falcon 9 can send up to 22,800kg (50,265 pounds) into low-Earth orbit, and with each launch reported to cost about US$74 million, that implies a full-capacity launch cost of roughly US$3,245 per kg.

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

item.title: [SCMP] Southeast Asian scammers’ new disguise: your leader’s face
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1758

AI Summary:

- **High-impact fraud in Singapore**: A scammer used a deepfake video of Prime Minister Lawrence Wong in a fabricated Zoom meeting to defraud a victim of US$3.8 million. The victim was lured via a WhatsApp message and attended the fake summit with other cloned officials. - **Regional pattern of AI abuse**: In Indonesia, a deepfake of President Prabowo Subianto was used to trick people into paying fake administrative fees for non-existent aid. In the Philippines, a deepfake of President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. endorsing an investment scheme convinced a victim to lose US$1.5 million. - **Growing criminal capability**: Generative AI is enabling scammers to convincingly impersonate trusted leaders, moving beyond simple forged images to create detailed, interactive fabrications that exploit human trust. - **Systemic threat**: An Interpol report identified AI-driven crime as a major emerging threat in Southeast Asia, noting that criminal networks are becoming more organized and efficient as technology outpaces regulation.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
There is a bogus version of Singapore’s prime minister that exists only to steal money. It connected to a Zoom call, thanked a stranger for his time and helped relieve him of US$3.8 million – with the real Lawrence Wong, for a time, none the wiser about what had happened.

The victim had received a WhatsApp message purporting to be from the secretary to the cabinet, inviting him to a private meeting.

What followed was not some doctored photo or a 15-second clip designed to fool a distracted doomscroll. It was an entire fabricated summit, complete with assembled officials including Wong, President Tharman Shanmugaratnam, cabinet minister Indranee Rajah and foreign dignitaries, all rendered convincingly enough to believe they were discussing funding tied to the Strait of Hormuz.

The synthetic Wong closed the meeting by thanking the victim for attending. Then a fake lawyer took the call and S$4.9 million (US$3.8 million) vanished into an account that no longer exists.

Southeast Asia’s scam industry has found a powerful new weapon, and it isn’t malware, cunning or even greed – it’s borrowed faces.

Criminals are increasingly cloning the region’s most trusted leaders using generative AI, wrapping fraud in the unimpeachable authority of a president’s voice or a prime minister’s visage and cashing in on the thing no security system can patch: human trust.

In Indonesia, a similar con surfaced within days of Prabowo Subianto’s 2024 election win, when an Instagram clip began circulating in which he appeared to ask viewers: “Who hasn’t received aid from me? What are your needs right now?”

The incoming president had said no such thing. But the voice was his, the face was his and for many, that was enough. Across 20 provinces, an unknown number of Indonesians each wired scammers “administrative fees” of up to 1 million rupiah (US$55), chasing government aid that was never coming and never had been.

In the Philippines, the fraud wore Ferdinand Marcos Jnr’s face, superimposed onto footage endorsing an online trading scheme with a polish good enough to convince one dentist to hand over 93 million pesos (US$1.5 million) before the illusion broke.

Three countries. Three cons. One unsettling realisation: artificial intelligence has stopped merely helping scammers write better emails. It has started manufacturing their credibility from scratch.

“If you’re not trained to look at it and you don’t have the discernment to know that it’s impossible for the president to be endorsing investment schemes, you will believe it,” said Art Samaniego, a Manila-based tech journalist and co-founder of ScamWatch Pilipinas. “Because you can clearly see the president’s face and he appears to be speaking.”

An Interpol report released in June identified AI-driven crime as one of Southeast Asia’s most urgent emerging threats, warning of criminal networks that are growing leaner and more organised as technology races ahead of regulation.

From January 2024 to March 2025, it found “phishing” attacks – where scammers send fake emails, messages, or links to trick victims into sharing sensitive data – and related swindles were the costliest form of cybercrime across Asia and the South Pacific, with a third of countries logging more than 10,000 cases apiece.

“These include the use of deepfake videos to impersonate public figures, celebrities, or business executives,” it said. “Such schemes have resulted in significant financial losses and have targeted both individuals and institutions.”

On criminal forums and Telegram channels, chatter about deepfakes surged 600 per cent in just one five-month period in 2024, the report found.

But strip away the technology and the con itself is nothing new. All the AI does, experts say, is play on the same old human weaknesses – fear, hope, desperation, deference to power – but with a more believable face.

“Many people are raised to respect authority,” said Jun Angelo Suglao, a clinical psychologist who chairs the Psychological Association of the Philippines’ mental health support group.

“So if someone calls and can impersonate someone we inherently perceive as good or trustworthy, our default instinct is to comply, not to doubt them.”

The elderly made for some of the easiest targets, Samaniego said, as many had been conditioned over long lifetimes to trust a phone call at face value.

But no one is immune from scammers, especially as the technology becomes more advanced. Voice cloning adds a crueller dimension still, mimicking the reassuring tones – or panicked cries – of a loved one.

“Hearing someone you care for cry for help triggers an immediate fight-or-flight response, completely shutting down your rational thinking,” Suglao said. “You act on pure emotion.”

Nobody understands that better than a Filipino domestic worker identified only as Maria, who reportedly believed she had found love with a Dubai prince on a dating app.

He wrote constantly. He placed video calls, warm and attentive, present in every way that mattered. But he was never real.

By the time Maria saw through the facade, she had sent 100,000 pesos – a year’s wages – for a “marriage certificate” and a fictitious “royal membership card” meant to secure her a job in Dubai, Agence France-Presse reported earlier this month.

“They prey on isolated individuals,” Suglao said of the scammers. “Slowly building a deep emotional connection with the goal of scamming them out of money.”

AI has not changed the criminals’ cruelty. It has simply given it a face worth falling for.

Jeremy Douglas, deputy director of operations at the UN Office on Drugs and Crime, confirmed to This Week in Asia that AI and other tech tools were increasingly being integrated into scam operations across Southeast Asia and further afield.

“It’s classic organised crime behaviour, innovating to accelerate business and stay ahead of law enforcement,” he said. “The use of deepfake images and videos, and mimicking or copying voice intonation and patterns of partners and contacts are on the rise and we can expect more sophisticated use going forward.”

Neal Jetton, who leads Interpol’s cybercrime directorate, pointed to estimates that global cybercrime losses hit US$10.5 trillion in 2025, with a 142 per cent rise forecast by the end of this decade.

“Cybercrime exists wherever anyone is using a smartphone or laptop … it is a global problem,” Jetton said, adding that AI had become “one of the main drivers”.

What makes the scams so effective, according to Interpol, is their reliance on social engineering techniques, exploiting human trust rather than technical vulnerabilities.

This has transformed so-called romance baiting into a booming subsector all of its own, part of a cyberfraud economy the UN Office on Drugs and Crime estimates has cost the world up to US$37 billion and counting.

The method rarely changes: weeks or months of patient, tender contact, before the big ask arrives.

Analysts are careful not to overstate the novelty, however. AI has not built a new criminal underworld, it has retooled an old one – at speed.

“These capabilities are enabling organised crime syndicates to radically change scam and associated money-laundering businesses,” Douglas said, adding that such operations were “professionalising, using fewer people to run or perpetuate scams” and sometimes switching jurisdictions or shifting from the notorious large scam compounds “into a model of networked locations”.

Samaniego has watched and reported on the retooling happening in real time.

“Before, if an email had wrong grammar, you could automatically tell it was a scam,” he said. “Now you can no longer identify it that way, because their grammar has become so good, because they’re using AI to create fake links and fake websites.”

“AI is not replacing cybercriminals, it’s making them more efficient … Their efficiency has doubled or tripled.”

AI is not replacing cybercriminals, it’s making them more efficient

Art Samaniego, ScamWatch Pilipinas

Even seasoned professionals occasionally fall foul of the scammers’ tactics.

Jetton recalled hearing of a cybercrime expert who clicked on a fraudulent email disguised as an Uber receipt, simply because he had recently used the e-hailing app.

“Awareness campaigns are helping, but we’re still seeing the human element – desperation, need, naivety. And the phishing emails are so good now,” he said, citing a Microsoft report that found a human was 4½ times more likely to click on a phishing email created by AI.

John Wojcik, senior threat researcher for Asia at cybersecurity firm Infoblox, said there had been “rapid acceleration” in the kinds of AI tools that scammers were using.

“From deepfakes, lifelike chatbots and complex, nuanced machine translation, to automated scam outreach, domain registration and phishing attacks, it’s clear this technology is being rolled out at scale within this criminal ecosystem,” he said, fed by a surge in “malware-as-a-service” kits sold on the dark web.

There is no silver bullet solution, Jetton concedes – only what he calls a “whole-of-society effort”.

And as AI-generated content edges towards the indistinguishable, old methods of detecting fakes, such as listening for a stilted phrase or watching for an unnatural blink, are fast losing relevance.

“As AI reaches human-level output, traditional visual or audio cues are becoming unreliable,” Samaniego said.

The only defence that still held, he said, was verification through a separate channel entirely.

“If you receive an urgent or high-stakes request, even if it looks or sounds exactly like someone you know, do not respond to the message or caller directly,” Samaniego said.

“If someone claiming to be your friend asks for money on social media, call him. If he texts, use other channels. Best if you can verify in person.”

His best advice for countering the scammers? Forcing them “off script”.

“AI models and scam scripts are excellent at handling expected flows but often falter when faced with unpredictable, human-centric interruptions,” he said. “Ask a question that other people won’t know the answer to.”

Suglao’s solution, meanwhile, was more community-focused.

“If a suspicious investment opportunity or a sudden emergency request comes up, run it by a trusted peer first,” he said. “Talking it out disrupts the high-pressure environment the scammer relies on.”

For ScamWatch Pilipinas’ Samaniego, pre-emptive measures such as strong cyber literacy are the best defence.

“Hearing or seeing someone talk online is no longer sufficient proof that what you’re seeing is genuine or real,” he said. “Because of AI, we all need new ways to verify identity.”

What his cybersecurity advocacy movement wanted, Samaniego said, was for people to already be educated “before the scam occurs” – so they never had the misfortune to become victims in the first place.

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

深层结构分析
消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Philippine rice output risks 30% collapse as ‘super’ El Nino strengthens
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1209

AI Summary:

- A "super" El Nino is strengthening in the Pacific, with a 62% chance of intensifying from October, threatening severe droughts in the Philippines starting in late 2024/early 2025. - Philippine agricultural authorities warn this could cause a collapse in national rice output exceeding 30%, potentially cutting production by up to 700,000 tonnes. - This is compounded by high global fertilizer and fuel prices, as well as potential export restrictions from major rice suppliers like Vietnam, Thailand, and India. - First-quarter 2026 rice production was already down 6.3% year-on-year to 4.4 million tonnes, marking the weakest start to the year since 2020. - The warning follows historical precedent: a previous super El Nino in 1997-98 triggered a 22% collapse in Philippine rice production.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
In the fields of central Luzon, farmers who planted their rice in June are watching the sky with worry.

Out over the Pacific, a familiar spectre is gathering strength – and with it, the threat of empty granaries and hungry households.

The Philippines has a plan for this year’s “super” El Nino, on paper at least. But to the Filipinos out actually working the paddies, that plan is barely perceptible.

“We don’t see anything visible, it’s all talk,” said Raul Montemayor, national manager of the Federation of Free Farmers, an NGO representing 250,000 of the country’s 10 million rural workers and agricultural labourers.

The warning signs have been building for months. Pagasa, the Philippine weather bureau, confirmed in a June 24 update that El Nino conditions were already present in the tropical Pacific, forecasting that they would strengthen by August through October, then intensify further later in the year.

Speaking at Pagasa’s 197th Climate Outlook Forum, held online that same day, Dr Ana Liza Solis, chief of the bureau’s climate monitoring and prediction section, put the odds of El Nino intensifying from October at 62 per cent.

In the near term, she said, the western side of Luzon and the Visayas could see “heavy rainfall events” through to September, offering a brief reprieve before conditions turn. “Dry condition and dry spells will likely start in November, while prolonged dry spells and droughts will be highly likely in early part of 2027,” Solis added.

A rise of more than 2 degrees Celsius (3.6 degrees Fahrenheit) in Pacific temperatures would qualify as “very strong or a super El Nino”, according to the bureau.

Such severe El Nino are relatively uncommon, but when one struck the Philippines previously, in 1997-98, it triggered a 22 per cent collapse in palay (raw, unhusked rice) production.

This time around, layered atop fertiliser and fuel price spikes amid the US-Israel war on Iran, Manila’s Department of Agriculture is bracing for losses that could exceed 30 per cent, according to agricultural economist Fermin Adriano, a former undersecretary in the department who has advised the UN Food and Agriculture Organization, the World Bank and the Asian Development Bank.

“It [El Nino] has been predicted by international meteorological organisations for months now, warning that the Asian tropical region will be hit hard by it,” Adriano told This Week in Asia.

Filipinos should brace themselves for “higher food prices if not actual supply shortages” in the coming months, he said – especially if major rice suppliers like Vietnam, Thailand and India choose to prioritise their own food security and halt exports.

In May, the Philippine Department of Agriculture warned a severe El Nino could slash national rice output by up to 700,000 tonnes. Data from the Philippine Statistics Authority shows palay production in the first quarter of 2026 had already slipped 6.3 per cent year on year, to 4.4 million tonnes – the weakest start to a year since 2020.

Central Luzon, the nation’s main rice-growing area and a region where farmers rely on irrigation, produced 18.8 per cent of the country’s palay in the first quarter alone. Any serious disruption there ripples outwards fast – especially so given the Philippines’ heavy dependence on imported rice, leaving it exposed on two fronts at once, should drought at home coincide with export restrictions abroad.

Fertiliser shortages have only exacerbated the issue. Even before El Nino’s worst effects are forecast arrive, the Iran conflict and associated closure of the Strait of Hormuz have already disrupted roughly 108,000 tonnes of urea from Qatar and 33,000 tonnes from Saudi Arabia, according to official figures.

The government has launched a response, with President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr activating an El Nino “action plan” in May, which his spokeswoman said would tackle water, food and energy security, alongside public safety and health. Yet progress has been hard to discern, according to Adriano.

“There is PR or media releases showing various agencies preparing for El Nino, but I am not sure how far they’ve progressed in terms of actual programmes or projects that will mitigate its effects,” he said. “Wala akong narinig.” (I haven’t heard anything)

What farmers actually need, according to Montemayor, are water impoundment projects such as dams and reservoirs. “They always talk about convening a technical working group, et cetera et cetera,” he said. “[But] I don’t know what else is happening.”

Pagasa’s forecasts suggest rainfall will hold until September and Central Luzon’s farmers, having planted in June, should be able to bring in a harvest around September or October, rains permitting.

But Montemayor sees the real reckoning arriving after that. “October will be when we will feel the intensity of El Nino [and see] less rain than normal,” he said.

“The season that I think will be most at risk is the following planting season, November, that’s when we might have a drought. If you have less than normal rainfall, that means farmers won’t be able to plant.”

Already, the country’s reservoirs are showing signs of strain. Water levels at Luzon’s two major dams of Pantabangan and Magat are low and Montemayor knows exactly how the government will ration what remains.

They will prioritise drinking water, then energy, and then, third, irrigation – if there is water left

Raul Montemayor, Federation of Free Farmers

“They will prioritise drinking water, then energy, and then, third, irrigation – if there is water left,” he said.

“It’s only July, and we’re talking about November, so there is time to prepare – fill up the dams, fix the irrigation systems, put up solar-powered irrigation. But I don’t see it,” Montemayor added. “What they’ll say is it’s not in the budget.”

Even the government’s stated priorities carry contradictions. Agriculture officials have reportedly pledged to focus on irrigation and water impounding, but Montemayor says there is a flaw in that strategy. “What if there’s no rain? The dams don’t get filled,” he said. Cloud seeding offered little comfort either. “If there are no clouds, you cannot seed them – it’s hit or miss.”

At the launch of a small reservoir project on Bohol Island last month, Marcos floated another idea: encouraging farmers to shift towards high-value, less water-intensive crops such as ube. It is a suggestion Montemayor treats with weary scepticism.

“Ube takes 11 months to grow,” he said. “Farmers will have a hard time waiting that long – and you have problems with seedlings, as well as technology and know-how on planting it.”

Drought-resistant rice seeds, often cited as a stopgap solution, fare little better when examined in detail. “I don’t think they have a lot of stock,” he added. “It takes a long time to produce them – two years.”

Even existing support has stalled: according to Montemayor, the government still has not finished distributing the cash aid it promised to cushion rice farmers against rising fuel costs.

With the Department of Agriculture reportedly bracing for historic rice production losses, Adriano worries about the fallout.

“If food supply shortages or skyrocketing food prices happen, then it will be a real emergency,” he warned. “It’s possible that public discontent might reach a boiling point.”

This Week in Asia has contacted the Philippine Department of Agriculture for comment.

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Malaysia has never had so many Chinese tourists. It wants more
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1447

AI Summary:

* **Record Growth and Ambitious Targets:** Chinese tourist arrivals in Malaysia rose 25% in 2025 to 4.7 million, and surged another 25% year-on-year in the first quarter of 2026. The country aims to welcome 7 million Chinese visitors in 2026. * **Strategic Promotion Campaign:** Malaysia's target is supported by visa-free travel, expanded direct flights to Chinese third-tier cities, and a digital marketing push on mega-apps like Douyin (TikTok) and Weibo. * **Competitive Regional Landscape:** Malaysia is benefiting as competitor Thailand cuts its Chinese tourist forecast due to safety and cost concerns, while Vietnam has become the region's top destination for Chinese visitors. * **Economic Impact and Appeal:** Foreign tourist receipts in Malaysia increased to 110.6 billion ringgit (US$26.8 billion) in 2025. The country's multicultural society and reputation for safety are key attractions for Chinese travelers. * **Traveler Behavior:** Chinese tourists are increasingly exploring beyond traditional hotspots like Kuala Lumpur, using social media platforms to discover and visit lesser-known sites.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Jane Lyu flew to Malaysia on Tuesday to visit a city that she had never heard of until recently. The 32-year-old engineer from Guangxi, southern China, first spotted it on Weibo.

Now, standing outside the pink-domed Putra Mosque in Putrajaya, she explains through a translation app how the country’s administrative capital, a planned city barely three decades old, ended up as the first stop on her Malaysian itinerary.

Lyu, who arrived as part of a company trip that began in Singapore, is one of millions of Chinese tourists poised to visit Malaysia this year – and Malaysia is counting on all of them.

Her group decided to look beyond the buzz of Kuala Lumpur in favour of Putrajaya’s staid grandeur, long, empty roads flanked by government offices and the Putra Mosque’s lakefront arches opening onto a wide ceremonial square that is perfect for photographs.

“Before this, we never heard of the Putra Mosque but we saw it on [microblogging site] Weibo and Douyin [the mainland Chinese version of TikTok]. We had to go. it looked so beautiful,” Lyu said

“On social media, we saw durian stalls as well. We can’t wait to eat the fresh durian.”

Travellers like Lyu are exactly the type Malaysia is targeting with its Visit Malaysia 2026 campaign. The country aims to welcome 7 million Chinese tourists this year, banking on visa-free travel, expanded air links to third-tier Chinese cities and a digital campaign built around mainland mega-apps such as RedNote, Douyin and Weibo.

Tourism Malaysia, the government-run tourism promotion board, said Chinese arrivals rose 25 per cent to 4.7 million last year, outpacing overall tourist growth of 11 per cent to 42.2 million. In the first quarter of this year alone, Chinese arrivals climbed a further 25 per cent year on year to 1.41 million.

It comes as Thailand – long the No 1 Southeast Asian destination for Chinese travellers – has lost some of its allure. Its travel agents’ association has cut its 2026 Chinese arrivals forecast from 9 million to 7 million, citing safety fears over scam-related crimes and higher travel costs.

Vietnam, which drew a record 21.2 million international visitors in 2025 with China as its biggest market, claimed the top spot regionally for Chinese visitors last year.

Even so, Malaysia holds its own as a Southeast Asian tourism powerhouse, not just in arrivals but also in revenue. Foreign tourist receipts rose to 110.6 billion ringgit (US$26.8 billion) in 2025 – up from 95.3 billion ringgit the year before – ranking among the top three regionally.

Malaysia’s pitch to Chinese visitors rests on numerous advantages, including its Muslim-majority yet multicultural society, ease of communication and a reputation as a safer destination than regional rivals.

Asia travel market analyst Gary Bowerman said Chinese visitors were often intrigued by Malaysia’s “tri-ethnic population” and the way Chinese heritage had become woven into a broader tapestry of Malay and Indian cultures.

“This is comparatively rare in Asia and young Chinese tourists are eager to learn more about a country that has strong ethnic Chinese history and traditions, but which are integrated with Malay and Indian cultures and sensibilities,” he said.

With 22 per cent of its population ethnic Chinese and predominantly Mandarin-speaking, Malaysia offers a cultural affinity not easily found elsewhere in the region, according to Sam Huang, a tourism professor at Edith Cowan University in Australia.

Social proof matters too: Bowerman said Chinese outbound travellers relied heavily on peer insights and word of mouth, with Malaysia currently “scoring well on Chinese social media for its diversity and sense of difference”.

As of February, 871 weekly flights connected Malaysia to 30 Chinese cities, providing the connectivity to convert online interest into arrivals.

Food is another major draw. Angie Ng, president of the Malaysia Inbound Chinese Association, said motivations for visiting diverged along generational lines, with older travellers seeking history and culture, while younger visitors gravitated towards Instagrammable islands, diving and cuisine.

But a love of “makan-makan”, the playful Malay phrase for eating, unites most Chinese visitors.

Durian has become the clearest emblem of this voracious appetite. A protocol signed by Malaysia and China in 2024 opened the Chinese market to fresh Malaysian durian – previously limited to pulp, paste and frozen whole fruit – unlocking a higher-value trade tied to orchards, agritourism and social media storytelling.

“Different tourists have different demands,” Ng said. “A lot of tourists go to Penang because of durian tours … A lot of people go to Terengganu because of island tours.”

The economics of Chinese tourism are not always straightforward, however. A typical five-day, four-night trip can cost as little as 5,000 ringgit (US$1,200) per person, according to Ng, including roughly 2,000 ringgit for flights.

Questions persist about how much of that spending stays in Malaysia given the dominance of Chinese booking platforms such as Ctrip. But Ng cautions against oversimplification.

“The local operators still benefit and the hotels also benefit,” she said.

Nigel Wong, president of the Malaysian Association of Tour and Travel Agents (Matta), agreed that the benefits trickled down. “And not just the tour operators,” he said. “Everything from restaurants to retail outlets and hotels as well.”

Language barriers, local customs and transport logistics still leave plenty of business for Malaysian travel agents and local fixers, observers say, even as Chinese platforms continue to grow their footprint.

“While there is definitely competition, we are still seeing that these platforms can be part of the Malaysian ecosystem in dealing with Chinese tourists,” Wong said.

Tour groups are a sticking point, however. As in Thailand, Chinese group itineraries are often controlled by China-based operators who choose all the hotels, restaurants and tour stops in a closed economic loop that irks local traders.

But independent travellers are another matter altogether, according to Hannah Pearson, director of Southeast Asia-focused tourism consultancy Pear Anderson.

“For independent travellers, they are free to choose what and where they visit and that means they can visit those locally owned Malaysian businesses,” she said.

The real challenge, according to Pearson, is getting independent Chinese travellers to look beyond heavily promoted hubs such as Kuala Lumpur and Penang to places the algorithms have not yet made famous.

Malaysia has been welcoming Chinese tourists for decades. What has changed in recent years is how rapidly interest in a destination can be built online – and one platform now dominates the decision-making of millions of young travellers.

RedNote, the wildly successful Chinese lifestyle and travel app, was cited by 53 per cent of respondents to a survey last spring by Dragon Trail International as their top source of outbound destination information, ahead of Douyin at 48 per cent and online travel agencies such as Ctrip at 42 per cent.

Among travellers aged 18 to 35, around 60 per cent used RedNote for outbound travel research, compared with 26 per cent of those aged 45 to 65.

Sienna Parulis-Cook, director of marketing and communications at Dragon Trail International, a China-focused travel marketing agency, said the app consistently ranked as the top source of destination information in consumer surveys.

The social media space for the Chinese market is a very strategic tool

Nigel Wong, Malaysian Association of Tour and Travel Agents

“The online space, social media space for the Chinese market is a very strategic tool,” said Wong of Matta. “It’s very effective, and it will continue to be effective.”

Indeed, the spike in Chinese visitor numbers has brought a lot of spending and attention to Malaysia, but it has also had its downsides.

Chinese-only menus and signage are multiplying, heritage streets are growing crowded and poor etiquette at religious sites has also drawn concern. In 2018, two Chinese tourists caused an online furore after being filmed performing an inappropriate dance outside the Kota Kinabalu City Mosque, leading to a temporary visitor ban.

“Tensions will rise when the number of Chinese visitors is too overwhelming for a destination,” tourism professor Huang said. “We need to watch for potential overtourism in any tourist destination targeting Chinese visitors.”

Still, Malaysia has largely avoided the negative sentiment currently affecting Thailand, where worries over scams and safety concerns have kept some Chinese visitors away.

Huang also warned about the risk of social media-driven hype shaping tourists’ expectations before a trip had even begun.

“If tourists visit a place where the real experience cannot match their expectation as driven by the algorithms, they are more dissatisfied and may spread negative word of mouth,” he said.

Back in Putrajaya, Lyu and her friend were already scrolling through the app for their next destination, with one item firmly at the top of the list.

“I really want to try durian in Malaysia,” she said. “Everyone says you have to eat it here.”

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

深层结构分析
消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] China is now world’s No 1 science power, top Russian scientist Artem Oganov says
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1272

AI Summary:

- Russian scientist Artem Oganov received China's top international science and technology award, calling China the world's "No 1 science power," a prediction he first made in 2008. - Oganov cited China's scientific output, noting it accounts for one-third of global publications and holds the top spot in the 2026 Nature Index, surpassing the United States. - He highlighted that China's rise in scientific leadership has been faster and stronger than expected, occurring alongside its leading positions in industry and economy. - Oganov stressed the role of science as a "bridge" between cultures during an era of political division, stating the future belongs to bridge-builders. - A distinguished theoretical crystallographer and developer of the USPEX program, he continues active collaboration with China, visiting annually and planning new projects.

]]>

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

item.title: [SCMP] Why a Chinese academic claim over Batanes has unsettled the Philippines
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1152

AI Summary:

- A university symposium in Guangzhou has placed the Philippines’ northernmost province of Batanes at the centre of a new argument over history, geography and sovereignty, after scholars at the event claimed the islands belonged to Taiwan and, on that basis, fell under overall Chinese sovereignty. -...

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
A university symposium in Guangzhou has placed the Philippines’ northernmost province of Batanes at the centre of a new argument over history, geography and sovereignty, after scholars at the event claimed the islands belonged to Taiwan and, on that basis, fell under overall Chinese sovereignty.

The claim, made at a June 30 symposium hosted by Jinan University in Guangzhou, has been rejected by Philippine historians and officials, who dispute the scholars’ reading of geography, dynastic administration, cultural links and post-war treaties.

Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China, to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington opposes any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.

Beijing has not formally adopted the scholars’ position on Batanes, but the discussion has fuelled concern in Manila that an apparently academic argument could be used to test or shape a wider narrative around the islands, which sit near one of the most important waterways between the South China Sea and the Pacific.

The Guangzhou symposium was convened in reaction to a May 28 joint statement by the Philippines and Japan that the two would negotiate to delimit their exclusive economic zones and continental shelves, including areas east of Taiwan also claimed by mainland China.

Symposium participants said the Philippines-Japan agreement infringed on China’s sovereignty, and that the Batanes islands belonged to China.

“The islands are not merely closer to Taiwan in distance, but constitute a natural geographical extension of Taiwan,” said Ju Hailong, dean of the School of International Studies at Jinan University.

In a statement issued on Friday rejecting the symposium’s claims, the National Historical Commission of the Philippines challenged that geographical argument, saying “current satellite and oceanographic data by the Philippines and other nations show a continuous shelf extending from northern Luzon through the Babuyan and Batanes Islands, and into parts of the Taiwan archipelago”.

It said “the Philippines claims a greater right over the subject territories from this perspective”.

Ju had claimed that during the Ming and Qing dynasties, the islands were under the jurisdiction of the Taiwan prefecture.

But the commission said “the earliest known extensive documentation of the Batanes Islands by British explorer William Dampier in 1687 noted no trace of Chinese governance over the islands”.

It noted that “since 1783, the Philippine government and its legal predecessors have held continuous and undisputed sovereignty over Batanes”.

The symposium also claimed the Ivatan residents of Batanes could be linguistically and culturally linked with Taiwan’s Tao people, with heritage originating from mainland China.

The commission noted instead that the Ivatan people “lived in protected communities and traded with other maritime trading nations”.

According to the Chinese scholars, post-World War II treaties required Japan to return all territories it had seized from China and, because they regarded Batanes as part of Taiwan, the islands should have been included.

The commission rejected that premise, saying the islands could not be returned because “Japan cannot give to China what clearly belongs to the Philippines”.

In its statement, the commission rejected the Chinese scholars’ assertions, saying they had “no rational basis in substantive research and operate from evident bad faith. They would never stand under scrutiny in academic discourse outside of the People’s Republic of China.”

Philippine foreign affairs department acting spokeswoman Analyn Ratonel said on Friday: “Flights of fancy should not be dignified with a response. Nevertheless, the Philippines’ sovereignty over Batanes is settled and not up for debate.”

She pointed out that when Manila approved a consular district of the Chinese consulate general in the northern city of Laoag, the Chinese request explicitly included Batanes.

Some Philippine officials and analysts said the symposium should not be viewed only as an academic event, warning that it could help test or shape public perceptions around Batanes.

Renato de Castro, a distinguished professor in the international studies department of De La Salle University Manila who once taught at Jinan University in 2007, said the symposium was unlikely to have taken place without political space from Beijing, arguing that “this is probably a prelude to a possible offensive in terms of psychological warfare”.

Speaking in a news forum on Saturday, retired rear admiral Roy Vincent Trinidad, spokesman for Philippine operations in the South China Sea, described what was happening as “salami-slicing in the information domain”.

He said it could begin with a simple statement before moving towards a point where “it’s officially adopted by the government”.

Former congressman Robert Ace Barbers, who once offered his province of Surigao del Norte to host US troops, told This Week in Asia that claiming Batanes “is another part of bullying, this is what we call creeping invasion”.

Raymond Powell, director of SeaLight, a maritime transparency project of Stanford University’s Gordian Knot Centre, cautioned during the news forum on Saturday that dismissing such claims would be “playing directly into Beijing’s hand”, as they served a broader purpose.

“The Chinese government needs the ‘possibility’ that it owns Batanes to justify” its actions in waters claimed by the Philippines, Powell said in a social media post on Monday, adding that Beijing “isn’t ready to say it directly” when it came to Batanes, therefore it “left it to academics”.

Philippine Defence Secretary Gilberto Teodoro called the Chinese scholars’ assertions a “quasi-state statement”.

The concern is sharpened by Batanes’ location near Taiwan and the Luzon Strait, a strategic corridor between the South China Sea and the Pacific.

Batanes comprises 10 islands, with the northernmost one, Mavulis, a mere 142km southeast of Taiwan. It is being built up by the Philippine military as part of its Comprehensive Archipelagic Defence Concept, which refocuses the country’s security strategies to include defence of its exclusive economic zone.

Teodoro has described the islands as “the spearhead of the Philippines as far as the northern baseline is concerned”.

In 2024, a Naval Forward Operating Base was constructed on the main island of Batan, while structures were built in Mavulis to house members of the Naval Forces Northern Luzon, a unit dedicated to defending the area.

Military drills have also taken place there in the two recent Balikatan, or shoulder-to-shoulder, exercises with the US and other nations, including Philippine and US marines rappelling from Black Hawk helicopters to the remote Batanes town of Itbayat.

Dr Ricardo Jose, an emeritus professor of history specialising in World War II, told This Week in Asia in 2024 that Batanes was strategically important in any conflict involving Taiwan.

Japanese forces had seized the airfield in Basco, Batanes, “because from Taiwan, Basco is almost halfway between Taiwan and the northern Luzon tip”.

De Castro said in a forum by think tank Stratbase on Friday that “the northern Philippines is key terrain – the control of which will give you a marked advantage over your adversary.”

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

深层结构分析
消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。
