item.title: [SCMP] Mujun Planet: one man’s quest to revive Japan’s artisanal legacy
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1954

AI Summary:

- **Founder and Project**: Shinya Kobayashi, a 39-year-old designer and artist, established "Mujun Planet" on a mountain in Shimane prefecture, Japan, a self-sustaining artisanal village built over six years to address the crisis of craftsman succession. - **Facilities and Focus**: The site includes a blacksmith's forge, sawmill, woodworking shop, and charcoal kilns, with initial emphasis on metalworking. Knives forged there are sold globally through his distribution company, Coelacanth Shokudo. - **Operational Model**: The project aims for self-sufficiency by using local raw materials, such as iron sand and charcoal from nearby Yunotsu for blades, and locally harvested wood for handles, reducing reliance on imported tools. - **Broader Context**: Kobayashi's work responds to the accelerating decline in

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Shinya Kobayashi points his phone camera at a sleek, black reptile, creating a trinity of S-shapes in the dirt, before calmly walking on. It is a remarkably cheerful day, with blue skies overhead and dense, emerald forest all around.

The 39-year-old designer, artist and father is taking me on a virtual tour of his own mountain, in Japan’s Shimane prefecture, along Honshu island’s western fringe. Nearby are a recently built blacksmith’s forge, sawmill, woodworking workshop, charcoal kilns and a renovated 200-year-old farmhouse, currently serving as storage space.

Mujun Planet, as Kobayashi has named the mountain and its tiny “village”, has been six years in the making. While the hardware might seem modest, he hopes its impact on the neighbouring town of Yunotsu, and the broader realm of Japanese craftsmanship, will be profound.

Metalworking is his focus for now. Blades from the forge made into knives are sold in Japan and around the world through Coelacanth Shokudo, his design company that, among other activities, manages the wholesale distribution of artisanal products.

All this and more are chronicled in the Hyogo native’s recently released, self-published book, Why Did Mujun’s Shinya Kobayashi Buy a Mountain?

“I have long worked to resolve the issue of artisan succession,” he tells me. “However, there are numerous problems that cannot be solved without entering the mountains to work, and I want people to understand why.”

To appreciate the seemingly tenuous connection between the two is to plunge into the depths of Kobayashi’s world – an adventure he has chosen for himself. On the surface, his mission revolves around self-sufficiency, breaking free from the dependence on imported tools necessary for various artisanal endeavours. His mountain and its surroundings serve as a natural reservoir of raw materials.

Take the knife – an essential tool across all crafts. It is why a forge was among the first components at Mujun Planet, operated by 29-year-old blacksmith Koharu Yamaguchi. As Kobayashi enters her workspace during our virtual tour, visible behind her is a tray of neatly arranged blades, their sharp edges catching the light.

Yamaguchi receives a steady supply of iron sand and charcoal from Yunotsu, historically renowned for tatara, the ancient Japanese method of ironmaking. For the knife handles, wood is harvested from the large branches of trees, as part of ongoing forest maintenance.

“The decline of craftsmanship has accelerated dramatically in recent years,” says Kobayashi. “I have always believed that nurturing successors for steelmaking artisans is vital to stemming this tide, and this conviction has now become a tangible reality.”

He is not alone in his beliefs. Growing numbers of young Japanese are eager to pursue careers in artisanal trades, and many are modernising traditional processes and finding innovative ways to share their stories. But few have adopted Kobayashi’s maverick methods.

His decision to buy a mountain is closely tied to his mission of promoting satoyama. Literally meaning “village” (sato) and “mountain” (yama), it originally described the landscape between these two realms.

Over time, it has evolved into a philosophy that deeply respects nature and tradition, advocating sustainability and living in rhythm with the seasons.

The past six years have been dedicated to laying a foundation for this vision. At Mujun Planet, overgrown trees have been cleared, mountain streams rejuvenated and neglected agriculture plots revived and nurtured.

As much as possible, local cedar is used, such as for the construction of buildings. In the farmhouse, Kobayashi’s pride is palpable as he highlights a smooth, 4.5-metre-long beam – crafted from a single trunk – supporting the roof. Plans are under way to turn the space into an atelier for emerging artisans.

“Sustainability is most crucial in resolving the issue of succession,” Kobayashi says. “It exists only through cycles and it is precisely because they have been practised for so long that we have traditional crafts. Within them lies an unbroken culture and way of life.

“That’s why I believe it is essential to revive the spirit of satoyama, which has been forgotten over the last 100 years or so.”

Compounding this challenge is society’s diminishing appreciation of Japan’s mountains. The costs associated with their management and maintenance have soared, leading many families to hesitate at the prospect of passing down or, indeed, inheriting such legacies.

Kobayashi says that after World War II, conifers such as cedar and cypress were planted as part of a new national policy for post-war reconstruction. Unfortunately, the initiative was quickly overshadowed by the influx of cheap, uniform meranti wood from Southeast Asia. This coincided with a population explosion in Japan that spurred the construction of inexpensive, simple houses throughout the country.

As a result, the conifers were left to languish and are now severely degraded. Undeterred by these challenges, however, Kobayashi seized an opportunity in 2020, when he learned from a local temple that a nearby 200,000 square metre mountain was being sold for just 1 million yen (US$6,230 at that time).

Today, the Mujun Planet community comprises seven members, three of whom are full-time “residents”, including Kobayashi. Their lodgings are a two-minute drive away, between the mountain and Yunotsu, and visitors are accommodated in a guest house behind his home.

Their workday begins at 8.30am and ends at sunset. In between caring for free-range chickens, the members engage in woodworking, grass cutting and tree-felling. Kobayashi himself travels far and wide occasionally – such as to design events in Europe and, recently, Singapore – to attract others to join him on his planet.

He also gives talks and holds workshops, including hosting a training and team-building retreat for a high-end blade-making brand.

Already, his efforts are beginning to bear fruit. Kobayashi speaks of young, like-minded nationals and non-Japanese finding their way to Mujun Planet, eager to become craftspeople or to experience satoyama. One, in particular, has embraced the lifestyle wholeheartedly: a Canadian-Chinese construction worker from the United States, who wants to build a house on the mountain, is currently working with Kobayashi on its design.

Then there’s the elderly Japanese blade maker whose speciality is scissor-making. Kobayashi tells how the man had given up on finding his successor only to change his mind after learning about Mujun Planet. He now plans to help train someone to take over his business.

While it might be easy to blame the dismal state of artisan succession on youthful indifference or the inability of senior craftspeople to think outside the box, the causes run deeper.

“The root of the problem lies in the social structure itself,” Kobayashi says, adding that one consequence is the homogeneity of many Japanese regions. “Japan became an economic powerhouse with astonishing speed, alongside its post-war reconstruction. Massive capitalist economies, and [the allure of] convenience, have ultimately devoured the regions and their cultures. Most Japanese regions now look identical – the same houses, the same shops, the same scenery.”

Moreover, issues of low profitability persist, stemming from inadequate distribution channels and ignorance around branding and marketing. Kobayashi has also heard of parents who choose not to support the continuation of their crafts. These elderly artisans want their children ­­– whose university education they funded ­– to find well-paid, city-based jobs, he says.

Kobayashi understands the push and pull personally, having witnessed similar issues in his own family’s traditional paper-mounting business in Ono city, Hyogo prefecture. He recalls a bustling business during his primary school years, in the 1990s, before Japan’s economic bubble burst and fortunes declined in the 2000s. “I increasingly saw my father looking rather idle. I’d often chime in with suggestions such as, ‘Why don’t you try sales?’ ­which led to frequent arguments.”

After his father, Michiaki, took over what would become a third-generation business, he made little effort to attract more customers, believing that they would appear if he simply waited. “I felt a strong sense of crisis seeing him just waiting like that,” recalls Kobayashi. “At the same time, I gradually came to understand that it was very difficult for him to suddenly adopt new ideas or put them into action.”

External factors compounded their challenges. Paper mounting involves replacing washi paper on sliding doors, folding screens and hanging scrolls marking significant ceremonies, such as weddings and funerals.

However, many of these events have moved away from the traditional settings of homes. “Culture has disappeared from communities,” Kobayashi says, adding that most Japanese now aspire to Western-style residences, rendering such time-worn Japanese features obsolete.

“The decline of regional culture, economy and local craftsmanship feels like just the tip of the iceberg. Perhaps I still want to do something about this today – or perhaps I just wanted to help my father. He passed away in March [2025].”

Kobayashi has the foundations for action. He studied product design at the Osaka University of Arts, graduating in 2010. The following year, he founded Coelacanth Shokudo to help revitalise traditional crafts.

“I was a young man who didn’t know his right from his left, a know-it-all with a head full of design education only applicable in the city,” he says. “But my enthusiasm was second to none. I was confident that with passion, ideas and skills, I could overcome any obstacle. My sense of urgency stemmed from a feeling that if I didn’t act quickly, it would be too late.”

Since 2013, Coelacanth Shokudo has focused on developing sales channels in Europe and America, collaborating with artisan businesses to generate income. This was formalised with the launch of the wholesale brand “Mujun” (meaning “contradiction”) in 2016.

From this initiative emerged a diverse range of products, including homeware, scissors, bags and wrapping cloth (furoshiki) made from vintage kimono fabric. Two years later, Mujun Workshop was established as a platform to nurture young craftspeople who would succeed retiring artisans.

Then there are the products he has designed for others, Kinjo Japan included. Kobayashi’s silicone cup collection, sold in Hong Kong at Wing On department stores, consists of silicone-rubber drinking vessels as transparent as regular glass and inspired by the Japanese kiriko tradition of glass cutting.

Kobayashi appears to have all his ducks in a row. Back on the mountain, he swings his phone from side to side, enthusiastically discussing upcoming projects.

Coils of hosepipes await connection to a small river about 200 metres up the mountain, promising an independent water supply. A barely visible track between tall hedges will soon become a road leading to fields where rice will be cultivated. He is also expecting the arrival of a Chinese-made dynamo to provide stable, independent power.

After the tour, he sits cross-legged on a dirt road with a colleague, who multitasks as his interpreter.

“Good internet connection here,” he says, when asked if he’d prefer moving to a more comfortable spot, such as the cedar-wood picnic bench and table built by the community.

He hopes to continue nurturing the satoyama way of life through Mujun Planet, while forging new connections domestically and internationally. On the family front, his wife, Misato, is learning the art of creating hanging scrolls from his mother, Misako, and he is exploring introducing the technique into his own art. Although his family’s paper-mounting enterprise ended with his father, some of the skills promise to live on.

“My ultimate goal is to sustain this satoyama life with my family. My dream is to be a sculptor, carving stone and wood using chisels I forge myself from iron sand,” he says. “I want to create a future where families engaged in various crafts live in these mountains, where children roam freely through the hills, drink mountain water and catch insects.”

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

深层结构分析
消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Liu Qian on Chinese innovation, the US rivalry and women holding up half the sky
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 3764

AI Summary:

- **Broad Scope of US-China Rivalry**: Liu Qian states the competition extends beyond trade into technology, ideology, and global collaboration, warning that continued unhealthy rivalry would harm both nations and the world. - **Limited Direct Economic Impact from Tariffs**: Exports now account for about 19% of China's GDP, with US-bound exports representing only about 2.8% of total GDP. Even a complete loss of the US market would impact only 2-3% of China's economy. - **Resilience and Shift in Export Markets**: Chinese exporters have adapted resiliently, and export portfolios have shifted significantly. The US is now the third-largest export destination, behind ASEAN and the EU, with fast-growing exports to markets like Brazil and Africa. - **Expert Profile**: Liu Qian is the founder of Wusawa Advisory, former managing director of The Economist Group in Greater China, and the sole Chinese representative in the UN Women Leaders Network core working group.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Liu Qian is the founder of Wusawa Advisory and formerly the managing director of The Economist Group in Greater China. She is also a prominent advocate for gender equality, and the only Chinese representative in the core working group of the UN Women Leaders Network. In this interview, she discusses how Chinese innovation differs from Western innovation, where the US-China rivalry is headed and the critical role of women’s voices in policymaking.

This interview first appeared in SCMP Plus. For other interviews in the Open Questions series, click here.

In recent months, much of the discourse around China’s economy has centred on its external challenges, particularly on how US tariffs might impact Chinese exports. Is the rise in global trade protectionism a significant cause for concern, or is this an overemphasised aspect of China’s economic reality?

To start, it’s important to first note that the current US-China rivalry extends beyond trade and has expanded into areas such as technology, ideologies and global collaboration. If this unhealthy competition continues, it will hurt not only both countries but also the world.

That said, in terms of trade and economics, the concern has been exaggerated. At the peak of exports’ importance to the Chinese economy, they comprised around 36 per cent of China’s GDP. Today, they make up only about half of that, after accounting for roughly 19 per cent of China’s GDP last year. And within that 19 per cent, exports to the US made up only 14.7 per cent of the total, behind the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (Asean) and the European Union.

If you do the maths, that means exports to the US made up around 2.8 per cent of China’s GDP. So, even if the tariff war were to wipe out the entire US export market, it would only impact about 2 to 3 per cent of China’s economy.

China’s largest export destination is now Asean. Even though part of the exports to Asean might eventually go to the US – let’s say, hypothetically, half of the 16 per cent that goes to Asean – it would still represent only a small proportion of China’s GDP.

Obviously, there are broader and longer-term elements to consider regarding tariff wars. For instance, unemployment will hurt private consumption and social stability; uncertainties will cloud business sentiment and investment strategies; and supply chains will need to adjust to accommodate the shifting demand.

But we have seen exporters adapt resiliently and creatively – via different routes, to new markets, and with different products or even packaging. And exports have generally held steady this year.

More importantly, there are fast-emerging export destinations beyond the US. China’s exports to Brazil, for instance, grew by 23.3 per cent last year, and exports to Africa for the first eight months this year, by 25.9 per cent. The export portfolio of China has shifted significantly over the years.

So, while many people still tend to think China relies heavily on exports – especially to the US – exports are no longer as central to China’s economy as before, and the US’ importance has waned proportionally. The Chinese domestic economy now matters far more to China than exports do.

I want to argue against the concept of raising private consumption to grow an economy. It should really be the other way around

Domestically, China is pinning hopes on private consumption being a key economic growth driver, with the government rolling out a series of initiatives to boost spending. How effective has this been?

Not very, and I think focusing on private consumption for the sake of consumption itself is the wrong strategy. Yes, private consumption is now a major engine for China’s economy, but understanding why it hasn’t been growing as fast as expected in recent years is crucial. I’d say there are three reasons.

The first one is income. People were used to disposable income growing by double digits, or at least above 8 per cent, for two decades – until Covid hit. Nowadays, the growth is no longer as much as before.

The second reason is unemployment, which is quite a worry and concern for China at the moment. The government even stopped publishing the unemployment rate for young adults in 2023 [for five months while recalculating the figure to exclude students]. The official number was above 20 per cent then, but some labour economists said the actual number may have been much higher.

The third reason is the wealth effect. More than 90 per cent of Chinese households own homes, and more than 20 per cent of Chinese households own multiple homes.

Previously, when the property market was growing well, even if you weren’t working, you were becoming wealthier every day just by owning a house. Now, with property prices down, many lack that sense of wealth and security to spend.

For consumers, if their income is not growing as fast, if they have a much higher chance of becoming unemployed, and if their wealth is shrinking, why would they have the confidence to spend? Lots of policy suggestions involve giving people subsidies to encourage them to spend, but if the fundamentals aren’t there, then direct stimulus and subsidies would only be scratching the surface.

And on a deeper level, I want to argue against the concept of raising private consumption to grow an economy. It should really be the other way around, that people will spend more if a country and its people are richer. Here’s an easy way to think about this: no household can become richer by spending more. When a family makes more money, then it will spend more – not the other way around. Higher private consumption as a proportion of GDP is the result – not the cause – of a country’s growth.

If we look at World Bank data from 2023, countries such as Somalia and Haiti have the highest ratio of household consumption as a percentage of GDP, above 100 per cent, while developed countries such as Ireland and Singapore have some of the lowest ratios of private consumption to GDP. Thus, spending more to grow a country is not a universal formula for success. We shouldn’t spend for the sake of spending; rather, when a country grows richer, its people can afford to spend more.

If we place excessive emphasis on mistakes, then the safest thing is to not do anything, and thus, it’s guaranteed that no mistakes will be made

So, what do you see as the most significant and promising bright spot in China’s economy?

There are four elements in a classic macroeconomic model of growth: land, capital, people and technology. The technology factor is the exponential element – and China’s tech sector and innovation can give us a lot of hope. Behind this are the very hardworking and resilient Chinese people. And the labour productivity in certain private sectors is particularly impressive.

In a way, I think of China’s innovation as if the country is an enormous venture capital field. For instance, Silicon Valley is characterised by failing fast and pivoting fast. There’s a lot of competition, there’s a lot of money supporting that, and entrepreneurs are very much encouraged to try. If it’s right, brilliant – they make it. If it’s wrong, they fail, but then they pivot very quickly, and they move on to something new and hopefully right the next time.

China, in a sense, operates similarly. It has so many hardworking and innovative people, and they’re trying all these new things. But the problem now is that the prevailing mentality is focused on avoiding mistakes rather than celebrating successes.

Obviously, we need to prevent and punish careless and irresponsible mistakes. But a balance is needed – if we place excessive emphasis on mistakes, then the safest thing is to not do anything, and thus, it’s guaranteed that no mistakes will be made.

The current macro and policy environment is not yet friendly enough for entrepreneurs in China. They still have many concerns, with many uncertainties, and they don’t feel fully empowered. I would really hope to see the macro policy environment shift more towards celebrating successes – that it’s OK to fail, as long as you have good intentions and keep trying to succeed – rather than stressing the avoidance of mistakes.

Some critics argue that Chinese innovation mainly re-engineers existing global technologies rather than achieving foundational breakthroughs. Does China truly innovate? How would you characterise China’s innovation style?

Chinese innovation differs markedly from Western innovation, but that doesn’t mean China isn’t innovative. In fact, China is very innovative.

The Western notion of innovation – as outlined in Peter Thiel’s book, Zero to One – is about breakthroughs and creating something brand new. Chinese innovation takes a different approach. Let’s consider the comparison of “zero” to “one”, and I’ll outline four types of Chinese innovation.

First, China often takes a “one” and turns it into “1.1”, but on a massive scale. China has a population of more than 1.4 billion and can sell to the rest of the world, which has a population of more than 8 billion – this is a large market. So, if China achieves “1.1”, it can potentially scale that to “8.8 billion”.

A prime example is Ant Group. It originated from the 2004 launch of Alipay, six years after PayPal. But then it began adding its own characteristics, services and products, and then scale – the idea being, as the name “Ant” suggests, that it’s small but abundant. [Ant is an affiliate of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the Post.]

Alipay was integrated into Ant Group, which was founded in October 2014. Within just six years, the company’s estimated market cap when it was initially going for IPO in 2020 was US$310 billion, which was huge for a relatively young company. It began not with a new concept, but by adding its own innovative characteristics and applying them to the mass market. It wasn’t “zero” to “one”, but it was “one” to “1.1”, then to over a billion, and that’s also innovation.

The second type of innovation involves taking an existing “one” and making it in a different and possibly better way. A good example is DeepSeek. It was founded only two years ago, but in January, everyone was talking about it – it swept the world away. Basically, it uses a very different computational method than ChatGPT. It’s more efficient and it does it cheaper. So, yes, OpenAI started ChatGPT – amazingly significant – but DeepSeek does the “one” differently, and possibly better.

The third type of innovation is taking different “ones” and combining them into a “10”. A good example would be WeChat. In the beginning, WeChat’s parent company, Tencent, started with QQ, and QQ was learning from ICQ. And then, over the years, while Silicon Valley produced products such as Facebook, WhatsApp and Instagram, WeChat became a combination of all of them, and you can even do online shopping like on Amazon, and hire taxis like on Uber.

It almost serves as a Windows system – a platform for everything. Many people don’t even use Baidu to search for things any more; they will just use WeChat, because it’s now this information universe. You go there, and you can get everything done. This is a third form of Chinese innovation, starting with different “ones” and merging them into a “10”.

The fourth type of innovation focuses intensely on “0.1” within a “one”, making that element the best in its class and applying it across sectors. An example would be CATL, which doesn’t make entire electric cars, but specialises solely on the “0.1” – the batteries. It’s the largest firm in the industry and powers everything. It already supplies batteries for a third of the electric vehicles around the world, with Tesla also being an important client. Last year, CATL successfully tested a flight for an electric plane, and has even launched electric cruise ships.

Now, CATL’s ambition is not just to work on batteries – their ambition is to electrify whatever can be electrified. They just focus on the “0.1”, but they do it so well that they empower all the “ones” in different sectors. This is absolutely also innovation, which is why I say China just innovates differently.

The two [US] political parties can’t agree on anything, but the containment of China is one thing they can agree on

The first administration of US President Donald Trump tried to curb China’s tech advancements, but some say it accelerated China’s self-reliance instead. As we navigate a second Trump term, what new dynamics do you foresee?

I wouldn’t say it accelerated self-reliance. Rather, it forced China to pivot. For instance, China is denied access to the most premium chips, but China can use chips that are good enough and focus on their applications and business.

And China can pivot to other markets. Like I said earlier when we were talking about trade, it’s not just the US and European markets any more. We’re now in a very different phase of globalisation. Traditionally, we think about trade as China selling to the US and Europe – these were the big markets. But nowadays, the Global South is the emerging market.

And entrepreneurs always find a way. Chinese manufacturers are innovating to sell better and cheaper products, and to more people and different countries. At the core of economics, it’s all about incentives. Money motivates, and people will find a way.

This time, though, President Trump has accumulated more power. At the moment, it’s mostly related to trade and economics, but I am concerned that it will escalate to other areas as well, such as security and finance realms. And it’s important to note that it’s not just about China itself; we also have to look at American domestic politics.

Politically, it’s a more divided country at the moment. And I joke – but I mean it – that with all the political, economic and social divisions in the US right now, the only thing that unites the US is China. The two political parties can’t agree on anything, but the containment of China is one thing they can agree on. So, I am worried that there will be more attacks on China, and they won’t just be trade and technology related.

You’ve previously alluded to the Thucydides Trap in the context of US-China relations. Is conflict inevitable, and what can the US, China and the international community do to avert such an outcome?

Professor Graham Allison’s Thucydides Trap theory provides a very insightful, alarming and powerful historical reference point for US-China dynamics. Out of 16 past cases when there was one existing dominant power and one emerging power, 12 resulted in war. So, it’s telling us that there is a very high likelihood of conflict, and we need to be alarmed, but at the same time, war is not inevitable.

For instance, it’s very important to note that when Sparta and Athens went to war, neither of the kings actually wanted to go to war. It was really the very hawkish people on both sides who made it impossible for the kings not to go to war. In a way, the kings had to give in to public sentiment.

On this, ironically, China can have better cards to play. Relatively speaking, China can more effectively manage and steer public opinion. This offers some hope – that what makes the US and China different from the Thucydides Trap is that China’s side could avoid being hijacked by irrational public sentiment.

The Thucydides Trap is an important theory and stark warning, but psychology suggests that what we focus on can actually lead to what we get. So, while it’s important we are fully alert to the risks of war and do everything we can to prevent it, overemphasising risks, on the other hand, could lead us to act in ways that increase the possibility of war, as both sides start to act more defensively and inward-looking rather than exploring more possibilities of collaboration and joint interests. I don’t know which of the two is better, but averting conflict will require very self-disciplined and wise world leaders to collectively make the right, informed choices.

And this is not just about the US and China. In physics, the most stable shape is a triangle, but we currently lack a third pillar that can stabilise the two countries. Before, I had hoped Europe could play that role, given its economic power and global influence, but European nations are also stuck at many fronts and they can’t, unfortunately, reach an agreement among themselves on many key issues. So, I’ve shifted my hopes to the emerging markets. Instead of being stuck between the geopolitical rivalries of the US and China and forced to choose a side, perhaps those nations could come together and broker a path forward by pressuring the two superpowers to work together.

Curiosity and personal experience are our best tools to bridge perception gaps … come to China and see first-hand

Your insights today reveal a more nuanced reality of China than is often portrayed in mainstream media. How can we correct this perception gap?

It will take quite some time to fix this, as the lenses are different, misperceptions are too prevalent, and many have moved beyond facts and rational discussions to emotions.

A lot of it is about trust building – otherwise, the two sides will only push each other away. Whether through bridges of businesses or cultural exchanges, one of the most effective ways to build understanding is for outsiders to visit China.

Even if you don’t like China at the moment, it’s worth recognising that China is important enough for you to get out of your comfort zone and see for yourself. Curiosity and personal experience are our best tools to bridge perception gaps. So, I’d say come to China and see first-hand – it’s very different.

As a woman who has navigated leadership roles in the workplace and now runs your own advisory, what are the most significant barriers limiting the career advancement of women?

There are two types of barriers: those that we can work on ourselves and those that are rooted in systemic gender inequality.

For example, globally and including in China, there’s about a 25 per cent gender income gap. Traditional wisdom says that women don’t negotiate for salaries enough. For instance, a survey in Linda Babcock’s book, Women Don’t Ask, found that men negotiate their salaries eight times more often than women, on average. This is something that, as women, we ourselves can do better, because traditionally we have been brought up to be more amicable, accepting and kind, as opposed to being assertive and firm in asking for what we want.

But a study from UC Berkeley two years ago found that women graduates from a top MBA programme were actually negotiating more often than their male counterparts – yet were rejected more frequently. This highlights the systemic barriers: even when we adopt behaviours traditionally seen as successful, we might still be met with resistance.

Another critical barrier is the disproportionate burden of domestic work and childcare. In China, while women are now pursuing higher education in greater numbers than men and maintain a relatively high participation in the labour force, women still do 2.6 times more housework and childcare after marriage. This imbalance will have to be changed for women to be able to contribute more to economic growth and social development.

But despite the barriers, I think we’re already making big advancements overall. I believe that the rise of female power – and the rise of China – are the two key defining themes of this century.

We must ensure our voices are heard – not just for ourselves, but for the nations’ economic growth and social progress

What unique opportunities do you see for women today?

Women view the world, and change the world, differently.

Our lenses are different and can be the key ones. Take the field of economics as an example – in the Chinese version of Paul Samuelson’s classic textbook, Economics, only less than half a page, out of 1,187, was dedicated to discussing women’s issues. But female economists look at economics differently.

For example, in 2022, a group of Chinese male economists argued in a report that the reason Chinese people were no longer having as many babies was that it was too expensive. They calculated the direct costs of raising a child in China, from birth to 18 years old – including milk powder, housing and education – to be around US$66,000.

I have complex emotions for this study. On one hand, it’s an important study, and I much appreciate their work in calculating this and the attention it drew from government and society and the widespread debate it sparked. But on the other hand, it was a classic example of how male economists view the world differently – they looked only at the direct out of pocket costs.

This approach overlooks a critical concept in economics: opportunity cost. Leading international economic studies show that, on average, a woman sacrifices about 20 per cent of her lifetime income to have a child. This is known as the parenthood penalty, also referred to as the motherhood penalty, since it disproportionately affects mothers.

So, I worked with professors Zhao Yaohui and Zhang Xiaobo from Peking University to examine this issue in China. We estimated that the motherhood penalty alone costs US$78,000 – more than all the direct costs of raising a child combined.

Fertility policies need to be focused in the right direction. If they consider only the direct costs studied by male economists, rather than the more important indirect costs, i.e. the motherhood penalty, then the policy direction would be wrong, resources misallocated and results not effective.

That’s why it is important to have more women, whether as government officials, economists or business managers, at the table. We must ensure our voices are heard – not just for ourselves, but for the nations’ economic growth and social progress.

China’s famous saying, that women hold up half the sky, captures this well. Ultimately, we’re all carrying the torch from previous generations on to the next. While there are times we take two steps forward and one step back, I’m convinced that – especially given rising education levels and economic opportunities – we will find our way.

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

深层结构分析
消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] How Hong Kong is fighting to redefine its future with an institutional moat, wealth surge
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 2164

AI Summary:

* **A debate persists over Hong Kong's future**, with critics like Stephen Roach citing a loss of autonomy, China's economic slowdown, and geopolitical tensions, while proponents point to strong financial indicators like its top-ranked IPO fundraising and high placement in global financial center indices. * **Hong Kong's traditional economic "moat" has eroded** as mainland China developed its own infrastructure, leading to losses in dominance like shipping (overtaken by Shanghai) and economic scale (surpassed in GDP by Shenzhen in 2018). * **The city is attempting to redefine its competitive edge** by relying more explicitly on its institutional strengths, such as its rule of law and regulatory framework, to adapt to a changing world order where its role as an exclusive gateway to China is diminishing. * **Some sectors show resilience and change**, with signs its property market may have bottomed out, though it faces volatility, and its status as a top global financial hub remains a key asset it seeks to protect.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
For decades, warnings of Hong Kong’s terminal decline have arrived in periodic waves.

When Stephen Roach, the former chairman of Morgan Stanley Asia, ignited a firestorm with his February 2024 assertion that “Hong Kong is over”, he pointed to a toxic cocktail of domestic and external pressures.

Roach, who doubled down on his “wake-up call” a year ago, argued that the city’s economic glory was being extinguished by a loss of political autonomy following 2020’s national security law, the spillover from mainland China’s protracted malaise, and the city’s precarious position in the crossfire of Sino-US tensions.

Despite the prominent American economist’s perceived gloom, the “Hong Kong is back” camp – led by a defiant local government – is wielding evidence to the contrary. The city claimed the IPO fundraising crown last year and ranked behind only New York and London in the latest Z/Yen Global Financial Centres Index.

Behind the heated debate lies a fundamental question: in an era defined by shifting capital flows, geopolitical fragmentation and evolving regulatory frameworks, how can Hong Kong keep shining as a globally leading financial centre?

“Hong Kong must adapt to a changing world order,” said Anthony Cheung, chair professor in public administration at the Education University of Hong Kong. As the city’s traditional intermediary role between China and global markets erodes, he argued, it must rely more explicitly on its institutional strengths to recalibrate its competitive position.

A shrinking moat in the wake of China’s rise

Historically, Hong Kong’s competitive “moat” – the structural barriers protecting its status – was built on its role as the exclusive gateway for global capital entering a mainland China that was only beginning to open its arms to investment.

In the 1980s, the height of this moat was measured by the city’s relative economic might. Its gross domestic product was equivalent to around 20 per cent of mainland China’s total output.

Today, that landscape has transformed following the outpaced economic take-off of the mainland – now the world’s second-largest economy and widely seen as being on a path to eventually surpassing the United States.

As the mainland has been developing its own infrastructure, Hong Kong’s traditional pillars have come under pressure. Its dominance in regional shipping has long since given way to Shanghai, which is now looking for a bigger slice of the offshore-finance pie. Meanwhile, Hong Kong’s property sector has experienced significant volatility, particularly since the pandemic, but some analysts suspect it “has become the first major city in China whose property market has bottomed”.

And in terms of GDP, Hong Kong was surpassed in 2018 by neighbouring Shenzhen – the southern metropolis home to telecoms giant Huawei Technologies, internet services firm Tencent, drone giant DJI and electric vehicle leader BYD.

Its important competitors might share one or a few advantages, but it is hard to collect all of them like Hong Kong

But despite these challenges, Hong Kong retains a rare collection of institutional advantages that remain difficult for mainland rivals to replicate. Le Xia, chief economist for Asia at BBVA Research, identified the city’s enduring moat as a combination that includes its British-inherited common law system, first-class financial infrastructure and appealing geographic location.

“Its important competitors might share one or a few advantages, but it is hard to collect all of them like Hong Kong,” he said, also noting the city’s deep talent pool and long-held reputation as a global financial centre.

Hong Hao, chief investment officer at Hong Kong-based Lotus Asset Management, put it simply: “There is no place like Hong Kong in China.”

He noted that Hong Kong remains an international capital market with free capital flows, making it the only truly global financial centre within China’s sovereign framework.

“Mainland China relies on Hong Kong more than ever as a bridge between its economy and the global market,” Hong said.

From ‘superconnector’ to ‘super value-adder’

From the launch of the “Brand Hong Kong” platform a quarter-century ago to promote the city to a global audience, to the ambitious “Hello Hong Kong” and “Hong Kong is back” campaigns of the post-pandemic era, authorities have deployed a multipronged strategy to revive the city’s status – with manoeuvres ranging from hosting high-stakes investment summits to implementing sweeping tax overhauls.

The 2025 policy address by Hong Kong Chief Executive John Lee in September pinned hopes on leveraging China’s national development strategies – including five-year development plans, the Greater Bay Area blueprint and the Belt and Road Initiative, along with a push to internationalise the yuan. Lee also unveiled the government’s GoGlobal Task Force aimed at encouraging firms across the border to use Hong Kong as a platform for expanding overseas.

Just last month, he announced plans to create Hong Kong’s first five-year development blueprint, calling the plan’s formulation a “historic step”. And on the official News.gov.hk platform, he discussed how the city government was helping both local and mainland businesses expand their global reach.

“When we talk about being a ‘superconnector’,” Lee said, “we can, of course, introduce and connect. But it is more than connect – we do value-adding.”

This strategy, aiming to not only link China and global markets, but also generate higher value across capital flows, has included outreach to the Middle East and Central Asia to woo businesses back to Hong Kong.

This ambition was on full display at the Hong Kong FIC and Bond Connect Summit on Tuesday, as officials announced the trial operation of a gold clearing and settlement system. The move, supported by expanded physical vaults, marks a “milestone” in the city’s bid to evolve into an international hub for trading precious metals, and into a future global price-setter.

Efforts have already yielded high-profile wins, including last year when Hong Kong claimed the crown as the world’s top IPO-fundraising venue, raising US$37.4 billion across 119 listings, including two of the top five globally.

And while the Hong Kong stock exchange surrendered that crown during the first half of this year to Nasdaq following SpaceX’s blockbuster listing last month, Hong Kong saw its IPO proceeds register a year-on-year rise of about 84 per cent from January to June – consolidating the region’s position as a primary offshore financial hub for Chinese issuers.

Meanwhile, assets and wealth managed in the city rose 20 per cent, year on year, to a record HK$42.2 trillion (US$5.38 trillion) in 2025, underscoring the city’s edge over Switzerland as a wealth hub, according to the Securities and Futures Commission. The city also issued two stablecoin licences in April, drawing international attention to its ambition of becoming a global crypto hub.

The battle for talent and capital

Hong Kong has pivoted towards more aggressive fiscal incentives to bolster its competitiveness against regional rivals. This includes long-time “frenemy” city Singapore, which competes with Hong Kong across several ranking lists and has lured some high-profile financial industry executives and Chinese billionaires in recent years.

In June, Hong Kong authorities proposed tax incentives targeting fund managers, including preferential treatment for performance-based bonuses and carried interest, as part of a broader effort to attract global asset-management talent.

Under the proposed framework, qualifying private equity funds approved by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority could see eligible carried interest taxed at 0 per cent – a stark contrast to the 24 per cent in Singapore and significantly higher rates in the US and Britain.

This follows the 2023 introduction of a preferential tax regime for family-owned investment holding vehicles, offering qualifying family offices a 0 per cent profits tax rate on eligible income to lower entry barriers for global wealth.

‘Beijing’s discretion’ and the fragile moat

While Beijing’s support is vital for the city, some have expressed concerns about it being a double-edged sword.

Christopher Marquis, a Chinese-management professor at Cambridge Judge Business School, argued that some of Hong Kong’s core strengths – such as currency convertibility – are not fully autonomous. “The privileges, of course, exist at Beijing’s discretion,” he said.

The city’s dominance is currently anchored in having the “world’s deepest renminbi liquidity pool outside the Chinese mainland”, with deposits of around 1 trillion yuan (US$147.4 billion), according to the Hong Kong Monetary Authority. The hub also handles more than 70 per cent of global offshore yuan payments and settlements.

If your moat is not fully under your control, can it still really be considered yours

Christopher Marquis, Cambridge Judge Business School

Although Beijing has reaffirmed its pledge to support Hong Kong’s role, analysts warn that these advantages could be under pressure. The city’s long-term pricing power in the offshore yuan market could be diluted as mainland China expands its offshore financial functions in Shanghai as part of a broader push to internationalise the currency.

Marquis argued that this made it all the more important for Hong Kong to strengthen the institutional foundations that underpin investor confidence, rather than relying solely on inherited structural advantages.

“If your moat is not fully under your control, can it still really be considered yours,” he asked.

Internationally, the rivalry between Beijing and Washington has served as a catalyst for Hong Kong’s IPO market, with analysts saying it offers an advantage over New York as a listing destination. However, appearing on the US radar is often accompanied with uncertainties.

Investor sentiment was further unsettled by comments in April 2025 from US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent that “everything is on the table”, regarding the possibility of Washington delisting Chinese companies from American stock exchanges as part of its trade negotiations, adding that it was up to US President Donald Trump to decide.

This prompted a number of firms, including Alibaba and Baidu, to reassess cross-border listing strategies. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

Jacky Tang, chief investment officer for emerging markets at Deutsche Bank in Hong Kong, said the proximity to mainland China’s corporate ecosystem and investor base had become increasingly valuable, with global capital markets becoming more fragmented and geopolitical considerations playing a larger role in listing decisions.

“Hong Kong is shifting from ‘backup venue’ to ‘default international venue’ for a growing group of Chinese issuers,” Tang said.

However, the persistent reliance on mainland issuers has raised questions over whether Hong Kong can preserve its status as a truly global financial centre. Market participants suggest that this concentration is far from sufficient, and that long-term resilience requires a more diversified issuer and investor base.

“The dominance of mainland Chinese companies in Hong Kong’s IPO market is likely to continue in the medium term,” said Tommy Ong, managing director of T.O. & Associates Consultancy, describing it as “a natural result” of Beijing’s 2024 capital market guidelines.

Mainland Chinese companies accounted for more than 90 per cent of both total funds raised and deal volume in the first half of 2026, according to a Deloitte research report published in June.

Ong recommended that Hong Kong diversify both the geographical mix of its issuers and the range of products available in its capital markets, attracting more companies from Asia, particularly the Asean bloc.

Beyond equities, Hong Kong should leverage its strengths in bond and foreign-exchange markets by expanding products such as tokenised bonds to broaden the investor base, while developing offshore renminbi-denominated commodity futures to meet rising demand for risk-hedging from Chinese companies, Ong added.

If policies are unpredictable and volatile, investors who commit significant capital will never feel secure

Since the implementation of the National Security Law in 2020, debates over Hong Kong’s institutional autonomy have intensified, with some investors reassessing long-term exposure to the city’s markets.

For many market participants, the central concern is predictability, with regulatory consistency increasingly seen as a core determinant of investor confidence.

Economist Simon Lee Siu-po said Hong Kong’s strength has long rested on its stable and rules-based environment, which allows global capital to be deployed with clarity.

But he warned that Beijing’s recent regulatory interventions, when viewed alongside broader geopolitical uncertainties, have made that predictability less certain.

“International investors, fearing their capital could be trapped or affected by sudden policy interventions, are losing the confidence required to hold Hong Kong assets over the long term,” Lee Siu-po said.

More recently, tighter oversight of cross-border capital flows and enforcement actions targeting certain forms of offshore trading have reinforced a more cautious stance among parts of the global investment community.

The economist argued that maintaining Hong Kong’s status would depend on reinforcing a transparent and stable regulatory framework that reduces uncertainty for long-term investors, rather than relying on short-term market sentiment.

“If policies are unpredictable and volatile, investors who commit significant capital will never feel secure,” he said.

Additional reporting by Zoe Chan

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

item.title: [SCMP] The great AI reckoning: how China is flipping the script on US’ new industrial revolution
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1988

AI Summary:

* The article frames the current China-US competition in AI as a modern inversion of the historical "Needham Question," which asked why the Industrial Revolution occurred in Europe, not China. Today's central question is why China is now contributing significantly to global innovation and possesses a distinct dynamism. * The West's long-held dismissive view of Chinese innovation—characterizing it as reliant on copying or subsidies—has shifted. China is now recognized as a global leader in cutting-edge fields, including electric vehicles and humanoid robots. * US officials, such as Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, have explicitly stated that China is the biggest risk to American AI leadership, underscoring the high stakes perceived by the US government. * Writer Kaiser Kuo describes this shift as "the great reckoning," where the West, and the US in particular, is forced to confront and reassess its assumptions about technological hierarchy and exceptionalism in light of China's advances.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
As the United States marks the 250th anniversary of its founding, it confronts a new world order dominated by its relationship with China. In this wide-ranging series, we examine the pressure points and possibilities in those ties, from hard tech to soft power. Here, Vincent Chow looks at how China challenges core American assumptions about innovation and technology, and the historical stakes of their competition in artificial intelligence.

In 1969, the renowned British sinologist Joseph Needham posed a series of questions about China so influential that they became known as the “Needham Question”.

At its core, Needham was trying to reconcile China’s past glories with what he saw in the China of the mid-20th century.

Namely, why did the 18th-century Industrial Revolution happen in Europe and not China, a country that had led the world in technological innovation up until the 15th century, and what were the reasons for technology and science in China stagnating for centuries thereafter?

Like Needham, Erik Baark – professor emeritus at the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology – also spent much of his decades-long career researching Chinese innovation and technology. But at a guest lecture at the Hong Kong Baptist University last month, Baark proposed a fundamentally different question – an inversion of the original.

“The central question is now this,” said Baark, author of Innovation and China’s Global Emergence. “Why is China now able to contribute to global science and innovation? More than that, why does there even seem to be an alternative dynamism in Chinese innovation?”

For decades, the West questioned China’s ability to innovate and to invent truly original technologies. China can only copy Western technology or be reliant on fat government subsidies, the critics claimed.

But this dismissive attitude has seen a clear shift in recent years as China has established itself as a global leader in a range of cutting-edge technologies, from electric vehicles to humanoid robots.

“The biggest risk to AI is China getting ahead of us,” US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said at an event last week, underscoring the magnitude of the threat the Trump administration sees in China.

Chinese-American writer Kaiser Kuo called this “the great reckoning” in a viral essay in October. The term reflects a collective “recognition” in the West of China’s technological advances, even spawning a growing tourism industry where foreign investors and policymakers go on factory tours in China to see first-hand just how advanced the country has become.

“This reckoning touches all of humanity,” Kuo wrote. “[B]ut it falls … hardest on the United States, where assumptions about exceptionalism and hierarchy are most exposed and most fiercely denied.”

In other words, it is the US today that is trying to reconcile the past and the present – with one particular technology at the centre of its worries.

For years now, artificial intelligence has been compared with the Industrial Revolution in its expected impact and importance.

German economist Klaus Schwab called AI the “fourth” industrial revolution in a 2015 book, while the release of ChatGPT by US company OpenAI in late 2022 has even spurred claims that AI has already ushered in the fifth such revolution.

Successive US administrations have endorsed such claims. Jake Sullivan, National Security Adviser during the Biden administration, said that “AI will define the future”.

More recently, the Trump administration compared AI’s importance with the Industrial Revolution, in that it “presents a profound economic inflection point with the potential to significantly increase the GDP of countries that embrace it”.

For the US, the civilisational stakes of its AI competition with China have become undeniable. For the first time since its founding 250 years ago, the US today is undergoing simultaneous technological, political and geopolitical revolutions, Kyle Chan, a fellow at the Brookings Institution, said.

Back then, the world was dominated by European empires such as Britain and France. To break away from the British Crown, American revolutionaries launched their independence campaign in 1765, just as the first stirrings of the Industrial Revolution were emerging in Great Britain with the rise of steam engines and other innovations that would transform the world from human-driven economies to ones powered by machines.

The US once again finds itself in the midst of overlapping revolutions, confronting technological, political and geopolitical revolutions all at the same time with the rise of AI, authoritarianism at home and abroad, and China.

“The US made the most of the Industrial Revolution, which transformed it into the world’s only superpower,” Chan said. “This time, in the age of AI, it is unclear whether the US or China will make the most of this new technological wave.”

Given the high stakes, both the US and China have obsessed over their AI capabilities in relation to each other, with the strength of their respective frontier foundational models drawing the bulk of the attention.

The gap was most apparent in late 2022 upon the release of ChatGPT. The development caught China by surprise, with leading Chinese experts estimating at the time that the gap was as large as three years.

But China has caught up rapidly since then. The first breakthrough was Hangzhou start-up DeepSeek’s release of R1 in January 2025, which stunned the world due to its low reported research and development cost. Almost overnight, the gap was no longer years but months.

Another major moment occurred last month with the release of GLM-5.2 by Beijing-based Zhipu AI, known as Z.ai internationally. The model showed powerful capabilities around the same time as the Trump administration curbed the roll-out of frontier US models for national security reasons, underscoring to many the alternative path that Chinese models represent.

According to research institute Epoch AI, Chinese models were more than a year behind their US counterparts as recently as May 2024. This has narrowed to as little as four months, it said. Nathan Lambert, author of the AI newsletter Interconnects, puts GLM-5.2 around seven months behind the US frontier.

China’s achievements in AI models reflect the country’s rapid advances in innovative capacity more broadly, Baark said. Last year, the country broke into the top 10 most innovative economies in the world on the World Intellectual Property Organisation’s Global Innovation Index, capping off a rapid ascent from 35th in 2013.

This came on the back of an explosion in China’s R&D spending.

A March report from the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) found that China’s R&D investment in 2024 surpassed the US once purchasing power measures were factored in, soaring to 3.93 trillion yuan (US$551 billion) last year, according to official figures.

“China was very behind decades ago in innovation, but it has caught up very fast in the past few decades,” Dong Jielin, a former researcher at Tsinghua University’s China Institute for Science and Technology Policy and one of China’s leading experts on the history of technology and innovation, said.

For decades, Dong studied the innovation ecosystems of both the US and China through roles in academia and the private sector. But her rigorous approach meant that, while acknowledging China’s undeniable advances in innovation, she still believed China had yet to master the “zero-to-one” innovation.

In the AI community, a common term researchers use to refer to such innovations is “paradigms”, meaning a technical breakthrough that lays the groundwork for an entirely new field of inquiry and innovation.

Dong also emphasises this agenda-setting quality as central to the innovation that matters most to her.

Though Chinese models have narrowed the gap with their US counterparts, all the leading ones, including DeepSeek R1 and GLM-5.2, are still trained on advanced US semiconductor chips despite years of efforts by China to achieve self-reliance across the entire AI stack.

Both DeepSeek R1 and GLM-5.2 are also the type of AI models – large language models with strong coding ability – that were first pioneered by US start-ups OpenAI and Anthropic.

“Neither DeepSeek nor Zhipu really represent zero-to-one innovation because they improve upon US inventions through better engineering,” said Dong, the author of A Brief History of Human Technological Innovation. “Maybe China might eventually solve the problem of zero to one, but it’s not there yet.”

But not everyone agrees with this definition of innovation. For Baark, the HKUST emeritus professor, the challenge posed by China to the US is precisely its alternative approach to technological innovation.

In the realm of AI models, this is most apparent in two ways. First, while US models still lead in absolute capability, Chinese models lead in another key dimension – cost-efficiency.

Over the past year, third-party benchmark firm Artificial Analysis has consistently found that Chinese models are global leaders – beating their US counterparts – in maximising the amount of “intelligence” users can get per dollar spent. This increasingly matters as the dawn of agentic AI forces users and enterprises to seek ways to keep AI costs down.

Secondly, most frontier Chinese models are released as open-weight models, meaning that their weights – the parameters encoding their “intelligence” – are made freely available for developers and enterprises globally to use and customise for their own use cases.

These cost advantages have made Chinese models a prime target for US accusations that they are achieved through the “theft” of US models, with Anthropic and numerous US politicians all increasingly vocal in levelling the charge.

The claim is that Chinese models are entirely reliant on the “distillation” of US models, in which Chinese models are illicitly trained on US model outputs.

For Baark, the irony is that the US itself engaged in “industrial espionage” throughout much of its early years more than two centuries ago. Back then, it was the British who saw the strategic value of its new inventions and sought to make sure it would not fall into the hands of the young US, the rising power at the time.

Famously, the first US treasury secretary, Alexander Hamilton, coordinated an elaborate espionage campaign to steal and bring these technologies to the US.

“The US itself went through this catching-up process during the Industrial Revolution that China today is in,” Baark said.

“I wish they could therefore put some of their security worries aside and see the emergence of other centres of innovation as something they can actually benefit from.”

The cries of foul play also increasingly ring hollow in the Global South, where customers and firms are increasingly priced out of accessing frontier US models at premium prices.

Vince Iswara, CEO of DANA, one of Indonesia’s biggest digital wallet providers with more than 200 million users, said the company’s growing integration of Chinese open-weight models had led to a 50 per cent gain in company productivity while helping to keep R&D costs down.

Innovation under heavy cost and technological constraints is a daily reality in emerging markets. Iswara cites China’s digital payments revolution as typical of this model of innovation, where mass adoption of e-wallets was achieved through the proliferation of QR codes, in contrast to a Western payment model revolving around credit cards, which require expensive equipment such as card machines and ATMs.

Such constraints will not be unfamiliar to Chinese AI model developers. According to Epoch AI, the US has five times more AI supercomputers and three times better cutting-edge semiconductor chips than China.

Long-standing US export controls have restricted Chinese access to advanced US semiconductor chips from the likes of Nvidia, delivering a significant compute advantage for their US rivals.

But still, even with these difficulties, China has managed to remain competitive at the frontier of AI. For countries with even fewer resources, that in itself is a model of innovation.

“The China model is much more replicable for mass adoption [of new technologies] in emerging markets,” Iswara said. “There are a lot of things to learn from China.”

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

深层结构分析
消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Why South Korea’s submarine exports are running aground
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1023

AI Summary:

* South Korea lost two major recent submarine export bids: Canada's CPSP project (awarded to Germany's ThyssenKrupp Marine Systems) and Poland's Orka program (awarded to Sweden's Saab Kockums). * The losses highlight a shift where geopolitical ties (like NATO/EU membership) and preferential financing (e.g., the EU's €150 billion SAFE program, which Canada joined) increasingly outweigh pure commercial competitiveness. * This setback signals serious challenges for South Korea's submarine industry, which may face a shortage of new orders, potential excess capacity, and may need to restructure or pursue more local manufacturing partnerships abroad.

]]>
