item.title: [SCMP] Super Typhoon Bavi: Shanghai expects fallout from Friday, Beijing halts outdoor activities
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1078

AI Summary:

* **Scale and Forecast:** Super Typhoon Bavi covers approximately 940,000 sq km. It is forecast to move northwest, potentially making landfall in or brushing past Taiwan on Saturday before approaching Fujian or Zhejiang province with rainfall up to 40 cm. * **Official Warnings:** China's National Meteorological Centre has issued an orange typhoon alert (the second-highest level) and emphasized that heavy rain and impact will affect a much wider region than just the point of landfall. * **Widespread Disruptions:** Significant travel disruptions are occurring, with airlines in Hong Kong and Taiwan cancelling or rescheduling dozens of flights to and from destinations like Taipei, Okinawa, and Zhoushan. * **Regional Impact Timeline:** Shanghai expects to feel the outer effects from Friday with increasing wind and rain. Beijing has also halted some outdoor activities in preparation.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Worried residents in China are preparing for Super Typhoon Bavi to land, stocking up on supplies and rethinking travel plans.

Satellite imagery from China’s official weather forecaster shows Bavi covering nearly 940,000 sq km (363,000 square miles) – an area about nine times the size of the eastern province of Zhejiang or 850 times that of Hong Kong.

Hui Xinyi, a high school English tutor in the Zhejiang capital of Hangzhou, is among those constantly refreshing weather apps and scrolling through social media for updates.

“Whether it will actually make landfall here or just brush past is still up in the air. It’s this uncertainty that makes people really panic,” she said.

Living on the 25th floor of a high-rise building, Hui plans to tape her windows and stock up on instant food and bottled water in case strong winds cause power or water shortages. She said disruptions might force her to move her classes online or postpone them altogether.

Tang Wanmei, a resident of Suzhou in neighbouring Jiangsu province, was also closely monitoring the typhoon’s path. She is scheduled to fly from nearby Shanghai to Yunnan province in the southwest on Monday and is worried that the storm could derail her travel plans.

“My biggest concern now is whether my flight from Shanghai will be cancelled,” she said.

Her worry is widely shared. On the Chinese social media platform RedNote, many users expressed concern about the cost of changing flights and hotel reservations, while others said airlines had yet to announce whether flights would be affected.

In Shanghai, strong wind and rain on Thursday were accompanied by a slight drop in temperature. From Friday, the city is expected to be affected by outer layers of the typhoon, with increasing wind and scattered showers.

Bavi had weakened slightly on Thursday morning but still maintained super typhoon status, the National Meteorological Centre said.

It was forecast to move northwest at up to 20km/h (12mph), making landfall in or brushing past Taiwan on Saturday before closing in on Fujian or Zhejiang province, where it could bring heavy rainfall of up to 40cm (15.7 inches).

The forecaster urged the public not to focus solely on the super typhoon’s point of landfall, warning that extreme rainfall would impact a much wider region.

On Thursday morning, the centre upgraded its typhoon alert to orange, the second-highest level on a four-tier system.

Flight schedules across the region have been severely disrupted. Hong Kong Airlines cancelled flights between Hong Kong and Okinawa and Taipei for Friday and Saturday, while Greater Bay Airlines cancelled its Hong Kong-Taipei and Hong Kong-Zhoushan services.

Airlines operating out of Taiwan cancelled or rescheduled dozens of flights as weather authorities prepared to issue warnings. The island’s Central Weather Administration issued a sea warning on Thursday afternoon, with a land warning expected early on Friday.

Taipei’s China Airlines announced widespread disruptions, bringing forward one Taoyuan-Okinawa service on Thursday before cancelling all Taoyuan-Okinawa and Kaohsiung-Okinawa flights on Friday. The carrier also cancelled all departures from Taoyuan to North America, Europe and Australia scheduled after 6pm on Friday.

Tigerair Taiwan adjusted several Japan flights on Thursday, bringing forward its Taoyuan-Okinawa and Okinawa-Kaohsiung services while delaying round-trip flights between Taoyuan and Nagoya.

Taipei’s Starlux Airlines warned that flights between July 9 and July 12 could be delayed, rescheduled or cancelled because of the storm, urging passengers to check the latest flight information before travelling.

Thai VietJet Air cancelled four services linking Bangkok, Taoyuan and Okinawa scheduled for Friday, while China Southern Airlines scrapped several Taiwan routes on Friday and Saturday, including flights between Taipei and Wuhan, Shanghai, Shenzhen, Guangzhou and Zhengzhou.

Taiwan’s Maritime and Port Bureau said most ferry services to offshore islands would be suspended on Friday and Saturday because of rough seas, while its Highway Bureau planned to close the Tamkang Bridge on Friday, as wind speeds were expected to reach a severe Force 10.

Residents across Taiwan rushed to stock up on emergency supplies ahead of the typhoon’s arrival.

The storm’s indirect impact will also be felt in northern mainland China. Officials in Beijing announced an emergency response on Thursday afternoon – suspending school, sporting events and hazardous outdoor activities while encouraging residents to work from home.

On Wednesday night, authorities in the Taishan tourist spot in eastern Shandong province announced a temporary closure of the scenic mountainous area, urging hikers to descend as soon as possible. They said severe weather was expected, including thunderstorms, strong winds, hail, tornadoes and short-term heavy rainfall.

In the south, people continued to deal with the aftermath of Typhoon Maysak, in which 39 people died and nine remain missing in Guangxi Zhuang autonomous region, according to authorities in the provincial capital Nanning on Thursday.

Landslides, floods, strong rainstorms and tornadoes have led to deaths across China since the annual flood season began on July 1, with meteorologists warning the country faces “complex” disaster prevention challenges this year.

A zoo in Guangxi has lost more than 100 animals in the flooding, with the zookeeper appealing for public help on social media.

According to an official notice issued on Wednesday, two zebras, 30 peacocks, two ostriches, nine deer and multiple parrots were among the animals lost when some of the cages at the Guigang Zoo were damaged.

“To ensure public safety and that of those animals, we implore the public to provide tips to assist in finding them,” the zoo said. “Please keep a safe distance, as some animals, including ostriches and raccoons, can attack in fear.”

At least one zebra survived the deluge, appearing momentarily at a nearby village, according to a post on social media. It was unclear whether the animal had been captured since.

Zookeeper Wang Liyuan told local media that when the flooding hit on Monday, staff secured all carnivores in their enclosures to prevent them from escaping and harming the public. However, three lions have since been confirmed to have drowned.

More than 20 species of herbivores were lost and the zoo suffered an estimated loss of 4 million yuan (US$588,000), Wang said in an interview published on Thursday. The article included an image of multiple collapsed cages and snapped trees at the zoo, all covered in mud.

Elsewhere in the city of Guigang, more than 12,000 teachers and students were stranded in school buildings by the floodwaters. They were being rescued by boat, state broadcaster CCTV said on Wednesday night.

Additional reporting by Lawrence Chung

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

深层结构分析
消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] As China’s tech firms adapt to AI era, workers worry they’ll be ‘optimised’ out of a job
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 2082

AI Summary:

- Chinese tech workers face growing anxiety over job security as companies like Meituan, Baidu, and Xiaomi conduct layoffs—often termed "optimisation"—with AI automation increasingly cited as a key driver. - The industry's focus has shifted from basic chatbots to advanced "AI agents" capable of executing complex tasks such as code writing, market research, and legal analysis, raising concerns about the replacement of white-collar roles. - Software engineering is particularly affected; major firms like Tencent and Anthropic report that a majority of code is now AI-generated, with human engineers transitioning toward supervisory and design-focused roles. - The trend reflects broader warnings from global tech leaders about AI displacing entry-level jobs, with Chinese companies actively restructuring to integrate AI into core workflows.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
When a friend checked in on a Meituan employee late last month to see if he had survived the latest round of corporate culling at the food delivery giant, he responded drily: “I don’t know whether it will be me next.”

Anxieties were running high at the company then, after chat screenshots circulated on Chinese social media claiming Meituan planned to slash up to half of its product roles by the end of June, coupled with deep cuts to other departments.

While Meituan quickly denied the rumours, the viral speculation struck a sensitive nerve across China’s tech industry. Behind the online claims, employees whisper that a quieter, more insidious form of retrenchment has been under way for months.

For years, tech workers in China have harboured a deep-seated dread of the word youhua – “optimisation”. The term serves as corporate shorthand for painful lay-offs, often masked by euphemisms like “organisational restructuring”.

This year, however, the term carries a chilling new undertone. The increasingly urgent question echoing through office corridors is no longer whether a worker is performing, but whether their job can be done by artificial intelligence.

Meituan is far from an isolated case. From Baidu to Xiaomi, China’s technology giants have been trimming their teams, according to internal sources and tech recruiters.

The shift follows prophetic warnings from global tech leaders. Last year, Dario Amodei, CEO of US generative AI pioneer Anthropic, cautioned that the technology’s rapid evolution could wipe out half of all entry-level white-collar jobs within five years.

Today, the tools driving that shift are capable of far more than conversations and data retrieval. The industry has rapidly pivoted from basic chatbots to autonomous “AI agents” – systems capable of writing code, conducting market research, analysing legal documents and executing multi-step business tasks.

Anthropic said that as of last month, more than 80 per cent of the code merged into its codebase had been written by its Claude AI model.

Software engineers, long viewed as possessing one of the most secure and sophisticated white-collar skill sets, have found themselves on the front lines of this automation wave. Coding, it turns out, is a task where AI models excel.

We now require engineers to be full-stack ... even if they are doing it side-by-side with AI

Rose Cao, talent acquisition specialist

“I have barely written a line of code by hand for almost a year,” said a veteran AI developer known to the public as Tuxi, who has worked for several of China’s major technology companies.

At a live-streamed industry event this month, senior Tencent Holdings executive Dowson Tong said most of the tech giant’s code written this year was generated by AI systems.

Tong said human engineers were becoming supervisors, spending more time focused on architecture and system design. They delegated the coding work to AI and only stepped in periodically to guide and correct its output.

Such changes have significantly raised the baseline requirements for landing or keeping a tech job.

“We now require engineers to be full-stack,” said Rose Cao, a talent acquisition specialist at a Chinese AI firm, adding that “even if they are doing it side-by-side with AI”, programmers were now expected to handle everything from front-end to back-end tasks.

AI automation is expanding beyond the engineering department. Specialised tools originally built for programmers – such as OpenAI’s Codex or Anthropic’s Claude Code – are evolving to support broader corporate workflows.

A parallel ecosystem of enterprise AI assistants has sprung up across China: Moonshot AI has pushed out Kimi Work; Tencent has rolled out WorkBuddy; ByteDance has launched Coze; and Alibaba Group Holding has introduced QoderWork CN. Alibaba is the owner of the South China Morning Post.

By embedding such tools into daily operations, businesses are eroding some of the traditional boundaries between various corporate functions, allowing a single employee to fulfil tasks that previously required a team of specialised workers.

Rather than being an optional résumé highlight, a headhunter who specialises in tech talent said that “AI fluency has become a requirement for many employers”, creating a hostile environment for fresh university graduates and inexperienced workers.

“It is hard to persuade an employer to hire a newcomer and spend more time and money training them when AI agents can already do a good job – and won’t quit,” she said.

It is a development that has prompted expressions of deep concern from some of the country’s leading economic minds. Cai Fang, a prominent labour economist and member of the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences, has warned that the initial shock wave of the AI boom will disproportionately hit “the young and the old”.

Cai’s research shows that traditional employment rates by age form an inverted U-shaped curve, where middle-aged workers experience the highest job stability, while younger and older cohorts face greater vulnerability.

He said AI was reinforcing that divide by eroding the economic value of entry-level skills for younger workers and also widening the digital capability gap for older employees who struggled to adapt to rapid technological advancement.

The economic data underlines the stakes.

China’s youth unemployment rate for 16- to 24-year-olds, excluding students, fell to 15.6 per cent in May but remained much higher than that of the whole urban population, according to data released by the National Bureau of Statistics on Monday. The Ministry of Education has estimated a record 12.7 million university graduates are preparing to enter the labour market.

But some insiders argued that blaming AI for the wave of lay-offs misdiagnosed a conventional corporate malaise.

“To say that companies are laying people off just because AI has improved workflows is basically a lie,” said Tuxi, the veteran developer, who argued that many of China’s largest tech conglomerates spent years inflating their headcount. AI had simply provided executives with a convenient justification to trim the fat, he said.

Global tech leaders have also pushed back against the narrative that AI is the primary engine of global job cuts. In an interview last month with Singaporean broadcaster CNA, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang dismissed a direct causal link between AI deployment and tech lay-offs as an explanation that was “too lazy”.

He argued that the implementation timeline of enterprise AI had not matched spikes in unemployment, suggesting that the volatility was driven by a mix of over-hiring and the staggering costs required to develop AI.

That financial strain can be seen among China’s tech titans, where resources are being aggressively diverted to fund the AI arms race.

TikTok owner ByteDance has ramped up investment in AI infrastructure this year, boosting its planned capital expenditure to over 200 billion yuan (US$29.5 billion) – an increase of at least 25 per cent compared with preliminary internal budgets discussed late last year, the SCMP reported last month.

Several current and former employees said ByteDance had been consolidating business lines to free up headcount and computing power for its flagship AI products, including its AI assistant Doubao, its AI coding environment Trae, and the Coze agent platform.

The internal realignment coincided with the rapid expansion of Seed, ByteDance’s foundation-model team. Launched in 2023, the Seed team has swollen to about 2,000 people.

Meituan is playing defence and offence. After weathering a brutal local-services price war with Alibaba that squeezed its profit margins last year, the company has trimmed secondary teams, according to employees.

However, it continues to recruit at the high end. Meituan’s “Beidou” talent programme for top-tier campus technology recruits features openings focused on next-generation frontiers: AI infrastructure, large language models, embodied intelligence, autonomous agents and drones.

Alibaba has also been trimming its headcount and pivoting its sprawling business towards AI. The e-commerce giant shed around 34 per cent of its staff last year, mainly by offloading offline retail operations, according to corporate disclosures.

The macroeconomic impact of AI on employment was expected to be neutral in the long run, GF Securities’ chief economist Guo Lei wrote in a recent report. Still, in the short term, the market might need to navigate a painful “displacement effect” before it could unlock the “reinstatement effect”, he said.

Guo said AI would fundamentally redistribute jobs, reshape the nature of jobs and alter income structures. Workers stuck in less differentiated roles would lose their labour market bargaining power, while those who mastered the use of AI stood to see their productivity soar.

As the transition accelerates, Chinese tech firms are steering clear of the blunt rhetoric used by some of their Silicon Valley counterparts.

In February, Block founder Jack Dorsey said the US payments firm would reduce its workforce from more than 10,000 to under 6,000, pointing explicitly to the deployment of “intelligence tools” and flatter, leaner organisational structures.

We must do everything possible to protect the livelihoods of hundreds of thousands of employees

Richard Liu Qiangdong, JD.com chairman

In contrast, Chinese tech companies rarely, if ever, admit publicly that human workers are being replaced by algorithms.

Richard Liu Qiangdong, the billionaire founder and chairman of e-commerce powerhouse JD.com – which employed 951,420 people across the company and its affiliates at the end of last year – stressed the company’s responsibility to protect jobs during a speech to staff last month.

“Facing the impact of new technologies … we must do everything possible to protect the livelihoods of hundreds of thousands of employees, including the jobs of our blue-collar brothers,” Liu said, making a reference to the company’s extensive logistics network.

At a recent Apec panel discussion, he said JD.com would retrain its roughly 700,000 delivery and other blue-collar workers in robot maintenance as AI and automation reshape last-mile logistics.

Beijing has stepped up regulatory oversight to ensure the AI transition does not trigger widespread social friction.

The State Council’s “AI Plus” action plan, released in August, called for stronger assessment of employment risks from AI applications, more AI skills training and guiding innovation resources towards areas with greater job creation potential, with the aim of reducing the technology’s impact on employment.

More recently, the State Council unveiled its employment blueprint for the five years ending in 2030. It placed a new mandate at the top of its agenda, saying the country must “strengthen the employment-first orientation of macroeconomic regulation … and adapt to the development of AI to promote employment and entrepreneurship”.

Chinese courts and labour arbitration bodies have also begun drawing limits around AI-related dismissals.

In April, a Hangzhou court ruled that a financial technology firm had unlawfully terminated a quality-control supervisor who had been auditing large language model outputs.

The company fired him after citing the cost advantages of replacing his labour with AI. The worker had rejected an internal transfer that would have slashed his monthly salary from 25,000 yuan to 15,000 yuan.

The court ruled that while adopting AI constituted a voluntary business choice and a standard business risk, it did not automatically qualify as a “major change in objective circumstances” – the legal threshold required under Chinese labour law to declare an employment contract impossible to perform.

The court declared that the fundamental purpose of AI should be to “liberate labour, promote employment and improve people’s livelihoods”.

In an essay prepared for this month’s Lujiazui Forum, an annual financial gathering in Shanghai, Cai, the economist, said that while AI offered a chance to lift China’s labour productivity, its disruptive side effects could not be ignored.

He warned of the risk of a widening of the digital divide between tech-forward and legacy industries, urban and rural areas, and individual workers, with short-term job displacement highly probable, creating a broader macroeconomic risk that would see household consumption lag rapid productivity gains.

The solution, Cai said, was to “invest in people and ensure that intelligence is put towards good”.

But worries persist for those toiling away in the cubicles of China’s tech hubs. Amid corporate denials of sweeping lay-offs, the quiet reality of “optimisation” continues to play out one desk at a time.

Confronted by what he viewed as an uncertain employment future, the Meituan employee contacted by a friend said “some outsourced workers have already been cleared out”, though he did not know the exact proportion.

While lay-offs happened every year, “this round feels quite big,” he said.

Additional reporting by Minxiao Chang

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Rolex Oyster celebrates 100 years of revolutionising watchmaking
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1062

AI Summary:

- The 1926 Rolex Oyster introduced the first hermetically sealed watch case, designed for everyday durability and water resistance, creating the blueprint for the modern mechanical wristwatch. - Its practical design was proven in 1927 when swimmer Mercedes Gleitze wore one across the English Channel, demonstrating its functionality. - The Oyster's innovations transformed wristwatches from fragile accessories into robust tools, helping to establish their widespread adoption for men. - Rolex's early success with the Oyster built the brand's reputation for precision and durability, aiding its growth into a leading Swiss watchmaker. - The watch's core concepts—water resistance, durability, and a screw-down crown—became standard features in the industry, defining modern horology.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Few timepieces can genuinely claim to have changed the course of watchmaking. As it celebrates its centenary this year, the Oyster stands not merely as Rolex’s longest-lasting creation, but also as the conceptual blueprint for the modern mechanical wristwatch. Its influence is embedded so deeply into contemporary horology that its once-radical ideas – water resistance, everyday durability – are now taken for granted.

Until the late 19th century, wristwatches had been regarded as feminine accessories while men relied on pocket watches that were protected from dust and moisture. Warfare transformed that convention. Soldiers, needing easy access to the time in combat, began strapping pocket watches to their wrists, giving rise to the “wristlet”. That practicality caught on beyond the battlefield, and, by the first world war, the wristwatch had firmly established itself among men, too, even though functional drawbacks still existed.

In 1926, Rolex was still far from the global force it would become. Founded in London in 1905 as Wilsdorf & Davis, by German businessman Hans Wilsdorf and his brother-in-law, Alfred Davis, the company had spent its early decades building credibility in precision timekeeping while harbouring ambitions to compete as an equal with long-established, prestigious Swiss manufacturers such as Vacheron Constantin, Blancpain and Patek Philippe.

In 1910, a Rolex became the first wristwatch to receive a chronometric certificate from the Official Watch Rating Centre in Switzerland. Four years later, another achieved the Class A certificate from Britain’s Kew Observatory, an honour previously reserved for marine chronometers. Precision was no longer the question. Durability was.

Wilsdorf understood that accuracy meant little if a watch could not survive real-life challenges. His answer arrived in 1926, by which time Rolex had relocated its base to Switzerland. The innovation that went into producing the Oyster was not simply water resistance – waterproof watches had existed in experimental forms before – but the creation of a hermetically sealed case designed for daily, long-term wear.

Rolex achieved this by engineering a system in which the bezel, case back and winding crown were screwed tightly against the middle case, forming an airtight architecture that protected the movement from water, dust and debris. The name captured the idea; like an oyster protecting the pearl within, the case became a shell safeguarding the movement. The watch could accompany its wearer through rain, travel, sport and work.

The new watch case’s first serious test came in 1927, when British swimmer Mercedes Gleitze attempted to cross the English Channel wearing a Rolex Oyster. After more than 10 hours in freezing waters, she and the watch emerged, the latter still running.

From there, the Oyster became a companion to other adventurers. On Malcolm Campbell’s land-speed record attempts in the 1920s and 30s, it endured extreme vibration, further proving that a wristwatch could maintain accuracy far beyond the controlled conditions of the watchmaker’s bench.

It accompanied pioneering aviators such as Chuck Yeager, surviving the thin air, freezing temperatures and physical strain of early high-altitude flight. It reached the highest peaks with mountaineers, proving that mechanical precision could endure where human performance itself was pushed to its limits.

Still, one challenge remained. Like all watches of its era, a Rolex required daily winding. That changed in 1931, with the Perpetual rotor. Using a freely oscillating weight powered by the motion of the wrist, Rolex created a self-winding mechanism that continuously replenished the mainspring. It eliminated the need for manual winding, reducing wear on the crown and preserving the integrity of the waterproof case.

The Oyster case and Perpetual rotor transformed the wristwatch into something new: an object that not only endured, but maintained itself, and in the process formed the foundation for an entire ecosystem of timepieces.

The Rolex Explorer, launched in 1953, was born from it. Shaped by high-altitude expeditions, it prioritised reliability and legibility – essential in low-oxygen conditions, freezing temperatures and reduced visibility.

The Submariner (also 1953) extended these principles beneath the waves, combining water resistance – initially rated to 100 metres and later 200 metres – with a rotating bezel that enabled divers to track elapsed immersion time. Its unidirectional 60-minute bezel became a safety feature, preventing accidental adjustment during a dive. High-contrast luminous markers and hands ensured legibility in low-light conditions.

The 1950s also saw the launch of the Rolex GMT-Master. Designed for Pan American World Airways pilots, it displays two time zones simultaneously and has a 24-hour rotating bezel and dedicated GMT hand that transform the watch into a navigation tool.

The Rolex Day-Date was launched in 1956 exclusively in precious metals – gold or platinum – setting it apart from the brand’s tool watches. It would become associated with political and cultural figures, most notably a United States president, Lyndon B. Johnson.

Later came the Rolex Cosmograph Daytona (1963), which was designed for racing drivers, combining a tachymeter bezel for calculating average speed, a precision timing mechanism for lap measurement and high-legibility sub-dials for use under pressure. Named after the Daytona International Speedway in Florida, its identity was amplified by celebrities such as actor and racing-car driver Paul Newman.

Other brands noticed how the watches of the Oyster family had become companions built to travel, endure and participate in life, and rose to the challenge. Omega refined water-resistant sports models and professional timing tools; Blancpain defined early modern diving capability with the Fifty Fathoms collection; IWC developed robust, engineer-led pilot’s watches; and Jaeger-LeCoultre explored durable, utility-driven complications such as those of the Memovox series.

But it had been the ingenuity of the Oyster case, 100 years ago, that opened those doors for others to follow.

To mark the centenary, Rolex has introduced commemorative Oyster Perpetual models that remain faithful to the design’s restrained identity. A 41mm version pairs a slate dial with yellow-gold detailing while a 36mm model references classic dial textures with subtle anniversary markings. Discreet engravings, including a “100” crown detail, acknowledge the milestone without disrupting the Oyster’s defining understatement.

The celebration has extended beyond product launches. In Shanghai, Rolex presented the “Oyster Story”, an immersive exhibition at the West Bund Dome that ran from June 10 to 28. The exhibition traced the Oyster’s evolution through archival pieces, technical components and interactive installations that revealed the engineering behind the design.

One hundred years on, the Oyster’s innovations have become industry standards, absorbed into the very language of watchmaking.

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

深层结构分析
消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

item.title: [SCMP] Philip Chan: from singing inspector to Hong Kong cinema legend
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1321

AI Summary:

* Born in Hong Kong in January 1945 during Japanese occupation; his grandfather was a retired military general, and his family faced financial hardship. * His mother sold his father's watch to buy milk powder for him as an infant after being refused a loan by her wealthy father. * He witnessed the 1956 riots in Mong Kok as a child, where he saw armored cars and machine-gun fire from his rooftop home. * Attended La Salle College and began his music career as the lead singer for a school band, performing at afternoon tea dances. * Followed his uncle into law enforcement, becoming a police inspector before his later career in cinema.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
I WAS BORN in Hong Kong in January 1945, during the Japanese military occupation. My parents were from Hong Kong, but my (paternal) grandfather was a general serving in the military in Guangxi. He had three wives and six children. During the civil war, he lost his command, retired to Guangzhou and then came south as the Japanese invaded. It was very tough. In fact, I read in my mother’s diary that when I was a baby, they had no money and she went to her father’s house to ask for a loan. Her father was a well-to-do businessman. He and his partners owned the Tabaqueria Filipina, which was a famous tobacco and wine company. He refused the loan because he didn’t like my father, who was just a foreman in the then-Sanitary Department. He also disliked my father because he was Cantonese and they were all Shanghainese. And my mother wrote in her diary that she was very disappointed.

SHE THEN TOOK my father’s watch and sold it on the waterfront to buy milk powder for me. The most touching part was when she wrote, “It was heartbreaking, but when I looked at my baby Kin, lying in my arms, I said to myself, ‘It’s all worth it.’”

I WAS THE FIRSTBORN, I have one sister and three brothers and we’re very tight. My most vivid memory of growing up is the riots in 1956. We lived in Mong Kok, on the rooftop of an abandoned factory, where there was a big covered area. There were about 12 or 13 households, all living there in separate wooden compartments – cubicles. Our family had two or three cubicles. It was crowded with people of various occupations – bonesetters, photographers and merchant sailors. I remember that day in October 1956, when the government tore down the Nationalist flag in Lei Cheng Uk Estate and there was an all-out riot. I was playing on the rooftop and we heard the sound of sirens. I looked down from our fourth-floor roof and I saw two armoured cars driving into Shantung Street, where our building was. British soldiers were chasing after a bunch of people dressed in black who were throwing rocks, bottles and other stuff at the soldiers. Suddenly there was a burst of machine-gun fire, then there was a boom, and a metal can shot up onto our rooftop and landed behind me. I was suddenly hit by the smoke and smell and I was in tears.

I WENT TO Good Hope School in Ngau Chi Wan, but after two years changed to La Salle College, where I stayed until I graduated in 1965. When I was in Form One or Two, I was in the school choir, and James Wong, who would later become the famous lyricist, was my senior. He played the harmonica and I admired him very much. Another schoolmate was a rhythm guitarist and part of a band called the Astro-Notes and they invited me to become the lead singer. It was the same era as the Kontinentals and Teddy Robin. We would perform at afternoon tea dances, from 3pm to 6pm, including at the Bayside, in the basement of Chungking Mansions. We also did a record with Diamond Records.

MY MOTHER’S BROTHER was a police inspector and I decided to try to enrol. I was a year younger than the minimum age requirement but Brother Casimir, my principal at La Salle, wrote a letter of recommendation, and they accepted me. I enjoyed my six months of training – firearms and combat, drills, discipline and all that. It was heaven for a young man of 19.

AT FIRST, I was sent to the Kowloon City Division as a police inspector, then I became a detective inspector very fast. In May 1974, when I was chief inspector in charge of CID (Criminal Investigation Department) in Mong Kok, there was a bank robbery. A gunman burst into the Po Sang Bank in Shanghai Street and demanded money. He fired a couple of shots inside as a warning to us, so it was quite intense. He got some money and then barricaded himself inside with 11 hostages. We had a lot of conversations with the gunman, asking him to give up. He asked for food. TVB sent a news team, and they put cameras on the rooftops. That’s when I was interviewed. Finally, the staff members jumped him, and that’s how the whole thing finished.

I HAD THREE MAJOR CASES in less than two years. The second one was the mutilation murder in August 1974 (of sex worker Lau Fu-man) in Mong Kok. When the woman’s body was found, sections of her face and other body parts had been cut off. These were found hidden between an air conditioner and a wall. We put those parts back onto her face and got an artist to draw a sketch of her to put in the newspapers. Her mother and her two children identified her from the sketch. We eventually found (the killer, Leung Siu-ping). He was a construction worker. He had strangled her. The third major case was a Taiwanese woman who, along with her lover, killed her husband, making it look like a robbery. I’ll save the details of that for my autobiography.

FOR 11 YEARS, including my time with the police, I sang on commercial jingles and as backing vocals on records, composed and written by Joseph Koo and James Wong. I did jingles for muesli, cognac and cigarettes. It was a different era. I was nicknamed the “Singing Inspector” because I wrote the lyrics and went on television to sing and promote road safety for children.

I RESIGNED FROM THE police in 1976, when acclaimed actress Siao Fong-fong and director Leong Po-chih asked me to be a researcher for their crime thriller, Jumping Ash. Coached by Siao, I learned to become a scriptwriter. She was my mentor and helped me transition to making movies. I joined the film team and learned about music selection, editing, production and sound effects. The film was a box office hit. Then, I emceed television shows.

I MET MY WIFE, Nona (Cheung Yung-yung), in 1969. She was an actress for Shaw Brothers and also Shanghainese. I have a special liking for the Shanghainese because of my mother’s side. We got married in 1973 and our first child was born in 1974. We’ve been married a long time. My wife suffers from dementia and has had that for more than 10 years. We have two daughters and we’re all very close.

THE MOST UNUSUAL PLACE I filmed in was Kowloon Walled City. That was for Long Arm of the Law (1984), directed by Johnny Mak. I wrote the script and it is my favourite film. The final gunfight scene was in the Walled City, which was infested with triads and drugs. The residents didn’t like people going in and disturbing their livelihoods. But Johnny Mak was a fantastic director and somehow we prevailed. That film left a valuable visual history of such a unique and mysterious place.

IN THE 1990s, I was in charge of Metro Broadcast, where I steered the station’s focus towards entertainment. I also worked on a lot of comedies with brothers Sam, Michael and Ricky Hui, as well as Richard Ng, John Shum and also Sammo Hung. When it comes to acting, I’m always a detective – scolding Chow Yun-fat or Jackie Chan. What I love most is directing comedies, like Chocolate Inspector (1986), with Michael Hui. I enjoy telling stories.

MY NEXT VENTURE is an autobiography, 陳欣健 – 我的風雲往事 (Philip Chan: Braving the Storms – Hong Kong 1945-1976), to tell the story of my life as an eyewitness to the tumultuous history of Hong Kong. It’s coming out on July 18 at this year’s Hong Kong Book Fair.

This interview has been edited and condensed for clarity

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述

消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Why and how the media industry can fit into Hong Kong’s first 5-year plan
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1032

AI Summary:

* Hong Kong's media industry has historically been conservative in R&D spending, treating technology as support rather than a core growth engine, which now faces an existential challenge from AI, large language models, and Web3 technologies. * These technologies are key components of China's 15th five-year plan, presenting an opportunity for Hong Kong's media sector to align with the national strategy, especially as their data (journalism, archives) is crucial for training high-quality, culturally relevant AI models. * International publishers are already monetizing this asset class; for example, News Corp signed a reported US$250 million, five-year deal with OpenAI, while Hong Kong's media industry has remained largely passive, risking both financial opportunities and local narrative control. * Government support exists but has not reached the media sector; as of March 2024, Hong Kong's Innovation and Technology Fund had approved 220 projects, with none going to media outlets, due to industry inertia and bureaucratic silos that separate media from tech start-ups. * The article argues addressing this requires a fundamental shift from both the industry (to pursue genuine innovation and commercial products) and the government (to bridge administrative silos and include media in tech initiatives).

]]>

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

深层结构分析
消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

item.title: [SCMP] How China’s rare missile test sparked hysterical hypocrisy
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1047

AI Summary:

• China conducted a submarine-launched test missile with a dummy warhead into the South Pacific, giving advance notice to major countries in the region, including the US and Japan. • The US House Select Committee on China condemned the test as "aggression" and cited a projection that China aims to have 1,000 nuclear warheads by 2030. However, the article notes the US currently holds over 5,000 warheads compared to China's approximately 620, and the US refuses to adopt a "no first use" policy. • The US State Department criticized the short notice (a few hours) as insufficient, though it did not define a "standard practice." The article contrasts this with recent US Minuteman III ICBM tests that received little coverage. • Australian and New Zealand media widely reported the test, sometimes linking its timing to a new Fiji-Australia defence pact, while similar US missile tests received minimal attention.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
A Chinese nuclear-powered submarine fired a test missile into international waters in the South Pacific. Major countries in the areas were given advance notice. Even Japan, situated closer to the north Pacific, was warned beforehand. And the United States, located on the other side of the globe? Yes, of course it was given notice as well.

Still, all hell broke loose, as pundits and politicians rushed to denounce the Chinese missile test carrying a dummy warhead. Considering what they themselves do, it’s almost funny, making you wonder whether these people are being theatrical or serious?

The US House Select Committee on China called it an act of “aggression” towards US allies and other “like-minded partners in the Indo-Pacific”.

“China’s long-range ballistic missile launch wasn’t just a routine drill,” it said in a public statement. “As China races to have 1,000 nuclear warheads by 2030, we must have a defence industrial base that supports deterrence against China and helps our allies and partners defend themselves.”

It’s not clear how the committee came up with the warhead figure and timeline. Currently, the US possesses more than 5,000 nuclear warheads with 1,770 actively deployed, while China has about 620 with 34 deployed. Beijing has voluntarily committed to a “no first use” of nuclear weapons while the US has consistently refused.

The US State Department complains that the United States was only given a few hours’ notice ahead of the missile test, far short of “standard practice”. It did not specify what that mythical standard practice is.

In March, the US launched a Minuteman III intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM) from the Vandenberg Space Force Base, California. The missile landed near the Marshall Islands 6,700 kilometres away 24 minutes later. In November, a similar ICBM test was launched, landing in the same vicinity. Minuteman III can carry multiple nuclear warheads.

While the Chinese missile test has dominated Australian and New Zealand mainstream news reports, the US tests were barely mentioned, if at all, then as now. Instead, a mutual defence pact signed between Fiji and Australia around the time of the Chinese missile test was frequently offered as an explanation.

Here’s a typical piece from David Vallance, a research associate at Australia’s Lowy Institute’s international security programme, published in The Guardian.

“Coming on the day Fiji became Australia’s fourth formal treaty ally, after the US, New Zealand and Papua New Guinea, this test’s timing is interesting,” he wrote. “It reads as a provocation at best, outright coercion at worst.” He continues: “These things take time … it is possible the signing of the Ocean of Peace Alliance pact caused it to move the test up the timetable … How does demonstrating that a nuclear-armed missile can strike targets thousands of kilometres away illustrate Beijing’s commitment to ‘a path of peaceful development’?”

A valid question. Perhaps Vallance could ask the same about the US missile tests.

Australian Foreign Minister Penny Wong called the Chinese test “destabilising”, adding that it came “in the context of a rapid military build-up by China, which is lacking in the transparency and reassurance as to intent that the region expects.”

Her boss, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese, pointed out the obvious, saying the Chinese ICBM was capable of causing “considerable damage” if weaponised. Well, Tony, that was why it carried a dummy rather than an actual nuclear warhead.

Politicians and pundits from New Zealand, Japan and Taiwan are all similarly hysterically scathing. They all neglected to mention that the US – a country on the other side of the Earth – has held at least 15 ballistic missile tests fired into the Pacific since 2021.

For China? This was the second time since 1980, the last one being in September 2024 when a land-based ICBM was test-fired from Hainan Island at the country’s southern tip.

The hysterical hypocrisy is, of course, par for the course with those Western allies. Last month, the US, Britain, France and Germany raised the alarm that Chinese coastguard vessels patrolling the east coast of Taiwan were threatening regional stability and freedom of navigation. They neglected to mention that Japan and the Philippines were seeking to delineate the highly disputed waters that China also claims as its exclusive economic zone (EEZ).

Since none of these countries recognise Taiwan as an independent country, they cannot claim to be defending the island’s territorial waters. So what are they complaining about? Western allies sail warships through the Taiwan Strait about once every month.

Let that sink in. US Navy destroyers carry out the majority of such “freedom of navigation” operations. They are frequently joined by or coordinated with allies such as Canada, Australia, the UK, France, Japan, New Zealand, and Germany.

The US alone makes transits every few months independently. Allied transits occur multiple times a year, with US-allied nations conducting multiple joint or individual passages annually. The US and allied navies regularly team up for joint, back-to-back transits, sometimes occurring just days after Chinese military drills.

And yet, Australia and New Zealand went berserk in February last year when three Chinese warships sailed through international waters and Australia’s EEZ in the Tasman Sea.

When the Western allies do it, and they do it far more frequently, it’s exercising “freedom of navigation”. But when the Chinese do it, but rarely, they are threatening regional stability and freedom of navigation.

It rather reminds me of how every foreign port or cargo terminal invested in by Chinese corporations is reported as potential dual-use or possible naval base for the Chinese military.

So far, China has exactly one overseas naval base in Djibouti. But even this needs to be put in context. Djibouti hosts five major foreign naval and military facilities – China, the US, France, Italy and Japan – with waterfront access on the Horn of Africa. They are within walking distance of each other. Why? Because it’s a national business and a major part of Djibouti’s economy.

How many military bases does the US operate overseas? At least 70 primary bases, not counting hundreds of facilities and installations around the world, as well as undeclared and hidden sites.

Does anyone seriously think China would bother to attack countries and island states in the South Pacific? Not unless they attack first.
