item.title: [SCMP] Was Xi’s stance on China-North Korea military ties also a message for US, Russia?
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1212

AI Summary:

- Chinese President Xi Jinping explicitly called for enhanced military exchanges with North Korea during his recent summit, marking a notable emphasis on defense cooperation between the two nations. - The presence of both countries' defense ministers at the meeting was a rare and significant gesture, highlighting the elevated role of military ties in their relationship. - North Korean state media did not report Xi’s remarks on expanding military cooperation, suggesting Pyongyang may be cautious about overtly deepening military links with Beijing. - Analysts interpret Xi’s stance as a strategic signal to the United States that China has a firm military ally in North Korea, while also advising Pyongyang not to overly prioritize its growing military partnership with Russia.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
North Korea is gaining in strategic importance for China to counter the United States, but Pyongyang may refrain from intensifying military ties with Beijing, analysts say.

During his meeting with North Korean leader Kim Jong-un on Monday, Chinese President Xi Jinping said both sides should “enhance exchanges in diplomacy, law enforcement and military affairs”, according to state news agency Xinhua.

Despite pledges from both sides to strengthen strategic communications, denuclearisation of the Korean peninsula was not included in their statements on the meeting, a sharp contrast with Xi’s previous visit in 2019.

Also in a departure from their previous summits, Xi and Kim were accompanied by their defence ministers, Dong Jun and No Kwang-chol. It is the first time since 1992 that a Chinese defence minister has accompanied a Chinese president to North Korea.

It is also the first known case of a senior Chinese defence official visiting the country since Miao Hua, then-director of the Chinese Central Military Commission’s Political Work Department, visited in 2019.

While a Chinese military delegation reportedly travelled to North Korea for the first time in six years in October last year, the level of the delegation was not revealed. The prominence given to military matters this week was an addition to the traditional priorities of economic and diplomatic affairs.

However, North Korea’s state media did not report Xi’s remarks on increasing military exchanges between the two countries, despite extensive coverage of the Chinese president’s visit.

Kang Jun-young, a Chinese studies professor at Hankuk University of Foreign Studies in Seoul, said Xi’s remarks showed Washington that Beijing had “firmly secured a military ally” while at the same time signalling “caution against excessive military closeness” between Pyongyang and Moscow.

Beijing officially rejects formal, binding military alliances, but it signed the 1961 Treaty of Friendship, Cooperation and Mutual Assistance with North Korea – China’s only standing mutual defence agreement with another country.

In Kang’s view, North Korean media did not mention Xi’s comments because an open acknowledgement of improved military ties with Beijing could place a “considerable burden” on Pyongyang, considering it was making significant progress in military engagement with Moscow.

Sydney Seiler, who was the national intelligence officer for North Korea on the US National Intelligence Council from 2020 to 2023, said Beijing sought to send “subtle messages” that Pyongyang would be “well-advised” not to neglect China-North Korea relations.

While Chinese readouts of the visit suggested military exchange as part of multi-sector cooperation the two countries could pursue, this did not translate into “bilateral, or even trilateral, military exercises in the near future”, he said.

“For now, Russia-[North Korea] relations do not appear to have been dealt a significant blow: Russia is the lower-cost, less-burdensome partner for North Korea,” said Seiler, who is a senior adviser for the Korea chair at the Washington-based Centre for Strategic and International Studies.

“Kim is likely to look for low-risk, limited opportunities to expand cooperation with China but in a more reserved and cautious manner.”

Pyongyang is thought to have received Russian technological help to develop its conventional weapons in exchange for sending troops and weaponry to aid Moscow’s war in Ukraine. The two countries also signed a mutual defence treaty in June 2024, which mandates that an armed attack on either country requires the other to provide military assistance, marking the peak in North Korea-Russia defence ties.

Cho Han-bum, a senior research fellow at the Korea Institute for National Unification in Seoul, said that while closer military ties between Pyongyang and Moscow prompted China’s effort to regain and strengthen its influence over North Korea, Pyongyang might still take a cautious approach.

“Given that North Korea has always maintained equidistant diplomacy, or walked a tightrope between China and Russia, it is wary of military cooperation with China that crosses the red line,” Cho said.

The possibility of increased military exchanges between China and North Korea comes as Pyongyang continues to advance its nuclear and conventional weapons programme.

A day before last week’s announcement of Xi’s visit, North Korean state media reported that Kim had just toured a newly opened nuclear material production plant and pledged to “exponentially strengthen” his country’s nuclear force while “numerically and continuously” accelerating its expansion.

On Sunday – the day before Xi’s arrival – Kim’s sister Kim Yo-jong remarked that North Korea’s status as a nuclear power was an “absolute, indeterminate boundary”, and that “hostile forces” such as the US “must cast aside their delusions of denuclearisation”.

While China has previously supported a nuclear-free Korean peninsula, its recent actions reveal a pivot from that position.

In an op-ed published by North Korean state newspaper Rodong Sinmun before his arrival, Xi stressed opposing “hegemonism and power politics, and all ambitions and schemes that seek the revival of militarism and endanger regional safety and stability” but there was no mention of denuclearisation.

It was in contrast with Xi’s op-ed in the same publication ahead of his last visit seven years ago, which highlighted the promotion of peace and dialogue on the Korean peninsula.

China’s official policy advocates for the complete denuclearisation and stability of the Korean peninsula through phased, multilateral dialogue. However, Beijing’s policy appeared to depart from its original stance in November when it omitted support for the nuclear-free Koreas in its latest white paper “China’s Arms Control, Disarmament and Nonproliferation in the New Era”.

Lim Eul-chul, from the Institute for Far Eastern Studies at Kyungnam University, said the differences in coverage of the summit revealed North Korea’s wariness of its system “becoming subordinate to the Chinese economic model or global supply-chain systems”.

China had a stance of “de facto acquiescence” to North Korea’s status as a nuclear power through “intentional silence”, Lim said, citing Xi “wholeheartedly” supporting the “Korean-style socialist feat” of designating itself as a nuclear power in its constitution.

“This appears to be an attempt to secure a security alliance between North Korea and China to counter South Korea, the US and Japan through military cooperation in exchange for the tacit acceptance of nuclear possession.”

Patrick Cronin, the Asia-Pacific security chair at the Washington-based think tank Hudson Institute, said Xi’s broad calls for increased trade and military exchange were a “strategic signal” to Moscow and Washington.

“Beijing is reinforcing the message that China, not Russia, remains North Korea’s indispensable partner,” he said.

“At the same time, it is reminding Washington that China holds considerable leverage over Pyongyang and should therefore be given greater deference in any future diplomatic efforts to maintain stability on the peninsula.”

Kang noted that Xi’s visit to North Korea itself amplified Pyongyang’s strategic importance for China, which was also shown by the Chinese president’s repeated remarks emphasising strategic cooperation and communication.

“As a reaction to the US strengthening South Korea and Japan into alliances, China is bringing North Korea, traditionally under its influence … To do so, it has to relatively acknowledge North Korea’s status.”

However, drawing from the historical record, Kang added that North Korea would refrain from being too influenced by any single nation, whether China or Russia.

Commentary - As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks:

从消费疲软到资本退潮：内地餐饮股在港遇冷的政治经济学透视

现象描述
内地餐饮品牌赴港上市遇冷，股价普遍大跌，投资者因担忧消费疲软而回避相关资产。

政治经济学定位
这一现象直接反映了资本循环在流通环节遭遇的阻滞。根据马克思在《资本论》中揭示的规律，“商品首先是一个外界的对象，一个靠自己的属性来满足人的某种需要的物”。餐饮品牌的股票，作为虚拟资本的一种形式，其价值最终锚定于实体商品（餐饮服务）的价值实现。当前，内地消费市场疲软，零售销售数据收缩，意味着大量餐饮商品的“使用价值”未能有效转化为“价值”的货币实现。商品价格围绕价值波动是价值规律的作用形式，但当普遍性的有效需求不足时，价格将长期且大幅度地低于价值，导致企业利润被压缩，甚至无法收回成本。这直接动摇了其股票作为未来收益索取权的估值基础，引发资本（尤其是国际金融资本）的撤离。

深层结构分析
消费疲软并非简单的信心问题，而是深刻生产关系矛盾在流通领域的体现。餐饮行业作为终端消费部门，其困境是上游生产领域矛盾积累的结果。一方面，如报道所言，原料成本上升与激烈竞争限制了提价能力，这揭示了生产成本（包括不变资本和可变资本）与实现价值之间的紧张关系。另一方面，“人工智能采用影响入门级工作”导致的劳动力市场压力，指向了资本有机构成提高（用机器替代活劳动）带来的相对过剩人口问题，这削弱了广大劳动者的购买力。消费市场的萎靡，本质上是社会总产品实现条件恶化的征兆，即生产与消费之间出现了结构性失衡。这种失衡根植于以追求剩余价值为核心的资本积累模式，当分配关系未能使劳动者报酬与生产力增长同步时，最终会反噬资本自身的循环。

阶级/利益分析
在此轮波动中，各方利益与权力流动清晰可见。受损者首当其冲是广大的中小投资者和持有相关股票的基金，其财富因股价暴跌而缩水；其次是餐饮行业的从业者，面临就业不稳与薪酬增长乏力的风险；最终是依赖消费拉动的整体经济。受益者相对有限，主要是能够通过资本市场操作（如做空）获利的国际投机资本，以及可能借机收购廉价资产的产业资本。权力流向显示，资本（尤其是跨国金融资本）具有高度流动性和避险本能，其“用脚投票”加剧了市场波动，并对国内产业政策形成了外部约束。监管层对红筹架构审查的收紧，也可视为一种试图加强对资本跨境流动控制的制度性反应。

主流叙事批判
主流媒体与官方话语常将问题归结为“消费信心不足”、“天气因素”或“外部地缘冲突”。这些叙事将结构性矛盾归因于暂时的、偶然的或外部的冲击，掩盖了问题的核心。例如，将零售下滑归因于“燃料价格上涨”和“美国-以色列对伊朗战争”，便回避了国内分配格局与劳动报酬份额这一内生关键因素。同样，强调个别企业如蜜雪冰城的营收增长，却忽视其股价暴跌背后资本对整个行业盈利模式可持续性的质疑，这是一种用个别增长表象来掩盖普遍性积累危机的话术。这些叙事未能触及《资本论》所揭示的商品价值实现危机的深层逻辑。

解放前景
从政治经济学角度，出路在于调整生产关系以适应生产力的发展，并疏通社会总资本的循环。首先，根本性地调整分配关系，通过提高劳动报酬在初次分配中的比重、强化社会保障，从而有效提升占人口绝大多数的劳动者的消费能力，这是化解生产与消费矛盾的关键。正如马克思地租理论启示的，超额利润的分配方向（流向食利者还是用于扩大再生产与社会福祉）关乎经济的长期健康。其次，规范和引导资本运动，抑制过度金融化与投机，引导资本更多流向实体经济的技术创新与产业升级，提升产业资本有机构成的质量，而非仅追求短期套利。再次，强化国家的宏观调节角色，通过财政与税收政策进行再分配，并制定产业政策确保关键行业的有序竞争与可持续发展，避免陷入恶性价格战与资源浪费。最终，这要求将“满足人的需要”这一商品的使用价值维度，置于比“价值增殖”更优先的位置来组织经济活动，从而为经济循环注入持久而健康的动力。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Philippine rice output risks 30% collapse as ‘super’ El Nino strengthens
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1209

AI Summary:

* **Severe El Niño Forecast:** The Philippine weather bureau (Pagasa) confirms El Niño is present and forecasts a 62% probability it will intensify into a "super" El Niño (defined by a Pacific temperature rise over 2°C) from October onwards. * **Major Agricultural Impact:** The Department of Agriculture is preparing for potential rice output losses exceeding 30%, which could amount to a reduction of up to 700,000 tonnes. This follows a 6.3% year-on-year slip in first-quarter 2026 palay production. * **Timeline of Drought:** While some heavy rainfall is expected until September, dry conditions and dry spells are likely to begin in November, with prolonged droughts highly probable in early 2027. * **Historical Precedent and Compounding Risks:** The last severe El Niño in 1997-98 caused a 22% collapse in rice production. Current risks are compounded by existing high fertilizer and fuel prices and the potential for major rice exporters (Vietnam, Thailand, India) to halt exports to secure their own supplies.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
In the fields of central Luzon, farmers who planted their rice in June are watching the sky with worry.

Out over the Pacific, a familiar spectre is gathering strength – and with it, the threat of empty granaries and hungry households.

The Philippines has a plan for this year’s “super” El Nino, on paper at least. But to the Filipinos out actually working the paddies, that plan is barely perceptible.

“We don’t see anything visible, it’s all talk,” said Raul Montemayor, national manager of the Federation of Free Farmers, an NGO representing 250,000 of the country’s 10 million rural workers and agricultural labourers.

The warning signs have been building for months. Pagasa, the Philippine weather bureau, confirmed in a June 24 update that El Nino conditions were already present in the tropical Pacific, forecasting that they would strengthen by August through October, then intensify further later in the year.

Speaking at Pagasa’s 197th Climate Outlook Forum, held online that same day, Dr Ana Liza Solis, chief of the bureau’s climate monitoring and prediction section, put the odds of El Nino intensifying from October at 62 per cent.

In the near term, she said, the western side of Luzon and the Visayas could see “heavy rainfall events” through to September, offering a brief reprieve before conditions turn. “Dry condition and dry spells will likely start in November, while prolonged dry spells and droughts will be highly likely in early part of 2027,” Solis added.

A rise of more than 2 degrees Celsius (3.6 degrees Fahrenheit) in Pacific temperatures would qualify as “very strong or a super El Nino”, according to the bureau.

Such severe El Nino are relatively uncommon, but when one struck the Philippines previously, in 1997-98, it triggered a 22 per cent collapse in palay (raw, unhusked rice) production.

This time around, layered atop fertiliser and fuel price spikes amid the US-Israel war on Iran, Manila’s Department of Agriculture is bracing for losses that could exceed 30 per cent, according to agricultural economist Fermin Adriano, a former undersecretary in the department who has advised the UN Food and Agriculture Organization, the World Bank and the Asian Development Bank.

“It [El Nino] has been predicted by international meteorological organisations for months now, warning that the Asian tropical region will be hit hard by it,” Adriano told This Week in Asia.

Filipinos should brace themselves for “higher food prices if not actual supply shortages” in the coming months, he said – especially if major rice suppliers like Vietnam, Thailand and India choose to prioritise their own food security and halt exports.

In May, the Philippine Department of Agriculture warned a severe El Nino could slash national rice output by up to 700,000 tonnes. Data from the Philippine Statistics Authority shows palay production in the first quarter of 2026 had already slipped 6.3 per cent year on year, to 4.4 million tonnes – the weakest start to a year since 2020.

Central Luzon, the nation’s main rice-growing area and a region where farmers rely on irrigation, produced 18.8 per cent of the country’s palay in the first quarter alone. Any serious disruption there ripples outwards fast – especially so given the Philippines’ heavy dependence on imported rice, leaving it exposed on two fronts at once, should drought at home coincide with export restrictions abroad.

Fertiliser shortages have only exacerbated the issue. Even before El Nino’s worst effects are forecast arrive, the Iran conflict and associated closure of the Strait of Hormuz have already disrupted roughly 108,000 tonnes of urea from Qatar and 33,000 tonnes from Saudi Arabia, according to official figures.

The government has launched a response, with President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr activating an El Nino “action plan” in May, which his spokeswoman said would tackle water, food and energy security, alongside public safety and health. Yet progress has been hard to discern, according to Adriano.

“There is PR or media releases showing various agencies preparing for El Nino, but I am not sure how far they’ve progressed in terms of actual programmes or projects that will mitigate its effects,” he said. “Wala akong narinig.” (I haven’t heard anything)

What farmers actually need, according to Montemayor, are water impoundment projects such as dams and reservoirs. “They always talk about convening a technical working group, et cetera et cetera,” he said. “[But] I don’t know what else is happening.”

Pagasa’s forecasts suggest rainfall will hold until September and Central Luzon’s farmers, having planted in June, should be able to bring in a harvest around September or October, rains permitting.

But Montemayor sees the real reckoning arriving after that. “October will be when we will feel the intensity of El Nino [and see] less rain than normal,” he said.

“The season that I think will be most at risk is the following planting season, November, that’s when we might have a drought. If you have less than normal rainfall, that means farmers won’t be able to plant.”

Already, the country’s reservoirs are showing signs of strain. Water levels at Luzon’s two major dams of Pantabangan and Magat are low and Montemayor knows exactly how the government will ration what remains.

They will prioritise drinking water, then energy, and then, third, irrigation – if there is water left

Raul Montemayor, Federation of Free Farmers

“They will prioritise drinking water, then energy, and then, third, irrigation – if there is water left,” he said.

“It’s only July, and we’re talking about November, so there is time to prepare – fill up the dams, fix the irrigation systems, put up solar-powered irrigation. But I don’t see it,” Montemayor added. “What they’ll say is it’s not in the budget.”

Even the government’s stated priorities carry contradictions. Agriculture officials have reportedly pledged to focus on irrigation and water impounding, but Montemayor says there is a flaw in that strategy. “What if there’s no rain? The dams don’t get filled,” he said. Cloud seeding offered little comfort either. “If there are no clouds, you cannot seed them – it’s hit or miss.”

At the launch of a small reservoir project on Bohol Island last month, Marcos floated another idea: encouraging farmers to shift towards high-value, less water-intensive crops such as ube. It is a suggestion Montemayor treats with weary scepticism.

“Ube takes 11 months to grow,” he said. “Farmers will have a hard time waiting that long – and you have problems with seedlings, as well as technology and know-how on planting it.”

Drought-resistant rice seeds, often cited as a stopgap solution, fare little better when examined in detail. “I don’t think they have a lot of stock,” he added. “It takes a long time to produce them – two years.”

Even existing support has stalled: according to Montemayor, the government still has not finished distributing the cash aid it promised to cushion rice farmers against rising fuel costs.

With the Department of Agriculture reportedly bracing for historic rice production losses, Adriano worries about the fallout.

“If food supply shortages or skyrocketing food prices happen, then it will be a real emergency,” he warned. “It’s possible that public discontent might reach a boiling point.”

This Week in Asia has contacted the Philippine Department of Agriculture for comment.

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

港股波动：资本流动表象下的结构性矛盾

现象描述： 港股科技与半导体股逆势上涨，但受中东地缘政治及通胀担忧拖累，恒生指数收跌，市场呈现资金博弈与区域风险交织的复杂态势。

政治经济学定位： 此次波动是全球金融资本在特定时空背景下进行流动、重新配置与风险对冲的集中体现。它涉及资本运动的多重维度：一是不同区域资本市场间的套利资本流动（如空头回补、南向资金）；二是虚拟资本（股票）价格脱离实体基本面的剧烈波动；三是产业资本在科技、半导体等战略领域的估值重估。

深层结构分析： 现象背后是金融全球化背景下资本积累的内在矛盾。一方面，实体经济（如中国生产商价格指数上涨）与金融市场（如股市短期涨跌）出现背离。另一方面，资本为寻求增殖，在全球不同区域市场间快速转移。正如马克思在《资本论》中所指出的，“较多的工人在同一时间、同一空间，为了生产同种商品，在同一资本家的指挥下工作，这在历史上和逻辑上都是资本主义生产的起点。” 当前全球产业链的深度整合（如半导体）与金融市场（如港股作为国际金融中心）的紧密勾连，使得“同一资本”的指挥棒已延伸至全球范围，生产与流通、实体经济与虚拟经济的矛盾在更宏大的尺度上展开。

阶级/利益分析： 此轮波动中，国际与内地金融资本是核心参与者与潜在受益者。通过空头回补操作，全球机构投资者管理了其跨区域投资组合的风险。内地资金南向流入及政策支持（如南向交易扩容），则强化了内地资本对香港金融市场乃至全球资产配置的影响力。相反，依赖金融市场稳定进行长期融资的实体产业资本（特别是出口导向型制造业）面临更高不确定性。普通投资者，尤其是缺乏信息与资金优势的散户，其资产更容易在市场剧烈波动中受损。利润（剩余价值）在金融部门与生产部门间的分配可能进一步失衡，金融资本的支配地位得到巩固。

主流叙事批判： 主流财经话语倾向于将此次波动归因于“地缘政治风险”、“技术性反弹”、“资金流动”等中性化表述。例如，分析师称“近期抛售过度”引发反弹，或强调“空头回补”与“南向资金”的支撑作用。这种叙事将复杂的资本积累过程与阶级利益博弈，简化为市场参与者的情绪与技术性操作，掩盖了资本市场波动对实体经济的剥蚀作用，以及金融资本在全球范围内攫取剩余价值并转移风险的本质。马克思在《资本论》中深刻指出，“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 港股的涨跌，本质上是虚拟资本的重新定价游戏，其影响却实实在在地通过融资成本、财富效应等渠道，传导至现实的生产与雇佣关系之中。

解放前景： 要打破“金融繁荣”与“实体经济承压”的循环，关键在于遏制金融资本的过度膨胀与投机冲动，将经济重心锚定于生产性积累。这要求：第一，强化对虚拟资本运动的监管，防止其过度脱离实体创造价值的能力。第二，提升实体经济的劳动生产力与技术自主性，正如工场手工业与机器大工业曾创造新的社会生产力，当前对半导体等“卡脖子”领域的突破，应服务于更平等、更可持续的生产关系构建，而非仅仅成为金融炒作的题材。第三，改革国际金融体系与分配机制，削弱金融霸权，使资本流动更多服务于各国实体经济的协调发展，而非少数金融集团的套利。真正的出路在于重新确立产业资本与生产性劳动的主导地位，使虚拟资本服务于而非主宰于现实财富的创造与公平分配。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Why a Chinese academic claim over Batanes has unsettled the Philippines
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1152

AI Summary:

- A university symposium in Guangzhou has placed the Philippines’ northernmost province of Batanes at the centre of a new argument over history, geography and sovereignty, after scholars at the event claimed the islands belonged to Taiwan and, on that basis, fell under overall Chinese sovereignty. -...

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
A university symposium in Guangzhou has placed the Philippines’ northernmost province of Batanes at the centre of a new argument over history, geography and sovereignty, after scholars at the event claimed the islands belonged to Taiwan and, on that basis, fell under overall Chinese sovereignty.

The claim, made at a June 30 symposium hosted by Jinan University in Guangzhou, has been rejected by Philippine historians and officials, who dispute the scholars’ reading of geography, dynastic administration, cultural links and post-war treaties.

Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China, to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington opposes any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.

Beijing has not formally adopted the scholars’ position on Batanes, but the discussion has fuelled concern in Manila that an apparently academic argument could be used to test or shape a wider narrative around the islands, which sit near one of the most important waterways between the South China Sea and the Pacific.

The Guangzhou symposium was convened in reaction to a May 28 joint statement by the Philippines and Japan that the two would negotiate to delimit their exclusive economic zones and continental shelves, including areas east of Taiwan also claimed by mainland China.

Symposium participants said the Philippines-Japan agreement infringed on China’s sovereignty, and that the Batanes islands belonged to China.

“The islands are not merely closer to Taiwan in distance, but constitute a natural geographical extension of Taiwan,” said Ju Hailong, dean of the School of International Studies at Jinan University.

In a statement issued on Friday rejecting the symposium’s claims, the National Historical Commission of the Philippines challenged that geographical argument, saying “current satellite and oceanographic data by the Philippines and other nations show a continuous shelf extending from northern Luzon through the Babuyan and Batanes Islands, and into parts of the Taiwan archipelago”.

It said “the Philippines claims a greater right over the subject territories from this perspective”.

Ju had claimed that during the Ming and Qing dynasties, the islands were under the jurisdiction of the Taiwan prefecture.

But the commission said “the earliest known extensive documentation of the Batanes Islands by British explorer William Dampier in 1687 noted no trace of Chinese governance over the islands”.

It noted that “since 1783, the Philippine government and its legal predecessors have held continuous and undisputed sovereignty over Batanes”.

The symposium also claimed the Ivatan residents of Batanes could be linguistically and culturally linked with Taiwan’s Tao people, with heritage originating from mainland China.

The commission noted instead that the Ivatan people “lived in protected communities and traded with other maritime trading nations”.

According to the Chinese scholars, post-World War II treaties required Japan to return all territories it had seized from China and, because they regarded Batanes as part of Taiwan, the islands should have been included.

The commission rejected that premise, saying the islands could not be returned because “Japan cannot give to China what clearly belongs to the Philippines”.

In its statement, the commission rejected the Chinese scholars’ assertions, saying they had “no rational basis in substantive research and operate from evident bad faith. They would never stand under scrutiny in academic discourse outside of the People’s Republic of China.”

Philippine foreign affairs department acting spokeswoman Analyn Ratonel said on Friday: “Flights of fancy should not be dignified with a response. Nevertheless, the Philippines’ sovereignty over Batanes is settled and not up for debate.”

She pointed out that when Manila approved a consular district of the Chinese consulate general in the northern city of Laoag, the Chinese request explicitly included Batanes.

Some Philippine officials and analysts said the symposium should not be viewed only as an academic event, warning that it could help test or shape public perceptions around Batanes.

Renato de Castro, a distinguished professor in the international studies department of De La Salle University Manila who once taught at Jinan University in 2007, said the symposium was unlikely to have taken place without political space from Beijing, arguing that “this is probably a prelude to a possible offensive in terms of psychological warfare”.

Speaking in a news forum on Saturday, retired rear admiral Roy Vincent Trinidad, spokesman for Philippine operations in the South China Sea, described what was happening as “salami-slicing in the information domain”.

He said it could begin with a simple statement before moving towards a point where “it’s officially adopted by the government”.

Former congressman Robert Ace Barbers, who once offered his province of Surigao del Norte to host US troops, told This Week in Asia that claiming Batanes “is another part of bullying, this is what we call creeping invasion”.

Raymond Powell, director of SeaLight, a maritime transparency project of Stanford University’s Gordian Knot Centre, cautioned during the news forum on Saturday that dismissing such claims would be “playing directly into Beijing’s hand”, as they served a broader purpose.

“The Chinese government needs the ‘possibility’ that it owns Batanes to justify” its actions in waters claimed by the Philippines, Powell said in a social media post on Monday, adding that Beijing “isn’t ready to say it directly” when it came to Batanes, therefore it “left it to academics”.

Philippine Defence Secretary Gilberto Teodoro called the Chinese scholars’ assertions a “quasi-state statement”.

The concern is sharpened by Batanes’ location near Taiwan and the Luzon Strait, a strategic corridor between the South China Sea and the Pacific.

Batanes comprises 10 islands, with the northernmost one, Mavulis, a mere 142km southeast of Taiwan. It is being built up by the Philippine military as part of its Comprehensive Archipelagic Defence Concept, which refocuses the country’s security strategies to include defence of its exclusive economic zone.

Teodoro has described the islands as “the spearhead of the Philippines as far as the northern baseline is concerned”.

In 2024, a Naval Forward Operating Base was constructed on the main island of Batan, while structures were built in Mavulis to house members of the Naval Forces Northern Luzon, a unit dedicated to defending the area.

Military drills have also taken place there in the two recent Balikatan, or shoulder-to-shoulder, exercises with the US and other nations, including Philippine and US marines rappelling from Black Hawk helicopters to the remote Batanes town of Itbayat.

Dr Ricardo Jose, an emeritus professor of history specialising in World War II, told This Week in Asia in 2024 that Batanes was strategically important in any conflict involving Taiwan.

Japanese forces had seized the airfield in Basco, Batanes, “because from Taiwan, Basco is almost halfway between Taiwan and the northern Luzon tip”.

De Castro said in a forum by think tank Stratbase on Friday that “the northern Philippines is key terrain – the control of which will give you a marked advantage over your adversary.”

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

港股波动：资本流动表象下的结构性矛盾

现象描述： 港股科技与半导体股逆势上涨，但受中东地缘政治及通胀担忧拖累，恒生指数收跌，市场呈现资金博弈与区域风险交织的复杂态势。

政治经济学定位： 此次波动是全球金融资本在特定时空背景下进行流动、重新配置与风险对冲的集中体现。它涉及资本运动的多重维度：一是不同区域资本市场间的套利资本流动（如空头回补、南向资金）；二是虚拟资本（股票）价格脱离实体基本面的剧烈波动；三是产业资本在科技、半导体等战略领域的估值重估。

深层结构分析： 现象背后是金融全球化背景下资本积累的内在矛盾。一方面，实体经济（如中国生产商价格指数上涨）与金融市场（如股市短期涨跌）出现背离。另一方面，资本为寻求增殖，在全球不同区域市场间快速转移。正如马克思在《资本论》中所指出的，“较多的工人在同一时间、同一空间，为了生产同种商品，在同一资本家的指挥下工作，这在历史上和逻辑上都是资本主义生产的起点。” 当前全球产业链的深度整合（如半导体）与金融市场（如港股作为国际金融中心）的紧密勾连，使得“同一资本”的指挥棒已延伸至全球范围，生产与流通、实体经济与虚拟经济的矛盾在更宏大的尺度上展开。

阶级/利益分析： 此轮波动中，国际与内地金融资本是核心参与者与潜在受益者。通过空头回补操作，全球机构投资者管理了其跨区域投资组合的风险。内地资金南向流入及政策支持（如南向交易扩容），则强化了内地资本对香港金融市场乃至全球资产配置的影响力。相反，依赖金融市场稳定进行长期融资的实体产业资本（特别是出口导向型制造业）面临更高不确定性。普通投资者，尤其是缺乏信息与资金优势的散户，其资产更容易在市场剧烈波动中受损。利润（剩余价值）在金融部门与生产部门间的分配可能进一步失衡，金融资本的支配地位得到巩固。

主流叙事批判： 主流财经话语倾向于将此次波动归因于“地缘政治风险”、“技术性反弹”、“资金流动”等中性化表述。例如，分析师称“近期抛售过度”引发反弹，或强调“空头回补”与“南向资金”的支撑作用。这种叙事将复杂的资本积累过程与阶级利益博弈，简化为市场参与者的情绪与技术性操作，掩盖了资本市场波动对实体经济的剥蚀作用，以及金融资本在全球范围内攫取剩余价值并转移风险的本质。马克思在《资本论》中深刻指出，“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 港股的涨跌，本质上是虚拟资本的重新定价游戏，其影响却实实在在地通过融资成本、财富效应等渠道，传导至现实的生产与雇佣关系之中。

解放前景： 要打破“金融繁荣”与“实体经济承压”的循环，关键在于遏制金融资本的过度膨胀与投机冲动，将经济重心锚定于生产性积累。这要求：第一，强化对虚拟资本运动的监管，防止其过度脱离实体创造价值的能力。第二，提升实体经济的劳动生产力与技术自主性，正如工场手工业与机器大工业曾创造新的社会生产力，当前对半导体等“卡脖子”领域的突破，应服务于更平等、更可持续的生产关系构建，而非仅仅成为金融炒作的题材。第三，改革国际金融体系与分配机制，削弱金融霸权，使资本流动更多服务于各国实体经济的协调发展，而非少数金融集团的套利。真正的出路在于重新确立产业资本与生产性劳动的主导地位，使虚拟资本服务于而非主宰于现实财富的创造与公平分配。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] How China’s rare missile test sparked hysterical hypocrisy
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1047

AI Summary:

* China conducted a nuclear-powered submarine missile test in the South Pacific, giving advance notice to major countries including the United States, Japan, and others. * The US House Select Committee on China and the State Department condemned the test, with the committee alleging it was aggressive and linked it to China's projected nuclear buildup, though the article disputes the cited figures. * The article highlights a disparity in media coverage, noting extensive reporting on the Chinese test in Australia and New Zealand, while similar US ICBM tests (like the Minuteman III launches) receive minimal attention. * Analysis presented in the article suggests a "hysterical hypocrisy" from Western critics, questioning why China's notified test is framed as provocative when the US possesses a larger nuclear arsenal and conducts its own tests. * The piece implies a double standard in international response, contrasting the strong political reactions to China's action with the relative silence on US missile activities.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
A Chinese nuclear-powered submarine fired a test missile into international waters in the South Pacific. Major countries in the areas were given advance notice. Even Japan, situated closer to the north Pacific, was warned beforehand. And the United States, located on the other side of the globe? Yes, of course it was given notice as well.

Still, all hell broke loose, as pundits and politicians rushed to denounce the Chinese missile test carrying a dummy warhead. Considering what they themselves do, it’s almost funny, making you wonder whether these people are being theatrical or serious?

The US House Select Committee on China called it an act of “aggression” towards US allies and other “like-minded partners in the Indo-Pacific”.

“China’s long-range ballistic missile launch wasn’t just a routine drill,” it said in a public statement. “As China races to have 1,000 nuclear warheads by 2030, we must have a defence industrial base that supports deterrence against China and helps our allies and partners defend themselves.”

It’s not clear how the committee came up with the warhead figure and timeline. Currently, the US possesses more than 5,000 nuclear warheads with 1,770 actively deployed, while China has about 620 with 34 deployed. Beijing has voluntarily committed to a “no first use” of nuclear weapons while the US has consistently refused.

The US State Department complains that the United States was only given a few hours’ notice ahead of the missile test, far short of “standard practice”. It did not specify what that mythical standard practice is.

In March, the US launched a Minuteman III intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM) from the Vandenberg Space Force Base, California. The missile landed near the Marshall Islands 6,700 kilometres away 24 minutes later. In November, a similar ICBM test was launched, landing in the same vicinity. Minuteman III can carry multiple nuclear warheads.

While the Chinese missile test has dominated Australian and New Zealand mainstream news reports, the US tests were barely mentioned, if at all, then as now. Instead, a mutual defence pact signed between Fiji and Australia around the time of the Chinese missile test was frequently offered as an explanation.

Here’s a typical piece from David Vallance, a research associate at Australia’s Lowy Institute’s international security programme, published in The Guardian.

“Coming on the day Fiji became Australia’s fourth formal treaty ally, after the US, New Zealand and Papua New Guinea, this test’s timing is interesting,” he wrote. “It reads as a provocation at best, outright coercion at worst.” He continues: “These things take time … it is possible the signing of the Ocean of Peace Alliance pact caused it to move the test up the timetable … How does demonstrating that a nuclear-armed missile can strike targets thousands of kilometres away illustrate Beijing’s commitment to ‘a path of peaceful development’?”

A valid question. Perhaps Vallance could ask the same about the US missile tests.

Australian Foreign Minister Penny Wong called the Chinese test “destabilising”, adding that it came “in the context of a rapid military build-up by China, which is lacking in the transparency and reassurance as to intent that the region expects.”

Her boss, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese, pointed out the obvious, saying the Chinese ICBM was capable of causing “considerable damage” if weaponised. Well, Tony, that was why it carried a dummy rather than an actual nuclear warhead.

Politicians and pundits from New Zealand, Japan and Taiwan are all similarly hysterically scathing. They all neglected to mention that the US – a country on the other side of the Earth – has held at least 15 ballistic missile tests fired into the Pacific since 2021.

For China? This was the second time since 1980, the last one being in September 2024 when a land-based ICBM was test-fired from Hainan Island at the country’s southern tip.

The hysterical hypocrisy is, of course, par for the course with those Western allies. Last month, the US, Britain, France and Germany raised the alarm that Chinese coastguard vessels patrolling the east coast of Taiwan were threatening regional stability and freedom of navigation. They neglected to mention that Japan and the Philippines were seeking to delineate the highly disputed waters that China also claims as its exclusive economic zone (EEZ).

Since none of these countries recognise Taiwan as an independent country, they cannot claim to be defending the island’s territorial waters. So what are they complaining about? Western allies sail warships through the Taiwan Strait about once every month.

Let that sink in. US Navy destroyers carry out the majority of such “freedom of navigation” operations. They are frequently joined by or coordinated with allies such as Canada, Australia, the UK, France, Japan, New Zealand, and Germany.

The US alone makes transits every few months independently. Allied transits occur multiple times a year, with US-allied nations conducting multiple joint or individual passages annually. The US and allied navies regularly team up for joint, back-to-back transits, sometimes occurring just days after Chinese military drills.

And yet, Australia and New Zealand went berserk in February last year when three Chinese warships sailed through international waters and Australia’s EEZ in the Tasman Sea.

When the Western allies do it, and they do it far more frequently, it’s exercising “freedom of navigation”. But when the Chinese do it, but rarely, they are threatening regional stability and freedom of navigation.

It rather reminds me of how every foreign port or cargo terminal invested in by Chinese corporations is reported as potential dual-use or possible naval base for the Chinese military.

So far, China has exactly one overseas naval base in Djibouti. But even this needs to be put in context. Djibouti hosts five major foreign naval and military facilities – China, the US, France, Italy and Japan – with waterfront access on the Horn of Africa. They are within walking distance of each other. Why? Because it’s a national business and a major part of Djibouti’s economy.

How many military bases does the US operate overseas? At least 70 primary bases, not counting hundreds of facilities and installations around the world, as well as undeclared and hidden sites.

Does anyone seriously think China would bother to attack countries and island states in the South Pacific? Not unless they attack first.

item.title: [SCMP] Japan wants 60 million tourists, but China isn’t sending them
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1074

AI Summary:

* Japan received a record 42.7 million foreign visitors in 2023, a 16% increase from the previous year, and has set a government target of 60 million annual arrivals by 2030. * Inbound and domestic tourism now accounts for approximately 5.6% of Japan's GDP, valued at 34.3 trillion yen (about $211 billion), making it the country's second-largest export sector after automobiles. * Significant public and private investment is underway to support this growth, including major expansions at Narita Airport and plans to increase train frequency to and from airports. * The push towards tourism is a strategic response to declining competitiveness in traditional export industries like electronics and automobiles, which face strong international competition, particularly from China. * The shift is part of a longer-term economic restructuring following Japan's "lost decades" and the bursting of its asset bubble in the 1990s, with tourism identified as a key sustainable growth engine.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Japan has found a new export industry and it doesn’t come off an assembly line. It arrives by plane, spends freely and is, increasingly, what Tokyo is banking its economic future on.

Last year, 42.7 million foreign visitors arrived in the country, a record that shattered the previous year’s high by nearly 16 per cent. Now Tokyo wants to turn that surge into something more lasting: a permanent pillar of growth to rival, or even outlast, the industries that built modern Japan.

To achieve that ambition, the government has set a target of 60 million arrivals a year by 2030, with billions being funnelled into the infrastructure needed to get there.

At Narita Airport, the main international gateway serving the Japanese capital, crews are extending the second runway to 2,500 metres (8,200 feet) and building an entirely new 3,500-metre (11,480-foot) third runway, as part of a multi-year expansion that aims to push annual capacity from 340,000 flights to half a million.

Rail planners, meanwhile, are working to nearly double train frequency between Narita and central Tokyo by the early 2030s, with new extensions set to link two lines directly to Haneda Airport to the capital’s south, aiming to make domestic transfers easier.

Private capital has followed the government’s lead. Hotels are opening across every price bracket, from backpacker hostels to luxury resorts, and a new generation of travel companies is emerging to serve them.

“Tourism is one of the fastest-growing areas of the Japanese economy,” said Martin Schulz, chief policy economist at Fujitsu’s Global Market Intelligence Unit.

“It has a strong impact on domestic services, such as hotels and restaurants, and it acts as a major help to the broader economy … Tourism may not be the single largest engine, but it is a very important one because of the impact on labour and so on.”

Tourism, domestic and inbound combined, now accounts for roughly 5.6 per cent of Japan’s gross domestic product, according to government figures, worth an estimated 34.3 trillion yen (US$211 billion) a year.

That makes it the country’s second-biggest export earner, trailing only an automobile industry that finds itself increasingly under siege from US tariff pressures and booming Chinese competition in electric vehicles, especially.

Therein lies the logic of Tokyo’s tourism push. Japanese firms that once dominated global markets in electronics, heavy engineering, financial services and cars are losing ground, not least to Chinese rivals encroaching on territory long considered unassailable.

Tourism, meanwhile, offers a rare growth story at a moment when many of the old ones are losing their punch.

Schulz traces the pivot back to the 1990s and the wreckage of Japan’s “lost decades”. After the property bubble burst and manufacturers began shifting production overseas, banks were left nursing mountains of bad debt.

It soon became clear that restructuring alone would not be enough, Schulz said. Japan needed new revenue streams and it found two: digital services and tourism.

“Japan could see that other mature nations, like those in Europe, all had strong tourism sectors with investment and income flowing into those economies from overseas,” he said.

The turnaround began with visas. Rather than exclusively courting the relatively small, relatively wealthy travel markets of Europe and North America, Japan opened its doors to the vast populations of neighbouring Asian countries, wagering on volume over exclusivity.

There were, inevitably perhaps, some speed humps along the way. The Tohoku earthquake, tsunami and nuclear crisis of 2011 sent arrivals tumbling before a slow recovery eventually took hold. Then came 2020: after a record-breaking 2019, the Covid-19 pandemic brought global travel to a standstill overnight.

Each time, Japan bounced back – and last year’s numbers suggest the recovery has now overshot its pre-pandemic ambitions entirely. Visitors spent 9.5 trillion yen in 2025, another record, drawn in part by a weak currency that has turned Japan into one of the world’s best-value destinations.

Momentum has cooled only slightly this year. Arrivals in the first five months of 2026 dipped a marginal 1.1 per cent, to 17.94 million, but officials insist the 60-million target remains firmly within reach.

Success has brought its own complications, however. “There is a need to diversify to different parts of the country, but the information capacity is very limited,” said Kumi Kato, a tourism professor at Japan’s Wakayama University.

Nearly 70 per cent of all overnight stays by foreign visitors cluster in just five prefectures: Tokyo, Osaka, Kyoto, Hokkaido and Okinawa. Kyoto alone accounted for almost 63 million domestic and international visitors last year, leaving its historic streets and temples groaning under the strain.

The result, according to Kato, has been a kind of tourism monoculture: familiar routes, familiar sights and lines of foreign visitors trailing tour guides with flags through the same handful of postcard locations.

“Japan is affordable now and we are getting tourists that are visiting for the first time but are not going to different parts of the country,” she said.

“We see more of the stereotypical tours to the same places in Kyoto and Tokyo, with groups of foreign tourists following a guide with a flag.”

Schulz identified another flaw in the grand tourism plan that no amount of domestic investment could fix: geopolitics.

“Theoretically, Japan is trying to distance itself from the Chinese economy at a time of strained ties,” he said. “But the Chinese government is still able to exert pressure … as it has ordered its nationals not to go to Japan.”

Relations hit a fresh nadir in November, when Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi told the country’s parliament that a Taiwan “contingency” could require a military response – remarks that infuriated Beijing, which regards the island as a part of China to be reunited by force if necessary.

Most countries, including Japan and the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but are opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and Washington is committed to supplying it with weapons.

The fallout was immediate, in tourism terms. While arrivals from South Korea rose more than 14 per cent in May and those from Taiwan climbed 14.6 per cent, visitor numbers from mainland China plummeted 60.4 per cent to just 310,000, marking the sixth straight monthly decline.

“There are lots of tourists arriving from other parts of Asia, but China is using its travellers as another lever to pressure Japan,” Schulz said. “It’s easy for Beijing to do and it hits Japan hard.”

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

港股波动：资本流动表象下的结构性矛盾

现象描述： 港股科技与半导体股逆势上涨，但受中东地缘政治及通胀担忧拖累，恒生指数收跌，市场呈现资金博弈与区域风险交织的复杂态势。

政治经济学定位： 此次波动是全球金融资本在特定时空背景下进行流动、重新配置与风险对冲的集中体现。它涉及资本运动的多重维度：一是不同区域资本市场间的套利资本流动（如空头回补、南向资金）；二是虚拟资本（股票）价格脱离实体基本面的剧烈波动；三是产业资本在科技、半导体等战略领域的估值重估。

深层结构分析： 现象背后是金融全球化背景下资本积累的内在矛盾。一方面，实体经济（如中国生产商价格指数上涨）与金融市场（如股市短期涨跌）出现背离。另一方面，资本为寻求增殖，在全球不同区域市场间快速转移。正如马克思在《资本论》中所指出的，“较多的工人在同一时间、同一空间，为了生产同种商品，在同一资本家的指挥下工作，这在历史上和逻辑上都是资本主义生产的起点。” 当前全球产业链的深度整合（如半导体）与金融市场（如港股作为国际金融中心）的紧密勾连，使得“同一资本”的指挥棒已延伸至全球范围，生产与流通、实体经济与虚拟经济的矛盾在更宏大的尺度上展开。

阶级/利益分析： 此轮波动中，国际与内地金融资本是核心参与者与潜在受益者。通过空头回补操作，全球机构投资者管理了其跨区域投资组合的风险。内地资金南向流入及政策支持（如南向交易扩容），则强化了内地资本对香港金融市场乃至全球资产配置的影响力。相反，依赖金融市场稳定进行长期融资的实体产业资本（特别是出口导向型制造业）面临更高不确定性。普通投资者，尤其是缺乏信息与资金优势的散户，其资产更容易在市场剧烈波动中受损。利润（剩余价值）在金融部门与生产部门间的分配可能进一步失衡，金融资本的支配地位得到巩固。

主流叙事批判： 主流财经话语倾向于将此次波动归因于“地缘政治风险”、“技术性反弹”、“资金流动”等中性化表述。例如，分析师称“近期抛售过度”引发反弹，或强调“空头回补”与“南向资金”的支撑作用。这种叙事将复杂的资本积累过程与阶级利益博弈，简化为市场参与者的情绪与技术性操作，掩盖了资本市场波动对实体经济的剥蚀作用，以及金融资本在全球范围内攫取剩余价值并转移风险的本质。马克思在《资本论》中深刻指出，“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 港股的涨跌，本质上是虚拟资本的重新定价游戏，其影响却实实在在地通过融资成本、财富效应等渠道，传导至现实的生产与雇佣关系之中。

解放前景： 要打破“金融繁荣”与“实体经济承压”的循环，关键在于遏制金融资本的过度膨胀与投机冲动，将经济重心锚定于生产性积累。这要求：第一，强化对虚拟资本运动的监管，防止其过度脱离实体创造价值的能力。第二，提升实体经济的劳动生产力与技术自主性，正如工场手工业与机器大工业曾创造新的社会生产力，当前对半导体等“卡脖子”领域的突破，应服务于更平等、更可持续的生产关系构建，而非仅仅成为金融炒作的题材。第三，改革国际金融体系与分配机制，削弱金融霸权，使资本流动更多服务于各国实体经济的协调发展，而非少数金融集团的套利。真正的出路在于重新确立产业资本与生产性劳动的主导地位，使虚拟资本服务于而非主宰于现实财富的创造与公平分配。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] A cloud above the clouds: US, China race to make space a computing platform
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1852

AI Summary:

* The U.S. and China are engaged in a new "space race" focused on building a digital infrastructure layer in space, moving beyond historical competition for symbolic firsts. * A key U.S. player, SpaceX, is driving competition with plans for orbital data centers, which is influencing global demand for metals, chips, and rockets. * China is actively developing its commercial space sector, with initiatives like the "Three-Body Computing Constellation" project aiming to build a thousand-satellite network for space computing. * The core technical shift is from a traditional "sense in space, compute on Earth" model to "space-based primary computing," where data processing occurs directly in orbit via satellite networks. * Chinese experts state the strategic goal is to send computing power and AI into space, viewing it as a critical battlefield for technological competition that cannot be neglected.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
In 1957, the Soviet Union kicked off the Cold War’s space race, shocking the world by sending Sputnik, the world’s first artificial satellite, into orbit.

Four years later, it did the same to cosmonaut Yuri Gagarin, making him the first human in space. In 1969, the US provided another image that defined the era: Neil Armstrong leaving the Eagle lunar lander and planting the American flag on the moon, declaring it “one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.”

For the two superpowers, the space race was an ideological contest measured in firsts – with each milestone setting down a marker for technological know-how, national pride and a renewed commitment to push beyond the bounds of Earth.

Half a century later, a new space race is taking shape, with China emerging as one of the leading actors.

However, this competition is about more than symbolic firsts. Countries are seeking to make the technological achievements necessary to occupy, operate and utilise space, creating a new layer of digital infrastructure through satellites, networks, sensors and computing systems.

SpaceX, the US tech giant gearing up for what is expected to be the world’s biggest initial public offering as early as next month, has become a major player in this contest, releasing plans for orbital data centres, an experimental technology that is nonetheless sending ripples through global supply chains by driving demand for base metals, chips, satellites and rockets.

China, nurturing its own commercial space sector, is racing to keep up. Industry insiders and analysts based in the country said space was the new battlefield for technological competition, and Beijing could not afford a strategic absence.

“Talking too much about ‘the first’ is meaningless in a truly innovative context,” said Wang Jian, an academician at the Chinese Academy of Engineering, adding he wanted the country to “send computing power and AI into space”.

Wang, a founder of Alibaba Cloud, helped build China’s cloud computing industry. He now leads a research institute backed by Zhejiang’s provincial government, known as Zhejiang Lab. Its flagship Three-Body Computing Constellation project aims to build a thousand-satellite network for space computing.

“Space computing refers to shifting computing capability into orbit,” said Lin Guangrong, constellation communications systems architect at Chinese satellite maker GalaxySpace. “[It lets] satellites and spacecraft perform sensing, processing and data transmission directly in space.”

The ultimate vision is “space-based primary computing” – data centres that float in a layer above the actual clouds – according to China’s Academy of Information and Communications Technology, a think tank affiliated with the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology.

For decades, the aerospace industry operated under a standard procedure: sense in space, compute on Earth. Satellites were merely orbital cameras and mirrors, capturing raw data and waiting for a designated “downlink window” to beam it to a terrestrial ground station.

But a paradigm shift is under way, accelerated by rapid changes in the terrestrial economy and their attendant challenges.

On Earth, there are fears the boom in artificial intelligence data centres – described by Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang as “the largest infrastructure buildout in human history” – could run into crippling physical constraints such as land shortages, strained power grids and access to the water required for cooling.

“Current advances in AI are dependent on large terrestrial data centres,” wrote SpaceX CEO Elon Musk in a February memo announcing SpaceX’s acquisition of xAI. “In the long term, space-based AI is obviously the only way to scale.”

Tech leaders have begun to make the case for moving heavy AI workloads into orbit – where solar energy is abundant and the vacuum of space, in theory, would provide unlimited coolant – arguing it is the only sustainable way to ease the compounding infrastructure pressures on Earth’s ecosystems.

But more immediately, space computing would serve as a boon for data preservation – a wide-ranging benefit.

“As the number of satellites in orbit continues to grow alongside mission density, the volume of space data is rising rapidly,” Lin said.

Wang said about 90 per cent of remote-sensing satellite data was discarded in space because Earth-based networks could not handle the sheer volume of information.

That could result in delays and, sometimes, missed signals. Information about floods, wildfires, ship movements or cosmic ray bursts lasting only seconds could lose much of its value by requiring a downlink window before being analysed on Earth.

China entered the space race relatively late. Its first satellite, Dongfanghong 1, lifted off from Jiuquan in 1970 and broadcast the revolutionary song “The East Is Red” from orbit.

“From the perspective of technological competition, China cannot afford a strategic absence,” said Mi Lei, founder of CAS Star, a venture capital firm backed by the Chinese Academy of Sciences whose portfolio spans low-Earth-orbit communications, launch companies and satellite makers.

“Space is too important. In the new round of technological competition, a country has to be good at everything.”

While China does not face the same severe electricity and land constraints limiting the US capacity for data centre construction, it wants a central role in the next race.

Its advantage lies in its ability to fully mobilise a centralised industrial system – uniting components, satellites, rockets, launch sites, measurement systems, applications and a manufacturing base – under state command, according to a recent report by a research institute affiliated with the information technology ministry.

That holistic ecosystem allows China to bypass some of the fragmented commercial inefficiencies that can plague Western tech deployment.

Chinese authorities have voiced their support for space-based computing, sending a clear signal for capital and industry players. At a news conference last month, an official from the ministry said China would “support forward-looking research into space computing technologies and promote the orderly development of the space-computing industry”.

In the blueprint for the latest five-year plan, which runs through 2030, the commercial space sector was listed as one of 10 new industries and growth areas China would nurture and develop, as Beijing for the first time included the goal of becoming a “space power” among its national “strong country” objectives.

Against that backdrop, new research labs and companies are emerging.

The Three-Body Computing Constellation, designed to solve space’s data problem in space with AI, successfully launched an initial batch of 12 computing satellites in May last year. It is aiming for 100 next year and is equipping each spacecraft with an onboard computing unit capable of delivering up to 744 trillion operations a second.

Zhejiang Lab said in February it had deployed 10 AI models in orbit, including a remote-sensing model, an astronomical time-domain model, and a Qwen large language model from Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the South China Morning Post. Several of the models can be updated while in orbit.

In one recent experiment, satellites equipped with cosmic ray detectors used a space-based astronomical model to rapidly identify and classify gamma-ray bursts.

Zhejiang Lab also established in-orbit network links between six satellites this year, marking a significant step towards inter-satellite networking.

“The field is still in an early phase of exploration and technical validation,” said Lin of GalaxySpace.

The most immediate hurdle facing China’s ambition is the infrastructure required to get orbiting computers into the sky efficiently – an area where the US holds a long-standing advantage.

Driven by tech titans with deep pockets, the American aerospace industry has made strides in high-frequency, low-cost reusable rockets.

SpaceX completed roughly 165 orbital launches last year, with that rapid-fire capability enabling the construction of Starlink – a fully functioning global broadband low-Earth-orbit network with over 10,000 satellites.

Musk’s company is now leveraging that dominance, seeking US regulatory approval for a constellation of up to 1 million solar-powered satellites engineered specifically as orbital data centres. Such a massive fleet would solidify America’s architectural dominance over the lower atmosphere.

China, however, has yet to master high-frequency, SpaceX-style reusable rocket technology.

While its Qianfan and Guowang megaconstellation plans are ambitious, each has only launched a few hundred satellites so far.

Compounding the hardware gap is the nature of the competition itself. Beijing is primarily relying on state-directed labs, targeted pilot programmes, and a still-maturing commercial space industry, while Washington’s space computing push is being propelled by an aggressive, cash-rich alliance of private AI and chip giants.

Google is advancing its Project Suncatcher, a moon-shot effort to link solar-powered satellites equipped with its proprietary Tensor Processing Units in an orbital AI cloud.

AI start-up Anthropic struck a deal with SpaceX this month to use the latter’s Colossus 1 data centre, while signalling interest in a broader partnership to develop gigawatt-scale AI computational processing power in orbit.

Nvidia, meanwhile, unveiled a Space-1 Vera Rubin module at its GTC conference in March, describing it as part of a new accelerated computing platform for space applications. The company has already placed an H100 graphics processing unit in orbit on a satellite launched by Starcloud, a start-up it backs.

Yet both sides must confront the laws of physics, which favour no superpower.

“To make space computing work at scale, onboard computing, inter-satellite links, space-ground integration and thermal management all have to move forward together,” Lin said.

High-power computing units generate massive amounts of heat, but cooling is an issue in such a brutal environment. While space is cold, it is also a vacuum – meaning there is no air for convection.

Instead, heat must be laboriously conducted through the structural body of the spacecraft and radiated away through large thermal panels, making high-power space computing a heavy, complicated and expensive thermodynamic challenge.

Then comes the power paradox. Running trillions of operations per second requires immense energy, forcing engineers to develop lightweight, foldable, radiation-resistant solar arrays that must constantly balance thermal design with spacecraft orientation.

It is a systems-level conundrum, and no current solar cell has presented a clear solution. Every material choice involves severe trade-offs between weight, flexibility, lifespan and radiation tolerance.

To combat these challenges, industry players like GalaxySpace have not only poured capital into mass production of synthetic aperture radar satellites, but also into complex pumped fluid-loop thermal management systems and advanced flexible solar arrays.

According to a paper written by Google researchers, the engineering hurdles also encompass high-bandwidth inter-satellite laser communications, radiation-hardened components and long-term hardware reliability in an environment hostile to delicate silicon.

Google’s researchers were not as positive as Musk, who frames orbital data centres as a near-term possibility. They said the cost of launching and operating a space-based data centre would not become economically comparable to terrestrial infrastructure until the mid-2030s, assuming launch prices fall below US$200 per kg.

To put that into perspective, SpaceX says its Falcon 9 can send up to 22,800kg (50,265 pounds) into low-Earth orbit, and with each launch reported to cost about US$74 million, that implies a full-capacity launch cost of roughly US$3,245 per kg.

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

港股波动：资本流动表象下的结构性矛盾

现象描述： 港股科技与半导体股逆势上涨，但受中东地缘政治及通胀担忧拖累，恒生指数收跌，市场呈现资金博弈与区域风险交织的复杂态势。

item.title: [SCMP] What happens when Indonesia’s top corruption buster is suspected of graft himself?
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1021

AI Summary:

* **High-Level Scandal:** Febrie Adriansyah, Indonesia’s former deputy attorney general for special crimes, has been named a suspect in a major corruption and money-laundering investigation. * **Asset Seizure:** Authorities recovered 74kg of gold bars and over US$15 million in cash from a residence linked to Adriansyah, with a total estimated value of approximately US$26 million. * **Economic & Governance Concerns:** The scandal raises questions about institutional integrity in Indonesia, where a weak rupiah has already eroded investor confidence. Analysts note the case could either further damage trust or be seen as a positive step in combating corruption. * **Institutional Dilemma:** The investigation will be handed over to the Attorney General’s Office, creating a situation where the institution must now prosecute its own former senior official. * **Historical Tensions:** The case reflects long-standing institutional frictions between police and military personnel, stemming from reforms made after the fall of the Suharto dictatorship in 1998.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Rows of gold bars, stacks of US and Singapore dollars and seven suitcases found inside a locked safe have become the defining images of one of the biggest scandals to hit Indonesia’s law-enforcement establishment in years.

Police said they recovered 74kg (160lbs) of gold, plus more than US$15 million in multiple currencies from a house linked to Febrie Adriansyah, who until last week served as Indonesia’s deputy attorney general for special crimes, and is now suspected of corruption.

Authorities estimate the bullion and cash found at the Sentul residence in Bogor, southeast of Jakarta, are worth about 476 billion rupiah (US$26 million). Images of neatly arranged gold bars and bundles of banknotes have featured prominently across Indonesian television and news websites since the raids.

The scandal raises serious questions for Indonesia, where the rupiah tumbled 10 per cent in the past year to become Asia’s worst-performing currency, over concerns about institutional integrity and policymaking. A big question is whether the discovery of the riches shows that safeguards against corruption are working, or whether a succession of scandals indicates corruption is worsening.

“Any corruption case involving a senior government official can negatively affect investor confidence, as it raises concerns about governance and the investment climate,” said Henry Wibowo, director at Alphagate Capital in Jakarta. “However, if this reflects a genuine commitment by the government to strengthen governance and clean up corruption, it could ultimately be viewed positively.”

The case has created an unusual institutional dilemma. Police said the investigations would be transferred in stages to the Attorney General’s Office, meaning the institution Febrie led until days ago would now handle proceedings against its former senior official.

News outlet Tempo meanwhile reported an apparent confrontation involving police investigators and military personnel during the searches. The military said its presence was part of an authorised assignment to protect prosecutors and was not intended to obstruct the investigation.

The episode reflects tensions dating to the separation of the police from the armed forces after the fall of president Suharto, according to Kevin O’Rourke, an independent political analyst who publishes Reformasi Weekly. The late dictator’s fall in 1998 ushered in both democracy and a security overhaul aimed at curbing the military’s role in politics and domestic law enforcement.

“This is the latest iteration of long-standing tensions originating in the change in the institutions’ respective roles upon the fall of Suharto,” O’Rourke said.

Police on Saturday named Febrie and businessman Don Ritto as suspects in corruption and money-laundering investigations after questioning 15 witnesses, consulting two experts and searching 13 locations in Jakarta and nearby Sentul. Indonesia’s Corruption Eradication Commission separately said it suspected the Sentul residence was formally registered under the name of an unrelated nominee.

Febrie has acknowledged the property where the gold and cash were found was his long-time private residence but denied wrongdoing or ownership of the assets. He said the gold and money “have an owner”, without publicly identifying them, and that their provenance would be explained through the legal process.

Immigration authorities have barred Febrie and Ritto from leaving Indonesia for 20 days, but Febrie had not been detained as of the latest public statements by prosecutors. Ritto could not immediately be reached for comment.

The police announcement came a day after Febrie resigned. The Attorney General’s Office said his departure was intended to safeguard the integrity, objectivity and neutrality of law enforcement.

As Indonesia’s top corruption prosecutor and the operational head of one of President Prabowo Subianto’s flagship enforcement programmes, Febrie wielded influence across criminal investigations, state revenue and natural-resource governance.

As head of the Attorney General Office’s Special Crimes Division, he supervised cases involving state tin miner Timah, oil company Pertamina, flag carrier Garuda Indonesia, the free meals programme and former education minister Nadiem Makarim.

There have been criticisms of some of those cases. Nadiem’s recent conviction and 10-year prison sentence raised questions among observers over how Southeast Asia’s largest economy distinguishes corruption from disputed policy and business decisions.

Febrie also chaired the implementing team of Prabowo’s Forest Area Enforcement Task Force, helping lead the campaign to reclaim land and revenue from palm-oil and mining companies accused of operating illegally in forest areas.

In May, Prabowo presided over the handover of 10.3 trillion rupiah in penalties and other state revenue, along with 2.4 million hectares (6 million acres) of recovered forest land. The government has also said the task force has taken control of almost 5.9 million hectares used for palm-oil plantations and more than 12,000 hectares used for mining.

On Monday, the Ministry of Defence convened a closed-door meeting of the group, and it was unclear if Febrie remained chairman. Representatives from the military and the Attorney General’s Office attended, though no police officials were seen, even though the National Police is also represented on the task force.

Task force spokesman Barita Simanjuntak said after the meeting that its work would continue uninterrupted because it was governed by institutional mechanisms rather than any single official. He declined to say who would replace Febrie, referring questions to the attorney general.

Police said Febrie was suspected of committing corruption offences while serving as a state official, including in connection with the handling of a case involving state insurer Asabri. The investigations also involve alleged irregularities linked to coal supplies for state electricity company PLN and a debt settlement involving a Krakatau Steel-related entity, though authorities haven’t detailed his alleged role in each matter.

It is unclear if the inquiry will affect ongoing investigations connected with the high-profile prosecutor, but the likelihood is that the episode will reinforce concerns about corruption in the country.

Indonesia ranks 109th in Transparency International’s 2025 Corruption Perceptions Index, below Zambia and Sri Lanka and down 10 places from 2024, when former general Prabowo was elected president.

Prabowo has repeatedly vowed to clamp down on corruption, though his flagship, roughly US$15 billion food-for-kids programme is currently the subject of a corruption inquiry, with his hand-picked head of the operation named as a suspect.

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

港股波动：资本流动表象下的结构性矛盾

现象描述： 港股科技与半导体股逆势上涨，但受中东地缘政治及通胀担忧拖累，恒生指数收跌，市场呈现资金博弈与区域风险交织的复杂态势。

政治经济学定位： 此次波动是全球金融资本在特定时空背景下进行流动、重新配置与风险对冲的集中体现。它涉及资本运动的多重维度：一是不同区域资本市场间的套利资本流动（如空头回补、南向资金）；二是虚拟资本（股票）价格脱离实体基本面的剧烈波动；三是产业资本在科技、半导体等战略领域的估值重估。

深层结构分析： 现象背后是金融全球化背景下资本积累的内在矛盾。一方面，实体经济（如中国生产商价格指数上涨）与金融市场（如股市短期涨跌）出现背离。另一方面，资本为寻求增殖，在全球不同区域市场间快速转移。正如马克思在《资本论》中所指出的，“较多的工人在同一时间、同一空间，为了生产同种商品，在同一资本家的指挥下工作，这在历史上和逻辑上都是资本主义生产的起点。” 当前全球产业链的深度整合（如半导体）与金融市场（如港股作为国际金融中心）的紧密勾连，使得“同一资本”的指挥棒已延伸至全球范围，生产与流通、实体经济与虚拟经济的矛盾在更宏大的尺度上展开。

阶级/利益分析： 此轮波动中，国际与内地金融资本是核心参与者与潜在受益者。通过空头回补操作，全球机构投资者管理了其跨区域投资组合的风险。内地资金南向流入及政策支持（如南向交易扩容），则强化了内地资本对香港金融市场乃至全球资产配置的影响力。相反，依赖金融市场稳定进行长期融资的实体产业资本（特别是出口导向型制造业）面临更高不确定性。普通投资者，尤其是缺乏信息与资金优势的散户，其资产更容易在市场剧烈波动中受损。利润（剩余价值）在金融部门与生产部门间的分配可能进一步失衡，金融资本的支配地位得到巩固。

主流叙事批判： 主流财经话语倾向于将此次波动归因于“地缘政治风险”、“技术性反弹”、“资金流动”等中性化表述。例如，分析师称“近期抛售过度”引发反弹，或强调“空头回补”与“南向资金”的支撑作用。这种叙事将复杂的资本积累过程与阶级利益博弈，简化为市场参与者的情绪与技术性操作，掩盖了资本市场波动对实体经济的剥蚀作用，以及金融资本在全球范围内攫取剩余价值并转移风险的本质。马克思在《资本论》中深刻指出，“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 港股的涨跌，本质上是虚拟资本的重新定价游戏，其影响却实实在在地通过融资成本、财富效应等渠道，传导至现实的生产与雇佣关系之中。

解放前景： 要打破“金融繁荣”与“实体经济承压”的循环，关键在于遏制金融资本的过度膨胀与投机冲动，将经济重心锚定于生产性积累。这要求：第一，强化对虚拟资本运动的监管，防止其过度脱离实体创造价值的能力。第二，提升实体经济的劳动生产力与技术自主性，正如工场手工业与机器大工业曾创造新的社会生产力，当前对半导体等“卡脖子”领域的突破，应服务于更平等、更可持续的生产关系构建，而非仅仅成为金融炒作的题材。第三，改革国际金融体系与分配机制，削弱金融霸权，使资本流动更多服务于各国实体经济的协调发展，而非少数金融集团的套利。真正的出路在于重新确立产业资本与生产性劳动的主导地位，使虚拟资本服务于而非主宰于现实财富的创造与公平分配。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] China’s Xi Jinping arrives in a North Korea armed with advanced warships and AI drones
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1045

AI Summary:

* North Korea has developed its largest-ever guided-missile destroyer, the *Choe Hyon*-class (5,000 tonnes), with first-of-kind phased-array radar and vertical launch systems, possibly with Russian technical support. It can launch ballistic, cruise, and hypersonic missiles and may be nuclear-capable. * Pyongyang is also constructing its first nuclear-powered strategic submarine (8,700 tonnes), a development that could enhance its underwater deterrent by allowing longer, faster submerged operations, complicating regional missile defense. * Combat experience from deploying troops and equipment in Ukraine has significantly advanced North Korea's expertise in drone warfare and unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) technology. * These conventional and nuclear-capability advancements, aided by Russian military technology, are helping North Korea reshape the military balance of power against South Korea and the United States.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
When Chinese President Xi Jinping arrived in Pyongyang on Monday, he was entering a country undergoing notable development in both its nuclear programme and conventional combat capacity.

North Korea has been aided in these developments by Russian military technology and economic aid, received in exchange for sending troops and equipment to support Moscow’s war in Ukraine. These advances could help Pyongyang threaten the balance of power against South Korea and the United States.

The construction of Pyongyang’s Choe Hyon-class guided-missile destroyer, which has a displacement of up to 5,000 tonnes, was first unveiled in December 2024. It is North Korea’s largest-ever surface combatant.

Its first ship in the class, the Choe Hyon, was launched in April last year, while the second, the Kang Kon, was relaunched the following June after it capsized during its launch ceremony a month earlier. The two vessels are undergoing sea trials before being delivered to the North Korean navy.

Despite wider scepticism about the extent of damage to the Kang Kon during the incident, North Korean leader Kim Jong-un attended the sea trial of the vessel, state media reported last week.

It said the most important task in the new five-year national defence development policy was the “rapid strengthening of naval forces to play a part in nuclear war deterrence”.

Kim earlier called for two additional Choe Hyon-class destroyers to be deployed every year. The third vessel is reportedly under construction at the Nampo Shipyard near Pyongyang and scheduled to be launched by October.

The Choe Hyon-class destroyers are North Korea’s first naval vessels equipped with phased-array radar and vertical launch systems or VLS, possibly with technical support from Russia.

North Korean state media said the destroyer could launch various types of ballistic and cruise missiles for anti-aircraft, anti-ship and anti-submarine missions, and that some had hypersonic capabilities and potential nuclear armament.

North Korea is also ramping up its underwater fleet, regarded as one of the largest in the world in terms of numbers, and building its first nuclear-powered submarine, following Kim’s announcement in 2021 that the project had moved beyond the design and research phase.

Despite doubts around Pyongyang’s technological capabilities, the plan was realised in December when Kim visited the shipyard that was reportedly constructing a “nuclear-powered strategic guided-missile submarine” with a displacement of 8,700 tonnes.

Nuclear-powered submarines utilise on-board reactors, which allow a submarine to operate underwater at higher speeds for longer periods than conventional submarines, possibly complicating regional missile defence for the US and its allies.

As North Korean troops continue to engage in the Ukraine war, its military has gained significant combat experience in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) warfare, as it builds its own military drone fleet.

Going beyond its older reconnaissance models, North Korea’s new drone fleet incorporates new features for strategic surveillance UAVs, AI-integrated suicide drones and loitering munitions.

In 2023, North Korea unveiled the Saetbyol-4 and Saetbyol-9 strategic surveillance drones, modelled after the US RQ-4 Global Hawk and MQ-9 Reaper, respectively, and designed for deep-penetration intelligence gathering.

North Korea has also tested and showcased its Kumsong-series tactical attack drones, which debuted at the country’s 2024 weaponry exhibition. These low-cost loitering munitions are built for mass production and are produced in one of two shapes: a cross-shaped structure with X-shaped wings at the front and back; and a manta ray-shaped structure.

These shapes suggested possible imitation of foreign models, such as Russia’s Lancet-3 and Israel’s Harop suicide drone, respectively.

According to North Korean media and some external analysis, the drones have artificial intelligence (AI) technology capable of autonomous target detection and flight operations, with likely technical support from Russia. Reports indicate Pyongyang has sent workers and experts to Russian drone production facilities, such as those building Iranian Shahed drones, to rapidly scale their own capacities.

North Korea often makes announcements about its nuclear weapons programme around major diplomatic engagements with China.

Only a day before his trip to Beijing and summit with Xi in September, Kim visited a factory producing nuclear missile materials, including a new solid-fuel engine to be used for the Hwasong-19 and the new “next-generation” Hwasong-20 intercontinental ballistic missile.

Shortly after returning from that trip, Kim oversaw a test of a solid-fuel missile engine, which is more difficult to detect and more survivable than liquid-fuel weapons.

Last week, just a day before Xi’s visit to North Korea was announced, North Korean state media revealed that Kim had inspected a new plant that produces weapons-grade nuclear material.

Analysts said Xi’s visit could indirectly advance North Korea’s military build-up as Pyongyang strengthened diplomatic ties with Beijing and Moscow.

Kang Jun-young, a professor of Chinese studies at Hankuk University of Foreign Studies in Seoul, said Pyongyang’s attempt to strengthen relations with China was akin to “managed dependency”, as North Korea sought to “maintain its autonomy through close ties with Russia and the pursuit of dialogue with the US”.

He said restored North Korea-China relations could serve as a “pillar” for North Korea’s development of surface ships and nuclear submarines, with Russia as the technological supporter and China a “diplomatic and economic safety net”.

“These changes are highly likely to accelerate North Korea’s weapons development indirectly rather than through direct technology transfer,” Kang said.

“The recovery of trade between North Korea and China has improved conditions for procuring parts, materials and funding, which could enhance the sustainability of naval modernisation, though it is difficult to guarantee a qualitative leap.”

Lim Eul-chul, a professor at the Institute for Far Eastern Studies at Kyungnam University, said China now had to deal with a North Korea whose military capabilities had been “further strengthened”, potentially pushing Beijing to use Pyongyang to contain both US and Japanese military advancements.

“For China, effectively and properly countering the militarisation of not only the US but also Japan is of paramount importance,” Lim said.

“In this regard, it seems they have concluded that strategically utilising North Korea’s military capabilities would be more beneficial.”
