item.title: [SCMP] UK deal to serve as testing ground for India’s Western trade ambitions
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1048

AI Summary:

* The UK-India free-trade agreement eliminates tariffs on 99% of Indian exports and reduces duties on 90% of UK goods, providing immediate relief to businesses. * Bilateral trade rose 10% year-on-year to £47.9 billion by the end of 2025, positioning India as the UK's 11th-largest trading partner. * The deal serves as a crucial test for India's broader Western trade ambitions, offering a "practical blueprint" ahead of negotiations with other partners like the US. * Long-term success depends on overcoming implementation challenges, including customs compliance, product standards, and regulatory barriers, particularly for smaller Indian companies.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
India’s free-trade agreement with the United Kingdom will take effect on Wednesday, offering businesses on both sides immediate tariff relief while giving New Delhi an early test of the concessions, compliance demands and implementation challenges it may face in future deals with other Western powers.

UK exports in sectors such as luxury vehicles and spirits are expected to benefit, while Indian textiles and leather products will gain as the agreement eliminates tariffs on 99 per cent of exports from India and reduces duties on 90 per cent of British goods, analysts say.

“The earliest benefits will be tariff reductions on goods, simplified customs procedures, and improved market access for businesses in the UK and India,” said Christopher Blackburn, a London-based analyst.

“Bureaucratic hurdles that have caused headaches in the past will be removed or reduced.”

But analysts said the India-UK Free Trade Agreement’s longer-term value would depend on how effectively both governments and businesses handled customs compliance, product standards and other practical barriers to trade.

The agreement would provide a “practical blueprint” for resolving issues such as tariffs, services, mobility of people and regulatory cooperation, Blackburn said. “It’s good news for both sides.”

The value of total bilateral trade rose year-on-year by 10 per cent to £47.9 billion (US$64.2 billion) at the end of 2025, making India the UK’s 11th-largest trading partner, according to the British government.

The deal is being implemented at a crucial juncture for India as a long-pending agreement with the United States, one of its largest trading partners, is still being finalised.

For the UK, the deal is equally important strategically because it eases access to one of the world’s largest consumer markets, analysts say.

“Businesses on both sides will benefit at a moment when the global economy is growing increasingly fragmented and unpredictable. The main challenge will be implementation,” said Uday Chandra, a political-science professor at India’s Ashoka University.

Indian industry has had a mixed record with free-trade agreements: labour-intensive sectors such as textiles and pharmaceuticals secured greater market access, while manufacturing industries dealt with import pressures.

The main challenge for smaller Indian companies with the UK agreement would be adapting to new criteria such as determining product origin and customs procedures concerning the application of tariffs and export quotas, Chandra said.

“Indian exporters will also have to meet British regulatory, environmental and quality standards. British firms will have to work through the complexity of the Indian market,” he said.

India’s trade and foreign investment regulations are often seen as intricate, requiring navigation of sector-specific caps and strict foreign exchange rules.

“The success of the [free-trade agreement] will, therefore, depend on the extent to which both governments resolve everyday obstacles to trade,” Chandra said.

Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi and his British counterpart Keir Starmer signed the deal in July last year. At the time, British government estimates said the pact could add £4.8 billion, or about 0.13 per cent, to the country’s gross domestic product.

Starmer resigned as UK prime minister and leader of the ruling Labour Party last month, but has continued to serve as a caretaker prime minister until a new Labour chief is selected.

Analysts say the political changes are unlikely to affect the trade agreement because it enjoys broad cross-party support in the UK.

Starmer has cited the trade pact as an important political achievement with one of the world’s largest and fastest-growing economies, Chandra pointed out.

Despite this, Chandra said Labour would still need to show that the deal “produces investments, export orders and jobs” to boost support for the party after its recent defeats in local elections.

Biswajit Dhar, an economics professor at Delhi’s Council for Social Development, said how the agreement was implemented would be equally or more important for India, though “on the face of it there are a lot of market opportunities for both countries”.

More importantly, he said Indian industry had to be ready to meet environmental and labour standards to fully tap the market opportunities offered by the trade deal.

India’s exports to the UK will be subject to a Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism – a charge on carbon-intensive imports such as cement – that is scheduled to launch next year and scale up gradually, similar to the one implemented by the European Union.

Dhar said sectors in India such as iron and steel, fertilisers, cement, hydrogen and aluminium could be affected.

“We don’t have the preparedness. Our industries have not really looked at the global emission norms very seriously,” he said.

For Dhar, such readiness is particularly important because India and the EU are also expected to implement their free-trade agreement soon. The two sides concluded negotiations in January and are reviewing the treaty’s legal provisions.

Indian exports to the UK and the EU could also increasingly face stringent labour standards and sustainability requirements, driven by recent free-trade agreements and other laws, Dhar said.

The local industry would need to be sufficiently prepared to deal with such issues because India was set to conclude trade pacts with other major powers such as Canada and Australia which could enforce similar standards, Dhar said.

“All these issues have implications for other markets as well,” he said.

Delhi and Canberra have already implemented an interim deal called the Australia-India Economic Cooperation and Trade Agreement and announced plans for a broader free-trade pact.

Earlier in March, India and Canada formally relaunched trade negotiations for their Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement, following a diplomatic thaw after ties were strained three years ago over Ottawa’s accusations of Indian involvement in the assassination of Canadian Sikh activist Hardeep Singh Nijjar.

Chandra said the UK-India trade agreement would help Indian industry assess what it needed to do to take advantage of such deals.

“The UK agreement gives India valuable experience in implementing a comprehensive trade deal with an advanced Western economy. It also demonstrates that Delhi is willing to make significant concessions on tariffs, services, and government procurement when it receives meaningful market access in return,” he said.

“For British firms, there is a potential first-mover advantage. They will receive preferential access to India before the EU agreement is likely to enter into force later in 2026-27.”

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

港股波动：资本流动表象下的结构性矛盾

现象描述： 港股科技与半导体股逆势上涨，但受中东地缘政治及通胀担忧拖累，恒生指数收跌，市场呈现资金博弈与区域风险交织的复杂态势。

政治经济学定位： 此次波动是全球金融资本在特定时空背景下进行流动、重新配置与风险对冲的集中体现。它涉及资本运动的多重维度：一是不同区域资本市场间的套利资本流动（如空头回补、南向资金）；二是虚拟资本（股票）价格脱离实体基本面的剧烈波动；三是产业资本在科技、半导体等战略领域的估值重估。

深层结构分析： 现象背后是金融全球化背景下资本积累的内在矛盾。一方面，实体经济（如中国生产商价格指数上涨）与金融市场（如股市短期涨跌）出现背离。另一方面，资本为寻求增殖，在全球不同区域市场间快速转移。正如马克思在《资本论》中所指出的，“较多的工人在同一时间、同一空间，为了生产同种商品，在同一资本家的指挥下工作，这在历史上和逻辑上都是资本主义生产的起点。” 当前全球产业链的深度整合（如半导体）与金融市场（如港股作为国际金融中心）的紧密勾连，使得“同一资本”的指挥棒已延伸至全球范围，生产与流通、实体经济与虚拟经济的矛盾在更宏大的尺度上展开。

阶级/利益分析： 此轮波动中，国际与内地金融资本是核心参与者与潜在受益者。通过空头回补操作，全球机构投资者管理了其跨区域投资组合的风险。内地资金南向流入及政策支持（如南向交易扩容），则强化了内地资本对香港金融市场乃至全球资产配置的影响力。相反，依赖金融市场稳定进行长期融资的实体产业资本（特别是出口导向型制造业）面临更高不确定性。普通投资者，尤其是缺乏信息与资金优势的散户，其资产更容易在市场剧烈波动中受损。利润（剩余价值）在金融部门与生产部门间的分配可能进一步失衡，金融资本的支配地位得到巩固。

主流叙事批判： 主流财经话语倾向于将此次波动归因于“地缘政治风险”、“技术性反弹”、“资金流动”等中性化表述。例如，分析师称“近期抛售过度”引发反弹，或强调“空头回补”与“南向资金”的支撑作用。这种叙事将复杂的资本积累过程与阶级利益博弈，简化为市场参与者的情绪与技术性操作，掩盖了资本市场波动对实体经济的剥蚀作用，以及金融资本在全球范围内攫取剩余价值并转移风险的本质。马克思在《资本论》中深刻指出，“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 港股的涨跌，本质上是虚拟资本的重新定价游戏，其影响却实实在在地通过融资成本、财富效应等渠道，传导至现实的生产与雇佣关系之中。

解放前景： 要打破“金融繁荣”与“实体经济承压”的循环，关键在于遏制金融资本的过度膨胀与投机冲动，将经济重心锚定于生产性积累。这要求：第一，强化对虚拟资本运动的监管，防止其过度脱离实体创造价值的能力。第二，提升实体经济的劳动生产力与技术自主性，正如工场手工业与机器大工业曾创造新的社会生产力，当前对半导体等“卡脖子”领域的突破，应服务于更平等、更可持续的生产关系构建，而非仅仅成为金融炒作的题材。第三，改革国际金融体系与分配机制，削弱金融霸权，使资本流动更多服务于各国实体经济的协调发展，而非少数金融集团的套利。真正的出路在于重新确立产业资本与生产性劳动的主导地位，使虚拟资本服务于而非主宰于现实财富的创造与公平分配。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Rolex Oyster celebrates 100 years of revolutionising watchmaking
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1062

AI Summary:

* The Rolex Oyster, launched in 1926, is credited as the first practical waterproof wristwatch, designed with a hermetically sealed case (screw-down bezel, back, and crown) for everyday durability and protection from water, dust, and debris. * Its innovation was proven publicly in 1927 when British swimmer Mercedes Gleitze wore it during an 10+ hour English Channel crossing, with the watch remaining functional throughout. * Prior to the Oyster, wristwatches had only recently gained mainstream male adoption due to wartime utility, but their durability and precision for daily use were unresolved challenges. * The Oyster case's design, which protected the movement like an oyster shell safeguards a pearl, established the foundational blueprint for the modern mechanical wristwatch, making features like water resistance standard in contemporary horology.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Few timepieces can genuinely claim to have changed the course of watchmaking. As it celebrates its centenary this year, the Oyster stands not merely as Rolex’s longest-lasting creation, but also as the conceptual blueprint for the modern mechanical wristwatch. Its influence is embedded so deeply into contemporary horology that its once-radical ideas – water resistance, everyday durability – are now taken for granted.

Until the late 19th century, wristwatches had been regarded as feminine accessories while men relied on pocket watches that were protected from dust and moisture. Warfare transformed that convention. Soldiers, needing easy access to the time in combat, began strapping pocket watches to their wrists, giving rise to the “wristlet”. That practicality caught on beyond the battlefield, and, by the first world war, the wristwatch had firmly established itself among men, too, even though functional drawbacks still existed.

In 1926, Rolex was still far from the global force it would become. Founded in London in 1905 as Wilsdorf & Davis, by German businessman Hans Wilsdorf and his brother-in-law, Alfred Davis, the company had spent its early decades building credibility in precision timekeeping while harbouring ambitions to compete as an equal with long-established, prestigious Swiss manufacturers such as Vacheron Constantin, Blancpain and Patek Philippe.

In 1910, a Rolex became the first wristwatch to receive a chronometric certificate from the Official Watch Rating Centre in Switzerland. Four years later, another achieved the Class A certificate from Britain’s Kew Observatory, an honour previously reserved for marine chronometers. Precision was no longer the question. Durability was.

Wilsdorf understood that accuracy meant little if a watch could not survive real-life challenges. His answer arrived in 1926, by which time Rolex had relocated its base to Switzerland. The innovation that went into producing the Oyster was not simply water resistance – waterproof watches had existed in experimental forms before – but the creation of a hermetically sealed case designed for daily, long-term wear.

Rolex achieved this by engineering a system in which the bezel, case back and winding crown were screwed tightly against the middle case, forming an airtight architecture that protected the movement from water, dust and debris. The name captured the idea; like an oyster protecting the pearl within, the case became a shell safeguarding the movement. The watch could accompany its wearer through rain, travel, sport and work.

The new watch case’s first serious test came in 1927, when British swimmer Mercedes Gleitze attempted to cross the English Channel wearing a Rolex Oyster. After more than 10 hours in freezing waters, she and the watch emerged, the latter still running.

From there, the Oyster became a companion to other adventurers. On Malcolm Campbell’s land-speed record attempts in the 1920s and 30s, it endured extreme vibration, further proving that a wristwatch could maintain accuracy far beyond the controlled conditions of the watchmaker’s bench.

It accompanied pioneering aviators such as Chuck Yeager, surviving the thin air, freezing temperatures and physical strain of early high-altitude flight. It reached the highest peaks with mountaineers, proving that mechanical precision could endure where human performance itself was pushed to its limits.

Still, one challenge remained. Like all watches of its era, a Rolex required daily winding. That changed in 1931, with the Perpetual rotor. Using a freely oscillating weight powered by the motion of the wrist, Rolex created a self-winding mechanism that continuously replenished the mainspring. It eliminated the need for manual winding, reducing wear on the crown and preserving the integrity of the waterproof case.

The Oyster case and Perpetual rotor transformed the wristwatch into something new: an object that not only endured, but maintained itself, and in the process formed the foundation for an entire ecosystem of timepieces.

The Rolex Explorer, launched in 1953, was born from it. Shaped by high-altitude expeditions, it prioritised reliability and legibility – essential in low-oxygen conditions, freezing temperatures and reduced visibility.

The Submariner (also 1953) extended these principles beneath the waves, combining water resistance – initially rated to 100 metres and later 200 metres – with a rotating bezel that enabled divers to track elapsed immersion time. Its unidirectional 60-minute bezel became a safety feature, preventing accidental adjustment during a dive. High-contrast luminous markers and hands ensured legibility in low-light conditions.

The 1950s also saw the launch of the Rolex GMT-Master. Designed for Pan American World Airways pilots, it displays two time zones simultaneously and has a 24-hour rotating bezel and dedicated GMT hand that transform the watch into a navigation tool.

The Rolex Day-Date was launched in 1956 exclusively in precious metals – gold or platinum – setting it apart from the brand’s tool watches. It would become associated with political and cultural figures, most notably a United States president, Lyndon B. Johnson.

Later came the Rolex Cosmograph Daytona (1963), which was designed for racing drivers, combining a tachymeter bezel for calculating average speed, a precision timing mechanism for lap measurement and high-legibility sub-dials for use under pressure. Named after the Daytona International Speedway in Florida, its identity was amplified by celebrities such as actor and racing-car driver Paul Newman.

Other brands noticed how the watches of the Oyster family had become companions built to travel, endure and participate in life, and rose to the challenge. Omega refined water-resistant sports models and professional timing tools; Blancpain defined early modern diving capability with the Fifty Fathoms collection; IWC developed robust, engineer-led pilot’s watches; and Jaeger-LeCoultre explored durable, utility-driven complications such as those of the Memovox series.

But it had been the ingenuity of the Oyster case, 100 years ago, that opened those doors for others to follow.

To mark the centenary, Rolex has introduced commemorative Oyster Perpetual models that remain faithful to the design’s restrained identity. A 41mm version pairs a slate dial with yellow-gold detailing while a 36mm model references classic dial textures with subtle anniversary markings. Discreet engravings, including a “100” crown detail, acknowledge the milestone without disrupting the Oyster’s defining understatement.

The celebration has extended beyond product launches. In Shanghai, Rolex presented the “Oyster Story”, an immersive exhibition at the West Bund Dome that ran from June 10 to 28. The exhibition traced the Oyster’s evolution through archival pieces, technical components and interactive installations that revealed the engineering behind the design.

One hundred years on, the Oyster’s innovations have become industry standards, absorbed into the very language of watchmaking.

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

item.title: [SCMP] Wombs for hire: the painful price of Asia’s baby trade
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1289

AI Summary:

• **Illegal but Thriving Industry**: Commercial surrogacy is banned in Thailand, Laos, and Cambodia, yet an estimated US$15 billion global market in 2023 persists, with women paid $10,000–$15,000 to act as surrogates. • **Legal Gray Area in China**: While China does not explicitly outlaw surrogacy, it punishes medical professionals who facilitate it, creating a legal ambiguity that allows surrogate-born children to claim Chinese citizenship. • **Transnational Operations**: Due to legal constraints, the process often involves moving embryos and participants across multiple countries to find legal loopholes. • **Severe Crackdowns**: Thai authorities have recently sentenced four obstetricians and four brokers to up to 15 years in prison for running a transnational surrogacy ring, and detained additional Chinese nationals suspected of similar operations. • **Human Impact**: The trade is driven by demand from individuals, such as LGBTQ+ Chinese men or those affected by fertility issues, seeking family formation despite legal and emotional complexities.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
As the car bounces along a rural Thai road, a baby just a few months old watches the emerald green countryside rush by from Nicha’s* lap, oblivious to the countdown that has already begun.

Soon the child will be handed over to her father to be raised in China by grandparents whose own hopes of a larger family were curtailed by decades of state population control.

Nicha may never see the baby she gave birth to again, but she knows that is the painful price of commercial surrogacy.

This is her third time around the carousel of pregnancy, love, nurture and eventual separation that always ends with the same abrupt emotional wrench.

“It’s a deep bond, just like carrying and raising your own child,” Nicha told This Week in Asia, sharing videos taken on her phone of the smiling baby.

“My whole family has fallen in love including my own parents and children. It will be so hard to give up this baby, but I have to.”

Commercial surrogacy is illegal in Thailand, yet a shadow economy thrives in its place, one where women like Nicha are paid US$10,000 to US$15,000 to help strangers become parents.

Neighbouring Laos and Cambodia enforce similar bans. China, meanwhile, punishes medical professionals who facilitate the trade at home, though surrogacy itself is not explicitly outlawed there – a legal grey zone that lets surrogate-born children claim Chinese citizenship.

Legal constraints mean embryo transfer and fertilisation can span several territories, leaving surrogates and prospective parents to travel the world in search of safe legal ground.

If they get caught, the outcomes can be severe.

Earlier this month, a court in Bangkok handed down sentences of up to 15 years to four Thai obstetricians and four brokers, convicted over their roles in a transnational surrogacy ring.

Prosecutors traced the operation to Zhao Ran, a Chinese national in detention since 2020 for recruiting surrogate women and paying them for their eggs.

Days after the verdict, Thai police detained two more Chinese nationals at a hotel in the seaside town of Pattaya – a man and woman in their thirties and forties – who were suspected of running an illegal surrogacy business , according to Thailand’s Central Investigation Bureau.

None of which has done much to deter demand. Money, it turns out, still makes babies.

“The pathway to surrogacy has always existed,” said Jun*, a gay Chinese man in his thirties and the father of Nicha’s baby. “The only difference between people who can do it and those who can’t is financial means.”

For Jun, surrogacy offered a way around the stigma that still surrounds sexuality and childlessness in a culture where family remains paramount.

His egg donor was a Russian woman he met once, over a hurried meal, before their paths diverged for good. “After that we went our separate ways and never spoke again,” he said.

Less than a year later, Jun had a child.

Estimates from the United Nations put the global surrogacy market at nearly US$15 billion in 2023 – a figure that was expected to multiply six- or seven-fold within the following decade, as populations decline and people put off having children until later in life.

But surrogate mothers “receive only a small fraction” of the money the industry generates, according to a report by the UN special rapporteur on violence against women and girls published last year, with the bulk going to intermediaries such as clinics, brokers, recruiters and agents.

Legal precarity leaves surrogate mothers at risk of being punished, or of going unpaid under contracts that are unenforceable precisely because the trade is illegal. By its very nature, the UN special rapporteur notes, commercial surrogacy is defined by “harmful power imbalances”. Paying for a baby, in other words, is an ethical minefield.

Thailand banned commercial surrogacy for foreign parents in 2015, after a Thai surrogate had twins for an Australian couple who then refused to accept one of the babies, named Gammy, who was born with Down’s syndrome. Cambodia and Laos followed suit as allegations of exploitation of poor women emerged across the Mekong region.

After Thailand’s laws tightened, Russia and Ukraine became global surrogacy hubs, until war meant that Georgia and, increasingly, Armenia, have taken over as bases for clients from the US, China, Israel and India.

Given the industry’s shadowy nature, there are no comprehensive statistics on how many surrogate babies are born each year. But experts say China drives a large share of global demand.

Without an outright ban, “the domestic industry has been pushed underground or overseas”, said Dr Yingyi Luo, a research fellow at the University of Melbourne, adding that Chinese clients often preferred foreign surrogates seen as more likely to hand over the baby “cleanly”, without complication.

Luo, whose research spans the legal, ethical and cultural dimensions of surrogacy supply chains, says Chinese demand is diverse, from LGBTQ couples to people who married late or lost children and those with fertility challenges.

But she cautioned that surrogate mothers had few protections under the law and were for all intents and purposes “invisible” to authorities, “with little need to consider their consent, financial situation or psychological support”.

Still, the supply of surrogates from Southeast Asia shows no sign of drying up.

Poor, mainly rural women are enticed in by lump-sum payments to pare down debts, fund their children’s education or buy homes for elderly parents.

Recruitment happens through closed Facebook groups, said Nicha, a mother of two from a Thai farming community where the average monthly wage is US$270 to US$300 at best.

Lao, Filipino and Vietnamese women also form part of the global surrogacy supply chain, many making similar zigzagging journeys to evade authorities.

Nicha recalls a Thai mother who had an embryo from China transferred in Boten, on the Laos-China border, returned to Thailand for the first trimester, then travelled back to the mainland with the pregnancy hidden, but timed so the birth would happen close to the family paying for it.

The Zhao Ran case pushed the industry further underground, according to Thai police.

“Illegal surrogacy operations became far more complex,” a senior detective who has worked on such cases told This Week in Asia, speaking anonymously as he was not authorised to talk to media.

“The entire process, from sourcing and storing sperm and eggs, to embryo implantation and finally childbirth, became much more sophisticated, making it harder for investigators to uncover wrongdoing.”

Calls for legal reform are mounting in Thailand, to protect women from exploitation while bringing the trade in babies into a regulated space for surrogates and parents alike.

Reproductive healthcare should be a right

Nattawut Buapratum, Thai opposition lawmaker

Fertility clinics across the Thai capital would welcome the surge in demand a legal change might bring. But decriminalisation may still be years away.

“Reproductive healthcare should be a right,” said Nattawut Buapratum, an opposition lawmaker and former member of Thailand’s Committee for the Protection of Children Born Through Assisted Reproductive Technology. Under current law “in practice, access is largely limited to people who can afford it”, he said.

But surrogacy is a complex issue and even under a ban, Thai women are exposed to exploitation in commercial arrangements, according to Nattawut.

“We therefore need to carefully balance several competing considerations when amending the law: advances in medical technology, women’s rights and the rights of the child.”

As she prepares for the heartbreak of handing over a baby yet again, Nicha says this will be her last time.

“But the ones who do it, do it again and again,” she said. “We have no choice. There is nothing better we can do for money.”

*Names changed to protect interviewees’ identity

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

港股波动：资本流动表象下的结构性矛盾

现象描述： 港股科技与半导体股逆势上涨，但受中东地缘政治及通胀担忧拖累，恒生指数收跌，市场呈现资金博弈与区域风险交织的复杂态势。

政治经济学定位： 此次波动是全球金融资本在特定时空背景下进行流动、重新配置与风险对冲的集中体现。它涉及资本运动的多重维度：一是不同区域资本市场间的套利资本流动（如空头回补、南向资金）；二是虚拟资本（股票）价格脱离实体基本面的剧烈波动；三是产业资本在科技、半导体等战略领域的估值重估。

深层结构分析： 现象背后是金融全球化背景下资本积累的内在矛盾。一方面，实体经济（如中国生产商价格指数上涨）与金融市场（如股市短期涨跌）出现背离。另一方面，资本为寻求增殖，在全球不同区域市场间快速转移。正如马克思在《资本论》中所指出的，“较多的工人在同一时间、同一空间，为了生产同种商品，在同一资本家的指挥下工作，这在历史上和逻辑上都是资本主义生产的起点。” 当前全球产业链的深度整合（如半导体）与金融市场（如港股作为国际金融中心）的紧密勾连，使得“同一资本”的指挥棒已延伸至全球范围，生产与流通、实体经济与虚拟经济的矛盾在更宏大的尺度上展开。

阶级/利益分析： 此轮波动中，国际与内地金融资本是核心参与者与潜在受益者。通过空头回补操作，全球机构投资者管理了其跨区域投资组合的风险。内地资金南向流入及政策支持（如南向交易扩容），则强化了内地资本对香港金融市场乃至全球资产配置的影响力。相反，依赖金融市场稳定进行长期融资的实体产业资本（特别是出口导向型制造业）面临更高不确定性。普通投资者，尤其是缺乏信息与资金优势的散户，其资产更容易在市场剧烈波动中受损。利润（剩余价值）在金融部门与生产部门间的分配可能进一步失衡，金融资本的支配地位得到巩固。

主流叙事批判： 主流财经话语倾向于将此次波动归因于“地缘政治风险”、“技术性反弹”、“资金流动”等中性化表述。例如，分析师称“近期抛售过度”引发反弹，或强调“空头回补”与“南向资金”的支撑作用。这种叙事将复杂的资本积累过程与阶级利益博弈，简化为市场参与者的情绪与技术性操作，掩盖了资本市场波动对实体经济的剥蚀作用，以及金融资本在全球范围内攫取剩余价值并转移风险的本质。马克思在《资本论》中深刻指出，“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 港股的涨跌，本质上是虚拟资本的重新定价游戏，其影响却实实在在地通过融资成本、财富效应等渠道，传导至现实的生产与雇佣关系之中。

解放前景： 要打破“金融繁荣”与“实体经济承压”的循环，关键在于遏制金融资本的过度膨胀与投机冲动，将经济重心锚定于生产性积累。这要求：第一，强化对虚拟资本运动的监管，防止其过度脱离实体创造价值的能力。第二，提升实体经济的劳动生产力与技术自主性，正如工场手工业与机器大工业曾创造新的社会生产力，当前对半导体等“卡脖子”领域的突破，应服务于更平等、更可持续的生产关系构建，而非仅仅成为金融炒作的题材。第三，改革国际金融体系与分配机制，削弱金融霸权，使资本流动更多服务于各国实体经济的协调发展，而非少数金融集团的套利。真正的出路在于重新确立产业资本与生产性劳动的主导地位，使虚拟资本服务于而非主宰于现实财富的创造与公平分配。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Asia’s shaky food supply shudders as ‘super’ El Nino arrives
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1720

AI Summary:

• A "super" El Nino has officially formed, with Pacific ocean temperatures running 2.5°C above normal; forecasters give high odds it could be among the strongest since 1950. • Past super El Ninos have devastated key Asian crops, including Indian soybeans, Thai rice, Vietnamese coffee, and Indonesian/Malaysian palm oil. • Farmers, such as those in Thailand's cacao industry, face severe risks from extreme heat, drought, flooding, and disease that could wipe out harvests. • The event threatens food security for Asia's 4.7 billion people, hundreds of millions of whom depend directly on agriculture for their livelihoods.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
In the mist-wrapped hills of northern Thailand, where cacao trees erupt from the rich, nutrient-dense soil, three little words are spreading fear through a community of farmers.

“There is no way to know for certain,” Koranut Rattanayanyong told This Week in Asia. “But it could be a total wipeout.”

The weather event Koranut is dreading? A “super” El Nino.

Earlier this month, the climate phenomenon officially started to form over the Pacific, with a bloom of ocean heat running 2.5 degrees Celsius (4.5 degrees Fahrenheit) above normal temperatures.

The forecasts emanating from meteorological agencies across the world in the days since have been almost uniform in their alarm.

Australia’s Bureau of Meteorology said on Tuesday that this year’s El Nino “could peak at levels among the highest observed since 1950”. Its US counterpart gave almost two in three odds that it would be “very strong”.

And UN Secretary General Antonio Guterres warned on June 2 that this year’s event “will hit even harder, travel even farther and cross borders with devastating speed”.

El Nino is a recurring disruption. In essence, one forms when the Pacific Ocean runs a fever, redistributing heat and moisture in ways that can shatter the climatic rhythms on which billions of people depend. It brings drought to some regions, catastrophic flooding to others and the kinds of climatic extremes – hot days, dry soils, then sudden deluges – that can overwhelm crops.

A “super” event, scientists warn, would do all of that and more, across a wider geography, with greater intensity, for longer. It has levelled harvests before: a decade ago, a super El Nino devastated Indian soybean yields, Thai rice, Vietnam’s Robusta coffee crop and the palm oil output of both Indonesia and Malaysia.

For Asia and its 4.7 billion people – hundreds of millions of whom still live off the land – what comes next could be devastating.

Koranut has spent years building something unusual in the hills of Chiang Mai: a cacao economy.

Growers and grinders, processors and craft chocolatiers, all connected through his company Cocoa Baan Suan, operate a direct, farm-to-wrapper loop that ends in chocolate-coated nuts, dense brownies and bars of trendy “Dubai chocolate”.

This, he wants to show the world, is what rural Thailand could do if it moved up the value chain, from raw commodity to finished product, from subsistence to sophistication.

But cacao trees are highly sensitive to variations in heat and rainfall.

“Even if our water supply is good, if the heat above is that intense, the trees can’t survive,” Koranut said. “If there is waterlogging, the cacao trees will develop fungal problems. And if the yields are down … you can forget about all of this.”

Now, Koranut is waiting for a weather event that could erase it all. And he is far from alone.

In Pakistan, agriculture accounts for around 24 per cent of gross domestic product; in Laos, a similar share; in India, the government estimates that roughly 145 million people farm for a living, making up 16 per cent of the entire economy.

Even Southeast Asian industrial powerhouses such as Indonesia and Vietnam, not often thought of as agrarian economies, still depend on agriculture for 14 and 13 per cent of their output, respectively.

El Nino conditions will pour fuel on the fire of a warming world

UN Secretary General Antonio Guterres

This year’s super El Nino is arriving against a troubling backdrop. Sky-high fertiliser and fuel prices, a result of supply disruptions caused by the US-Israel war on Iran, led many Asian farmers to use less than needed this planting season.

Currencies across the region have also weakened, making food imports more expensive just as domestic production may be about to fall.

“The challenge is compounded … by the energy crisis, supply chain disruptions and the impact of weakening currencies on imports across markets from Indonesia to India,” said Vikash Sharma, director of Godrej International Trading and Investments, a Singapore-based commodity trading house.

“An El Nino of this nature will also have a ripple effect on places like Singapore and Hong Kong, both of which rely heavily on food imports.”

Nowhere, in other words, is likely to escape unscathed.

Ratings agency Fitch has warned of “sustained shortages” accelerating inflation. Futures prices for Arabica coffee, cacao and sugar have all climbed on commodity exchanges in anticipation of the fallout.

“Farm commodity markets are going to be highly volatile,” said Desh Ratna, a commodity market specialist operating out of Vietnam’s Ho Chi Minh City.

“Some parts of the world will experience dryness while others will see more rainfall. This, in turn, will impact yields and result in price spikes.”

That asymmetry may produce its own dislocations: if El Nino damages American soybean output – the foundation of the global animal feed supply – prices could rise across every food category that relies on it.

Some supply loss may be partially offset by farmers in India pivoting from water-hungry crops such as sugar cane and corn towards soybean, as officials there expect. But the overall direction of travel is clear.

From Bangkok to Tokyo, Asia’s cities were already adapting to a warming baseline. Cool rooms are being installed in public spaces. Outdoor workers are being sent home at midday.

Last year’s record temperatures extracted a grim toll in heatstroke deaths that governments are not eager to repeat.

The Philippines, perennially battered by Pacific storm systems, is steeling itself for a more violent typhoon season. Across Asia, conservationists are sounding alarms about biodiversity corridors that could be severed by wildfires if this year’s dry season turns extreme.

“El Nino conditions will pour fuel on the fire of a warming world,” UN chief Guterres warned this month. And the world is not ready.

In Nepal’s Chitwan district, not far from the flat agricultural plains that stretch south towards the Indian border, Ramesh Chandra Puri is worried about his summer rice crop.

“We have heard that this kind of weather is coming soon,” the 63-year-old said of the forecasts of unusually dry weather that have begun filtering through to his village. “But the government has not given us any support to prepare for it.”

His most recent harvest has been disrupted by weeks of heavy, unseasonal rain – too much water now, potentially too little later. The local traders paying to buy his grain are offering roughly half the government-mandated price of 3,600 Nepalese rupees (US$24) per 100kg.

That is rapidly eating away at the funds he needs to prepare his fields for the next crop, sown in late June or early July – without even taking into account the water needed to nourish it.

“If conditions are really dry this summer, we will have to resort to boring groundwater,” Puri said. “But if the hot and dry weather gets too intense, even boring does not work.”

A version of the predicament Puri finds himself in – caught between the immediate cruelty of low prices and the gathering threat of a climate event he cannot control – is now playing out among farmers across the continent.

For those who have irrigation, groundwater reserves or dams filled by last year’s rains may be enough to get through, according to Ajay Jakhar, who chairs Bharat Krishak Samaj, a farmers’ welfare organisation based in Punjab, one of India’s most productive agricultural states.

“The problem, however, is with farmers’ livelihoods in rain-fed areas such as Vidarbha in Maharashtra,” he said. Vidarbha, a region long associated in India with agrarian distress, severe debt and high rates of farmer suicides, does not need another bad season.

Roughly 45 per cent of the world’s most populous nation – some 660 million people – relies, directly or indirectly, on agriculture.

India’s Agriculture Minister Shivraj Singh Chouhan held a high-level meeting on Tuesday to outline a strategy for dealing with this year’s El Nino by redirecting resources and helping farmers pivot to more drought-resistant crops ahead of the monsoon planting season.

“Full preparations should be made in advance in those districts where there is a possibility of low rainfall or uneven rainfall,” he told the assembled officials present, adding that “weekly meetings will be held” to prepare.

India can boast community-led adaptation models, new irrigation techniques and a track record of feeding its population through shocks that would have caused crises a generation ago.

But the fertiliser shortage triggered by the Strait of Hormuz crisis – only now expected to ease as a US-Iran peace deal edges towards completion – will still be felt in the soil this season. And the heat has already been exceptional this year, even before El Nino arrives in earnest.

“The ongoing extreme heat conditions are causing alarm,” said Devinder Sharma, an independent food and trade policy analyst. “We are expecting El Nino, whose effect would be visible in July or August. This is a deadly combination for India.”

Should New Delhi’s confidence in its own resilience turns out to be misplaced, the consequences could spiral far beyond its borders.

“If there is any doubt about production, India will stop exporting food,” Jakhar said.

In a year when harvests are already under threat from Thailand to Indonesia to Vietnam, an export ban by India – one of the world’s largest food and agricultural exporters and its leading producer of rice – would land like a tonne of bricks on global markets.

Back in Chiang Mai, Koranut’s son Pinyawat thinks about the ecosystem his father has spent years building as the rain patters down.

“Really, we’re just getting started,” he said, sharing his hope that one day, cacao could become a valuable cash crop for the region.

Thailand produces around 1,500 to 2,000 tonnes of cacao each year: a fraction of the 600,000 tonnes produced by Indonesia, the world’s No 3 producer behind only the Ivory Coast and Ghana.

“But if the climate works against us, the yields won’t justify the costs,” Pinyawat said.

His father is more defiant, though not naively so. He talks about planting shade trees to protect the smaller cacao plants from direct heat, about new irrigation technology that might carry the crop through the driest stretches.

“Every problem has a solution,” Koranut said. “We just have to keep solving it … We have to fight.”

item.title: [SCMP] Trump to restore Iran blockade, charge fees for Strait of Hormuz shipping
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1115

AI Summary:

- U.S. President Donald Trump announced the reinstatement of a naval blockade on Iran, barring Iranian vessels and those trading with Iran from the Strait of Hormuz, while other countries' ships would retain access but face a 20% fee on cargo. - Trump stated the fee is intended to reimburse the U.S. for providing "safety and security" in the region, citing protection of Gulf allies and the cost of ongoing military actions. - The International Maritime Organization (IMO) responded by reaffirming its opposition to mandatory fees for strait transit, asserting there is no legal basis for such tolls on international navigation. - Maritime law experts suggested the proposal could be framed as an optional payment for U.S. naval protection services—a "fee for services rendered"—rather than a transit toll, similar to anti-piracy arrangements used elsewhere.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
US President Donald Trump said on Monday that the United States would begin charging commercial vessels for safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz as Washington reinstates its blockade of Iran following a renewed escalation in the conflict.

Trump said Iranian vessels and ships trading with Iran would be barred from using the strategic waterway, while other countries would retain access under a system that would impose a 20 per cent fee on eligible cargoes.

“We are reinstating the THE IRANIAN BLOCKADE, so named because it is only stopping Iran’s ships or customers from entering or leaving,” Trump said online. “All other countries will have fair and open use of the Strait.”

The US president said the charge would help pay for “any and all costs necessary to do the job of providing safety and security to this very volatile section of the World”.

The proposal prompted questions about whether Washington could legally impose fees on vessels using one of the world’s most important international waterways.

The International Maritime Organization said it was seeking further details on Trump’s announcement and reiterated its long-standing opposition to mandatory charges for passage through international straits.

“IMO stands firmly against charging fees for passage through straits used for international navigation,” a spokesperson for the UN shipping agency said.

“There is no legal basis through which to introduce mandatory tolls simply to transit through a strait.”

International maritime law experts said, however, that Trump’s proposal could instead be interpreted as an optional charge for naval protection services rather than a fee for transit itself.

“This is a fee for services rendered (protection), not a fee for transit,” said James Kraska, chair of international maritime law at the US Naval War College and a visiting professor at Harvard Law School.

“The regime of transit passage would remain unaffected,” he said, adding that vessels would not be required to pay for passage through the strait but could choose to pay for American naval protection while transiting the waterway.

Kraska compared the proposal to anti-piracy arrangements used in waters off Somalia, where commercial vessels have at times hired private security companies or government protection detachments to safeguard voyages through high-risk areas.

Elisabeth Braw, a senior fellow at the Atlantic Council, said shipping companies would likely be reluctant to accept any arrangement that could be interpreted as a charge for access to international waters.

“If a country, in this case the United States, starts charging tolls for access to a strait, then other countries can say, ‘Well, in our strait, we will start charging tolls too,’” she said, warning that the move could undermine the principle of freedom of navigation that has governed global shipping for decades.

Braw argued that comparisons with anti-piracy measures off Somalia were misplaced because shipping companies pay private security providers or armed guards rather than governments for access to international waters.

The latest escalation followed an Iranian attack on a container vessel in the Strait of Hormuz on Sunday, an incident that heightened concerns over shipping security and the stability of global energy markets.

US Central Command (Centcom) said on Monday that American forces had struck a submarine and ship maintenance facility at Iran’s Bandar Abbas naval base using unmanned surface vessels, describing it as the first combat use of sea drones by US forces.

Centcom said the attack was intended to degrade Iran’s ability to continue targeting commercial shipping in the region.

Before the conflict began, about one-fifth of global oil and gas supplies passed through the narrow waterway linking the Gulf to international markets.

Iran has exercised effective control over the strait since the outbreak of hostilities, turning it into one of Tehran’s most important sources of leverage in its confrontation with Washington.

The prospect of new costs for transiting Hormuz could carry particular significance for Asian economies, including China, Japan and South Korea, which rely heavily on energy imports shipped through the waterway.
