item.title: [SCMP] Is Japan a ‘spy paradise’ fuelling Russia’s war machine?
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1052

AI Summary:

- **Espionage Activity**: Russian agents allegedly operate freely in Japan due to the absence of a specific anti-espionage law, using cover roles (e.g., at Aeroflot) to procure critical technology for Russia's war in Ukraine. - **Key Components Acquired**: The operation targets microchips, transmitters, and machinery essential for advanced weapons systems. - **Evidence in Conflict**: Ukrainian officials report that electronic components from Japanese firms are found in approximately 90% of Russian missiles and drones used in Ukraine. - **International Pressure**: The exposure—possibly via a foreign intelligence tip-off—may reflect external frustration, urging Japan to take stronger action against the illicit tech transfers.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Concern that foreign spies can operate with impunity in Japan has deepened following a media report that dozens of Russian agents ordered to leave Western European countries have entered Tokyo and are buying components for Moscow’s war on Ukraine.

The New York Times reported on Sunday that Moscow’s agents were taking advantage of Tokyo’s failure to enact a law specifically designed to combat espionage and the large number of companies manufacturing components critical to the Kremlin’s war machine.

The coveted parts include microchips, transmitters and the cutting-edge machinery required to make advanced weapons systems.

The report identified Maksim Vladimirovich Filchenkov, ostensibly an employee of Russian airline Aeroflot, as leading the operation in Japan.

James Brown, a professor of international relations specialising in Russian affairs at Temple University’s Tokyo campus, told This Week in Asia that what stood out in the report was how the alleged spy was unmasked, suggesting it could have been a tip-off from a foreign intelligence agency.

“There have been no direct flights to Russia for the last four years, so what was he doing here?” said Brown, author of the 2025 book Cracking the Crab: Russian espionage against Japan, from Peter the Great to Richard Sorge.

“I wonder if a foreign agency just got frustrated that there is so much espionage activity going on in Japan that is contributing to dual-use goods being funnelled to Russia and that Japan is not doing enough to stop it.”

He said the information sharing could have been a calculated move that “forces the Japanese authorities to take some action”, and warned that evidence of the technology Moscow was accessing was damning.

Vladyslav Vlasiuk, the Ukrainian official overseeing sanctions policy, told Kyodo News in late June that electronic components made by Japanese firms had been recovered from around 90 per cent of the Russian cruise and ballistic missiles fired at Ukraine, as well as from drones.

According to Yakov Zinberg, a professor of international relations at Tokyo’s Kokushikan University, the revelation that Russian agents have been able to enter Japan claiming to be diplomats, businessmen or working for Russian companies, such as Aeroflot, but then set about obtaining sensitive technology without intervention from Japanese authorities, will be “embarrassing” to Tokyo.

It comes just six months after Japanese police submitted documents to prosecutors identifying a former official of Russia’s trade representation in Japan as having violated unfair competition laws. The man, who was not named, allegedly paid a Japanese employee of a machine tool manufacturer for information on its products.

Japanese authorities only belatedly learned the Japanese employee had first met the Russian in 2023 and were unable to detain Moscow’s agent because he had left the country 10 months earlier.

The Times article claimed Japan had become “a crucial part of the Russian war effort”.

Quoting officials from five Western intelligence agencies, the report said the operation was being spearheaded by Filchenkov, who was in fact Moscow’s most senior 20th Directorate official in Japan. The agency is controlled by the GRU, or the primary intelligence directorate of the Russian armed forces.

Describing Japan as a “spy paradise”, the report claimed Filchenkov arrived in Tokyo in February 2024 and quickly set up links with logistics firms to help ship goods to Russia, either by using falsified shipping records or via third countries, frequently Vietnam.

Proco Air was identified as one of the firms carrying out shipments, although owner Takehiko Miki has denied being aware that Filchenkov was linked to Russian intelligence or that he sent prohibited items to Russia.

While US journalists had traced Miki and evidence of his company’s role in shipments to Russia, he said Japanese authorities had never contacted him.

Japan’s foreign ministry has not commented on the case.

Brown said Japan’s delay in acting against agents from Russia and other rival nations had historical roots, but the current administration was trying to rectify the issue.

“Japan was a police state up until 1945, and after that they wanted to have a democratic system so the security services could not have too much power again,” he said.

That “overcorrection” left Japan open to significant threats because current laws did not explicitly state that engaging in espionage was illegal, Brown added.

That is changing, however, with Prime Minister’s Sanae Takaichi’s government passing a bill in May to create a national intelligence bureau that will meet for the first time this month. The intention is to follow that with legislation outlawing spying and creating an external intelligence agency.

“There is going to be lots of pushback from the left to these initiatives and claims that Japan is ‘going back to the dark days of the 1930s’, so in that sense having this spy story break right now is quite helpful to the government,” Brown said.

Zinberg, who is originally from St Petersburg, agreed that Russian agents were “at ease here in Japan because there is still no legislation against espionage”.

“It is much easier and safer for them to operate here than in Europe and they have access to lots of companies making the parts they need for their missiles and drones,” he said.

According to Zinberg, the operation is likely linked closely to Moscow’s large embassy in Tokyo and may also involve individuals in various public and private positions in Japan, including those who could surreptitiously smuggle items out of the country using falsified paperwork.

Japan’s security allies had good reason to be alarmed at this latest revelation, he warned.

“Europe is going to be very worried indeed and something like this is going to leave a lasting impression,” he said.

“I think this is going to significantly affect Japan’s security relationships moving forward.”

Observers point out that partners are likely to become increasingly wary about secrets leaking from Japanese companies engaged in joint development of advanced weapons, such as the Global Combat Air Programme with Britain and Italy.

Ukraine could also use the unmasking of Moscow’s agents in Japan to its own advantage, Zinberg said.

“I see Ukraine making very good use of it,” he said. “They are going to propagate this news – as they have done previously – to encourage Japan and its companies to take better measures to stop the transfer of these items.”

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

港股波动：资本流动表象下的结构性矛盾

现象描述： 港股科技与半导体股逆势上涨，但受中东地缘政治及通胀担忧拖累，恒生指数收跌，市场呈现资金博弈与区域风险交织的复杂态势。

政治经济学定位： 此次波动是全球金融资本在特定时空背景下进行流动、重新配置与风险对冲的集中体现。它涉及资本运动的多重维度：一是不同区域资本市场间的套利资本流动（如空头回补、南向资金）；二是虚拟资本（股票）价格脱离实体基本面的剧烈波动；三是产业资本在科技、半导体等战略领域的估值重估。

深层结构分析： 现象背后是金融全球化背景下资本积累的内在矛盾。一方面，实体经济（如中国生产商价格指数上涨）与金融市场（如股市短期涨跌）出现背离。另一方面，资本为寻求增殖，在全球不同区域市场间快速转移。正如马克思在《资本论》中所指出的，“较多的工人在同一时间、同一空间，为了生产同种商品，在同一资本家的指挥下工作，这在历史上和逻辑上都是资本主义生产的起点。” 当前全球产业链的深度整合（如半导体）与金融市场（如港股作为国际金融中心）的紧密勾连，使得“同一资本”的指挥棒已延伸至全球范围，生产与流通、实体经济与虚拟经济的矛盾在更宏大的尺度上展开。

阶级/利益分析： 此轮波动中，国际与内地金融资本是核心参与者与潜在受益者。通过空头回补操作，全球机构投资者管理了其跨区域投资组合的风险。内地资金南向流入及政策支持（如南向交易扩容），则强化了内地资本对香港金融市场乃至全球资产配置的影响力。相反，依赖金融市场稳定进行长期融资的实体产业资本（特别是出口导向型制造业）面临更高不确定性。普通投资者，尤其是缺乏信息与资金优势的散户，其资产更容易在市场剧烈波动中受损。利润（剩余价值）在金融部门与生产部门间的分配可能进一步失衡，金融资本的支配地位得到巩固。

主流叙事批判： 主流财经话语倾向于将此次波动归因于“地缘政治风险”、“技术性反弹”、“资金流动”等中性化表述。例如，分析师称“近期抛售过度”引发反弹，或强调“空头回补”与“南向资金”的支撑作用。这种叙事将复杂的资本积累过程与阶级利益博弈，简化为市场参与者的情绪与技术性操作，掩盖了资本市场波动对实体经济的剥蚀作用，以及金融资本在全球范围内攫取剩余价值并转移风险的本质。马克思在《资本论》中深刻指出，“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 港股的涨跌，本质上是虚拟资本的重新定价游戏，其影响却实实在在地通过融资成本、财富效应等渠道，传导至现实的生产与雇佣关系之中。

解放前景： 要打破“金融繁荣”与“实体经济承压”的循环，关键在于遏制金融资本的过度膨胀与投机冲动，将经济重心锚定于生产性积累。这要求：第一，强化对虚拟资本运动的监管，防止其过度脱离实体创造价值的能力。第二，提升实体经济的劳动生产力与技术自主性，正如工场手工业与机器大工业曾创造新的社会生产力，当前对半导体等“卡脖子”领域的突破，应服务于更平等、更可持续的生产关系构建，而非仅仅成为金融炒作的题材。第三，改革国际金融体系与分配机制，削弱金融霸权，使资本流动更多服务于各国实体经济的协调发展，而非少数金融集团的套利。真正的出路在于重新确立产业资本与生产性劳动的主导地位，使虚拟资本服务于而非主宰于现实财富的创造与公平分配。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] How Hong Kong is fighting to redefine its future with an institutional moat, wealth surge
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 2164

AI Summary:

* Prominent economist Stephen Roach argues Hong Kong faces decline due to lost autonomy, mainland China's economic struggles, and geopolitical tensions, countering the city's "Hong Kong is back" narrative. * Hong Kong's leadership contends the city is thriving, pointing to evidence such as leading global IPO fundraising and high rankings as a financial center. * The city's traditional competitive advantage as the exclusive gateway to China has eroded as mainland infrastructure develops and domestic rivals like Shanghai in shipping/finance and neighboring Shenzhen in GDP have surpassed it. * To maintain its global status, Hong Kong is being urged to adapt by relying more explicitly on its institutional strengths as its historical intermediary role diminishes.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
For decades, warnings of Hong Kong’s terminal decline have arrived in periodic waves.

When Stephen Roach, the former chairman of Morgan Stanley Asia, ignited a firestorm with his February 2024 assertion that “Hong Kong is over”, he pointed to a toxic cocktail of domestic and external pressures.

Roach, who doubled down on his “wake-up call” a year ago, argued that the city’s economic glory was being extinguished by a loss of political autonomy following 2020’s national security law, the spillover from mainland China’s protracted malaise, and the city’s precarious position in the crossfire of Sino-US tensions.

Despite the prominent American economist’s perceived gloom, the “Hong Kong is back” camp – led by a defiant local government – is wielding evidence to the contrary. The city claimed the IPO fundraising crown last year and ranked behind only New York and London in the latest Z/Yen Global Financial Centres Index.

Behind the heated debate lies a fundamental question: in an era defined by shifting capital flows, geopolitical fragmentation and evolving regulatory frameworks, how can Hong Kong keep shining as a globally leading financial centre?

“Hong Kong must adapt to a changing world order,” said Anthony Cheung, chair professor in public administration at the Education University of Hong Kong. As the city’s traditional intermediary role between China and global markets erodes, he argued, it must rely more explicitly on its institutional strengths to recalibrate its competitive position.

A shrinking moat in the wake of China’s rise

Historically, Hong Kong’s competitive “moat” – the structural barriers protecting its status – was built on its role as the exclusive gateway for global capital entering a mainland China that was only beginning to open its arms to investment.

In the 1980s, the height of this moat was measured by the city’s relative economic might. Its gross domestic product was equivalent to around 20 per cent of mainland China’s total output.

Today, that landscape has transformed following the outpaced economic take-off of the mainland – now the world’s second-largest economy and widely seen as being on a path to eventually surpassing the United States.

As the mainland has been developing its own infrastructure, Hong Kong’s traditional pillars have come under pressure. Its dominance in regional shipping has long since given way to Shanghai, which is now looking for a bigger slice of the offshore-finance pie. Meanwhile, Hong Kong’s property sector has experienced significant volatility, particularly since the pandemic, but some analysts suspect it “has become the first major city in China whose property market has bottomed”.

And in terms of GDP, Hong Kong was surpassed in 2018 by neighbouring Shenzhen – the southern metropolis home to telecoms giant Huawei Technologies, internet services firm Tencent, drone giant DJI and electric vehicle leader BYD.

Its important competitors might share one or a few advantages, but it is hard to collect all of them like Hong Kong

But despite these challenges, Hong Kong retains a rare collection of institutional advantages that remain difficult for mainland rivals to replicate. Le Xia, chief economist for Asia at BBVA Research, identified the city’s enduring moat as a combination that includes its British-inherited common law system, first-class financial infrastructure and appealing geographic location.

“Its important competitors might share one or a few advantages, but it is hard to collect all of them like Hong Kong,” he said, also noting the city’s deep talent pool and long-held reputation as a global financial centre.

Hong Hao, chief investment officer at Hong Kong-based Lotus Asset Management, put it simply: “There is no place like Hong Kong in China.”

He noted that Hong Kong remains an international capital market with free capital flows, making it the only truly global financial centre within China’s sovereign framework.

“Mainland China relies on Hong Kong more than ever as a bridge between its economy and the global market,” Hong said.

From ‘superconnector’ to ‘super value-adder’

From the launch of the “Brand Hong Kong” platform a quarter-century ago to promote the city to a global audience, to the ambitious “Hello Hong Kong” and “Hong Kong is back” campaigns of the post-pandemic era, authorities have deployed a multipronged strategy to revive the city’s status – with manoeuvres ranging from hosting high-stakes investment summits to implementing sweeping tax overhauls.

The 2025 policy address by Hong Kong Chief Executive John Lee in September pinned hopes on leveraging China’s national development strategies – including five-year development plans, the Greater Bay Area blueprint and the Belt and Road Initiative, along with a push to internationalise the yuan. Lee also unveiled the government’s GoGlobal Task Force aimed at encouraging firms across the border to use Hong Kong as a platform for expanding overseas.

Just last month, he announced plans to create Hong Kong’s first five-year development blueprint, calling the plan’s formulation a “historic step”. And on the official News.gov.hk platform, he discussed how the city government was helping both local and mainland businesses expand their global reach.

“When we talk about being a ‘superconnector’,” Lee said, “we can, of course, introduce and connect. But it is more than connect – we do value-adding.”

This strategy, aiming to not only link China and global markets, but also generate higher value across capital flows, has included outreach to the Middle East and Central Asia to woo businesses back to Hong Kong.

This ambition was on full display at the Hong Kong FIC and Bond Connect Summit on Tuesday, as officials announced the trial operation of a gold clearing and settlement system. The move, supported by expanded physical vaults, marks a “milestone” in the city’s bid to evolve into an international hub for trading precious metals, and into a future global price-setter.

Efforts have already yielded high-profile wins, including last year when Hong Kong claimed the crown as the world’s top IPO-fundraising venue, raising US$37.4 billion across 119 listings, including two of the top five globally.

And while the Hong Kong stock exchange surrendered that crown during the first half of this year to Nasdaq following SpaceX’s blockbuster listing last month, Hong Kong saw its IPO proceeds register a year-on-year rise of about 84 per cent from January to June – consolidating the region’s position as a primary offshore financial hub for Chinese issuers.

Meanwhile, assets and wealth managed in the city rose 20 per cent, year on year, to a record HK$42.2 trillion (US$5.38 trillion) in 2025, underscoring the city’s edge over Switzerland as a wealth hub, according to the Securities and Futures Commission. The city also issued two stablecoin licences in April, drawing international attention to its ambition of becoming a global crypto hub.

The battle for talent and capital

Hong Kong has pivoted towards more aggressive fiscal incentives to bolster its competitiveness against regional rivals. This includes long-time “frenemy” city Singapore, which competes with Hong Kong across several ranking lists and has lured some high-profile financial industry executives and Chinese billionaires in recent years.

In June, Hong Kong authorities proposed tax incentives targeting fund managers, including preferential treatment for performance-based bonuses and carried interest, as part of a broader effort to attract global asset-management talent.

Under the proposed framework, qualifying private equity funds approved by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority could see eligible carried interest taxed at 0 per cent – a stark contrast to the 24 per cent in Singapore and significantly higher rates in the US and Britain.

This follows the 2023 introduction of a preferential tax regime for family-owned investment holding vehicles, offering qualifying family offices a 0 per cent profits tax rate on eligible income to lower entry barriers for global wealth.

‘Beijing’s discretion’ and the fragile moat

While Beijing’s support is vital for the city, some have expressed concerns about it being a double-edged sword.

Christopher Marquis, a Chinese-management professor at Cambridge Judge Business School, argued that some of Hong Kong’s core strengths – such as currency convertibility – are not fully autonomous. “The privileges, of course, exist at Beijing’s discretion,” he said.

The city’s dominance is currently anchored in having the “world’s deepest renminbi liquidity pool outside the Chinese mainland”, with deposits of around 1 trillion yuan (US$147.4 billion), according to the Hong Kong Monetary Authority. The hub also handles more than 70 per cent of global offshore yuan payments and settlements.

If your moat is not fully under your control, can it still really be considered yours

Christopher Marquis, Cambridge Judge Business School

Although Beijing has reaffirmed its pledge to support Hong Kong’s role, analysts warn that these advantages could be under pressure. The city’s long-term pricing power in the offshore yuan market could be diluted as mainland China expands its offshore financial functions in Shanghai as part of a broader push to internationalise the currency.

Marquis argued that this made it all the more important for Hong Kong to strengthen the institutional foundations that underpin investor confidence, rather than relying solely on inherited structural advantages.

“If your moat is not fully under your control, can it still really be considered yours,” he asked.

Internationally, the rivalry between Beijing and Washington has served as a catalyst for Hong Kong’s IPO market, with analysts saying it offers an advantage over New York as a listing destination. However, appearing on the US radar is often accompanied with uncertainties.

Investor sentiment was further unsettled by comments in April 2025 from US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent that “everything is on the table”, regarding the possibility of Washington delisting Chinese companies from American stock exchanges as part of its trade negotiations, adding that it was up to US President Donald Trump to decide.

This prompted a number of firms, including Alibaba and Baidu, to reassess cross-border listing strategies. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

Jacky Tang, chief investment officer for emerging markets at Deutsche Bank in Hong Kong, said the proximity to mainland China’s corporate ecosystem and investor base had become increasingly valuable, with global capital markets becoming more fragmented and geopolitical considerations playing a larger role in listing decisions.

“Hong Kong is shifting from ‘backup venue’ to ‘default international venue’ for a growing group of Chinese issuers,” Tang said.

However, the persistent reliance on mainland issuers has raised questions over whether Hong Kong can preserve its status as a truly global financial centre. Market participants suggest that this concentration is far from sufficient, and that long-term resilience requires a more diversified issuer and investor base.

“The dominance of mainland Chinese companies in Hong Kong’s IPO market is likely to continue in the medium term,” said Tommy Ong, managing director of T.O. & Associates Consultancy, describing it as “a natural result” of Beijing’s 2024 capital market guidelines.

Mainland Chinese companies accounted for more than 90 per cent of both total funds raised and deal volume in the first half of 2026, according to a Deloitte research report published in June.

Ong recommended that Hong Kong diversify both the geographical mix of its issuers and the range of products available in its capital markets, attracting more companies from Asia, particularly the Asean bloc.

Beyond equities, Hong Kong should leverage its strengths in bond and foreign-exchange markets by expanding products such as tokenised bonds to broaden the investor base, while developing offshore renminbi-denominated commodity futures to meet rising demand for risk-hedging from Chinese companies, Ong added.

If policies are unpredictable and volatile, investors who commit significant capital will never feel secure

Since the implementation of the National Security Law in 2020, debates over Hong Kong’s institutional autonomy have intensified, with some investors reassessing long-term exposure to the city’s markets.

For many market participants, the central concern is predictability, with regulatory consistency increasingly seen as a core determinant of investor confidence.

Economist Simon Lee Siu-po said Hong Kong’s strength has long rested on its stable and rules-based environment, which allows global capital to be deployed with clarity.

But he warned that Beijing’s recent regulatory interventions, when viewed alongside broader geopolitical uncertainties, have made that predictability less certain.

“International investors, fearing their capital could be trapped or affected by sudden policy interventions, are losing the confidence required to hold Hong Kong assets over the long term,” Lee Siu-po said.

More recently, tighter oversight of cross-border capital flows and enforcement actions targeting certain forms of offshore trading have reinforced a more cautious stance among parts of the global investment community.

The economist argued that maintaining Hong Kong’s status would depend on reinforcing a transparent and stable regulatory framework that reduces uncertainty for long-term investors, rather than relying on short-term market sentiment.

“If policies are unpredictable and volatile, investors who commit significant capital will never feel secure,” he said.

Additional reporting by Zoe Chan

item.title: [SCMP] Meet CXMT’s Zhu Yiming: the engineer building China’s answer to global memory-chip giants
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1042

AI Summary:

* **Major IPO:** ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT) has launched a Shanghai IPO aiming to raise 29.5 billion yuan ($4.4 billion), with analysts predicting a potential market value of 3 trillion yuan. * **Founder's Vision:** Founder Zhu Yiming predicted as early as 2004 that the global memory chip industry would shift to China, aiming to become the country's top designer and maker. * **Financial Milestone:** Zhu reportedly pledged in 2018 not to draw a salary until CXMT became profitable, which the company achieved in the year prior to the IPO. * **Early Career:** Before CXMT, Zhu co-founded GigaDevice Semiconductor in a Silicon Valley garage in 2004 after working as an engineer in the U.S. memory-chip industry.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
When ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT) – China’s attempt to build a home-grown rival to the world’s top makers of memory chips – kicked off one of the country’s most anticipated initial public offerings (IPOs) of 2026 last week, the spotlight fell on its founder Zhu Yiming.

The Shanghai offering, set to start taking subscriptions on Thursday, aims to raise 29.5 billion yuan (US$4.4 billion). And some analysts predict that CXMT could eventually command a market value of 3 trillion yuan, placing it among China’s most valuable listed technology companies.

The listing will be a milestone for Zhu, who reportedly pledged in 2018 not to draw a salary from CXMT until the memory-chip project became profitable – which it did last year.

Yet Zhu’s wager on Chinese memory chips began long before CXMT. More than two decades ago, when he was a Silicon Valley engineer with a chip design but little money, Zhu predicted that the centre of the global memory industry would eventually shift towards mainland China.

In emails retained by Li Jun, one of his earliest investors, and later reproduced in a Tsinghua University alumni publication, Zhu observed that the industry had migrated from the US to Japan, and then to South Korea.

“It is time for China to play [a] role in this industry,” he wrote.

His long-term ambition was even bolder: “to be the biggest memory designer and maker in China”.

Zhu’s interest in technology had already paid off. While studying physics at Tsinghua, he took programming jobs rather than the tutoring work common among students, making 300,000 yuan a year by the late 1990s when the average annual income was about 10,000 yuan, according to Tsinghua’s alumni magazine. As the software he wrote moved closer to the underlying hardware, he became fascinated by the integrated circuits that China largely relied on foreign companies to supply.

He later studied electronic engineering at Stony Brook University in New York and worked for technology companies in the United States, including memory-chip developer Monolithic System Technologies.

By the summer of 2004, Zhu had decided to start his own company.

At a Starbucks in Silicon Valley, he made a second pitch to Li, a fellow Tsinghua alumnus and technology investor. Their first meeting had produced little interest. This time, Zhu had quit his job, registered GigaDevice Semiconductor and prepared a design for a memory chip that promised lower costs and power consumption.

The pitch persuaded Li to introduce Zhu to another investor, who provided US$100,000 and rented his garage to the fledgling company for cheap. Zhu and fellow engineer Shu Qingming turned their design into a working sample there and applied for a patent.

While the garage origins followed a familiar Silicon Valley script, what came next was more characteristically Zhu: move quickly, even when the money was not quite enough.

In 2005, a group of investors, many of them Tsinghua alumni, assembled US$920,000 for GigaDevice’s first major fundraising round. The target had been US$1 million, but Zhu decided it was close enough and returned to Beijing to start the company.

The firm’s first technology struggled to win over large electronics makers, which were reluctant to rely on an unknown Chinese supplier. GigaDevice changed course and secured its first domestic order from chip designer Rockchip. The contract, worth only 100,000 yuan, put its memory technology into chips for MP3 players.

GigaDevice eventually grew into one of China’s leading chip-design companies and listed in Shanghai in August 2016.

For many founders, that would have been the capstone of a successful entrepreneurial career. For Zhu, it coincided with the start of a more ambitious second act.

That year, he joined forces with the Hefei government to launch the project that became CXMT. While GigaDevice could design chips and outsource their production, CXMT would have to build multibillion-dollar factories, master manufacturing processes and compete with companies that had decades of expertise.

The most important thing in entrepreneurship is to stay alive

In 2018, Zhu stepped back from GigaDevice to focus on CXMT – and made his no-salary pledge.

CXMT began producing its first self-designed mainstream memory chip in 2019, giving mainland China a foothold in a market that was almost entirely supplied by foreign manufacturers.

One of Zhu’s important early moves was to secure licences to patents originally developed by Qimonda, a failed German memory maker spun out of Infineon Technologies. This gave CXMT an established intellectual-property base and reduced the risk of disputes with overseas rivals.

CXMT nevertheless remained loss-making for years as it built factories, recruited engineers and strived to improve its production yield.

Its fortunes changed dramatically as artificial intelligence data centres drove demand for memory to record levels. The boom also attracted Apple, which has reportedly tested CXMT chips for use in devices sold in China.

The IPO has also cast an earlier investment by Alibaba Group Holding in a new light. Its Hong Kong-listed shares jumped as much as 17.1 per cent last week amid a broader rally in Chinese AI stocks, just as its exposure to CXMT drew renewed attention. Subsidiaries of Alibaba own just under 5 per cent of the chipmaker after investing about 7.6 billion yuan since 2021, making Alibaba its largest industry investor.

Despite CXMT’s success, Zhu has not yet won his original wager. South Korea’s Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix, along with US firm Micron Technology, still control more than 90 per cent of the global DRAM market and remain ahead in scale and advanced products. CXMT must continue spending heavily on manufacturing technology while navigating cyclical prices and tightening restrictions on China’s access to semiconductor equipment.

The engineer who once handled technology, fundraising and early customer development now chairs a manufacturer that employed nearly 19,300 people at the end of 2025.

In an interview published by Tsinghua’s alumni magazine after GigaDevice’s listing, Zhu attributed the company’s success to choosing the right market and the hard work of his employees. He also expressed a dogged determinism that is likely to serve him well as he continues working on his prediction about the industry’s migration to China.

“The most important thing in entrepreneurship is to stay alive,” he said.

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

港股波动：资本流动表象下的结构性矛盾

现象描述： 港股科技与半导体股逆势上涨，但受中东地缘政治及通胀担忧拖累，恒生指数收跌，市场呈现资金博弈与区域风险交织的复杂态势。

政治经济学定位： 此次波动是全球金融资本在特定时空背景下进行流动、重新配置与风险对冲的集中体现。它涉及资本运动的多重维度：一是不同区域资本市场间的套利资本流动（如空头回补、南向资金）；二是虚拟资本（股票）价格脱离实体基本面的剧烈波动；三是产业资本在科技、半导体等战略领域的估值重估。

深层结构分析： 现象背后是金融全球化背景下资本积累的内在矛盾。一方面，实体经济（如中国生产商价格指数上涨）与金融市场（如股市短期涨跌）出现背离。另一方面，资本为寻求增殖，在全球不同区域市场间快速转移。正如马克思在《资本论》中所指出的，“较多的工人在同一时间、同一空间，为了生产同种商品，在同一资本家的指挥下工作，这在历史上和逻辑上都是资本主义生产的起点。” 当前全球产业链的深度整合（如半导体）与金融市场（如港股作为国际金融中心）的紧密勾连，使得“同一资本”的指挥棒已延伸至全球范围，生产与流通、实体经济与虚拟经济的矛盾在更宏大的尺度上展开。

阶级/利益分析： 此轮波动中，国际与内地金融资本是核心参与者与潜在受益者。通过空头回补操作，全球机构投资者管理了其跨区域投资组合的风险。内地资金南向流入及政策支持（如南向交易扩容），则强化了内地资本对香港金融市场乃至全球资产配置的影响力。相反，依赖金融市场稳定进行长期融资的实体产业资本（特别是出口导向型制造业）面临更高不确定性。普通投资者，尤其是缺乏信息与资金优势的散户，其资产更容易在市场剧烈波动中受损。利润（剩余价值）在金融部门与生产部门间的分配可能进一步失衡，金融资本的支配地位得到巩固。

主流叙事批判： 主流财经话语倾向于将此次波动归因于“地缘政治风险”、“技术性反弹”、“资金流动”等中性化表述。例如，分析师称“近期抛售过度”引发反弹，或强调“空头回补”与“南向资金”的支撑作用。这种叙事将复杂的资本积累过程与阶级利益博弈，简化为市场参与者的情绪与技术性操作，掩盖了资本市场波动对实体经济的剥蚀作用，以及金融资本在全球范围内攫取剩余价值并转移风险的本质。马克思在《资本论》中深刻指出，“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 港股的涨跌，本质上是虚拟资本的重新定价游戏，其影响却实实在在地通过融资成本、财富效应等渠道，传导至现实的生产与雇佣关系之中。

解放前景： 要打破“金融繁荣”与“实体经济承压”的循环，关键在于遏制金融资本的过度膨胀与投机冲动，将经济重心锚定于生产性积累。这要求：第一，强化对虚拟资本运动的监管，防止其过度脱离实体创造价值的能力。第二，提升实体经济的劳动生产力与技术自主性，正如工场手工业与机器大工业曾创造新的社会生产力，当前对半导体等“卡脖子”领域的突破，应服务于更平等、更可持续的生产关系构建，而非仅仅成为金融炒作的题材。第三，改革国际金融体系与分配机制，削弱金融霸权，使资本流动更多服务于各国实体经济的协调发展，而非少数金融集团的套利。真正的出路在于重新确立产业资本与生产性劳动的主导地位，使虚拟资本服务于而非主宰于现实财富的创造与公平分配。

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Hong Kong should build AI plan around ‘competitive’ strengths, industry leaders say
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1102

AI Summary:

• Industry leaders recommend Hong Kong focus its first five-year AI plan on specific competitive strengths like finance, healthcare, and urban development rather than broad technology coverage. • The city is building computing infrastructure, with most recent capacity already in high demand; institutions are urged to secure resources quickly. • Hong Kong is attracting AI talent from mainland China and overseas through targeted policies and schemes. • Proposals include establishing pilot programs for cross-border data transfers to build high-level industry datasets in collaboration with mainland partners. • The city aims to leverage its universities to bridge the gap between research/commercialization and support a transition to "agentic" AI applications.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Hong Kong should draft a five-year plan that includes an artificial intelligence (AI) focus that is not too broad, makes the most of its core strengths and attracts the best talent to develop the sector, industry leaders have said.

At the South China Morning Post’s China Conference 2026 on Tuesday, titled “Intelligence at Scale: Hong Kong’s AI-Powered Future”, speakers at two panel discussions considered how Hong Kong could deliver on its AI aspirations and the city’s readiness as a launch pad for mainland Chinese enterprises.

Zhang Xiaoyan, vice-president of the China Centre for Information Industry Development, an institution under China’s Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, said that as Hong Kong formulated its first five-year plan, it should refrain from drafting technology plans that were too broad.

“Hong Kong, because it’s relatively smaller in terms of economy, so your first five-year plan should not be a very broad one. Just like the mainland, especially in the AI sector, you do not need to cover all the frontier technologies,” Zhang said.

“You can pick the [strongest or] most competitive scenarios in Hong Kong, such as urban development, healthcare or finance, instead of manufacturing, because manufacturing in Hong Kong accounts for only 1 per cent of your general gross domestic product [GDP]. So let us learn from the mainland, but also keep [in line] with Hong Kong's features and advantages in AI development.”

Zhang added that the city should establish pilot programmes for cross-border data transfers to complement the mainland by building high-level industry data sets in collaboration with mainland partners.

Nicholas Chan Hiu-fung, a lawmaker and member of the Cyberport board of directors, said the city was increasing its computing infrastructure amid an evolving “digital workforce” that deployed agentic AI to perform actual tasks, as opposed to merely relying on generative AI to answer questions.

“From what I understand, most of the computing power that we’ve just recently built up has been snapped up by a lot of people demanding to use them, [but] we still have some left,” Chan said, urging companies and education institutions to “grab” computing power “as soon as you can”.

Another speaker, Hendrick Sin, a deputy to the National People’s Congress and president of the Hong Kong Internet Professional Association, noted that on top of China’s 15th five-year plan, Hong Kong was formulating its own five-year blueprint to ensure it pursued the best policies and attracted the right people.

“We are making sure we are not lagging behind and we see that with the right schemes and the right policies in place, we have attracted lots of talent. So we see a lot of overseas and mainland scientists and entrepreneurs are coming to Hong Kong to take part in the AI development,” he said.

Asked how Hong Kong could resolve the missing link between clinical trials and commercialising inventions, Sin pointed to Hong Kong’s five universities that topped global rankings and their contributions to research.

“The basic research is strong, we have very good professors [and] scientists, we have numerous research papers, I think they are cited many, many times globally,” he said.

Sin added that there were centres that helped or worked with these scientists, such as the government’s innovation initiative InnoHK, that collaborated with multinational universities to develop technologies and products.

He noted that the city offered robust infrastructure and professional services, with intellectual property protections that were globally recognised, biotechnology investors, and mechanisms that fast-tracked company listings on the local stock exchange.

When asked to share her experience of commercialising inventions such as AI software or robots, Jeanne Lim, founder and CEO of BeingAI, said that developers could achieve international compliance by aligning their platforms with mainland regulations on human-AI interaction.

“Obviously, [mainland] China is next to Hong Kong, it’s a big market, and it values human engagement, and interestingly, it has the first policy about human-AI interaction in the world,” she noted.

“So, if we have a robust way to meet those requirements, we’re actually OK around the world, which is very different from what people expect, right?”

At a separate panel about Hong Kong’s role as a launch pad for mainland firms, Bank of Communications’ general manager of corporate business management Marcus Wong Kin-ting noted a strategic shift in recent years, saying businesses had pivoted away from seeking US dollar-dominated financing towards multicurrency options to fund their diverse overseas ventures.

Raymond Mak Ka-chun, co-founder and CEO of Farmacy Group, a Hong Kong-based tech company developing mobile farm systems, said he saw increasing demand from the mainland and other parts of Asia for local partnerships and joint ventures.

He said the “Hong Kong brand” was recognised internationally and recalled how a Thai supermarket chain chose his firm over established US and German competitors in an early project.

“[The CEO told me] that when doing business, it’s not only about technological excellence, it’s also the trust built over years. They had a good experience working with Hong Kong partners …. We showed our strengths in understanding their Thai culture,” he said.

Hua Medicine’s executive director and CEO, Li Chen, said his Shanghai-based pharmaceutical company had benefited from Hong Kong’s “1+” drug approval marketing mechanism, which endorsed the firm’s anti-diabetic medication earlier this year.

The milestone had accelerated the company’s overseas expansion plans, he said, adding that the growth potential of Hong Kong’s biotech sector would increase as the city came closer to establishing its own equivalent of the US’ Food and Drug Administration.

“We will be able to leverage resources in the Greater Bay Area, from [pooling] patients and contracting research organisations to drug discovery development and manufacturing. The ecosystem already exists in the area.”

Professor Lo Yuk-lam, president of the Hong Kong Bio-Med Innotech Association, said that the city’s new drug regulator, known as the Centre for Medical Products Regulation (CMPR), would be key to pushing the city’s biotech development.

“Without the CMPR, I don’t think Hong Kong has too much future for bioeconomy,” said Lo, who is hailed as the “father of Hong Kong biotechnology”.

“If Hong Kong wants to have some new economy, bioeconomy is one of the major areas,” he stressed.

Gordon Zhou, chief healthcare solutions and strategy architect at Huawei’s Healthcare Business Unit, said there should be clear mutual recognition of health data systems between Hong Kong and the mainland to help build a successful drug regulator.

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:
