item.title: [SCMP] Hong Kong graduates at crossroads as AI takes over entry-level jobs, experts warn
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1095

AI Summary:

- AI's automation of entry-level tasks and employers' preference for immediate productivity are reducing training opportunities for Hong Kong graduates, leading some firms to hire imported workers instead. - Hong Kong's net employment outlook has dropped sharply to -9%, the second-worst among 42 countries surveyed, with health services (-47%) and technical services (-29%) being the weakest sectors. - Job vacancies for graduates on a key university platform have plummeted 51.5% since 2021, with information and communications openings falling by 76.7%. - Experts warn this trend risks cutting off the mid-level career ladder and disrupting the transfer of experience, potentially creating a persistent local talent shortage.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
AI’s rapid takeover of entry-level tasks is disrupting Hong Kong’s career ladder, leaving fresh graduates with fewer opportunities to gain practical experience, according to experts.

Lam Wai-kong, an employee representative on the Labour Advisory Board, said a growing emphasis on immediate productivity, coupled with a reluctance to invest in graduate training, had also led some employers to bypass local young people in favour of imported workers to fill technical roles.

He warned that this trend could result in a persistent shortage of mid-level local talent.

“One question is whether the ladder for upward mobility will be cut off midway,” Lam said. “The second is the concern about [the breakdown in] the transmission of experience.”

Lam, who is also a lawmaker for the labour sector, added that as the Northern Metropolis developed, incoming mainland Chinese and international technology firms might similarly sideline local graduates unless authorities imposed stricter hiring requirements tied to land allocation and tax incentives.

A third-quarter outlook survey by ManpowerGroup noted that the city’s net employment outlook – defined as the difference between the percentage of employers planning to increase staff and those planning to reduce staff – had plunged to minus 9 per cent, reflecting a sharp decrease of 20 percentage points compared with the previous quarter.

The survey added that Hong Kong’s hiring outlook was the second-worst among the 42 countries and regions surveyed.

Health and social services recorded the weakest outlook at minus 47 per cent, followed by professional scientific and technical services at minus 29 per cent, while the finance and insurance industry was positive at 33 per cent.

A report by the Legislative Council Secretariat in mid-May said that vacancies on the Joint Institution Job Information System – an online platform run by the city’s eight publicly funded universities – had plummeted by 51.5 per cent since 2021, driven by a 76.7 per cent decline in information and communications openings.

Jackie Wong, 24, spent the past year looking for a full-time job in photography or advertising, but had to settle for freelance work to make a living.

The 2025 mass media graduate said employers were reluctant to offer entry-level opportunities.

“How am I supposed to get experience if nobody will give me a chance? It is a vicious cycle,” Wong said, noting that most companies preferred to hire experienced people to save time and money on training.

While Wong believed artificial intelligence (AI) would not fully replace roles in the media industry, as many still required hands-on work, she said some jobs were already disappearing.

“Certain positions such as copywriters and transcribers are getting replaced by AI. This has reduced the number of available openings, forcing more people to compete for those remaining roles,” she said.

Lawmaker Elvin Lee Ka-kui said the structural shift was eroding entry points for young people.

“The deeper concern behind this trend is that if graduates find it harder to gain suitable first-job experience, it will eventually leave enterprises lacking mid-level talent with practical experience,” said Lee, who is also chairman of the Hong Kong Publishing Federation.

Lee added that the government should consider offering long-term wage subsidies or tax concessions to employers willing to convert standard junior roles into training positions, effectively co-funding entry-level positions.

Government data showed that private sector vacancies dropped by 12 per cent year on year to 48,610 in March. Vacancies for clerical support workers plummeted by 21 per cent to 3,910, while openings for professionals plunged by 16 per cent.

Wong Man-shun, unit-in-charge at the Hong Kong Federation of Youth Groups’ youth employment network, said that fresh graduates were squeezed into a hyper-competitive bottleneck, with entry-level postings attracting far more applicants than in previous years.

Data from the Legco Secretariat showed that first-time jobseekers accounted for 55.2 per cent of all unemployed young people in 2025, compared with 10.9 per cent of the overall unemployed population, underscoring how inexperienced graduates have borne the brunt of labour market displacement.

Wong added that as commercial firms and major local banks dismantled up to a third of their junior back-office roles, graduates had grown increasingly anxious after prolonged job searches.

“If they remain unemployed for 18 months, they can fall into what we call ‘learned helplessness’,” said Wong, who is also a social worker.

He said that graduates from social sciences disciplines – including anthropology, sociology and social policy – alongside those with business administration degrees, faced the greatest confusion because their disciplines lacked a clear vocational path or were too broad compared with structured professions such as nursing.

Roy Ying, a senior lecturer in marketing at Hang Seng University and co-chairman of advocacy and policy at the Hong Kong Institute of Human Resource Management, pointed out that the Joint University Programmes Admissions System required students as young as 17, often without any workplace exposure, to make life-shaping decisions under parental and peer pressure.

This, he said, could lead to disengagement, with students focusing on obtaining a degree rather than actively building technical skills.

Ying added employers wanted fresh graduates to perform “like 15-year veterans” with maturity, professional intuition and the ability to navigate ambiguity – traits that used to be developed across a decade of on-the-job learning.

Newly demanded skills included those that had historically appeared later in a career, such as strategic decision-making, stakeholder management, leadership and judgment, Ying added.

Ricky Chan Wai-chung, executive deputy chairman of the Federation of Hong Kong Industries, pointed out that hiring tech-savvy young people was a forward-looking cost-saving strategy, as their digital fluency eliminated corporate reliance on expensive external consultants.

Chan, who is also an employer representative on the Labour Advisory Board, said industry-academic integration should begin earlier at the secondary school level, citing private sector pipelines such as the federation’s “Innopreneur Experience Journey” and internship partnerships under City University’s “Global Research Enrichment and Technopreneurship” programme.

The Labour Department said that it had expanded its youth employment and training programme by raising the trainee age limit to 29 while offering employers monthly training subsidies of up to HK$5,000 (US$637), among other measures.

A Legco Secretariat paper noted that the policy change had resulted in a 46.4 per cent surge in applications last year.

But Lam said young graduates might have to shift to blue-collar roles as the fourth industrial revolution or Industry 4.0 – the integration of digital technologies with industries – gathered pace.

These would include jobs as drone pilots for the low-altitude economy or remote crane operators controlling heavy construction machinery from computer rooms using gaming joysticks.

Additional reporting by Kate Chan

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

金融表象与资本实质：香港股市波动的政治经济学透视

1. 现象描述
香港股市受区域科技股波动、中东地缘冲突及内地政策调整影响，呈现震荡分化格局，科技板块逆势上涨，但整体市场承压。

2. 政治经济学定位
从资本运动维度看，香港股市的波动是虚拟资本与实体资本脱钩的典型表现。股票价格（如智谱AI、中芯国际的暴涨暴跌）并非由现实生产过程中的价值创造直接决定，而是受资本化预期、流动性套利与地缘风险溢价驱动。马克思在《资本论》第三卷中指出：“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 此处科技股的剧烈波动，本质上是金融资本对剩余价值预期进行贴现与再定价的过程，而非对实体生产力的直接反映。

3. 深层结构分析
表面上，市场波动源于地缘冲突或政策调整，但深层是全球资本循环中不同利益集团的博弈。一方面，国际金融资本通过“资金空头”（funding shorts）将港股作为套利工具，为配置韩日等市场融资，这体现了金融资本全球流动中对区域市场的工具化利用；另一方面，内地政策扩容南向通及央行外储增配港股，实则通过制度性安排引导资本回流，试图稳定本土金融话语权。这种资本流动背后，是金融资产阶级内部（国际投行与本土政策资本）对资本积累主导权的争夺。

4. 阶级/利益分析
受益者：一是科技垄断资本（如AI、半导体企业），通过资本市场波动实现股权价值重估与套现；二是金融中介与投行，通过做空回补、套利交易获取手续费与价差收益；三是政策资本（如央行体系），通过制度性资本配置强化对香港金融市场的控制权。
受损者：一是中小投资者，其财富随虚拟资本波动被侵蚀；二是实体产业部门，尤其是文中提及的“生产商价格指数上涨挤压工厂利润”，反映产业资本在通胀与金融化挤压下的生存困境；三是全球无产阶级，其工资与社会保障被通胀进一步侵蚀。
权力流动体现为：金融资本通过定价权与流动性配置，实质性地影响生产资源分配，甚至绑架宏观经济政策。

5. 主流叙事批判
主流媒体（如文章引用分析师言论）将波动归因于“技术性反弹”“情绪修复”，掩盖了三个实质矛盾：其一，将金融波动简化为市场自发调节，掩盖了虚拟资本积累制度本身内嵌的危机倾向；其二，强调“政策利好”，却未触及资本分配中阶级权力的不对等——政策红利更多流向金融资产持有者而非劳动者；其三，回避地缘冲突背后的帝国主义资源争夺逻辑，将其简化为“市场情绪扰动”。马克思在《资本论》第一卷中揭示的资本主义生产关系的隐蔽性，在此处体现为金融话语对剥削关系的彻底遮蔽。

6. 解放前景
从政治经济学视角看，出路需直面三个层面：
第一，限制虚拟资本自我增殖的狂奔：如对金融交易征税、遏制高频套利，使资本重新锚定实体价值创造——正如马克思所言，工场手工业分工“发展了新的社会劳动生产力”，未来需构建以社会需求为导向、劳动者参与民主管理的生产组织形式。
第二，打破金融资本的全球霸权循环：区域资本市场应逐步摆脱对美元体系与投机性资本的依赖，通过多边结算机制与生产性投资，重塑“资本服务于生产”的关系。
第三，重建分配正义：当前“生产商价格指数上涨挤压利润”的危机，本质是剩余价值在产业资本与金融资本间分配失衡。需通过劳资集体谈判、超额利润税等工具，将积累的财富导向社会保障与公共生产。

虚拟资本的每一次剧烈波动，都是资本主义基本矛盾——生产的社会化与资本的私人占有——在金融领域的尖锐投射。唯有将金融权力纳入民主监督与社会规划，方能打破“虚拟资本暴增而现实财富匮乏”的困局，走向“每个人的自由发展是一切人的自由发展的条件”的生产关系。

（字数：758）

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Was Xi’s stance on China-North Korea military ties also a message for US, Russia?
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1212

AI Summary:

* Xi Jinping emphasized enhancing "military affairs" exchanges with Kim Jong-un, marking the first time since 1992 that a Chinese defence minister accompanied a president to North Korea. * The meeting's statements omitted any mention of denuclearisation, a notable shift from Xi's 2019 visit and a contrast with traditional diplomatic priorities. * Analysts suggest Xi's stance served as a message to the US that China has secured a military ally, while also signaling caution to North Korea against becoming excessively close with Moscow. * Despite Xi's remarks, North Korea's state media did not report the call for increased military ties, indicating Pyongyang's reluctance to burden its deepening military relationship with Russia.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
North Korea is gaining in strategic importance for China to counter the United States, but Pyongyang may refrain from intensifying military ties with Beijing, analysts say.

During his meeting with North Korean leader Kim Jong-un on Monday, Chinese President Xi Jinping said both sides should “enhance exchanges in diplomacy, law enforcement and military affairs”, according to state news agency Xinhua.

Despite pledges from both sides to strengthen strategic communications, denuclearisation of the Korean peninsula was not included in their statements on the meeting, a sharp contrast with Xi’s previous visit in 2019.

Also in a departure from their previous summits, Xi and Kim were accompanied by their defence ministers, Dong Jun and No Kwang-chol. It is the first time since 1992 that a Chinese defence minister has accompanied a Chinese president to North Korea.

It is also the first known case of a senior Chinese defence official visiting the country since Miao Hua, then-director of the Chinese Central Military Commission’s Political Work Department, visited in 2019.

While a Chinese military delegation reportedly travelled to North Korea for the first time in six years in October last year, the level of the delegation was not revealed. The prominence given to military matters this week was an addition to the traditional priorities of economic and diplomatic affairs.

However, North Korea’s state media did not report Xi’s remarks on increasing military exchanges between the two countries, despite extensive coverage of the Chinese president’s visit.

Kang Jun-young, a Chinese studies professor at Hankuk University of Foreign Studies in Seoul, said Xi’s remarks showed Washington that Beijing had “firmly secured a military ally” while at the same time signalling “caution against excessive military closeness” between Pyongyang and Moscow.

Beijing officially rejects formal, binding military alliances, but it signed the 1961 Treaty of Friendship, Cooperation and Mutual Assistance with North Korea – China’s only standing mutual defence agreement with another country.

In Kang’s view, North Korean media did not mention Xi’s comments because an open acknowledgement of improved military ties with Beijing could place a “considerable burden” on Pyongyang, considering it was making significant progress in military engagement with Moscow.

Sydney Seiler, who was the national intelligence officer for North Korea on the US National Intelligence Council from 2020 to 2023, said Beijing sought to send “subtle messages” that Pyongyang would be “well-advised” not to neglect China-North Korea relations.

While Chinese readouts of the visit suggested military exchange as part of multi-sector cooperation the two countries could pursue, this did not translate into “bilateral, or even trilateral, military exercises in the near future”, he said.

“For now, Russia-[North Korea] relations do not appear to have been dealt a significant blow: Russia is the lower-cost, less-burdensome partner for North Korea,” said Seiler, who is a senior adviser for the Korea chair at the Washington-based Centre for Strategic and International Studies.

“Kim is likely to look for low-risk, limited opportunities to expand cooperation with China but in a more reserved and cautious manner.”

Pyongyang is thought to have received Russian technological help to develop its conventional weapons in exchange for sending troops and weaponry to aid Moscow’s war in Ukraine. The two countries also signed a mutual defence treaty in June 2024, which mandates that an armed attack on either country requires the other to provide military assistance, marking the peak in North Korea-Russia defence ties.

Cho Han-bum, a senior research fellow at the Korea Institute for National Unification in Seoul, said that while closer military ties between Pyongyang and Moscow prompted China’s effort to regain and strengthen its influence over North Korea, Pyongyang might still take a cautious approach.

“Given that North Korea has always maintained equidistant diplomacy, or walked a tightrope between China and Russia, it is wary of military cooperation with China that crosses the red line,” Cho said.

The possibility of increased military exchanges between China and North Korea comes as Pyongyang continues to advance its nuclear and conventional weapons programme.

A day before last week’s announcement of Xi’s visit, North Korean state media reported that Kim had just toured a newly opened nuclear material production plant and pledged to “exponentially strengthen” his country’s nuclear force while “numerically and continuously” accelerating its expansion.

On Sunday – the day before Xi’s arrival – Kim’s sister Kim Yo-jong remarked that North Korea’s status as a nuclear power was an “absolute, indeterminate boundary”, and that “hostile forces” such as the US “must cast aside their delusions of denuclearisation”.

While China has previously supported a nuclear-free Korean peninsula, its recent actions reveal a pivot from that position.

In an op-ed published by North Korean state newspaper Rodong Sinmun before his arrival, Xi stressed opposing “hegemonism and power politics, and all ambitions and schemes that seek the revival of militarism and endanger regional safety and stability” but there was no mention of denuclearisation.

It was in contrast with Xi’s op-ed in the same publication ahead of his last visit seven years ago, which highlighted the promotion of peace and dialogue on the Korean peninsula.

China’s official policy advocates for the complete denuclearisation and stability of the Korean peninsula through phased, multilateral dialogue. However, Beijing’s policy appeared to depart from its original stance in November when it omitted support for the nuclear-free Koreas in its latest white paper “China’s Arms Control, Disarmament and Nonproliferation in the New Era”.

Lim Eul-chul, from the Institute for Far Eastern Studies at Kyungnam University, said the differences in coverage of the summit revealed North Korea’s wariness of its system “becoming subordinate to the Chinese economic model or global supply-chain systems”.

China had a stance of “de facto acquiescence” to North Korea’s status as a nuclear power through “intentional silence”, Lim said, citing Xi “wholeheartedly” supporting the “Korean-style socialist feat” of designating itself as a nuclear power in its constitution.

“This appears to be an attempt to secure a security alliance between North Korea and China to counter South Korea, the US and Japan through military cooperation in exchange for the tacit acceptance of nuclear possession.”

Patrick Cronin, the Asia-Pacific security chair at the Washington-based think tank Hudson Institute, said Xi’s broad calls for increased trade and military exchange were a “strategic signal” to Moscow and Washington.

“Beijing is reinforcing the message that China, not Russia, remains North Korea’s indispensable partner,” he said.

“At the same time, it is reminding Washington that China holds considerable leverage over Pyongyang and should therefore be given greater deference in any future diplomatic efforts to maintain stability on the peninsula.”

Kang noted that Xi’s visit to North Korea itself amplified Pyongyang’s strategic importance for China, which was also shown by the Chinese president’s repeated remarks emphasising strategic cooperation and communication.

“As a reaction to the US strengthening South Korea and Japan into alliances, China is bringing North Korea, traditionally under its influence … To do so, it has to relatively acknowledge North Korea’s status.”

However, drawing from the historical record, Kang added that North Korea would refrain from being too influenced by any single nation, whether China or Russia.

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

金融表象与资本实质：香港股市波动的政治经济学透视

1. 现象描述
香港股市受区域科技股波动、中东地缘冲突及内地政策调整影响，呈现震荡分化格局，科技板块逆势上涨，但整体市场承压。

2. 政治经济学定位
从资本运动维度看，香港股市的波动是虚拟资本与实体资本脱钩的典型表现。股票价格（如智谱AI、中芯国际的暴涨暴跌）并非由现实生产过程中的价值创造直接决定，而是受资本化预期、流动性套利与地缘风险溢价驱动。马克思在《资本论》第三卷中指出：“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 此处科技股的剧烈波动，本质上是金融资本对剩余价值预期进行贴现与再定价的过程，而非对实体生产力的直接反映。

3. 深层结构分析
表面上，市场波动源于地缘冲突或政策调整，但深层是全球资本循环中不同利益集团的博弈。一方面，国际金融资本通过“资金空头”（funding shorts）将港股作为套利工具，为配置韩日等市场融资，这体现了金融资本全球流动中对区域市场的工具化利用；另一方面，内地政策扩容南向通及央行外储增配港股，实则通过制度性安排引导资本回流，试图稳定本土金融话语权。这种资本流动背后，是金融资产阶级内部（国际投行与本土政策资本）对资本积累主导权的争夺。

4. 阶级/利益分析
受益者：一是科技垄断资本（如AI、半导体企业），通过资本市场波动实现股权价值重估与套现；二是金融中介与投行，通过做空回补、套利交易获取手续费与价差收益；三是政策资本（如央行体系），通过制度性资本配置强化对香港金融市场的控制权。
受损者：一是中小投资者，其财富随虚拟资本波动被侵蚀；二是实体产业部门，尤其是文中提及的“生产商价格指数上涨挤压工厂利润”，反映产业资本在通胀与金融化挤压下的生存困境；三是全球无产阶级，其工资与社会保障被通胀进一步侵蚀。
权力流动体现为：金融资本通过定价权与流动性配置，实质性地影响生产资源分配，甚至绑架宏观经济政策。

5. 主流叙事批判
主流媒体（如文章引用分析师言论）将波动归因于“技术性反弹”“情绪修复”，掩盖了三个实质矛盾：其一，将金融波动简化为市场自发调节，掩盖了虚拟资本积累制度本身内嵌的危机倾向；其二，强调“政策利好”，却未触及资本分配中阶级权力的不对等——政策红利更多流向金融资产持有者而非劳动者；其三，回避地缘冲突背后的帝国主义资源争夺逻辑，将其简化为“市场情绪扰动”。马克思在《资本论》第一卷中揭示的资本主义生产关系的隐蔽性，在此处体现为金融话语对剥削关系的彻底遮蔽。

6. 解放前景
从政治经济学视角看，出路需直面三个层面：
第一，限制虚拟资本自我增殖的狂奔：如对金融交易征税、遏制高频套利，使资本重新锚定实体价值创造——正如马克思所言，工场手工业分工“发展了新的社会劳动生产力”，未来需构建以社会需求为导向、劳动者参与民主管理的生产组织形式。
第二，打破金融资本的全球霸权循环：区域资本市场应逐步摆脱对美元体系与投机性资本的依赖，通过多边结算机制与生产性投资，重塑“资本服务于生产”的关系。
第三，重建分配正义：当前“生产商价格指数上涨挤压利润”的危机，本质是剩余价值在产业资本与金融资本间分配失衡。需通过劳资集体谈判、超额利润税等工具，将积累的财富导向社会保障与公共生产。

虚拟资本的每一次剧烈波动，都是资本主义基本矛盾——生产的社会化与资本的私人占有——在金融领域的尖锐投射。唯有将金融权力纳入民主监督与社会规划，方能打破“虚拟资本暴增而现实财富匮乏”的困局，走向“每个人的自由发展是一切人的自由发展的条件”的生产关系。

（字数：758）

item.title: [SCMP] Asia’s shaky food supply shudders as ‘super’ El Nino arrives
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1720

AI Summary:

* A "super" El Niño event has officially formed in the Pacific Ocean, with sea surface temperatures running 2.5°C (4.5°F) above normal. * Meteorological agencies forecast this event could peak at levels among the highest observed since 1950, with a high probability (nearly two-in-three) of being "very strong." * Past super El Niños have devastated key Asian crops, including Indian soybean, Thai rice, Vietnamese coffee, and Indonesian/Malaysian palm oil yields. * The event threatens widespread drought, flooding, and climatic extremes across Asia, posing a significant risk to food security and agricultural economies for the region's 4.7 billion people.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
In the mist-wrapped hills of northern Thailand, where cacao trees erupt from the rich, nutrient-dense soil, three little words are spreading fear through a community of farmers.

“There is no way to know for certain,” Koranut Rattanayanyong told This Week in Asia. “But it could be a total wipeout.”

The weather event Koranut is dreading? A “super” El Nino.

Earlier this month, the climate phenomenon officially started to form over the Pacific, with a bloom of ocean heat running 2.5 degrees Celsius (4.5 degrees Fahrenheit) above normal temperatures.

The forecasts emanating from meteorological agencies across the world in the days since have been almost uniform in their alarm.

Australia’s Bureau of Meteorology said on Tuesday that this year’s El Nino “could peak at levels among the highest observed since 1950”. Its US counterpart gave almost two in three odds that it would be “very strong”.

And UN Secretary General Antonio Guterres warned on June 2 that this year’s event “will hit even harder, travel even farther and cross borders with devastating speed”.

El Nino is a recurring disruption. In essence, one forms when the Pacific Ocean runs a fever, redistributing heat and moisture in ways that can shatter the climatic rhythms on which billions of people depend. It brings drought to some regions, catastrophic flooding to others and the kinds of climatic extremes – hot days, dry soils, then sudden deluges – that can overwhelm crops.

A “super” event, scientists warn, would do all of that and more, across a wider geography, with greater intensity, for longer. It has levelled harvests before: a decade ago, a super El Nino devastated Indian soybean yields, Thai rice, Vietnam’s Robusta coffee crop and the palm oil output of both Indonesia and Malaysia.

For Asia and its 4.7 billion people – hundreds of millions of whom still live off the land – what comes next could be devastating.

Koranut has spent years building something unusual in the hills of Chiang Mai: a cacao economy.

Growers and grinders, processors and craft chocolatiers, all connected through his company Cocoa Baan Suan, operate a direct, farm-to-wrapper loop that ends in chocolate-coated nuts, dense brownies and bars of trendy “Dubai chocolate”.

This, he wants to show the world, is what rural Thailand could do if it moved up the value chain, from raw commodity to finished product, from subsistence to sophistication.

But cacao trees are highly sensitive to variations in heat and rainfall.

“Even if our water supply is good, if the heat above is that intense, the trees can’t survive,” Koranut said. “If there is waterlogging, the cacao trees will develop fungal problems. And if the yields are down … you can forget about all of this.”

Now, Koranut is waiting for a weather event that could erase it all. And he is far from alone.

In Pakistan, agriculture accounts for around 24 per cent of gross domestic product; in Laos, a similar share; in India, the government estimates that roughly 145 million people farm for a living, making up 16 per cent of the entire economy.

Even Southeast Asian industrial powerhouses such as Indonesia and Vietnam, not often thought of as agrarian economies, still depend on agriculture for 14 and 13 per cent of their output, respectively.

El Nino conditions will pour fuel on the fire of a warming world

UN Secretary General Antonio Guterres

This year’s super El Nino is arriving against a troubling backdrop. Sky-high fertiliser and fuel prices, a result of supply disruptions caused by the US-Israel war on Iran, led many Asian farmers to use less than needed this planting season.

Currencies across the region have also weakened, making food imports more expensive just as domestic production may be about to fall.

“The challenge is compounded … by the energy crisis, supply chain disruptions and the impact of weakening currencies on imports across markets from Indonesia to India,” said Vikash Sharma, director of Godrej International Trading and Investments, a Singapore-based commodity trading house.

“An El Nino of this nature will also have a ripple effect on places like Singapore and Hong Kong, both of which rely heavily on food imports.”

Nowhere, in other words, is likely to escape unscathed.

Ratings agency Fitch has warned of “sustained shortages” accelerating inflation. Futures prices for Arabica coffee, cacao and sugar have all climbed on commodity exchanges in anticipation of the fallout.

“Farm commodity markets are going to be highly volatile,” said Desh Ratna, a commodity market specialist operating out of Vietnam’s Ho Chi Minh City.

“Some parts of the world will experience dryness while others will see more rainfall. This, in turn, will impact yields and result in price spikes.”

That asymmetry may produce its own dislocations: if El Nino damages American soybean output – the foundation of the global animal feed supply – prices could rise across every food category that relies on it.

Some supply loss may be partially offset by farmers in India pivoting from water-hungry crops such as sugar cane and corn towards soybean, as officials there expect. But the overall direction of travel is clear.

From Bangkok to Tokyo, Asia’s cities were already adapting to a warming baseline. Cool rooms are being installed in public spaces. Outdoor workers are being sent home at midday.

Last year’s record temperatures extracted a grim toll in heatstroke deaths that governments are not eager to repeat.

The Philippines, perennially battered by Pacific storm systems, is steeling itself for a more violent typhoon season. Across Asia, conservationists are sounding alarms about biodiversity corridors that could be severed by wildfires if this year’s dry season turns extreme.

“El Nino conditions will pour fuel on the fire of a warming world,” UN chief Guterres warned this month. And the world is not ready.

In Nepal’s Chitwan district, not far from the flat agricultural plains that stretch south towards the Indian border, Ramesh Chandra Puri is worried about his summer rice crop.

“We have heard that this kind of weather is coming soon,” the 63-year-old said of the forecasts of unusually dry weather that have begun filtering through to his village. “But the government has not given us any support to prepare for it.”

His most recent harvest has been disrupted by weeks of heavy, unseasonal rain – too much water now, potentially too little later. The local traders paying to buy his grain are offering roughly half the government-mandated price of 3,600 Nepalese rupees (US$24) per 100kg.

That is rapidly eating away at the funds he needs to prepare his fields for the next crop, sown in late June or early July – without even taking into account the water needed to nourish it.

“If conditions are really dry this summer, we will have to resort to boring groundwater,” Puri said. “But if the hot and dry weather gets too intense, even boring does not work.”

A version of the predicament Puri finds himself in – caught between the immediate cruelty of low prices and the gathering threat of a climate event he cannot control – is now playing out among farmers across the continent.

For those who have irrigation, groundwater reserves or dams filled by last year’s rains may be enough to get through, according to Ajay Jakhar, who chairs Bharat Krishak Samaj, a farmers’ welfare organisation based in Punjab, one of India’s most productive agricultural states.

“The problem, however, is with farmers’ livelihoods in rain-fed areas such as Vidarbha in Maharashtra,” he said. Vidarbha, a region long associated in India with agrarian distress, severe debt and high rates of farmer suicides, does not need another bad season.

Roughly 45 per cent of the world’s most populous nation – some 660 million people – relies, directly or indirectly, on agriculture.

India’s Agriculture Minister Shivraj Singh Chouhan held a high-level meeting on Tuesday to outline a strategy for dealing with this year’s El Nino by redirecting resources and helping farmers pivot to more drought-resistant crops ahead of the monsoon planting season.

“Full preparations should be made in advance in those districts where there is a possibility of low rainfall or uneven rainfall,” he told the assembled officials present, adding that “weekly meetings will be held” to prepare.

India can boast community-led adaptation models, new irrigation techniques and a track record of feeding its population through shocks that would have caused crises a generation ago.

But the fertiliser shortage triggered by the Strait of Hormuz crisis – only now expected to ease as a US-Iran peace deal edges towards completion – will still be felt in the soil this season. And the heat has already been exceptional this year, even before El Nino arrives in earnest.

“The ongoing extreme heat conditions are causing alarm,” said Devinder Sharma, an independent food and trade policy analyst. “We are expecting El Nino, whose effect would be visible in July or August. This is a deadly combination for India.”

Should New Delhi’s confidence in its own resilience turns out to be misplaced, the consequences could spiral far beyond its borders.

“If there is any doubt about production, India will stop exporting food,” Jakhar said.

In a year when harvests are already under threat from Thailand to Indonesia to Vietnam, an export ban by India – one of the world’s largest food and agricultural exporters and its leading producer of rice – would land like a tonne of bricks on global markets.

Back in Chiang Mai, Koranut’s son Pinyawat thinks about the ecosystem his father has spent years building as the rain patters down.

“Really, we’re just getting started,” he said, sharing his hope that one day, cacao could become a valuable cash crop for the region.

Thailand produces around 1,500 to 2,000 tonnes of cacao each year: a fraction of the 600,000 tonnes produced by Indonesia, the world’s No 3 producer behind only the Ivory Coast and Ghana.

“But if the climate works against us, the yields won’t justify the costs,” Pinyawat said.

His father is more defiant, though not naively so. He talks about planting shade trees to protect the smaller cacao plants from direct heat, about new irrigation technology that might carry the crop through the driest stretches.

“Every problem has a solution,” Koranut said. “We just have to keep solving it … We have to fight.”

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

金融表象与资本实质：香港股市波动的政治经济学透视

1. 现象描述
香港股市受区域科技股波动、中东地缘冲突及内地政策调整影响，呈现震荡分化格局，科技板块逆势上涨，但整体市场承压。

2. 政治经济学定位
从资本运动维度看，香港股市的波动是虚拟资本与实体资本脱钩的典型表现。股票价格（如智谱AI、中芯国际的暴涨暴跌）并非由现实生产过程中的价值创造直接决定，而是受资本化预期、流动性套利与地缘风险溢价驱动。马克思在《资本论》第三卷中指出：“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 此处科技股的剧烈波动，本质上是金融资本对剩余价值预期进行贴现与再定价的过程，而非对实体生产力的直接反映。

3. 深层结构分析
表面上，市场波动源于地缘冲突或政策调整，但深层是全球资本循环中不同利益集团的博弈。一方面，国际金融资本通过“资金空头”（funding shorts）将港股作为套利工具，为配置韩日等市场融资，这体现了金融资本全球流动中对区域市场的工具化利用；另一方面，内地政策扩容南向通及央行外储增配港股，实则通过制度性安排引导资本回流，试图稳定本土金融话语权。这种资本流动背后，是金融资产阶级内部（国际投行与本土政策资本）对资本积累主导权的争夺。

4. 阶级/利益分析
受益者：一是科技垄断资本（如AI、半导体企业），通过资本市场波动实现股权价值重估与套现；二是金融中介与投行，通过做空回补、套利交易获取手续费与价差收益；三是政策资本（如央行体系），通过制度性资本配置强化对香港金融市场的控制权。
受损者：一是中小投资者，其财富随虚拟资本波动被侵蚀；二是实体产业部门，尤其是文中提及的“生产商价格指数上涨挤压工厂利润”，反映产业资本在通胀与金融化挤压下的生存困境；三是全球无产阶级，其工资与社会保障被通胀进一步侵蚀。
权力流动体现为：金融资本通过定价权与流动性配置，实质性地影响生产资源分配，甚至绑架宏观经济政策。

5. 主流叙事批判
主流媒体（如文章引用分析师言论）将波动归因于“技术性反弹”“情绪修复”，掩盖了三个实质矛盾：其一，将金融波动简化为市场自发调节，掩盖了虚拟资本积累制度本身内嵌的危机倾向；其二，强调“政策利好”，却未触及资本分配中阶级权力的不对等——政策红利更多流向金融资产持有者而非劳动者；其三，回避地缘冲突背后的帝国主义资源争夺逻辑，将其简化为“市场情绪扰动”。马克思在《资本论》第一卷中揭示的资本主义生产关系的隐蔽性，在此处体现为金融话语对剥削关系的彻底遮蔽。

6. 解放前景
从政治经济学视角看，出路需直面三个层面：
第一，限制虚拟资本自我增殖的狂奔：如对金融交易征税、遏制高频套利，使资本重新锚定实体价值创造——正如马克思所言，工场手工业分工“发展了新的社会劳动生产力”，未来需构建以社会需求为导向、劳动者参与民主管理的生产组织形式。
第二，打破金融资本的全球霸权循环：区域资本市场应逐步摆脱对美元体系与投机性资本的依赖，通过多边结算机制与生产性投资，重塑“资本服务于生产”的关系。
第三，重建分配正义：当前“生产商价格指数上涨挤压利润”的危机，本质是剩余价值在产业资本与金融资本间分配失衡。需通过劳资集体谈判、超额利润税等工具，将积累的财富导向社会保障与公共生产。

虚拟资本的每一次剧烈波动，都是资本主义基本矛盾——生产的社会化与资本的私人占有——在金融领域的尖锐投射。唯有将金融权力纳入民主监督与社会规划，方能打破“虚拟资本暴增而现实财富匮乏”的困局，走向“每个人的自由发展是一切人的自由发展的条件”的生产关系。

（字数：758）

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Bhutan’s heritage arts become the frontline of cultural preservation as modernity dawns
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 3367

AI Summary:

- Following COVID-19, Bhutan has launched rapid modernization initiatives, including reducing its Sustainable Development Fee from US$200 to US$100 per night, developing the Gelephu Mindfulness City special administrative region, and restoring the Trans Bhutan Trail. - Key projects include the planned Gelephu International Airport (opening 2029, handling up to 123 flights daily) and the promotion of tourism and innovation to integrate the country into the global economy. - To balance modernization with cultural identity, Bhutan is reinforcing traditional heritage arts, such as the sacred practice of thangka painting, as a core element of its preservation strategy.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Bhutan today doesn’t look quite like the Bhutan of even just four years ago.

In monasteries, laminated QR codes for digital payments are displayed next to worn donation boxes. In Thimphu, the capital, there are solar panels on roofs, Chinese-made electric cars on roads, construction projects just paces apart – six storeys sprouting from the ground in humdrum blocks. Even the hand-painted road signs, once quippy rhymes and memorable alliterations, have become mundane: “No hurry, no worry” replaced with “Don’t look at your mobile phone while driving”.

How did the Land of the Thunder Dragon, a nation whose reputation for idyllic, down-to-earth, lo-tech living was built on its reclusion from the outside world, get here?

Since it emerged from the Covid-19 pandemic, Bhutan has launched, in quick succession, a slew of initiatives designed to bring the once-withdrawn kingdom into the 21st century – and the people of the 21st century into its mountainous reaches. From the biennial Snowman Race ultra-marathon and the restoration of the ancient Trans Bhutan Trail as a hiking attraction to the opening of the Bhutan Innovation Lab and even a reduction of its Sustainable Development Fee – the daily levy paid by travellers visiting the country – from US$200 to US$100 per night, Bhutan is embracing the new, the foreign and the lucrative.

In June, it hosted the inaugural Bhutan International Travel Mart, to bolster its position as an alluring getaway, but its biggest leap is still to come: the Gelephu Mindfulness City, a 2,600 sq km (1,000 square miles) special administrative region in which governing policies and business practices from around the world will be adopted and tested; those that prove successful will eventually be rolled out across the country. Gelephu International Airport, handling up to 123 flights daily, is slated to open in 2029.

All this forward momentum raises the question: how does Bhutan balance the realities of progress with the preservation of its traditions, arguably the one quality that makes this destination stand out for travellers? One possible solution: double down on the foundations of your unique culture – with a twist.

On a balmy day in late May, Kinley Tshering sits cross-legged on the cement porch of the Exotic Art Gallery, in a village on the edge of Punakha district, a short stroll from famed hilltop fertility temple Chimi Lhakhang.

He is hunched over, with his back to the rest of the world, his attention trained on a stretched canvas frame propped against the wall, unbothered by the flies zipping around his head, landing on his bare shoulder, inching around on his paint-splattered jeans.

Kinley Tshering deftly layers white paint on top of a black background, colouring tiny, exposed slivers of negative space between delicate, embossed linework applied in shimmering gold, his brush strokes so precise and small he looks as though he is hardly moving.

He is painting a thangka. Sacred illustrated scrolls bearing motifs of divine beings, mandalas (concentric designs representing the universe) and scenes from Buddhist texts, thangkas are ubiquitous in Bhutan’s religious sites and dzongs (fortresses). Just steps away from Kinley Tshering’s in-progress Vajrakilaya – a wrathful deity of Vajrayana Buddhism, Bhutan’s state religion – the workshop’s full collection is on display, a cacophony of colour and pattern.

Leaning against the doorframe, watching Kinley Tshering at work, is his older brother Kinga Tshering, the owner of the Exotic Art Gallery. “You see those two dragons? He is combining them with Vajrakilaya for strength and purity,” explains Kinga Tshering. “He’s putting a lot of different ideas into this. He will even wait for his dreams. Whatever idea he gets, whatever dream he dreams, he will add those elements.”

Lhazo devotional painting – one of the 13 traditional arts and crafts of Bhutan – is in the brothers’ blood: the thangka trade is a family speciality that has been passed down through three generations. Kinga Tshering studied under his father from a young age and went to the National Institute for Zorig Chusum, in Thimphu, to continue his education. He opened the Exotic Art Gallery “around seven, eight years ago”, taking orders for thangkas from temples and private households; the rest of his income is earned through the gallery. Kinley Tshering is one of seven brothers and cousins he has in his employ.

Before starting a thangka, the artist must prepare their pigments. Botanical and mineral ingredients are crushed and blended with water and animal glue, to protect colour intensity and repel insects.

“Sometimes, we use pure 24-carat gold. But that costs a lot, so we only use that for paintings for temples,” says Kinga Tshering. He nods at the painting of Vajrakilaya. “We get black from the charcoal of oak trees. Gold, from crushed pyrite; we get it from the mountains.

“Once a thangka is done, it’s done; it is difficult to repeat. Colours that we got two, three years ago, it’s not always possible to replace them; it all depends on what nature can give. I can mix pink again, but not the exact same; that’s the beauty of handmade painting.”

Despite being a creative art form, thangka work does obey strict fundamentals. A mandala has to be a mandala, Kinga Tshering stresses. Depictions of deities must adhere to prescribed postures and hand gestures. Deviations are considered “bad deeds”.

Even within these restrictions, the brothers’ approach to the art is attuned to their passions, emotions, imagination and “vibes”, unlike that of their father, who didn’t paint thangkas for creation’s sake and whose practice was entirely tied to commissions.

“We cannot force ourselves to paint; the energy has to come from the heart,” says Kinga Tshering. “If your mind is too busy, if you’re in pain – you don’t paint. Good moods give the best results.

“Around 60 per cent of the artwork we make comes from us thinking about what we want to paint; we discuss, we wait for our dreams.” Motifs of mythical creatures, flora or fauna that come to them are melded with the immutable central figure of their thangkas. In their blending of the traditional, the devotional and the personal, the brothers keep lhazo relevant.

The 13 forms of arts and crafts Bhutan recognises as part of its cultural identity are known collectively as zorig chusum (zo meaning “the ability to make”, rig meaning “science” or “craft” and chusum meaning “13”). Some, like lhazo, survive thanks to continued demand from Bhutanese traditionalists. Others, like cane- and bamboo-weaving (tsharzo) and blacksmithing (garzo), are struggling to maintain their place in a rapidly modernising country.

“When it comes to zorig chusum, tourists are more inclined towards painting and [textile] weaving because they see them in our daily lives, in our buildings and monasteries, as paintings on the walls, in the national dress,” says Kuenzang Choden, marketing manager for Asia-Pacific and Oceania at the tourism department. “But our mission is to promote all 13 arts and crafts, including the ones that receive less public attention, like tsharzo, garzo, parzo [wood carving], shagzo [wood turning], lugzo [bronze casting] and dozo [stonework and masonry].”

Khampa, project management specialist for the Handicrafts Association of Bhutan, an organisation comprising some 7,500 members who practise zorig chusum, believes that the determination to preserve heritage starts from within.

“We are not looking to promote craft as a ‘thing’. We want people to understand the significance of it and educate them so they can find value in living that culture,” he says.

“Our art is alive; it lives as skills in the hands of our artisans. As an outsider, we just see them as someone who makes a ‘product’. But they practise that art because it defines their life; there is meaning in weaving a piece of cloth. It defines their survival. It defines their purpose. It defines their identity,” he adds. “For us, these artisans are how we can achieve our vision of living Bhutanese culture and traditions.”

A sharp, clacking noise rings out across a room at the Royal Textile Academy (RTA), in Thimphu, bouncing off the walls: repetitive, insistent, percussive. A calloused hand reaches for a wide wooden slat, suspended horizontally, pressing it down against rows of woven thread to tighten the weave. Rods of bamboo, threaded through the warp, clatter hollowly.

Two master weavers are teasing cloth into existence, one weft at a time, working silently save for the sound of their equipment: a pair of formidable pangthag backstrap looms, constructed of timber. A waterfall of filaments cascades from the top of the apparatus, the fibres kept taut by a belt wrapped around each of the weaver’s hips.

“Among the 13 traditional arts and crafts, weaving is considered especially important because of its integral role,” says Chimi Lhakyi Dolma, assistant programme officer of the RTA, which was established in 2005, under the patronage of queen mother Sangay Choden Wangchuck, to preserve thagzo, the art of weaving. She is referring to the national dress, gho for men and kira for women, which must be worn in schools, government offices, religious sites and dzongs, at official ceremonies and on formal occasions.

As a domestic skill, thagzo is passed down from mother to daughter, Dolma explains, and a weaver’s ultimate test is the mastery of the exquisite kishuthara, a highly intricate silk made using trima, a labour-intensive technique of hand-coiling individual threads around the base yarn to create a raised effect that mimics embroidery. Beyond being an example of ability and endurance – weaving a garment using this technique can take anywhere from eight months to two years – a treasured kishuthara kira can be handed down the matrilineal line, creating generational bonds.

However, “home-based weaving traditions are declining day by day”, says Dolma, who is also the founder of textile brand Dakini by Jyenn. “Although their mothers weave, younger generations do not take an interest in the skill; to them, weaving is a chore. [RTA’s] mission is to promote weaving as an art.”

While the museum at the RTA functions as a repository of ancient fabrics, the institute also runs educational programmes and houses a studio in which five master weavers, from eastern Bhutan, ply their craft. One of them creates textiles for the royal family while the others weave for research and development purposes, and for the gift shop.

Nowadays, the RTA is exploring how time-honoured techniques can be applied in novel ways. Craftswomen are encouraged to expand beyond tried-and-tested colour combinations and experiment with shapes and motif placement.

New thinking is also revolutionising an indispensable tool. “A traditional backstrap loom is constraining and not comfortable for weavers who work on it for a prolonged time; it affects their health,” says Dolma. “We are working with the Jigme Namgyel Engineering College to semi-mechanise the loom to ease their workload.”

Several initiatives run by the RTA, including the annual National Design and Arts Competition and community programmes such as livelihood skills training, also support the thagzo craft.

“Sometimes, [rural artisans] weave for months, but when their textiles can’t be sold, it’s discouraging,” Dolma says. “So, we buy it from them. If their textiles are good, we sell them in our gift shop. Otherwise, we transform them into pencil bags or small souvenirs, and we give them to the students who visit us.”

Dolma and the RTA are not alone in shining a light on the plight of hand-woven arts. Independent labels such as She Bhutan – the brainchild of self-taught designer Gaymit Sada Lepcha – are also presenting thagzo through a contemporary lens.

“It started as a celebration of women through fashion and stories,” says Lepcha, who launched She Bhutan in 2013. “I wanted to do something for women, support women artisans and work closely with them.”

Her vision: jackets, coats and capes that blend modern silhouettes and fashion sensibilities with yathra, a colourful weave native to Bumthang district, in central Bhutan.

Made of sheep’s wool and yak’s hair, yathra is historically woven into blankets, throws, rugs, outerwear and scarves – dense, practical items suited to higher altitudes, long winters and punishing temperatures – using the thrithag horizontal-frame loom, a mechanism introduced to Bhutan in the early 20th century. Bold, geometric motifs make up the distinctive yathra weave, and producing just one panel of fabric, around 63.5cm in width and 2.5 metres in length, can take up to two weeks.

Lepcha, who was born in Samtse, a small town in southern Bhutan, had no background in thagzo or design, so getting She Bhutan off the ground was a leap of faith.

“Initially, it was difficult to find manpower,” she says. “Most of the women knew only how to weave traditional gho and kira. I needed people who were familiar with pattern-making for Western garments.”

Eventually, she established a rapport with a yathra specialist and a community of weavers in Bumthang who could apply their traditional skills to her modern designs. Using yathra as the “hero element”, Lepcha creates patterns and swatches for her craftspeople to follow. After the panels of fabric are woven, a seamstress stitches them together to create the final product.

“Before I started combining yathra with modern jackets, people didn’t think they could wear this textile in a different shape or form, because it’s so old and traditional,” Lepcha says. “Nobody had done it before.”

But Covid-19 threw a spanner in the works and She Bhutan was forced to go on hiatus.

In October 2025, when Lepcha felt ready to return, help came from an unexpected quarter: the first-ever Bhutan Fashion Week, a government-supported event celebrating the kingdom’s textile heritage. Twenty designers and labels presented their collections; Lepcha used the opportunity and the national platform to mount She Bhutan’s comeback. In January, the brand went back to bricks and mortar, opening a boutique in downtown Thimphu, to bring the Bumthang art form to the high street.

“Preserving heritage art doesn’t mean doing things in only the strictest, most traditional way. Preservation happens when an old art form is given new life,” she says. “By adding a contemporary touch to yathra, it is kept alive because people continue to wear it.”

With all this craftsmanship in the air, it was perhaps inevitable that other practices not previously undertaken in the kingdom have entered Bhutan. In 2019, the Bhutan Wine Company brought Western vinification to the small town of Yusipang. It resulted in all 21 lots of the inaugural 2023 vintage being auctioned at Bonhams in April last year for a total of US$74,250 – an unheard-of price for an upstart wine label – expanding alcohol-making possibilities beyond the home-brewing tradition of ara, Bhutan’s answer to moonshine.

Coffee culture is also knocking on Bhutan’s door. Home-grown bean farms and roasters such as Bhutan Mountain Coffee and independent cafes, like Ambient Cafe in Thimphu and CCH2O Cafe in Bumthang’s Ura valley, are finding footholds in a country traditionally married to butter tea (suja). Most of these are entrepreneurial endeavours started by returnees who have been influenced by their time abroad.

Initiatives such as the Voluntary Artists Studio, Thimphu, aka VAST Bhutan, a non-profit organisation supporting contemporary art, creativity and innovation, and the Kabjisa artist retreat-cum-art village, led by Asha Kama Wangdi, a co-founder of VAST Bhutan and one of the kingdom’s most respected modern artists, are part of a movement to nurture “a uniquely Bhutanese voice in contemporary art”.

Choden notes opportunities for zorig chusum to flourish among this proliferation of old-meets-new ingenuity: “We’re seeing tourists – who used to come to Bhutan for sightseeing – increasingly looking for authentic and immersive experiences. Many visitors now want to understand our culture more deeply and connect with local communities, so there’s a growing interest in Bhutanese crafts. It reflects a broader global trend of travellers seeking meaningful, culturally enriching moments.”

Attention, however, can be a mixed blessing. “When tourism grows, there is pressure to produce more and adapt to market demands. We already see it happening in Lhuentse [district], with kishuthara textiles. With the demand of kishuthara exceeding what the weavers can actually produce, there are machine-made kira being sold, [diluting] the authenticity of kishuthara and the hard work that goes into it,” says Choden.

Commercialisation and commodification are notable concerns, too. “We’re not in the business of arts and culture; we don’t want to sell our arts and culture,” says Khampa. “Bhutanese arts and crafts are deeply rooted in the spiritual teachings of Buddhism. A master craftsman can become an enlightened being in that field because they have practised it, they have lived that meaning of art. But if you are learning a skill just to sell it, you’ll never understand it. You would only understand markets, money, customers, challenges.

“If you are not living the art and the culture, you are not being true to yourself.”

Cultural conservation is proving to be a struggle between generations as well.

“Like any other country, young people are focused on digital progress and pursuing further education,” says Choden. “In western Bhutan, we have more job opportunities, so they leave their villages and towns in the east.” As a result, ageing generations are unable to pass down their know-how.

“Bhutan is known for living the culture, breathing the culture. With a younger generation less likely to pursue traditional arts and crafts as a profession, it diminishes our rich cultural heritage. When a master artisan passes away, we will have difficulty reviving this craft.”

Khampa says he believes in the evolution of arts and culture: “We don’t want it to be frozen in time. We want it to flow like a river, rather than be like a reservoir. But to make people understand, to live that particular arts and culture, we need to speak their language. We don’t want them to forget their culture.”

It’s true, Bhutan is changing. For those who romanticise the Buddhist kingdom in the Himalayan mountains as a mystical Shangri-La whose appeal is intrinsically tied to its “entrenched in the past” way of life, it’s time to lose the rose-tinted glasses. But while there are practical and ideological obstacles to overcome, Bhutan’s quest for modernity hasn’t taken the shine off its national cultural heritage. If anything, it has added fuel to the fire of legacy preservation, because to understand the Bhutan of 2026, it must still be experienced through its folk arts and crafts.

People such as Choden, who promote Bhutan to the world, are keenly aware of the tightrope they must walk between the loss of identity and being left in the dust.

“We cannot compete with machines in terms of production but we can make people understand that whatever a machine can produce, hands can do it far better the traditional way. We need to blend modernity and tradition so one does not overpower the other. We need to document the traditional techniques and raise awareness among locals and tourists about the value of authentic handmade products so they can cherish them.”

When it comes to the fate of zorig chusum, Khampa takes a guarded view: “It all depends on the people, if they are committed to continuing to live that art and culture. But we are optimistic that whatever we are doing right now [to educate and promote] will have a bearing on the future passion of Bhutanese people.”

Kinga Tshering feels hopeful about the path that lies ahead for lhazo. “Now, there are a lot more painters, compared with the last 10 years. It is going to increase again; because of schools like Zorig Chusum, anyone can learn to paint. People get to learn about the religion that they practise and there is work available, so they can see a future in this kind of art.”

Lepcha sees modernisation as motivation: “It gives me encouragement to preserve our textiles and our culture because I don’t want them to vanish into thin air someday; the more modern we become, the more I want to preserve thagzo. But we can do a lot more than just preserve; we can modernise and make tradition relevant to people today.”

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

金融表象与资本实质：香港股市波动的政治经济学透视

1. 现象描述
香港股市受区域科技股波动、中东地缘冲突及内地政策调整影响，呈现震荡分化格局，科技板块逆势上涨，但整体市场承压。

2. 政治经济学定位
从资本运动维度看，香港股市的波动是虚拟资本与实体资本脱钩的典型表现。股票价格（如智谱AI、中芯国际的暴涨暴跌）并非由现实生产过程中的价值创造直接决定，而是受资本化预期、流动性套利与地缘风险溢价驱动。马克思在《资本论》第三卷中指出：“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 此处科技股的剧烈波动，本质上是金融资本对剩余价值预期进行贴现与再定价的过程，而非对实体生产力的直接反映。

3. 深层结构分析
表面上，市场波动源于地缘冲突或政策调整，但深层是全球资本循环中不同利益集团的博弈。一方面，国际金融资本通过“资金空头”（funding shorts）将港股作为套利工具，为配置韩日等市场融资，这体现了金融资本全球流动中对区域市场的工具化利用；另一方面，内地政策扩容南向通及央行外储增配港股，实则通过制度性安排引导资本回流，试图稳定本土金融话语权。这种资本流动背后，是金融资产阶级内部（国际投行与本土政策资本）对资本积累主导权的争夺。

4. 阶级/利益分析
受益者：一是科技垄断资本（如AI、半导体企业），通过资本市场波动实现股权价值重估与套现；二是金融中介与投行，通过做空回补、套利交易获取手续费与价差收益；三是政策资本（如央行体系），通过制度性资本配置强化对香港金融市场的控制权。
受损者：一是中小投资者，其财富随虚拟资本波动被侵蚀；二是实体产业部门，尤其是文中提及的“生产商价格指数上涨挤压工厂利润”，反映产业资本在通胀与金融化挤压下的生存困境；三是全球无产阶级，其工资与社会保障被通胀进一步侵蚀。
权力流动体现为：金融资本通过定价权与流动性配置，实质性地影响生产资源分配，甚至绑架宏观经济政策。

5. 主流叙事批判
主流媒体（如文章引用分析师言论）将波动归因于“技术性反弹”“情绪修复”，掩盖了三个实质矛盾：其一，将金融波动简化为市场自发调节，掩盖了虚拟资本积累制度本身内嵌的危机倾向；其二，强调“政策利好”，却未触及资本分配中阶级权力的不对等——政策红利更多流向金融资产持有者而非劳动者；其三，回避地缘冲突背后的帝国主义资源争夺逻辑，将其简化为“市场情绪扰动”。马克思在《资本论》第一卷中揭示的资本主义生产关系的隐蔽性，在此处体现为金融话语对剥削关系的彻底遮蔽。

6. 解放前景
从政治经济学视角看，出路需直面三个层面：
第一，限制虚拟资本自我增殖的狂奔：如对金融交易征税、遏制高频套利，使资本重新锚定实体价值创造——正如马克思所言，工场手工业分工“发展了新的社会劳动生产力”，未来需构建以社会需求为导向、劳动者参与民主管理的生产组织形式。
第二，打破金融资本的全球霸权循环：区域资本市场应逐步摆脱对美元体系与投机性资本的依赖，通过多边结算机制与生产性投资，重塑“资本服务于生产”的关系。
第三，重建分配正义：当前“生产商价格指数上涨挤压利润”的危机，本质是剩余价值在产业资本与金融资本间分配失衡。需通过劳资集体谈判、超额利润税等工具，将积累的财富导向社会保障与公共生产。

虚拟资本的每一次剧烈波动，都是资本主义基本矛盾——生产的社会化与资本的私人占有——在金融领域的尖锐投射。唯有将金融权力纳入民主监督与社会规划，方能打破“虚拟资本暴增而现实财富匮乏”的困局，走向“每个人的自由发展是一切人的自由发展的条件”的生产关系。

（字数：758）

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Out of the shadows: Huawei’s ‘chip queen’ steps back into the spotlight with scaling law
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1836

AI Summary:

- Huawei's semiconductor head He Tingbo ("chip queen") re-emerged publicly after seven years to introduce the "Tau (τ) Scaling Law" at a major conference in Shanghai. - The proposed law claims a path to achieve transistor densities equivalent to a 1.4-nanometer process by 2031, crucially without needing advanced EUV lithography machines restricted by US sanctions. - The announcement has sparked industry debate over whether it represents a genuine breakthrough for China's tech self-reliance or an overly ambitious theory facing manufacturing hurdles. - Huawei's approach shifts the performance metric from physical size (per Moore's Law) to compressing electronic signal propagation time, integrating advanced packaging techniques into chip design. - Experts note the underlying physics is known, but the framework's synthesis is being compared in significance to Moore's Law, aiming to "redefine what 'advanced' means" in chipmaking.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
After seven years working in the shadows, He Tingbo stepped back into the limelight last month.

The head of Huawei Technologies’ secretive semiconductor business – widely dubbed the company’s “chip queen” – had been out of the public view since 2019, when Washington severed the Chinese company’s global access to advanced technology, including semiconductors.

Her retreat into the background became a symbol of Huawei’s battle for survival. That all changed last month on a global academic stage in Shanghai – the IEEE International Symposium on Circuits and Systems.

It was there that He introduced the “Tau (τ) Scaling Law”, which Huawei claimed could achieve transistor densities equivalent to the cutting-edge 1.4-nanometre process by 2031 – all without the need for advanced extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography machines out of reach due to US sanctions.

The announcement ignited a debate across the global semiconductor industry: Had Huawei truly unlocked a revolutionary chapter for China’s drive towards tech self-reliance, or was it just an ambitious theory, or even hype, destined to stumble when it came to the realities of manufacturing?

For half a century, the electronics industry treated Moore’s Law – the principle that the number of transistors on a chip doubles roughly every two years – as gospel. But as silicon structures approached atomic limits, geometric scaling was yielding diminishing economic returns and hitting an architectural brick wall.

Huawei’s Tau Law is an attempt to break free from this paradigm. It seeks to “redefine what ‘advanced’ means”, using time rather than physical size as the ultimate metric of progress, according to a briefing note issued last week by Shanghai-based semiconductor research firm ICwise.

Named after the Greek letter used in physics to denote propagation delay, the law focuses on compressing the time it takes for electronic signals to travel across devices and circuits.

Analysts and academics point out that the underlying physics is not entirely new. Instead, Huawei’s breakthrough lies in its ability to bring advanced packaging concepts – usually relegated to back-end manufacturing – into the front-end chip design phase.

“Huawei is leveraging design and packaging innovations to achieve some of the benefits typically associated with more advanced process nodes,” said Brady Wang, associate director at Counterpoint Research, adding that techniques like Design-Technology Co-Optimisation have been widely used by global firms for years.

However, distilling these disparate academic efforts into a unified law was what made the announcement “historically significant”, said Zhao Chun, a professor in the department of electrical and electronics engineering at Xian Jiaotong-Liverpool University.

According to Zhao, the meaning of the framework is directly comparable to Intel co-founder Gordon Moore’s seminal 1965 observation.

The physical manifestation of this theory is a proprietary architecture Huawei calls “LogicFolding”. While many industry watchers have dismissed it as a variation of advanced 3D packaging, He Tingbo stressed a crucial distinction: it weaves circuits together with a logic die at a “very small gear ratio” – a measure similar to the relationship between two gears engaged with each other.

Traditional 3D stacking or “chiplet” designs simply stitch separate, prefabricated chips closer together. In contrast, LogicFolding redesigns a single chip’s internal blueprint from the ground up, Zhao added.

Huawei’s semiconductor ambitions became a matter of national survival after the US blacklisted the company and its chip design unit, HiSilicon, which was later banned from working with global foundries like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC).

After a frantic period of stockpiling advanced chips and components, Huawei’s inventory finally ran dry in 2022, crippling its smartphone business.

It was during this crisis that He was tasked with reviving the company’s silicon heart. In a highly emotional internal letter in 2019, He revealed a long-held “spare tyres” strategy drawn up for this exact scenario, comparing the coming push to embarking on the “most tragic and heroic Long March in the history of science and technology”.

Recalling those dark days at the IEEE forum last month, He admitted to a period of deep frustration where she felt there was “no way out”.

Her epiphany, she said, came from Dujiangyan – the 2,000-year-old irrigation system in China’s southwest Sichuan province, built without modern machinery or electricity. She began to view US sanctions not as an insurmountable wall, but simply as engineering constraints to be solved.

That defiance bore fruit with the surprise launch of the Mate 60 Pro smartphone in 2023, powered by the 5G-capable Kirin 9000s processor.

A teardown by Canadian research firm TechInsights revealed that China’s top foundry, Semiconductor Manufacturing International Corporation (SMIC), had managed to manufacture the processor with 7nm technology using existing chipmaking tools pushed to their limits.

Huawei has since broken its code of secrecy on multiple fronts. Last September, it unveiled a three-year road map for its Ascend AI processors, positioned as domestic alternatives to chips from US giant Nvidia.

“There is actually no need to worry about the chip issue,” Huawei’s octogenarian founder and CEO Ren Zhengfei told People’s Daily last year. “By using methods like stacking and clustering, [our] computing performance is comparable to the state-of-the-art level.”

Driven by tightening US export controls, Ascend chips are gaining traction in Chinese data centres. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang recently said that the American titan’s share of China’s high-end AI chip market had plummeted from 95 per cent to virtually zero.

The shift has led to a growing sense of confidence in Shenzhen, where Huawei is headquartered. Xu Zhijun, Huawei’s rotating chairman, went as far as to thank Washington for the pressure.

“If it weren’t for the US forcing our country, company and industry, we would never have taken on a task like this,” Xu said in an interview with Chinese media following the Tau Scaling Law announcement. “We have the US to thank for enabling our nation’s semiconductor supply chain to truly grow and develop.”

The strategic implications of the Tau Scaling Law are reverberating globally. While Washington remains fixated on choking off China’s access to advanced lithography hardware over concerns the technology will aid China’s military, Beijing has poured billions into developing home-grown alternatives.

In what appears to be a retaliatory move, Beijing has squeezed US tech imports by withholding domestic sales approval for Nvidia’s H200 AI processors – even though Washington had already given the green light for exporting the chips.

“Nvidia is likely the most concerned by this development,” said He Hui, director of semiconductor research at Omdia. “Having already lost half of its market share in China, Nvidia now faces a reality where domestic chip performance has nearly closed the gap with the H200.”

Responding to the Tau Law announcement, Nvidia’s Huang called the architecture a “breakthrough for Huawei”, though he suggested it posed no immediate threat to the manufacturing hegemony of TSMC – Nvidia’s primary foundry partner.

Laila Khawaja, an analyst at Gavekal Research, said she viewed the announcement as a calculated strategic signal from China.

Huawei was “presenting foreign governments and companies with a choice: cooperate and share the potential upside from China’s experiment, or risk an alternative Chinese ecosystem that could erode their position in the critical China market – and potentially global markets in the long run,” Khawaja said.

The pivotal question remains: Can Huawei truly decouple from EUV lithography?

Phelix Lee, a senior equity analyst at Morningstar, notes that LogicFolding effectively bypasses the most advanced chipmaking tools – manufactured by Dutch firm ASML under a functional monopoly – by shifting the engineering burden to reducing data transmission latency.

However, Counterpoint’s Wang cautioned that while this approach extends the utility of older deep ultraviolet lithography, or DUV, machines, a significant gap remains in power efficiency, performance, speed and manufacturing throughput when compared to leading-edge EUV-based systems.

Translating a theoretical 1.4nm equivalence into commercial reality requires overcoming a mountain of supply chain chokepoints.

The most immediate enemy of LogicFolding is heat dissipation. ICwise noted that folding circuits into multiple active layers causes the heat density to jump five to 10 times.

Furthermore, the shift of design methodology from 2D to 3D would require dedicated chip design tools, another chokepoint for China. Traditional electronic design automation (EDA) tools are built for 2D flat wiring, while 3D LogicFolding requires multilayer layout, cross-layer timing closure, and cross-layer power integrity analysis – software that the domestic industry is still racing to develop.

Major players are rushing to seize the opportunity. Empyrean Technology, one of China’s best hopes in replacing Western EDA tools, said last week that it had developed a set of tools for 3D integrated circuit design as “a key carrier for the landing of Tau scaling law”.

Yield is yet another issue. According to ICwise, while Huawei’s 1.5-micrometre bonding pitch is highly advanced, achieving a commercially viable yield rate when stacking three to four active layers remained an extremely difficult task.

Because of these hurdles, some experts said Huawei’s playbook cannot be easily copied by others.

“We think Huawei’s approach is not replicable, especially in the next five years,” said Morningstar’s Lee. He pointed out that Huawei possessed a rare advantage: a vertically integrated ecosystem that spans chip design, manufacturing partnerships, consumer electronics and network infrastructure all under one corporate roof.

Across China’s semiconductor landscape, players from front-end design houses to back-end packaging firms have hailed the framework as a watershed moment for domestic chip self-sufficiency.

“[It] is a very advanced design concept,” said Ding Jingfeng, vice-president of technology at Chinese chip packaging giant Tongfu Microelectronics, speaking at an industry forum late last month.

“At least in chip design capabilities, Huawei is at the forefront of the world,” he said. “3D design is just beginning, and future designs will be more complex, evolving into more architectures and product forms based on applications.”

Han Chaoyang, vice-president of marketing at Hefei-based AI chip company ListenAI – which is currently developing on-device chips for large language model inference – noted that the framework provided vital guidance for domestic firms trying to outmanoeuvre physical fabrication bottlenecks.

Han said his company was applying similar system-level co-design principles, enabling 8nm chips to deliver a performance similar to 4nm graphics processing units while deploying optimised large language model algorithms.

“It is essentially a declaration, or even an appeal, from Huawei and the semiconductor industry in the post-Moore’s Law era,” Han said.

The ultimate proof of concept for Huawei and the broader Chinese tech ecosystem is fast approaching. This autumn, the first commercial iteration of the LogicFolding architecture is expected to debut inside the Kirin processors powering the company’s next generation of smartphones.

For the Huawei chip team under He, the “spare tyres” they started building years ago are now keeping Huawei on track.

“We not only opened up the road, but also built a highway,” she told People’s Daily after the Tau law was announced.

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

金融表象与资本实质：香港股市波动的政治经济学透视

1. 现象描述
香港股市受区域科技股波动、中东地缘冲突及内地政策调整影响，呈现震荡分化格局，科技板块逆势上涨，但整体市场承压。

2. 政治经济学定位
从资本运动维度看，香港股市的波动是虚拟资本与实体资本脱钩的典型表现。股票价格（如智谱AI、中芯国际的暴涨暴跌）并非由现实生产过程中的价值创造直接决定，而是受资本化预期、流动性套利与地缘风险溢价驱动。马克思在《资本论》第三卷中指出：“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 此处科技股的剧烈波动，本质上是金融资本对剩余价值预期进行贴现与再定价的过程，而非对实体生产力的直接反映。

3. 深层结构分析
表面上，市场波动源于地缘冲突或政策调整，但深层是全球资本循环中不同利益集团的博弈。一方面，国际金融资本通过“资金空头”（funding shorts）将港股作为套利工具，为配置韩日等市场融资，这体现了金融资本全球流动中对区域市场的工具化利用；另一方面，内地政策扩容南向通及央行外储增配港股，实则通过制度性安排引导资本回流，试图稳定本土金融话语权。这种资本流动背后，是金融资产阶级内部（国际投行与本土政策资本）对资本积累主导权的争夺。

4. 阶级/利益分析
受益者：一是科技垄断资本（如AI、半导体企业），通过资本市场波动实现股权价值重估与套现；二是金融中介与投行，通过做空回补、套利交易获取手续费与价差收益；三是政策资本（如央行体系），通过制度性资本配置强化对香港金融市场的控制权。
受损者：一是中小投资者，其财富随虚拟资本波动被侵蚀；二是实体产业部门，尤其是文中提及的“生产商价格指数上涨挤压工厂利润”，反映产业资本在通胀与金融化挤压下的生存困境；三是全球无产阶级，其工资与社会保障被通胀进一步侵蚀。
权力流动体现为：金融资本通过定价权与流动性配置，实质性地影响生产资源分配，甚至绑架宏观经济政策。

5. 主流叙事批判
主流媒体（如文章引用分析师言论）将波动归因于“技术性反弹”“情绪修复”，掩盖了三个实质矛盾：其一，将金融波动简化为市场自发调节，掩盖了虚拟资本积累制度本身内嵌的危机倾向；其二，强调“政策利好”，却未触及资本分配中阶级权力的不对等——政策红利更多流向金融资产持有者而非劳动者；其三，回避地缘冲突背后的帝国主义资源争夺逻辑，将其简化为“市场情绪扰动”。马克思在《资本论》第一卷中揭示的资本主义生产关系的隐蔽性，在此处体现为金融话语对剥削关系的彻底遮蔽。

6. 解放前景
从政治经济学视角看，出路需直面三个层面：
第一，限制虚拟资本自我增殖的狂奔：如对金融交易征税、遏制高频套利，使资本重新锚定实体价值创造——正如马克思所言，工场手工业分工“发展了新的社会劳动生产力”，未来需构建以社会需求为导向、劳动者参与民主管理的生产组织形式。
第二，打破金融资本的全球霸权循环：区域资本市场应逐步摆脱对美元体系与投机性资本的依赖，通过多边结算机制与生产性投资，重塑“资本服务于生产”的关系。
第三，重建分配正义：当前“生产商价格指数上涨挤压利润”的危机，本质是剩余价值在产业资本与金融资本间分配失衡。需通过劳资集体谈判、超额利润税等工具，将积累的财富导向社会保障与公共生产。

虚拟资本的每一次剧烈波动，都是资本主义基本矛盾——生产的社会化与资本的私人占有——在金融领域的尖锐投射。唯有将金融权力纳入民主监督与社会规划，方能打破“虚拟资本暴增而现实财富匮乏”的困局，走向“每个人的自由发展是一切人的自由发展的条件”的生产关系。

（字数：758）

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] China-India diplomats are shaking hands, but it’s no holds barred for web users. Why?
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1164

AI Summary:

• Chinese Ambassador Xu Feihong's positive remarks on China-India relations triggered a significant nationalist backlash on Chinese social media, with users accusing him of forgetting his role and making xenophobic comments. • Official diplomatic efforts to rebuild ties are progressing, including a surge in visa issuance (85,000 in Q1 2026), the reopening of the Kailash Manasarovar pilgrimage route, and India easing some Chinese investment restrictions. • This official thaw contrasts sharply with deep-seated public hostility on both sides, revealing a gap between state-led normalization and entrenched negative sentiment among internet users. • Viral incidents, such as videos of disruptive behavior by Indian tourists, have further inflamed online anger, while pragmatic voices warn that sensationalist narratives risk reinforcing stereotypes. • Rebuilding public trust is seen as a far greater challenge than restoring practical diplomatic and travel links, with online outrage being driven in part by the pursuit of attention and traffic.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
The Chinese ambassador to India’s attempt at bridge-building has triggered a fierce nationalist backlash back home, including accusations that he has lost sight of his role.

“The ambassador seems to have forgotten his actual job,” one social media user wrote this month of Xu Feihong, arguing that the envoy’s role was to make Indians more friendly towards China – not the other way round.

Another took a xenophobic turn, accusing Xu of “letting hundreds of thousands, even millions, of Indian ‘cockroaches’ flood into China” and “polluting the country”.

In June, Xu described China-India ties as being among the “most important” bilateral relationships in the world”, saying they had moved from a “reset and fresh start” to a “new level of development”.

Yet the online outrage extends far beyond Xu’s specific comments. It reflects deep-seated frustration over Beijing’s recent diplomatic overtures to New Delhi, with Xu – as China’s public face in India – becoming a lightning rod for the public anger.

But even as online commenters vent their fury, official state policy and public demand are moving in the opposite direction.

Six years after clashes on their disputed Himalayan border left 20 Indian and four Chinese soldiers dead, the two sides are finally moving to rebuild their shattered relationship.

The Chinese embassy in New Delhi reported issuing 85,000 visas to Indian passport holders in the first quarter of 2026. The surge comes as the neighbours work to mend diplomatic ties, alongside growing curiosity among many Indians regarding China’s rapid technological and economic growth.

Amid the thaw, China has reopened the Kailash Manasarovar pilgrimage route through Tibet, sacred to followers of Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism and Bon, while India has eased some restrictions on Chinese investment.

New Delhi is also considering allowing Chinese journalists to return for the first time since 2023, when the last Xinhua reporter left the country after being denied a visa extension. However, it has yet to reciprocate on travel permits, as Chinese nationals continue to face significant hurdles in securing Indian visas.

But a different battle is unfolding online, revealing the gap between government-led attempts at normalisation and decades of entrenched public hostility on both sides.

Observers say rebuilding public trust could prove far harder than restoring flights, visas and diplomatic engagement. Still, some pragmatic voices in China have pushed back against the backlash, warning that sensationalist portrayals of the other country – often drawn from isolated negative incidents – risk entrenching stereotypes. They argue that such narratives are driven less by patriotism than by the pursuit of online attention and traffic.

A series of viral incidents has further inflamed online sentiment. Since May, multiple videos circulating on Chinese social media have shown Indian tourists engaging in disruptive behaviour, including arguing loudly with hotel staff, dancing on the Great Wall, and placing their feet on seats aboard high-speed trains.

Many Chinese web users condemned such “uncivilised” conduct, saying such behaviour harmed the country’s social environment.

Meanwhile, clips of Indian tourists just walking through Chinese cities have prompted online questions about whether they had enough money to spend.

Another widely shared video clip from a visiting Indian influencer drew criticism for asking passers-by stereotypical questions, such as mocking the quality of China’s infrastructure and asking if they were wearing fake Gucci products.

A recent commentary in Chinese state-owned outlet The Paper warned about the rise of biased social media content exaggerating the “outrageous” or bizarre aspects of life in India, while amplifying alleged misconduct by Indians in China. This risked creating a fragmented and stereotypical view of the country, it cautioned.

“Although these posts may appear to be expressions of patriotism, they are actually designed to generate traffic and attention by stoking hostility and xenophobia,” the article said. “We cannot negate an entire country because of a few individuals.”

Last month, a heated online debate erupted between Indian and Chinese web users over social hierarchies, with some Indians arguing that China had a “caste-like system” comparable to India’s hereditary social structure.

To support their argument, they pointed to ancient China’s Shi-Nong-Gong-Shang social hierarchy and the modern hukou household registration system, arguing that both had fostered deep, enduring urban-rural disparities for migrant workers.

“Born rural? Tough luck. You’re basically a second-class citizen locked out of proper education, healthcare, housing subsidies, and decent jobs,” posted one user from India. “It’s a caste system … that traps hundreds of millions in poverty.”

A YouTube video on the issue, titled “Indians expose China’s caste system” and produced by an Indian influencer with 1.7 million subscribers, drew nearly 550,000 views.

Chinese social media users hit back, with one highlighting that in China’s administrative system “the children of farmers can become officials, merchants, or workers through their own efforts”.

“All countries in the world have these occupational divisions. Indians, with your level of understanding, it’s better not to come out and embarrass yourself.”

He Xianqing, an associate researcher at the National Institute for South China Sea Studies, noted there was “no doubt” that online and media-driven nationalism had had a negative impact on the positive development of China-India relations.

“If the peoples of the two countries do not understand each other and public support is weak, the momentum for cooperation between the two sides will be limited, which may eventually backfire on the cooperative relationship,” he warned.

But he also emphasised that such digital friction had its limits. “Nationalistic remarks or sentiments are irrational after all, and they are an unsystematic public opinion reaction,” he said, arguing that they would fail to derail the diplomatic momentum between the two governments.

“There are also some rational voices on both sides, and we should listen more to these rational voices.”

He also called on both sides to organise more Track 2 dialogues and cultural exchange activities, “using the power of experts to provide a rational voice for public opinion in both countries”.

Sourabh Gupta of the Institute for China-American Studies said nationalist rhetoric had long been a feature of the bilateral relationship, but largely on the Indian side. “The novel feature is that the sentiment is now beginning to be shared both ways.”

Gupta argued that the geopolitical disruption caused by US President Donald Trump had provided a “tailwind to the calculus of tentative rapprochement”. Just as New Delhi had largely learned to look past public opinion in managing ties with China, Beijing was also likely to see beyond nationalist rhetoric and press ahead.

“It is not as if the two governments are racing ahead of public opinion. Their bilateral relationship is no longer at the failing stage, but that’s about the best that can be said about the state of ties currently,” Gupta noted.

“China is not the first country in the Asia-Pacific region to see Indian tourists or workers in such dim light, and there might be a lesson for travelling Indians to internalise too in this regard.”

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

金融表象与资本实质：香港股市波动的政治经济学透视

1. 现象描述
香港股市受区域科技股波动、中东地缘冲突及内地政策调整影响，呈现震荡分化格局，科技板块逆势上涨，但整体市场承压。

2. 政治经济学定位
从资本运动维度看，香港股市的波动是虚拟资本与实体资本脱钩的典型表现。股票价格（如智谱AI、中芯国际的暴涨暴跌）并非由现实生产过程中的价值创造直接决定，而是受资本化预期、流动性套利与地缘风险溢价驱动。马克思在《资本论》第三卷中指出：“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 此处科技股的剧烈波动，本质上是金融资本对剩余价值预期进行贴现与再定价的过程，而非对实体生产力的直接反映。

3. 深层结构分析
表面上，市场波动源于地缘冲突或政策调整，但深层是全球资本循环中不同利益集团的博弈。一方面，国际金融资本通过“资金空头”（funding shorts）将港股作为套利工具，为配置韩日等市场融资，这体现了金融资本全球流动中对区域市场的工具化利用；另一方面，内地政策扩容南向通及央行外储增配港股，实则通过制度性安排引导资本回流，试图稳定本土金融话语权。这种资本流动背后，是金融资产阶级内部（国际投行与本土政策资本）对资本积累主导权的争夺。

4. 阶级/利益分析
