item.title: [SCMP] Fuchsia Dunlop tells how she fell for China’s cuisine and names her perfect dish
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 3752

AI Summary:

- Fuchsia Dunlop is a British author and chef recognized as the Western world's foremost expert on regional Chinese cuisines, especially Sichuanese, and has authored seven books on the subject. - Her interest began in 1994 while a student in Chengdu, leading her to study cooking in local restaurants and the Sichuan Higher Institute of Cuisine, including a formal three-month chef's training course. - She wrote her first Sichuan cookbook, published in 2001, to address the lack of English-language resources on the region's diverse food culture. - Dunlop views her work as a lifelong exploration of China through its cuisine, noting its vastness and regional diversity. - Her personal preferences for mooncakes include savory Shanghai-style minced pork versions and flaky Yunnan mooncakes with ham and sugar, in addition to sharing richer Cantonese varieties.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Fuchsia Dunlop is a British author and chef specialising in Chinese cuisine, particularly Sichuanese food. She has written seven popular books and is arguably the Western world’s most knowledgeable person about the diverse food cultures and cooking techniques of China’s various regions. In this interview, Dunlop talks about the comparisons that are often made between Chinese and Western cuisines, as well as her passion for China’s food culture and views on how it is changing.

What are your thoughts on mooncakes? Do you have a favourite flavour?

I enjoy the ritual of giving and sharing mooncakes around the Mid-Autumn Festival, especially when I’m at home in London, because it feels like another connection with China. I like the rich Cantonese mooncakes in small doses: a slice of a lotus seed paste mooncake with some green tea, for example. But my favourites are the savoury minced pork mooncakes made in Shanghai, and various Yunnan mooncakes. One classic Yunnan version is made with flaky pastry and stuffed with chopped Yunnan ham and sugar. I also love the Weishan mooncakes, which are larger and more like a regular cake, made with flour and eggs and sugar, with beautiful stuffings that might include red bean paste or rose petal jam.

How did your interest and research in Chinese cuisine begin, and has your enthusiasm changed over the years?

I went to China in 1994 as a British Council student. I attended Sichuan University as a postgraduate student in the history department.

But I had loved cooking more than anything since childhood. Finding myself in Chengdu, where the food was so exciting and so fresh and so different from any Chinese food I’d had before, I started learning to cook.

I persuaded the owners of little local restaurants near the university to let me study in their kitchens. And then this famous cooking school, the Sichuan Higher Institute of Cuisine, agreed to give me and a German friend some private cooking classes.

After I finished university, the teachers at the cooking school invited me to join a chef’s training course and I spent the following three months with 50 young people from Sichuan learning the basic skills of Sichuanese cooking.

When I went back to England I was struck by the fact that there really weren’t any Sichuan cookbooks in print that were in English.

There was this incredible Chinese region that was the size of a European country, but people in Britain knew virtually nothing about it, which is hard to believe now that Sichuan food has become so popular.

So I had this idea to write a Sichuan cookbook and over the next few years I made several trips back to Sichuan and did more research.

My first book came out in English in 2001. I never expected researching and writing about Chinese food to become my life’s work. But the more I learn – and China is so huge and the food is so diverse – the more I realise that writing about it can be a fascinating way to explore the country and Chinese culture.

People in China care so much about food: at a deep cultural level this seems to be how the Chinese have always expressed themselves.

How did your interest in Sichuan food extend to other regional cuisines?

My first book was quite successful, so afterwards I thought it would be interesting to look at another region. I decided to research Hunan cookery – and discovered that there was almost no material in English about Hunan food either.

I thought it would be interesting because it was another famously spicy region and clearly readers love to read spicy recipes and cook spicy Chinese food, which is very different from the Cantonese food that almost all the Chinese restaurants in the West were serving at the time.

In 2003 I went to Hunan for a couple of months and that was the start of my second book, Revolutionary Chinese Cookbook: Recipes from Hunan Province, which came out in 2006.

But in the meantime I had also visited Shanghai for the first time and that planted a little seed in my mind of interest in the cuisine of Jiangnan [the geographic region south of the Yangtze River].

Then in 2008 I started thinking that I’d really like to write a book about the region, and that eventually led to Land of Fish and Rice: Recipes from the Culinary Heart of China.

When I was studying in London more than five years ago, most of the Chinese restaurants were serving Sichuan food. Did that happen after your first book Sichuan Cookery?

When my Sichuan cookbook came out in 2001, there were no Sichuanese restaurants in central London. And all the Chinese supermarkets were Cantonese, so you couldn’t get authentic Sichuanese ingredients such as Pi Xian broad bean paste, for example. You could not get good, zingy Sichuan peppercorns because all the ingredients available were intended to be used for Cantonese cooking.

When my book came out, a lot of readers were amazed: at that time hardly anyone in Britain, even food writers, chefs and food experts, had tasted real Sichuan peppercorns.

People had no acquaintance with the mala – numbing and spicy – flavour of Sichuanese cuisine, which combines chilli heat with the tingling sensation on the lips that you get from Sichuan pepper. It’s hard to imagine now because mapo tofu, for example, is so well known, but at that time it wasn’t.

In 2006, there was a game changer – the first Sichuan restaurant in central London, which caused a lot of excitement and got fantastic reviews in all the newspapers.

That was the beginning of this big change that ended up as you described, by the time you were in London, with Sichuan food being a real feature of the London dining scene and everyone being familiar with it, not just in London, but in Manchester, Birmingham, Nottingham, Edinburgh.

In the past, there weren’t so many Chinese people living in Britain and the vast majority of Chinese restaurants catered to people who weren’t Chinese.

And I think the hugely significant change is that now we have so many Chinese students and other Chinese residents and visitors in Britain, so there’s a whole new generation of Chinese restaurants that are designed purely to cater for Chinese tastes. They don’t have to adapt their flavours to Western tastes.

So we now have the opportunity to try the kind of Chinese food that people in China want to eat themselves. And it also reflects changing culinary fashions in China.

Sichuan spice is not a problem. Sichuan pepper, if you haven’t had it before, is quite strange and disconcerting. But one of the things I always tried to do was to explain it to people and prepare them for it. So for most people, when they try Sichuan pepper in a cool environment where they feel relaxed, it’s strange at first, but then it’s wonderful.

I mean, why wouldn’t you love Sichuan food?

According to your research, what is the biggest misconception about Chinese food in the West, and how did it come about?

There are several misconceptions that have come out of a particular history. Early Chinese food was almost exclusively Cantonese, based on a single region, but also adapted to Western tastes.

And in Britain, the most familiar kind of Chinese food was the takeaway, which was extremely simplified – no bones, no shells, not so many vegetables, lots of fried foods. It was not designed for Chinese people to eat themselves. But this style of food really shaped perceptions of Chinese cuisine, both in Britain and in a similar way in the US.

The worst stereotype of Chinese food has been that it should be cheap. People will pay a lot of money for Japanese sushi, but while they may think Chinese food is tasty, they don’t necessarily see it as the kind of sophisticated, elite cuisine that you would pay serious money for.

Another misunderstanding is the idea that it’s unhealthy – again, because popular takeaway food involved a lot of fried food, few vegetables, few soups and so on. There is also a certain amount of Western prejudice against monosodium glutamate, which is perfectly normal in China, but Westerners don’t use it in home cooking or quality cooking, and so it’s very much stigmatised.

And then the other side of that coin is the idea that the Chinese eat a lot of weird, scary stuff like chicken feet and snakes.

The Chinese certainly do eat many ingredients that are seen as unusual or strange in the West, but this has always been seen through the lens of a notion that the Chinese eat such things because they were very poor and desperate, which is a terrible simplification, because obviously very rich Chinese people also like to eat duck tongues and goose feet.

There are other reasons Chinese people eat a lot of foods that Westerners reject. And they include this pleasure of texture, the pleasure of the sensation of food in the mouth, and also the Chinese idea of unusual and exotic food – the idea that since, for example, duck tongue is very small and there is only one of them in each duck, it’s a great privilege to have that particular part.

I think for Westerners it’s been a very strange picture because on the one hand, Chinese food has been hugely successful, everybody loves it. There’s no other foreign cuisine that’s been more popular globally than Chinese in a way, but it’s also been saddled with these rather negative prejudices that are very outdated.

As someone who is Chinese, you will know very well that these stereotypes are ridiculous. Because in China, yes, you do have cheap peasant food and street food, but you also have extremely extravagant, time-consuming gongfu food or banquet cooking, which is another aspect of Chinese food culture.

And then in terms of eating “weird” things, as I said, there are gastronomic reasons for this as well as economic reasons.

Finally in terms of health, I think no other culture is as insistent on the link between diet and health as the Chinese. In China, food has been seen as medicine for more than 2,000 years. And Chinese people talk about food and health all the time.

When I learned to cook Chinese food, I learned how to make vegetables taste delicious and how to eat food that is balanced and healthy as well as delicious. I think that is one of the most impressive things about Chinese cuisine.

What misconceptions do Chinese people have about Western food?

Just as Westerners can be very dismissive of Chinese food, Chinese people can be very dismissive of Western food.

All over China, people have told me that Western food is simple and boring, and a lot of people seem to think that it just consists of hamburgers and sandwiches.

And then recently there has been all this talk about “white people food”, which is just seen as completely uncouth and boring and depressing. And then there’s a very weird stereotype about British food – “dark cuisine”.

There’s an old-fashioned British dish from Cornwall called a stargazy pie and it’s a pie with fish heads poking up through the pastry. It’s a very obscure regional dish that I’ve never tried and most British people have never tried. But an English woman wrote a blog post about it that went viral in China, so it’s now almost certainly better known in China than it is at home.

I would say that a lot of Chinese people who have not had the chance to travel abroad make these huge generalisations about Western food that are just as silly as those some Westerners make about all Chinese food.

I’ve also found it very surprising that a lot of Chinese people have told me over the years that they think Western food is healthier because there’s this sort of tradition of scientific nutrition where you’re told how many calories and how much protein and that sort of thing.

But I find that very strange because it seems to me that people in China, certainly the older generation, have a knowledge of healthy eating that is incredibly deep and incredibly useful, whereas I think a lot of Westerners are actually quite confused about how to eat healthily.

There is also this idea that Chinese food can be quite oily and Western food is not. But again, I think that’s really strange because if you think about traditional Chinese food, it involves a lot of soups and steamed dishes and boiled dishes, and traditional Chinese home cooking does not use a lot of oil.

I think it’s quite sad to see that people in China are now making a lot of the same mistakes that we’ve already made in the West of cooking less and eating less fresh food. It is one of the unfortunate side effects of modernisation and industrialisation, isn’t it?

What do you think of Westernised Chinese food, such as the fast food restaurants popular in the US, like Panda Express?

You have to remember that Chinese food is not just the food of the country of China. It’s also the food of a huge Chinese diaspora.

So Chinese food is already part of global culture and it’s a presence in every city practically all over the world. Not only Chinese people living in the West want to eat Chinese food.

Chinese food doesn’t have to be one thing. I think people are always inventing and adapting and borrowing ideas from each other. For example, I recently wrote an article about Shanghainese Western food, which is a bit like Hong Kong-style Western food.

So in China you have versions of Western food that are completely separate from what Westerners actually want to eat. They are adapted to Chinese tastes, and I think in the West you also have Chinese food that’s not designed for Chinese people. It’s made for Westerners. I don’t have a problem with that. I think it’s fun to be inventive.

The only problem is when people start to judge Chinese food by Panda Express and think that it represents the cuisine of a very large and sophisticated gastronomic nation.

We just have to be a bit careful about judging it.

In many Chinese cities, the highly rated and expensive restaurants tend to be Japanese. This seems to be the case in other major cities around the world. Why is Japanese food more likely to be considered fine dining than Chinese food?

I think the status of food is always about money.

Japan got rich before China in the late 20th century, so its food acquired high status at a time when China was quite closed to the outside world and was not rich and did not have high status.

It’s the same within China with regional cuisines. For example, when there were many rich merchants from Anhui, Anhui food was considered one of the “eight major cuisines”. But when I was a student in Sichuan in the 1990s, all of the expensive restaurants were Cantonese because places like Hong Kong and Shenzhen got rich first.

What’s interesting now is that China is getting richer and more powerful, so I think the status of China and Chinese culture is rising. The problem at the moment is that we don’t have enough chefs in the UK. We have very tight immigration rules in Britain and it’s almost impossible these days for chefs to come here and work.

If we had more free movement of chefs, Chinese food here in Britain would become more ambitious and more expensive.

It’s even more important to find ways to communicate with each other, and it’s also incredibly important to try and understand each other

Do you face any challenges in defending Chinese food in the West?

The whole environment is becoming more complicated because of geopolitical tensions. Obviously the relationship between the West and China has become more complicated.

But I think in that context it’s even more important to find ways to communicate with each other, and it’s also incredibly important to try and understand each other.

So, for example, it’s fantastic that China is now more open to foreign tourists, because foreigners will go to China and actually see something of the country, meet people and have a real experience.

At the same time, the first encounters most foreigners have with Chinese culture often involve Chinese food, and it’s an area that’s not so politically contentious, a space where you can actually learn a lot about China and have a fantastic experience of China without being so complicated.

Have there been any obstacles to cultural exchange between China and Britain over the years? Could Chinese food serve as a bridge?

The real difference is that, as I’ve described, in the 1990s, when I started doing this work, the food was all Cantonese, now it’s very regional.

So we have restaurants in London that serve Sichuan food, as well as dishes from Chongqing, Hunan, and the northeast. We have some quite wonderful Shanghai supper clubs. Although we only see a small proportion of Chinese food because China is huge, already people can see that Chinese food is not one thing, that it’s very diverse.

And we now even have more northern food, so handmade noodles and breads, like Xian street food, that can just show different sides of Chinese food culture.

Some people say that if the Chinese ate less meat it would help slow down global warming. This is very controversial in China because meat is an integral part of Chinese cuisine. What are your views?

The truth is that we should all be eating less meat if we want to tackle climate change. The situation in China is that most people didn’t eat much meat until the 1990s. And now, with the reform and opening up that followed, everyone wants to eat more meat – more like the extravagant way we eat in the West.

So objectively it is a huge problem that you have over a billion people suddenly eating more meat. But I can understand the Chinese view that it’s really unfair.

Climate change is a global problem. It would be great if Chinese people were more aware of the role that eating meat plays in climate change, but people in the West also have to change their diets and eat less meat.

And I think traditional Chinese cuisine is a fantastic resource and inspiration for that. Because in traditional Chinese cuisine there are so many ways of using a little bit of meat to make vegetarian ingredients taste fantastic – like mapo tofu and dry-fried green beans.

There are all these dishes where you have a little bit of meat and lots of vegetables that are really delicious. We can learn a lot from traditional Chinese food about how to eat less meat.

But the main thing is that it is unfair for Westerners to put the whole burden on Chinese consumers without looking at their own behaviour.

How have Chinese eating habits changed over the decades? How much of the change is political?

The first thing is that so many young people don’t really cook. The older generation, when I lived in Chengdu, were really excellent cooks. Not only did they cook daily meals, they also made pickles, sausages and other preserves. You can really see how these skills are now being lost.

It’s also quite interesting to see how Chinese people are eating more industrialised and prepared food.

At the same time in Britain, there’s a book that came out two years ago about ultra-processed foods, looking at the harmful effects of eating too much processed food, and I see China making the same mistakes we’ve made, and it’s a real shame.

China has a fantastic food culture, which is a huge resource for how to eat healthily and deliciously.

So it’s sad to see that younger people are losing touch with that and I hope that as people in China become more health conscious, which they are, and as they become more aware of the environmental impact of how we eat, which I hope will become more widespread, then they will be able to see their own culture as an invaluable resource for eating more healthily and sustainably.

But I think it is more about economic changes. When I lived in Chengdu in the 1990s, there was very little manufactured food. There were no supermarkets and everyone went to fresh markets to buy vegetables and fish.

And now there’s a huge expansion of food production and factory farming in China. There’s mass production of meat and also of ready meals, as you mentioned, and even restaurants are serving food that’s pre-prepared.

It’s all driven by the same technology and economics as in the West, and also the whole sort of takeaway food system, the fact that you can order anything at home that you don’t have to cook.

Which dish do you think best represents Chinese cuisine?

If I had to choose one, I would probably choose mapo tofu.

The reason is that it’s a really good example of a recipe that uses a little bit of meat and very delicious fermented bean spices to give an amazing flavour to a plant food. For me, it’s a really good example of how Chinese food offers ways to eat healthily and sustainably but without sacrificing pleasure, because this dish is utterly delicious.

Do you have any ongoing projects?

At the moment I’m writing another cookbook, and it’s not just about one region. It’s more of a simple homestyle Chinese cookbook, a bit like my previous book Every Grain of Rice.

So it’s designed to give readers the tools of some traditional Chinese techniques to cook wonderful food at home, not always with Chinese ingredients, but to cook food that’s really delicious and quite healthy and exciting.

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

金融表象与资本实质：香港股市波动的政治经济学透视

1. 现象描述
香港股市受区域科技股波动、中东地缘冲突及内地政策调整影响，呈现震荡分化格局，科技板块逆势上涨，但整体市场承压。

2. 政治经济学定位
从资本运动维度看，香港股市的波动是虚拟资本与实体资本脱钩的典型表现。股票价格（如智谱AI、中芯国际的暴涨暴跌）并非由现实生产过程中的价值创造直接决定，而是受资本化预期、流动性套利与地缘风险溢价驱动。马克思在《资本论》第三卷中指出：“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 此处科技股的剧烈波动，本质上是金融资本对剩余价值预期进行贴现与再定价的过程，而非对实体生产力的直接反映。

3. 深层结构分析
表面上，市场波动源于地缘冲突或政策调整，但深层是全球资本循环中不同利益集团的博弈。一方面，国际金融资本通过“资金空头”（funding shorts）将港股作为套利工具，为配置韩日等市场融资，这体现了金融资本全球流动中对区域市场的工具化利用；另一方面，内地政策扩容南向通及央行外储增配港股，实则通过制度性安排引导资本回流，试图稳定本土金融话语权。这种资本流动背后，是金融资产阶级内部（国际投行与本土政策资本）对资本积累主导权的争夺。

4. 阶级/利益分析
受益者：一是科技垄断资本（如AI、半导体企业），通过资本市场波动实现股权价值重估与套现；二是金融中介与投行，通过做空回补、套利交易获取手续费与价差收益；三是政策资本（如央行体系），通过制度性资本配置强化对香港金融市场的控制权。
受损者：一是中小投资者，其财富随虚拟资本波动被侵蚀；二是实体产业部门，尤其是文中提及的“生产商价格指数上涨挤压工厂利润”，反映产业资本在通胀与金融化挤压下的生存困境；三是全球无产阶级，其工资与社会保障被通胀进一步侵蚀。
权力流动体现为：金融资本通过定价权与流动性配置，实质性地影响生产资源分配，甚至绑架宏观经济政策。

5. 主流叙事批判
主流媒体（如文章引用分析师言论）将波动归因于“技术性反弹”“情绪修复”，掩盖了三个实质矛盾：其一，将金融波动简化为市场自发调节，掩盖了虚拟资本积累制度本身内嵌的危机倾向；其二，强调“政策利好”，却未触及资本分配中阶级权力的不对等——政策红利更多流向金融资产持有者而非劳动者；其三，回避地缘冲突背后的帝国主义资源争夺逻辑，将其简化为“市场情绪扰动”。马克思在《资本论》第一卷中揭示的资本主义生产关系的隐蔽性，在此处体现为金融话语对剥削关系的彻底遮蔽。

6. 解放前景
从政治经济学视角看，出路需直面三个层面：
第一，限制虚拟资本自我增殖的狂奔：如对金融交易征税、遏制高频套利，使资本重新锚定实体价值创造——正如马克思所言，工场手工业分工“发展了新的社会劳动生产力”，未来需构建以社会需求为导向、劳动者参与民主管理的生产组织形式。
第二，打破金融资本的全球霸权循环：区域资本市场应逐步摆脱对美元体系与投机性资本的依赖，通过多边结算机制与生产性投资，重塑“资本服务于生产”的关系。
第三，重建分配正义：当前“生产商价格指数上涨挤压利润”的危机，本质是剩余价值在产业资本与金融资本间分配失衡。需通过劳资集体谈判、超额利润税等工具，将积累的财富导向社会保障与公共生产。

虚拟资本的每一次剧烈波动，都是资本主义基本矛盾——生产的社会化与资本的私人占有——在金融领域的尖锐投射。唯有将金融权力纳入民主监督与社会规划，方能打破“虚拟资本暴增而现实财富匮乏”的困局，走向“每个人的自由发展是一切人的自由发展的条件”的生产关系。

（字数：758）

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Why Citigroup CEO Jane Fraser left the US-China summit feeling optimistic
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1851

AI Summary:

* Citigroup CEO Jane Fraser attended the US-China summit in Beijing with 17 other business leaders, viewing the engagement as a positive sign of stabilization and collaboration between the two powers. * Fraser described the summit as a "meeting of equals," noting a consistent focus on "collaboration" rather than "de-risking," which she believes is good for the global economy. * The summit, the seventh face-to-face meeting between Presidents Trump and Xi, resulted in a commitment to build a "constructive relationship of strategic stability" and an agreement for a follow-up meeting in September. * Despite acknowledging existing tensions and areas of decoupling, Fraser remains optimistic and considers China a strategic imperative for Citi. * Jane Fraser, 58, is the global CEO of Citigroup, the former chair of its board, and is often referred to as the most powerful woman on Wall Street.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
For Citigroup’s global CEO Jane Fraser, her time in Beijing last month meant far more than a handshake and photograph with heads of state.

Fraser was among 17 business leaders US President Donald Trump invited to China as part of his three-day visit to Beijing from May 13 to May 15. Standing on the western steps outside the Great Hall of the People – an enormous building near Tiananmen Square that is the primary venue for China’s legislative activities, diplomatic receptions and state banquets – Fraser took in one of China’s most iconic panoramas.

Amid analyst caution about the structural tensions shaping the China-US relationship, some saw the meeting as little more than good optics. To Fraser, such takes missed the big picture, one that was broader and more encouraging.

“Two important powers in the world were engaging with each other, making sure there was an understanding of each other’s points of view,” she said in an exclusive interview.

As China courted foreign investment and its companies pursued global expansions, the summit reaffirmed stabilisation and collaboration as shared priorities, Fraser said. That meant China would remain a strategic imperative for Citi and many other firms.

“In the room, it felt much more like a meeting of equals,” Fraser said during an Asia trip late last month, before flying to Beijing again to meet clients and senior government leaders. There was recognition of China’s importance as a leader in many industries, and therefore “the discussion was very much about stabilisation”, she said, adding that there was “real investment” on both sides towards improving the economic relationship.

The number of US leaders at the summit, the support of the Trump administration for US companies engaging with China and the support of the Chinese government were all strong positive signs, she added.

The summit marked the seventh face-to-face meeting between Trump and President Xi Jinping. Focused on the US-Israel war on Iran, trade and the status of Taiwan, among other issues, it was also the first trip by a US leader to China since 2017. Following the meetings, both nations committed to establishing a “constructive relationship of strategic stability”, and the two leaders agreed to meet in the US in September.

While Fraser did not deny tensions between the two countries, she said she was encouraged to see their commitment to healthy ties.

“There are areas where there is a decoupling that has occurred, but instead of the word ‘de-risking’, we heard the word ‘collaboration’ all the time on both sides,” Fraser said. “I think that’s good for the world.”

Fraser, 58, is often referred to as the most powerful woman on Wall Street. Born in St Andrews, Scotland, she studied economics at Cambridge University. Before joining Citi, she was a partner at McKinsey & Company. She was named chair of Citi’s board in October. She is married with two children.

Last year, her pay climbed to US$42 million, elevating her into the ranks of the best-paid bosses on Wall Street as investors cheered her efforts to restructure the group’s businesses and streamline its management. Citi’s stock has gained 101 per cent since she took the helm in March 2021, and the company’s value is now around US$238.7 billion. In the latest recognition of her services to the global financial sector, she was awarded a damehood during King Charles III’s birthday honours last month.

Fraser’s presence at the Beijing summit highlighted the strategic importance of the Chinese market for the global bank, as well as its active pursuit of a wholly owned securities licence in China. The licence would permit Citi to directly operate an onshore brokerage and investment banking business in mainland China, allowing it to compete against already licensed peers like JP Morgan, Goldman Sachs and UBS.

Beijing reportedly approved the licence during the summit, after a four-year process, but it has not yet been issued.

“I’m very hopeful we do get it, because the role we play in connecting China and the world, the world and China, it requires more of the breadth and depth of the local capital market and capabilities,” Fraser said. “It would be beneficial for our Chinese clients.”

Fraser said the banking group was focused on serving Chinese companies looking to expand overseas as well as clients with cross-border needs, adding that many of her bank’s international clients, including those from the US, had been showing growing interest in China and its world-leading companies in many industries.

In the first four months of this year, 20,113 new foreign-invested enterprises were established in China, representing a 6.8 per cent year-on-year increase, according to data from the Ministry of Commerce. By source, investment flows into China grew significantly from Luxembourg (up 110 per cent), Switzerland (61 per cent), France (58 per cent) and the US (25 per cent).

“We see a lot of the American companies recognising that if you don’t know what’s happening in China and don’t understand where that is … then you are going to be irrelevant,” she said. “It doesn’t necessarily mean to say that they’ll be as competitive as the Chinese companies, but they want to understand.”

Citi is known as the “flower flag” bank in China, a nickname that originated in 1902 when locals thought the stars on the American flag flown above the first Shanghai branch of Citi predecessor International Banking Corporation looked like a representation of flowers.

The bank has shifted its strategy to primarily focus on institutional banking, capital markets and corporate services. It has largely completed an exit from 14 international consumer markets, including mainland China, India and Taiwan. Two years ago, it completed the sale of its mainland consumer wealth portfolio to HSBC.

Citi has advised on a number of big cross-border transactions in the last 12 months, including acting as an exclusive financial adviser to US-based General Mills in the sale of its Haagen-Dazs shops in mainland China to an investor group including China-based lemon tea chain Ningji.

Today, Citi serves 70 per cent of the Fortune 500 companies operating in China, as well as over 300 local enterprises. In the last five years, the bank helped raise more than US$300 billion for Chinese clients from global capital markets, according to data from analytics platform Dealogic.

China stepped up policy support for cross-border financing in May by raising the leverage ratios for overseas lending by foreign banks in the country. The move aimed to promote trade and investment as well as accelerate the global use of the yuan amid rising demand for cross-border financial services as Chinese companies quicken their pace of overseas expansion.

The People’s Bank of China, the National Financial Regulatory Administration, the China Securities Regulatory Commission and the State Administration of Foreign Exchange announced major financial measures separately at the Lujiazui Financial Forum last month.

“Obviously, for Chinese clients – as China goes more global – it is very useful to have the renminbi internationalised and to make sure they have the asset base behind the needs and ability to hedge,” Fraser said. “Given the size of China and the globalisation of Chinese companies, the internationalisation of the renminbi is something clients want and need, and we’ll be there to support them.”

While at the moment the US dollar was “in debt” it would remain the reserve currency “for a long time”, she said.

Looking ahead, Fraser said the increase in cross-border trade and Chinese companies going global was “a major trend that will not change”. Changes in money flows would create more opportunity in Asia, including mainland China and Hong Kong, she added.

The world was seeing increasing flows between China and other regions – including the Middle East and Latin America – mediated through Hong Kong, as well as important flows to the United States and Europe, according to Fraser.

“China has enormous scale, but it’s still young in developing that scale,” she said, adding that clients wanted to use Hong Kong as a springboard to mainland China, ensuring the city would “play an absolutely critical role” in the new phase of globalisation.

Fraser emphasised the bank’s long commitment to the city, which she said had a special place in her heart. “We’re unique in having a 124-year history in Hong Kong,” she said.

Hong Kong had a “long and deep history of being one of the big global hubs and [financial] centres,” Fraser said, highlighting the city’s talent base, infrastructure and regulatory framework as a solid ecosystem.

The city was “not just a gateway to China” but a “China plus”, providing access to where “all the new innovations are coming from and how they are being applied”, she said.

Last year, Citi took part as a primary liquidity provider in the enhanced offshore yuan bond repo scheme introduced by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) and the PBOC, acting as the only US bank among the 11 dealers in the programme.

As Citi continues to develop digital asset solutions to meet the market’s needs, Fraser praised Hong Kong’s technological innovations and regulatory framework to support such developments. With Beijing’s support, she said the HKMA was doing well in establishing frameworks for many innovations that would shape the future financial system, citing the successful tokenised bond issuances.

“What I really appreciate here is the regulation is forward-looking,” she said.

The city made history by launching the world’s first tokenised government green bond in 2023. This was followed by the issuance of the world’s first multicurrency digital bond in 2024, as well as the largest digital bond issuance at the time of issuance and the first digital bond to integrate tokenised central bank money in the form of e-CNY and e-HKD in 2025.

A lot of Chinese wealth is flowing through Hong Kong, as the city’s market offers sophistication, breadth and depth, as well as investment access to different global opportunities in the region and beyond, Fraser said.

She noted that family offices from China and around the world were setting up in Hong Kong, and said Citi planned to expand its wealth-management business to serve them.

During her trip to Hong Kong in late June, Fraser met Victor Fung Kwok-king, group chairman of the Li & Fung group of companies, which Citi has served for 117 years. They discussed the next phase of reinvention, including how the bank could support the group on topics including blockchain, logistics, AI and digitisation.

In the first quarter of this year, Citi’s net income jumped 42 per cent year on year to US$5.8 billion on US$24.6 billion in revenue, the highest quarterly trading haul in a decade.

“What we have proven in the last four years is not only could we do all of that restructuring of the organisation, but we grew,” she said, adding that there was a lot of growth and upside ahead, which should make investors “very happy”.

“Our competitors should be fearful,” Fraser said, adding she planned to celebrate her damehood with her family following her packed Asia trip.

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

1. 现象描述

item.title: [SCMP] Why did only 2 Hong Kong developers put up their hands for Northern Metropolis project?
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 2025

AI Summary:

- Hong Kong's government received two bids for an 11-hectare pilot site in Hung Shui Kiu, the first tender under its Northern Metropolis blueprint to transform 30,000 hectares near the mainland border. - Analysts considered the two submissions a success given the current property downturn, noting that large consortiums of local and mainland Chinese firms are expected to become the norm for such complex projects. - The outcome sets a reassuring precedent for future mega-tenders in areas like San Tin and Fanling North, providing a blueprint that may bolster investor confidence. - Questions remain about sustaining momentum for upcoming tenders and whether the government will adapt future tender terms based on this initial result.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Stretches of green farmland and shimmering ponds, winding village roads and sprawling brownfield logistics yards all lend Hung Shui Kiu in the New Territories a patchwork look of rustic Hong Kong standing in stark contrast to its glistening skyscrapers.

This rural landscape is slated for radical transformation under the government’s ambitious Northern Metropolis blueprint and is primed to be reinvented as a modernised logistics hub in the near future.

However, the premise of this transformation relies heavily on market buy-in. The project faced its first litmus test with the closing of the site tender last Friday, which was also the government’s first new large-scale land disposal tender.

The tender has defied conservative expectations by drawing two submissions, comprising one solo bid and one massive joint venture.

“Even with only one bid, the government will treat it as a success,” said Hannah Jeong, head of valuation and advisory services at CBRE Hong Kong.

Others have hailed the turnout as a success given the current property downturn and noted that large consortiums made up of local and mainland Chinese firms would become the norm for Northern Metropolis developments due to the sheer scale and complexity of these multi-use plots.

This outcome, they said, had also set a reassuring precedent for the government’s coming mega-tenders, while providing a vital blueprint that would bolster investor confidence as future development parcels were rolled out in San Tin and Fanling North.

The results raise questions about whether momentum can be sustained for future tenders and what permutations may work well to optimise development. Will more players be encouraged to take on the risk or will they continue to hold back? Will the government adapt the terms of future tenders based on the recent outcome? Analysts say these are issues that will decide the development of the megaproject.

The Hung Shui Kiu pilot site marked the first land sale under the government’s new large-scale land disposal model for the Northern Metropolis. The authorities’ blueprint is to transform 30,000 hectares (74,130 acres) of land near the mainland border into an economic and residential hub.

As part of the first batch of the most construction-ready land within the megaproject, the roughly 11-hectare pilot site is designed for double duty, boosting the city’s housing supply while anchoring innovation and technology (I&T) industries right at the border.

Beyond this specific tender, the wider Hung Shui Kiu area is being strategically positioned as a high-end professional services and logistics hub. The master blueprint features dedicated zones for commerce, recreation and conservation, alongside a newly announced roughly 22-hectare university town area.

The cluster as a whole is designed to cultivate a mature, self-sustaining community by seamlessly connecting different sites within the megaproject.

The Northern Metropolis has been touted repeatedly as a top priority for the current administration, capped most recently by a visit by Xia Baolong, director of the Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office (HKMAO), who underscored Beijing’s call to accelerate construction.

The initiative serves as a critical strategic test of whether Hong Kong can successfully reinvent its role within the country’s broader I&T landscape. City leader John Lee Ka-chiu is in the midst of a large-scale consultation exercise to draft Hong Kong’s own five-year plan that will align with national development, specifically Beijing’s blueprint covering 2026 to 2030.

Hence, last week’s tender was closely watched as a test of whether the government can persuade property companies to take on a broader role in delivering its economic development agenda.

“The central government was watching how the developers play their part,” an establishment source told the South China Morning Post, without elaborating.

The 10.5-hectare site under the tender comprises three residential plots and three enterprise and technology park sites, with capacity for about 3,120 homes and 280,000 square metres (3.01 million sq ft) of industrial floor space.

Unlike traditional land sales, bids are assessed under a two-envelope system, with 30 per cent weighting given to the land premium offered and 70 per cent to non-price factors, including proposals for technology park development and plans to attract strategic industries, investment and jobs.

Henderson Land Development submitted a stand-alone bid, while Sino Land partnered with five companies – China Merchants Land, China Resources Land (Overseas), China Overseas Land & Investment, CTG Investment and Chinese e-commerce firm JD.com.

Other major developers, including Sun Hung Kai Properties, New World Development and CK Asset Holdings, did not take part.

Against the backdrop of a sluggish economic recovery, industry observers had low expectations for the tender before the closing deadline, the SCMP learned.

Jeong, of CBRE Hong Kong, said the firm had expected all along a maximum of two bidders for the plot of land, adding that limited commercial feasibility and operational difficulties acted as the key deterrents for potential bidders.

“The three land sites in Hung Shui Kiu are either triangular or irregularly shaped,” she said, noting that these irregular layouts posed development challenges for firms.

She said that a major “operator gap” existed in meeting the enterprise and technology (E&T) requirements, noting that residential developers were struggling to identify suitable partners with a proven track record and the financial strength to meet the government’s stringent criteria, especially since the tender required the development of a site spanning about 10,190 square metres.

She added that overall returns became marginal once potential E&T losses were factored in, meaning the risk-adjusted return simply did not justify the massive capital and time commitment required.

Industry analysts and government officials viewed the final turnout of only two bidders as a clear success.

“Two bids for a project of this complexity and scale should be viewed as a qualified success, not a disappointment,” Vincent Cheung, managing director at Vincorn Consulting and Appraisal, said, noting that the pilot area was the first of its kind under an entirely new large-scale land disposal framework.

“There is no market precedent, no comparable benchmark transaction.”

He said the formation of a multiparty consortium bid showed that “the pairing of mainland state-owned enterprise capital, local developer execution capability and industrial anchors has now been validated as a workable structure”.

“It [the consortium bid] reflects a calibrated, rather than fearful, risk posture … in an environment of elevated interest rates and subdued residential sentiment, aggregating complementary competencies across housing, technology and logistics is a highly sophisticated response,” he said.

He added that rather than a sign of corporate weakness, multiparty risk distribution was a rational way to meet the government’s complex scoring criteria.

Conversely, he said, Henderson Land’s decision to bid unilaterally was strategically consistent with its position as one of the largest private landowners in the New Territories.

“This allows the developer to maximise the government’s premium offset mechanisms by leveraging its massive local land bank,” he said.

Cheung said that the broader outcome provided a constructive, if cautious, signal that the Northern Metropolis framework could successfully attract well-resourced consortiums.

Commenting a day after the tender closed, Secretary for Development Bernadette Linn Hon-ho said that unlike previous stand-alone residential or commercial land tenders, the new Northern Metropolis model had achieved the government’s goal of pairing traditional developers with industrial operators.

She said she was particularly pleased that the bids differed from past tenders for residential or commercial sites, which often attracted “homogeneous” bidders.

However, asked whether the tender results had met Beijing’s expectations, a source said that the business sector had not faced much pressure as it was a free market and companies simply bid after calculating costs to ensure profitability.

The source added that the government was likely to have faced far more pressure than the private sector and authorities had been acting as matchmakers behind the scenes.

“They provided developers with lists of mainland artificial intelligence companies that have expressed interest in partnering on the project,” he said.

However, he said that because many of these tech firms were not household names and remained unfamiliar to the local commercial property sector, developers needed more time to assess them.

“The developers simply need more time to vet and get comfortable with these prospective tech partners,” the insider said.

He said that several major developers had conducted site visits and expressed initial interest in bidding, but many hit a bottleneck during discussions to create joint ventures.

He said that joint ventures with unfamiliar AI firms involved massive capital distribution, complex profit-sharing models and intricate legal agreements, processes that some consortiums simply could not finalise before the tight deadline.

He noted that some mainland AI firms were equally unfamiliar with Hong Kong’s large-scale land disposal frameworks, so many had adopted a “wait-and-see” approach, choosing to observe how this initial tender performed before committing to future tenders in the region.

Economist Simon Lee Siu-po said it was difficult for traditional developers to bid alone, adding that forming consortiums helped share the risk, especially for megaprojects.

He noted that back in the 2000s, several property developers tried to diversify and transform their businesses but ultimately failed.

“It is very difficult to get developers to pivot into industrial sectors because they are so accustomed to the traditional model of buying land, building properties and selling them,” he said, noting that developers were only now beginning to explore new business models.

Looking ahead to Fanling North and San Tin, the other two pilot areas earmarked for large-scale land disposal, the market expected the government to roll out the former first, given its more mature ancillary infrastructure.

“The objective conditions in San Tin are simply not mature enough yet,” another insider said, pointing out that critical transport infrastructure, such as some of the nearby proposed Northern Link railway stations, would not be ready until at least 2032.

He added that this prolonged infrastructure lag was expected to temporarily dampen developers’ appetite for bidding on San Tin plots.

The source said he expected the government would closely monitor the market’s response to the Hung Shui Kiu results and work to modify future tendering terms to better align with developer interest and financial realities.

“It was a highly, highly technical bid. Small players won’t have the appetite not just for risk but complexity; so it’s up to the big players to do their part,” the source said.

Kathy Lee, head of research and retail consultancy at Colliers, said the tender had successfully forced a structural evolution, drawing out cross-border joint ventures that were capable of executing long-term ecosystem building rather than conventional land banking.

Another source also warned that the market should brace for relatively few bidders, citing a total of fewer than five in the coming Northern Metropolis land sales, noting that the new tendering model represented a radical shift from traditional practices.

He added that historically, developers were only required to bid for land to build and sell flats. However, the current framework introduced a new, industry-driven style that bundles residential plots with information technology and industrial land, requiring property firms to partner directly with industrial operators.

“The industry needs time to adapt to this new model,” the source said.

The insider added that the sheer scale of these multi-use sites made it incredibly difficult for small to medium-sized developers to participate.

“These developers typically lack the immense capital required for such long-term, large-scale projects,” the source said, noting that the payback period for advanced industrial hubs was significantly longer compared with traditional residential developments, where profits were realised much faster through flat sales.

While forming consortiums offered a potential avenue for smaller developers to enter the Northern Metropolis market, the source noted they were likely to have very little say in the decision-making process.

As a result, smaller players were expected to pivot back to traditional investments such as urban redevelopment projects, where the development cycle was familiar and they could maintain full control over the asset.

“Only the major conglomerates can afford to enter the game now,” he said.

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

金融表象与资本实质：香港股市波动的政治经济学透视

1. 现象描述
香港股市受区域科技股波动、中东地缘冲突及内地政策调整影响，呈现震荡分化格局，科技板块逆势上涨，但整体市场承压。

2. 政治经济学定位
从资本运动维度看，香港股市的波动是虚拟资本与实体资本脱钩的典型表现。股票价格（如智谱AI、中芯国际的暴涨暴跌）并非由现实生产过程中的价值创造直接决定，而是受资本化预期、流动性套利与地缘风险溢价驱动。马克思在《资本论》第三卷中指出：“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 此处科技股的剧烈波动，本质上是金融资本对剩余价值预期进行贴现与再定价的过程，而非对实体生产力的直接反映。

3. 深层结构分析
表面上，市场波动源于地缘冲突或政策调整，但深层是全球资本循环中不同利益集团的博弈。一方面，国际金融资本通过“资金空头”（funding shorts）将港股作为套利工具，为配置韩日等市场融资，这体现了金融资本全球流动中对区域市场的工具化利用；另一方面，内地政策扩容南向通及央行外储增配港股，实则通过制度性安排引导资本回流，试图稳定本土金融话语权。这种资本流动背后，是金融资产阶级内部（国际投行与本土政策资本）对资本积累主导权的争夺。

4. 阶级/利益分析
受益者：一是科技垄断资本（如AI、半导体企业），通过资本市场波动实现股权价值重估与套现；二是金融中介与投行，通过做空回补、套利交易获取手续费与价差收益；三是政策资本（如央行体系），通过制度性资本配置强化对香港金融市场的控制权。
受损者：一是中小投资者，其财富随虚拟资本波动被侵蚀；二是实体产业部门，尤其是文中提及的“生产商价格指数上涨挤压工厂利润”，反映产业资本在通胀与金融化挤压下的生存困境；三是全球无产阶级，其工资与社会保障被通胀进一步侵蚀。
权力流动体现为：金融资本通过定价权与流动性配置，实质性地影响生产资源分配，甚至绑架宏观经济政策。

5. 主流叙事批判
主流媒体（如文章引用分析师言论）将波动归因于“技术性反弹”“情绪修复”，掩盖了三个实质矛盾：其一，将金融波动简化为市场自发调节，掩盖了虚拟资本积累制度本身内嵌的危机倾向；其二，强调“政策利好”，却未触及资本分配中阶级权力的不对等——政策红利更多流向金融资产持有者而非劳动者；其三，回避地缘冲突背后的帝国主义资源争夺逻辑，将其简化为“市场情绪扰动”。马克思在《资本论》第一卷中揭示的资本主义生产关系的隐蔽性，在此处体现为金融话语对剥削关系的彻底遮蔽。

6. 解放前景
从政治经济学视角看，出路需直面三个层面：
第一，限制虚拟资本自我增殖的狂奔：如对金融交易征税、遏制高频套利，使资本重新锚定实体价值创造——正如马克思所言，工场手工业分工“发展了新的社会劳动生产力”，未来需构建以社会需求为导向、劳动者参与民主管理的生产组织形式。
第二，打破金融资本的全球霸权循环：区域资本市场应逐步摆脱对美元体系与投机性资本的依赖，通过多边结算机制与生产性投资，重塑“资本服务于生产”的关系。
第三，重建分配正义：当前“生产商价格指数上涨挤压利润”的危机，本质是剩余价值在产业资本与金融资本间分配失衡。需通过劳资集体谈判、超额利润税等工具，将积累的财富导向社会保障与公共生产。

虚拟资本的每一次剧烈波动，都是资本主义基本矛盾——生产的社会化与资本的私人占有——在金融领域的尖锐投射。唯有将金融权力纳入民主监督与社会规划，方能打破“虚拟资本暴增而现实财富匮乏”的困局，走向“每个人的自由发展是一切人的自由发展的条件”的生产关系。

（字数：758）

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Philippines looks to space for bird’s-eye view of South China Sea threats
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1051

AI Summary:

* The Philippines aims to establish a military space centre by 2028 to enhance surveillance, communications, and command capabilities, particularly for monitoring the South China Sea. * Analysts cite significant hurdles, including the need for satellite platforms, ground stations, secure data systems, and trained personnel ("humanware"), as major constraints on the ambition. * A sovereign space capability is viewed as strategically valuable for providing persistent surveillance and could be integrated into the military's "kill chain" for targeting, increasing operational flexibility. * The military is considering two main paths: developing its own satellites, which requires greater investment and expertise, or partnering with allies and commercial entities to use existing systems. * The initiative is part of a broader military modernization drive that includes coastal missile batteries, radars, drones, and secure networks, potentially underpinning a more integrated targeting system.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
The Philippines is preparing to create a military space centre by 2028, a move that could help Manila strengthen surveillance, communications and command across its archipelago and in contested areas of the South China Sea.

But analysts said the ambition would be constrained by the technical, financial and personnel hurdles of turning space assets into military capability.

Philippine military chief General Romeo Brawner Jnr said on Tuesday that the initiative would enhance communications, command and control, missile systems and drone operations, laying the groundwork for a future space command.

The proposal was presented to President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr at the military’s midyear command conference, following the president’s directive in December for the armed forces to develop their own satellite capability.

“It will be a centre. We need to start small first, and later on, we will expand into a space command or space force,” Brawner told reporters, adding that the plan envisaged the use of military satellites.

A space command would give the military a sovereign platform for persistent surveillance not only of the South China Sea but across the entire Philippine archipelago, said Rommel Jude Ong, a retired Philippine Navy rear admiral who now lectures at the Ateneo School of Government.

“A notional space capability requires a satellite platform, a ground station to receive data, a processing centre and a secure distribution system to the end users. But more important is the ‘humanware’. It takes time to train people to operate such systems efficiently,” Ong said.

While he said defence cooperation arrangements with partners remained strong, sovereign capability would grant Philippine security forces greater operational flexibility.

“That would be particularly valuable if the space programme were integrated into the [military’s] kill chain,” he said, referring to the systems used to find, track and, if needed, strike a target.

Manila has not publicly outlined a formal kill-chain doctrine comparable to those of the United States or South Korea. But analysts say its modernisation drive – including coastal missile batteries, long-range radars, drones, maritime patrol aircraft, secure command-and-control networks and the proposed space command – could underpin a more integrated targeting and response system.

Experts said the 2028 target was achievable provided the project’s scope stayed modest and Manila pursued inter-agency cooperation alongside partnerships with countries that had established commercial space capabilities.

Given the timeline, the military has two broad options: launch satellites of its own or contract the use of allied or commercial systems, according to Vincent Kyle Parada, a former defence analyst for the Philippine Navy.

“Both options have their trade-offs. Launching satellites would require a greater initial investment, both in terms of resources as well as the expertise required to develop and maintain them,” Parada said.

“Using existing satellites may fast track space capability development in the short term, but the level of dependency that entails may lead to issues of availability in the long run.”

Parada doubted the military would establish a dedicated “space force” akin to those of the US or China. Instead, he said the proposed centre would likely sit under one of the three major service branches before eventually being upgraded into a joint space command.

“The question of whether it can acquire satellites by 2028 aside, establishing a space centre in anticipation of that capability is something that makes sense,” he said.

Brawner said the military was working with the Philippine Space Agency (PhilSA) on the initiative. “We might start with orbiting satellites. These will all be interconnected,” he said.

PhilSA has primarily operated low-Earth-orbit satellites such as Diwata and Maya, and already runs geostationary satellites, which orbit roughly 36,000km (22,400 miles) above Earth and remain fixed over a single location to provide continuous communications and surveillance.

In 2022, the Department of National Defence and PhilSA signed a memorandum of understanding to strengthen national security through cooperation on satellite applications, intelligence sharing, research and training.

The military needed a timely, accurate picture of its operating environment, particularly in the South China Sea, Parada said.

The Philippine military already has access to intelligence-sharing initiatives such as Canada’s satellite-based dark-vessel detection system, along with its own monitoring stations, but it still relies heavily on manned surveillance platforms.

“Domain awareness is a central component of any defence or deterrence strategy in the South China Sea,” Parada said. “Having dedicated satellites would significantly increase its ISR [intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance] capabilities and, crucially, strengthen command and control over its units to ensure operational effectiveness.”

A space command would give the Philippines a sovereign capability resilient to foreign political influence, said Justin Kyle Malarayap, a defence analyst and visiting assistant professor at Arellano University in Manila. It would also improve military communications and interoperability, including in remote areas where signals are often degraded or disrupted by terrain.

“For any meaningful military space capabilities to be developed by the Philippines, it will need the collaboration of its more technically experienced allies such as the US, Japan and Australia,” he said.

“In the long term, the Philippines can opt to network its satellite with other countries. The proposed space command may model this arrangement through Nato’s Alliance Persistent Surveillance from Space, which incorporates both allied and commercial space surveillance data.”

Chris Gardiner, CEO of the Institute for Regional Security, said the wars in Ukraine and Iran had highlighted the importance of space for command and control and operational advantage.

He said Ukraine’s experience showed the need for greater self-reliance to avoid being left at the mercy of another country, or company, switching off a system or denying access at a crucial moment.

“The reality in the short term is that the Philippines, like Australia, will need to rely on the US in the space domain. The Philippines, collaborating with Australia and Japan, however, should be exploring increased investment in independent satellite systems and in shared space-based ISR,” Gardiner said.

For Brawner, the military must prepare for future conflicts rather than wait for threats to emerge, as modern warfare is increasingly defined by drones, missiles, cyber capabilities and space technologies.

“The most important thing is really to look at the situation and plan for the future. If you start acting once the threat is there, it’s already too late,” he said.

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

item.title: [SCMP] In Malaysia’s Johor, Chinese voters prize affordability over politics
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1106

AI Summary:

* Chinese voters in Johor are primarily concerned with high living costs and housing affordability, viewing these local economic issues as more critical than broader geopolitical narratives around Chinese investment. * The election features a key contest for the Chinese vote between the Democratic Action Party (DAP) and the Malaysian Chinese Association (MCA), with DAP aiming to defend its urban seats. * Major recent Chinese-linked investments include ByteDance's RM1.5 billion data centre and Rianlon's RM1.27 billion base, part of Johor's total RM110 billion in approved investments for the previous year. * Demographically, Johor's population is about 60% Bumiputera, 33% Chinese, and 6.6% Indian, and its election has drawn 172 candidates for 56 seats. * The political perception of large Chinese-funded projects like Forest City has shifted; they are no longer the dominant electoral issue, with voter focus now on whether local economies benefit residents.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Koo can see Johor changing around him, even if the promised wealth has not reached his bank account.

The 30-year-old service worker has lived for 25 years in Skudai, a busy Johor Bahru suburb anchored by universities, older housing estates and the kind of Chinese shoplot economy that helped make the Democratic Action Party (DAP) a force in Malaysia’s southern state.

He sees cranes, rail works and projects built by Chinese companies. But before Saturday’s state election, and as the China-linked boom loses its political shine, the question nagging at him is more prosaic than geopolitics: whether Johor can still offer a decent living to Malaysians who do not want to chase the Singapore dollar just next door.

“My primary concern is skyrocketing housing prices and living costs,” Koo, who asked to be identified only by his surname, told This Week in Asia. “Parties need to prove that the economy in Johor can support those who work in Johor, instead of forcing them to go into Singapore to make a living.”

That unease cuts into one of the most closely watched fights in Johor’s election: the Chinese vote.

DAP, the Chinese-majority party in Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s Pakatan Harapan (PH) coalition, is trying to defend its urban base against Barisan Nasional’s (BN) Malaysian Chinese Association (MCA), BN’s long-time Chinese partner, which is leaning on its Johor business and state links to regain ground.

Mainland Chinese buyers and China-linked investment still carry political meaning in Johor, analysts say, especially in places shaped by the Iskandar development corridor. But among local Chinese voters, the argument has moved from questioning whether China money should come in, to whether locals can afford the boom it helps create.

Recent China-linked investments in Johor include TikTok parent ByteDance’s 1.5 billion ringgit (US$368 million) data centre expansion in Kulai and Tianjin-based Rianlon Corporation’s 1.27 billion ringgit research and development and manufacturing base.

Last year, Johor drew 110 billion ringgit in total approved investments.

Johor, home to just over 4 million people, is Malaysia’s southern gateway to Singapore. Bumiputera, Malaysia’s official category for Malays and indigenous groups, make up about 60 per cent of the population, with Chinese nearly 33 per cent and Indians 6.6 per cent. Its 56-seat election has drawn 172 candidates.

DAP Secretary General Anthony Loke told This Week in Asia on Monday that his party’s minimum target was to defend the 10 seats it won in 2022, even as it contests 17 this time.

Forest City, the Country Garden-backed mega development on reclaimed islands off Johor’s coast near Singapore, was once the perfect political villain: vast, China-funded and widely cast by critics as a luxury enclave built for outsiders rather than Malaysians.

In the 2018 general election, it fed the narrative that BN, under jailed former prime minister Najib Razak, had allowed too much foreign money to shape Malaysia’s future.

That anger has not disappeared, but it has lost its old potency. Forest City has fewer sharp edges now, while the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ), data centres, manufacturing and logistics have made Chinese and Singapore-linked capital part of the growth pitch for both BN and PH.

Francis E. Hutchinson, principal fellow at the ISEAS – Yusof Ishak Institute, said mainland Chinese presence was “not politically salient” in this campaign because cost of living was dominating the race, while both the Johor government and Anwar’s federal administration supported new investment into sectors with some China participation.

Samuel Tan, founder of property consultancy Olive Tree, said Forest City “does not appear to be the electoral flashpoint it was in 2018”, when it became a lightning rod for attacks on Umno and BN over claims of selling out to foreigners.

Tan said the backlash then was sharper among Malay voters than their Chinese counterparts.

Among Chinese voters now, he said, the mood was more pragmatic, with cost of living, jobs and business opportunities weighing more heavily than party loyalty or old arguments over Forest City.

“Forest City is now more of a governance or economic-delivery symbol, tied to JS-SEZ credibility, than an ethnic wedge issue,” Tan said.

Forest City’s early buyer base, along with newer arrivals, tenants and investors from China, does not form an electoral bloc because most are not Malaysian voters. But their presence still colours how voters read Johor’s rapid transformation.

Peter T.C. Chang, former deputy director of Universiti Malaya’s Institute of China Studies, said recent mainland Chinese migrants mattered politically “more as a symbolic presence than as a direct electoral constituency”.

“They have become a proxy for wider debates about China-linked investment, housing affordability, local employment and Johor’s rapid transformation,” Chang said.

Voters are now weighing grand projects against their own household maths.

In its Johor election manifesto, PH has promised 250,000 high-paying jobs, a 30 per cent rise in median wages, 80,000 affordable homes and a Balik Johor programme to bring home professionals working in Singapore or abroad by 2031. BN countered with a plan to create 200,000 quality jobs and expand Johor’s economy to 260 billion ringgit by 2030.

Malaysia and Singapore last year signed the JS-SEZ agreement, targeting 50 projects and 20,000 skilled jobs in the first five years, along with smoother movement across one of the world’s busiest borders.

Loke, who is also transport minister, said the SEZ, RTS Link and border improvements depended heavily on federal ministries, making PH’s presence in Johor crucial.

“We have been losing a lot to Singapore,” he said, naming manufacturing, services, shipping and bunkering as sectors that Malaysia could have held more strongly.

“Instead of our people crossing the border every day, they can still work in Johor, but at the same time with better-paying jobs.”

MCA could still lean on its old image as the business-minded Chinese party in Johor. But Chang said mainland Chinese investors were a “double-edged sword” for the party, especially when projects such as Forest City were seen as helping foreign investors more than local communities.

Chin Yee Mun, director of Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman’s Tun Tan Cheng Lock Institute of Social Studies, said Chinese support for DAP had become “more conditional”, shaped by high expectations after years of reform slogans and disappointment when delivery fell short.

“Main issues remain bread-and-butter concerns, governance and equal treatment,” Chin said.

Voters like Koo are still unconvinced. “Salary in Johor is definitely not as high as Kuala Lumpur,” he said, “but the living cost in Johor Bahru is as high as, if not higher than, KL.”

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

金融表象与资本实质：香港股市波动的政治经济学透视

1. 现象描述
香港股市受区域科技股波动、中东地缘冲突及内地政策调整影响，呈现震荡分化格局，科技板块逆势上涨，但整体市场承压。

2. 政治经济学定位
从资本运动维度看，香港股市的波动是虚拟资本与实体资本脱钩的典型表现。股票价格（如智谱AI、中芯国际的暴涨暴跌）并非由现实生产过程中的价值创造直接决定，而是受资本化预期、流动性套利与地缘风险溢价驱动。马克思在《资本论》第三卷中指出：“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 此处科技股的剧烈波动，本质上是金融资本对剩余价值预期进行贴现与再定价的过程，而非对实体生产力的直接反映。

3. 深层结构分析
表面上，市场波动源于地缘冲突或政策调整，但深层是全球资本循环中不同利益集团的博弈。一方面，国际金融资本通过“资金空头”（funding shorts）将港股作为套利工具，为配置韩日等市场融资，这体现了金融资本全球流动中对区域市场的工具化利用；另一方面，内地政策扩容南向通及央行外储增配港股，实则通过制度性安排引导资本回流，试图稳定本土金融话语权。这种资本流动背后，是金融资产阶级内部（国际投行与本土政策资本）对资本积累主导权的争夺。

4. 阶级/利益分析
受益者：一是科技垄断资本（如AI、半导体企业），通过资本市场波动实现股权价值重估与套现；二是金融中介与投行，通过做空回补、套利交易获取手续费与价差收益；三是政策资本（如央行体系），通过制度性资本配置强化对香港金融市场的控制权。
受损者：一是中小投资者，其财富随虚拟资本波动被侵蚀；二是实体产业部门，尤其是文中提及的“生产商价格指数上涨挤压工厂利润”，反映产业资本在通胀与金融化挤压下的生存困境；三是全球无产阶级，其工资与社会保障被通胀进一步侵蚀。
权力流动体现为：金融资本通过定价权与流动性配置，实质性地影响生产资源分配，甚至绑架宏观经济政策。

5. 主流叙事批判
主流媒体（如文章引用分析师言论）将波动归因于“技术性反弹”“情绪修复”，掩盖了三个实质矛盾：其一，将金融波动简化为市场自发调节，掩盖了虚拟资本积累制度本身内嵌的危机倾向；其二，强调“政策利好”，却未触及资本分配中阶级权力的不对等——政策红利更多流向金融资产持有者而非劳动者；其三，回避地缘冲突背后的帝国主义资源争夺逻辑，将其简化为“市场情绪扰动”。马克思在《资本论》第一卷中揭示的资本主义生产关系的隐蔽性，在此处体现为金融话语对剥削关系的彻底遮蔽。

6. 解放前景
从政治经济学视角看，出路需直面三个层面：
第一，限制虚拟资本自我增殖的狂奔：如对金融交易征税、遏制高频套利，使资本重新锚定实体价值创造——正如马克思所言，工场手工业分工“发展了新的社会劳动生产力”，未来需构建以社会需求为导向、劳动者参与民主管理的生产组织形式。
第二，打破金融资本的全球霸权循环：区域资本市场应逐步摆脱对美元体系与投机性资本的依赖，通过多边结算机制与生产性投资，重塑“资本服务于生产”的关系。
第三，重建分配正义：当前“生产商价格指数上涨挤压利润”的危机，本质是剩余价值在产业资本与金融资本间分配失衡。需通过劳资集体谈判、超额利润税等工具，将积累的财富导向社会保障与公共生产。

虚拟资本的每一次剧烈波动，都是资本主义基本矛盾——生产的社会化与资本的私人占有——在金融领域的尖锐投射。唯有将金融权力纳入民主监督与社会规划，方能打破“虚拟资本暴增而现实财富匮乏”的困局，走向“每个人的自由发展是一切人的自由发展的条件”的生产关系。

（字数：758）

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] US opens door for Turkey’s return to F-35 stealth jet programme – but Israel’s not happy
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1038

AI Summary:

- The US plans to lift sanctions on Turkey imposed over its purchase of Russian S-400 missile systems, signaling a potential reset in defense relations. - A possible deal involves Turkey transferring its S-400 systems to a third country in exchange for being readmitted to the F-35 stealth fighter program. - While President Trump can waive sanctions, Congress must still approve Turkey's return to the program, which is not yet secured. - Israel strongly opposes the move, with Prime Minister Netanyahu arguing it could undermine Israel's technological and military superiority in the Middle East. - Analysts describe the development as a "strategic reset" that strengthens Turkey's position as an indispensable NATO ally for the US.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
The United States is set to resume military sales to Turkey, Nato’s second-largest military power, after President Donald Trump said sanctions imposed against Ankara over its procurement of Russian air defence systems seven years ago would soon be lifted.

“We don’t want to sanction friends,” Trump told journalists at the Nato summit in the Turkish capital on Wednesday, while emphasising his “good chemistry” with President Recep Tayyip Erdogan.

Trump further said he would soon take a final decision on restoring Turkey’s participation in the Joint Strike Fighter (JSF) programme – raising expectations among observers of a “face-saving” deal in which Ankara would transfer its Russian S-400 systems to a third country in return for US F-35 stealth warplanes.

Turkey was ejected from the F-35 programme – for which it was manufacturing some components – in 2019 over US concerns that the cutting-edge jet’s unrivalled stealth capabilities could be compromised by its exposure to Russian technology.

The understanding reached between Washington and Ankara “is a strategic reset in spirit but not yet in practical terms”, said Asli Aydintasbas, director of the Turkey project at the Brookings Institution in Washington. “Clearly, President Trump values his relationship with Erdogan and wants Turkey to be a central player in his Nato, Europe and Middle East policies.”

After years of turmoil, “this is the closest Erdogan has come to the reset with Washington he has been seeking” – but it is “not full normalisation yet”, she said. “The congressional part of the equation is still not there.”

While Trump can use his executive power to waive sanctions imposed on Turkey’s defence production industry under the Countering America’s Adversaries Through Sanctions Act (Caatsa), Congress must be convinced that the threat posed by the Russian S-400s has been removed before its access to the JSF can be reinstated.

For Erdogan, the lifting of Caatsa sanctions, movement on engines for the Kaan fighter, and the prospect of returning to the F-35 conversation “all signal that Turkey is being brought back into the Western defence-industrial fold after years in the cold”, Aydintasbas said. “It strengthens his argument that Turkey is an indispensable ally with a direct line to Trump.”

Nonetheless, Turkey’s acquisition of the F-35 is bound to face fierce opposition from Israel’s many supporters in Congress.

In the build-up to the Nato summit, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu made multiple appearances on US television to voice his opposition to the lifting of sanctions and the F-35 sale to Turkey.

With Trump saying he had “an obligation” to supply Turkey with up to 80 US-built F110 engines for its indigenous Kaan stealth fighters, Netanyahu complained that Israel’s air force could lose its technological superiority over rivals in the Middle East.

“Everybody understands that notwithstanding the personal friendship that President Trump has with Erdogan, it doesn’t make Turkey a friendly state to the United States,” Netanyahu said on CNN on Tuesday. “To the contrary … he threatens to destroy my country!”

Washington is legally bound, under the Naval Vessel Transfer Act of 2008, to ensure that any arms sale in the Middle East does not erode Israel’s military edge over its neighbours.

The US president responded to Netanyahu’s claims in Wednesday’s press conference, saying: “I hear some people saying about their relationship with Israel.”

If Turkey had wanted to clash with Israel, it “could have gotten into the fight on the other side”, Trump said, describing the Nato member as ‘a very powerful military nation”.

“Maybe they didn’t do that because of me,” he added.

At no point during the Middle East’s conflicts since October 2023 has war ever appeared likely between the two countries, despite exchanges of harsh rhetoric.

Israel is the “biggest impediment to a reset” of defence cooperation between US and Turkey, according to Aydintasbas.

The Israeli government “seems determined to use its access to Trump and US Congress” to prevent Turkey’s return to the F-35 programme, she said. “Israel wants to preserve its own military edge in the region and views Turkey as its top regional competitor.”

Both countries are locked in a rivalry for regional influence and view each other as expansionist.

“The larger issue is political and strategic,” she said, with Israel increasingly viewing Turkey not just as a critic of the Gaza war or its occupation of southwest Syria, but as a long-term regional competitor.

Turkey, for its part, sees Israel as “becoming too dominant after the Iran war and wants to prevent a regional order shaped entirely by Israeli military power”, Aydintasbas said.

However, former Pentagon official David Des Roches said Israel “is not the important issue” in determining Turkey’s participation in the JSF programme.

“The resistance will come from within the US government. It is very, very, very protective of the multi-hundred-billion-dollar investment we’ve made in stealth,” said Des Roches, a senior fellow at the Washington-based Gulf International Forum.

Having agreed last year to sell F-35s to Saudi Arabia, Trump also wanted to offer the JSF to the United Arab Emirates, but “he can’t do that because of our requirements to protect stealth”.

Similarly, Washington does not share Israel’s view that the acquisition of fifth-generation fighters by Turkey and other US partners in the region poses a threat.

“The American view is that if a region is able to defend itself, the US does not have to,” Des Roches said. “We do not regard any of our partners as countries that spread instability. We regard them as countries that enforce stability, that benefit from the rules-based order, and that benefit from free trade.”

Trump’s decision to bring Turkey back in from the cold was motivated by the need for Ankara’s cooperation against Russia in the Black Sea, and against Iran and its proxies in Syria and Iraq, according to Des Roches.

“Right now, Iran is the biggest issue in the region, and whatever beef the US has with Turkey is much, much smaller,” Des Roches said. All the same, Turkey would “have to get rid of the S-400s if it’s going to get the JSF”.
