item.title: [SCMP] 80 years on from WWII, China’s interests lie in peaceful Asia-Pacific: Rana Mitter
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 4368

AI Summary:

* World War II is identified as a fundamental, yet often underestimated, factor in shaping modern China due to the massive political and economic trauma caused by Japan's invasion, which included millions of deaths and destroyed infrastructure. * The war's devastating conditions created a revolutionary environment, enabling the Chinese Communist Party to develop its revolution in the countryside, rather than a mere reform movement. * Many structures and aspects of later socialist China, such as the work unit system and increased social roles for women, emerged during the war period. * The war contributed significantly to the political unification of China by eliminating rival warlord factions, a process later solidified by the 1949 revolution. * The analysis suggests that China currently struggles to leverage diplomatic opportunities like commemorations, often defaulting to a narrative of complaint rather than focusing on concrete, positive engagement.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Rana Mitter is a leading historian whose research focuses on the impact of Japan’s invasion of China during the second world war on the development of Chinese politics, society and culture. He is S.T. Lee Chair in US-Asia Relations at the Harvard Kennedy School and previously taught at Oxford University on the politics of modern China. He was also director of Oxford’s China Centre.

This interview first appeared in SCMP Plus. For other interviews in the Open Questions series, click here.

As China prepares to commemorate the 80th anniversary of the end of World War II, what are your thoughts on the role of the conflict in shaping contemporary China?

World War II is one of the most important factors in the shaping of modern China, but it’s often underestimated, both in China and the West.

This was the greatest political and economic trauma of the 20th century. The invasion of China by Japan led to many millions of deaths. Many Chinese became refugees within their own country.

The infrastructure and the communications built in the 1920s and 1930s were largely smashed into pieces, so that was a huge influence in terms of having to start again after the war.

The war made it more likely that China would have a revolution rather than just reform after it ended, because economic and social conditions became so devastatingly bad that it opened up space for the Chinese Communist Party to really develop the revolution in the countryside.

Beyond that, many aspects of modern China that we think of as part of the revolution actually emerged during the war – such as the system of work units for example, that existed for a long time in socialist China under Mao Zedong and even beyond.

The role of women became more prominent in Chinese life during the war years. Many younger women also became involved in national defence, health and nutrition, trying to make society after the war better for families and more stable.

During the 1920s and 1930s, China was heavily split between rival military leaders. At the end of the war, China was much closer to becoming unified as a country, as many of those warlords were eliminated during the wartime period.

Although it would finally be the 1949 revolution under Mao that provided a solid unification of the country, the process began in the wartime years.

World War II continues to be very important in terms of remembering a time when different factions and parties, nationalists, communists and others came together against an outside enemy and were able to find a sense of national purpose.

How could Beijing use the 80th World War II anniversary commemoration as an opportunity to improve people-to-people exchanges with the West?

The problem is that China still isn’t very good at seizing diplomatic opportunities because it tends to fall back on language that is mainly about China’s complaints, rather than about opportunities that could really open up.

The opportunities tend to be expressed in very broad, positive but vague terms, and the complaints tend to be very specific. And the problem is, it’s quite hard to get people on your side if you don’t actually engage in a dialogue that seems as if both sides are genuinely talking to each other.

An example is cutting down the EU-China summit from two days to one. Beijing also insisted that the Europeans should come to Beijing, when actually it was China’s turn to come to Europe.

So Europeans have done these things – they’re diplomatic people. They say OK, but it’s not the easiest start in terms of suggesting that China is opening up a new conversation, where China is willing to actually be expansive and creative in its thinking.

So maybe a sort of reset of Chinese diplomacy is an opportunity to do that. So far, there aren’t many signs of that. China has, in the past couple of years, withdrawn from the famous Wolf Warrior diplomacy, but it hasn’t yet worked out what sits in its place.

Do you think Chinese officials understand how their diplomacy is viewed from an outside perspective?

Very smart Chinese diplomats are aware that China has a PR problem. Just because people now find the US much less predictable doesn’t mean that they think China is a safe bet.

So, finding ways to essentially define China – not just in words, but in actions and in patterns of behaviour – as a reliable partner, that’s the really key challenge for Chinese diplomacy in the next few years.

The language is sometimes there – not always, but sometimes – but the actions involve a lot of quite difficult and long-term effort. And so far, if that’s happening, it’s only at a very early stage of that process.

A lot of Chinese diplomats, publicly and privately, have said they thought the previous Wolf Warrior effort was a mistake, asking what comes next and how China actually takes advantage of the changing world order.

That will involve more than just hoping that people will feel alienated from America and automatically seek out China. They won’t. Well, that will not be enough on its own.

World War II ended in the Pacific theatre with two atomic bombs dropped on Japan. Now, 80 years later, Britain and France have announced joint cooperation on nuclear weapons and the US has once again deployed nuclear forces in the UK. What does this say about the future of nuclear weapons?

Nuclear non-proliferation is definitely much weaker than it has been. Many of the Cold War-era treaties between the Soviet Union and the US seem to be essentially falling away. Britain and France are primarily concerned with their own major security vulnerability as the future American position on Russia remains uncertain.

A lot of different actors are looking at new threats in the world and trying to work out whether their current nuclear stance is sustainable. Ukraine gave up nuclear weapons in 1994, which made it vulnerable to the Russian invasion. Many countries will be looking at Ukraine’s experience and trying to avoid that.

The US has engaged directly with Russia to end the Ukraine war but those warmer ties now seem to have cooled off. What is your view of the changing dynamic between them?

Right now, the person who is probably having to think most carefully about changing the US position is Russian President Vladimir Putin.

For much of the first part of the year, the Donald Trump administration tried to find ways to bring Moscow back into the negotiations about Ukraine.

But in the past few weeks, it’s become clear that President Trump thinks Putin has no intention of coming to a peace agreement. It’s possible that he may take a much more confrontational attitude with Russia.

The world was very surprised when Trump actually made the decision to bomb Iran. His general doctrine is that getting into wars is a bad idea, but when he considers it necessary he is prepared to use force – and that’s something Putin might now have to think about.

How could this changing dynamic in Russia-US ties affect China?

Maybe several months ago, there were some people in Washington who thought that it might be possible to detach Russia from China in a “reverse Nixon”, or “reverse Kissinger” play, but that is not a very widely held idea any more, for two reasons.

First, people realise that there is a very close relationship between Russia and China. This was made clear by Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi when meeting his European Union counterparts. For the first time, it was much more explicit that China would not wish to see Russia lose.

The other factor is the American idea that they could detach Russia from China and bring it close to the US has fallen away. People have seen that Putin has not changed his policy towards Ukraine at all and is bombing it more fiercely than before.

We’re likely to see a scenario in which the US tries to neutralise Russia as much as possible while still looking for a negotiation on Ukraine, but essentially there probably will be more concentration on pushing back against China alone.

What conditions could bring about the end of the war?

We don’t know what conditions it will take to come to an end, but we do know that, at least in the past few weeks, President Trump has reauthorised the supply of weaponry to Ukraine to fight back against Russia, which at one point he was holding back on.

Now it’s clear that weaponry is going to Ukraine and the war may last longer than would have seemed the case just a few weeks ago, when it seemed Ukraine might run out of ammunition.

The question becomes whether Russia can maintain its all-out war-based economy and conscription of soldiers, while keeping inflation – which is very, very high – under control.

The longer the Ukraine war continues, the less likely it is that Russia can do that, and the more weapons that are supplied by the Americans, along with the Europeans, the longer the Ukrainians can keep fighting.

Under former president Joe Biden, Washington and other Western capitals formed a united stance on a wide range of China-related issues. How will Trump’s presidency change that alliance on China?

Europeans are looking to negotiate on their own issues with the US, such as tariffs. The question of China has been put a bit on the back shelf but essentially, the issue is that China provides useful supply chains.

There is also a problem, in particular for Europe, of whether it can draw on China’s markets but not be overwhelmed by Chinese exports, particularly in areas like autos and electric batteries. Those problems are not really changed by the relationship with the US.

The EU has a whole variety of fundamental issues it needs to solve – about how it creates a new industrial model, where it cooperates with China and where it needs to restrict its market.

And in some ways, it’s learning from China, because China has heavily restricted its own market in some areas, such as online service provision. So the Europeans will also pick and choose where they feel vulnerable, opening up in certain areas and closing in others.

The Trump administration is still figuring out its policy on China. There are different strands of thinking that you can see from the outside, but how important they are in relation to each other, and how they will balance out in the end, is still unclear.

For instance, there are some traditional hawks, such as Secretary of State Marco Rubio and others, who take a view on China that in some ways would not have been that different from Republicans seven or 10 years ago.

But you also have the whole technology world – Silicon Valley and beyond. A lot of them are still very interested in making sure that the relationship with China is smooth.

Apple is one of the most profitable and innovative companies in the United States, but huge amounts of its production and connection still take place in China. Of course, some production has moved to India, but China is still really important to a company like Apple.

There has been a growing interest in moving more production and intellectual property back to the US but there’s no sense yet that this is a complete disconnection from China, and that points in a different direction from the hawks.

Then there is the question of what Trump himself thinks. It’s interesting to look at the tariff policy, because tariffs have been put on China but also on other countries, including traditional US allies.

Also, when Trump says “I like to talk to my good friend Xi Jinping. We can talk about things” – what does he want to talk about? Not clear.

Compared with other presidents, Trump seems to really like personal diplomacy. In other words, he believes that his personal relationships with leaders are the important thing.

Speaking of leadership diplomacy, what conditions do you think will be needed for Trump and Xi to meet?

I think a meeting between the two leaders is quite likely. Trump is announcing that the deal has been done with China. He would only want to go to China if the deal is kind of there, so he can mark it in a big ceremony and have a prominent meeting with Xi.

I don’t think he would want to go to China to negotiate. We’re moving into a phase where the US wants to get most of its tariff deals done within the next couple of months.

During his first term, Trump had a very grand welcoming ceremony provided by Xi in Beijing. I think he would like to do that again – a big, grand meeting that Trump will think of as a historic event, that showcases he’s able to do negotiations and deals with China in a way that other presidents haven’t been able to do. I think he would want to push quite hard in that direction. That’s my guess.

Are the two presidents likely to get along?

I think that’s perfectly possible. You might remember that when Trump invited Xi to Mar-a-Lago, he said that he gave Xi a big piece of chocolate cake. He never talked about giving chocolate cake to German chancellor Angela Merkel.

That’s a sign that he feels there’s some sort of ability to speak leader to leader in that sense. Xi has said less about Trump, but he hasn’t said any very publicly hostile things personally about him.

Will it stop competition? No. Will it stop conflict? Maybe. In some areas – I would single out technology – I think the US and China are going to be increasingly in competition and there will be less sharing of intellectual property or scientific development.

That would mean other countries could benefit from both, or they may have to choose a side. This will not change because of a meeting.

But can the two leaders talk about lowering the temperature and avoiding misunderstandings that could lead to military confrontation? That might be more possible.

Turning to China’s security environment, how might Beijing’s relations with its neighbours change, given the uncertainties of Washington’s policies during the Trump administration?

My guess is that China will be assessing how solid and secure American security guarantees are in Asia. There’s some evidence that the United States might want to maintain and maybe deepen its relationship with standing allies Japan, Australia and South Korea.

We know that Trump is interested in the Korean peninsula. In his first term, he wanted to do personal diplomacy with North Korean leader Kim Jong-un. But even when South Korea is going through political change and a lot of turmoil, there’s no sense that I’ve seen that the United States is stepping back from its relationship.

I would say that if you are looking from Beijing, the conclusion to draw at the moment is that things will change much less in Asia than in Europe.

Supposing American security guarantees, naval presence and alliances were withdrawn from Asia – the first thing that would be likely to happen is that Japan and South Korea would think hard about getting their own nuclear weapons. They have the capacity, science and the technology. They could do it, but they won’t do it at the moment.

in a strange way, Beijing’s best bet for keeping the region relatively nuclear-free is to encourage the Americans to stay. And most of its neighbours would share that analysis.

They want the Chinese market, but they also want American security. They don’t want either one to disappear. I think that’s what makes the calculation different in Asia and that will affect the way that China and the US deal with other countries in the region.

What about the South China Sea?

The South China Sea is probably one of the most potentially dangerous flashpoints, along with Taiwan.

The South China Sea has more of a chance of running out of control because it’s not just between two or three entities – seven or eight are involved. The US is not so directly involved in that.

But I would say that China is missing a big soft power opportunity, which it could take up at a time of uncertainty about American security intentions, of being able to provide reassurance to regional neighbours like the Philippines.

If China is not looking to increase its maritime and territorial claims, it could create a much better basis of trust in the region. But right now there’s much more military activity from China, not just near the Philippines but also off the coast of Australia, and that’s heightening tensions in the region.

How is the importance of Taiwan to Beijing shaping the security environment?

One of the most interesting things is that after repeatedly trying to take the island, Mao Zedong basically said to then-US president Richard Nixon, when they met in Beijing in 1972, that the Taiwan problem could be left to the side for 100 years.

That’s a reminder that actually the Taiwan question has not been a constant one at the forefront of China’s relationship with the world.

Right now, there are a whole variety of reasons that China needs to think about its changing place in the world. It has an opportunity to influence the world order at a time when the United States is changing its role.

It also has significant economic problems at home, in terms of creating new forms of employment, dealing with the way technology has changed labour markets, and stimulating domestic consumption, which the party has repeatedly said is a really important domestic priority.

In that context, you could say that with all those important problems, Taiwan doesn’t appear as a major solution or issue that will help to resolve any of them.

If you look at Chinese policy priorities, it’s very clear that the single most important task for the government is the economy.

China needs the Asia-Pacific region to be economically productive to get its economy going again. That means the attention has to be on the domestic economy and how it relates to a peaceful, calm Asia that does not have any sort of confrontation.

In five to 10 years, if current trends continue, the Asia-Pacific region could be even more of a driver of the global economy than it is now. It can become the most economically dynamic region in the world, with lots of foreign direct investment and expansion.

However, for that vision of Asia to emerge, the region needs to stay peaceful and stable and absolutely avoid wars. Any regional confrontations could put that economic future in danger and that would be very bad for China and its neighbours.

Alongside the nationalism that comes with the promise of building a powerful China, there is also a revival of traditional Chinese culture in both society and the official narrative. How do you think this will shape China’s governance and foreign policy?

This is one of the most interesting and important elements of what’s going on in thought and ideology in China today.

First of all, there is a real interest in what some people call traditional thought, or pre-modern thought, which includes the Confucian tradition but also lots of other areas, such as Taoism.

The Chinese government has been sponsoring publication of China’s canon of official writings, with the parts that suit contemporary politics tending to be stressed.

For instance, Confucian thinking does have an interest in proper hierarchy and this is something that is obviously very attractive to the Communist Party, because it wants people, essentially, to be stable and ordered.

But there are other ideas – such as individual self-development or self-cultivation, which could lead more to the idea that people should have individual rights – that are less popular with the party.

China’s political culture since 1949 has been heavily shaped by the Marxism and Leninism that underpins the Chinese Communist Party.

Many of the ideas are still there and you can hear them in the language of Xi and other top leaders. Even while they are praising traditional Chinese culture and wisdom, they are also pointing out that Marxism and Leninism still have significance.

How are these two things compatible? Actually, it’s not easy.

There was a TV show broadcast in Hunan province that got a lot of criticism on Chinese social media. It was called When Marx Met Confucius and was a way of trying to talk about the difficulties in combining these ideas.

There is actually quite an interesting and difficult tension in the way that these two thought systems operate in China today, but I think that they are both very much part of the debate.

How has the Chinese intellectual landscape changed in the past 10 years? Do you share the view that it can be summarised as a rise in nationalism, Sinification of ideas and declining critical voices? What other trends are you seeing and what are their effects?

In some ways, China’s intellectual sphere has narrowed, at least in public, but in other important ways, it has expanded.

The narrowing is real, and probably the biggest obstacle that China places in its own path to developing new ideas at home and being able to exert influence abroad. It’s difficult to persuade the wider world that you’re a force for new thinking if new thinking at home is heavily restricted.

In terms of subjects, including history, most people would say that the room for publication and discussion is much more limited than it was 10, 15 or 20 years ago. Many aspects of modern history are harder to research and write about in China today than in the past.

The most productive recent time intellectually in China was the late 1990s and early 2000s, when there was the most capacity to be able to speak more freely. That time will need to come again as China comes to grips with its new role in the world. It’s not possible to have a very narrowed intellectual sphere and a very wide influence.

But there are some areas where I think things are broader, and I’m particularly thinking about social media and the way it has expanded and grown so significantly in China in the past decade.

Lots of ideas actually are discussed at a popular level, even when there’s censorship a lot of ideas still get out there – not about top-level politics as that’s too sensitive to permit – but if you think about things like concerns about economic change, people’s concerns about their own financial situation, ideas about the position of women, society, feminism, those are quite visible in Chinese social media.

That is not the same thing as two or three scholars sitting in a think tank writing some big paper, but in terms of understanding social trends and how they are shaping China today, social media has been very valuable to reflect those changes.

How has the China studies community been affected by the changes of recent years? What will the subject look like, with a growing percentage of scholars outside the country unable to conduct interviews and on-the-ground research in China?

You have put your finger on one of the most problematic issues – the number of students studying Chinese languages and doing higher-level research in the West on China is going down significantly. It was never very high, but it was going up, and now the numbers are shrinking.

One reason is the sense that doing serious research in China – particularly for social scientists but also for researchers who need to use archives and libraries – is much more difficult than it used to be.

What real China researchers need is to be able to travel and set up their own agendas, to be able to do interviews at the local level that enable them to understand how local government works, or to collect large amounts of data that enables them to see how effective or ineffective particular policies are.

This is the work of real social science, and it involves transparency and openness on data and a willingness to welcome the gathering of data, rather than assuming that it has to be banned for security reasons.

So, although more scholars are coming back to China, opening up more capacity to be able to do real research in China is the next barrier that needs to be overcome.

There are a lot of brilliant Chinese students in many countries in the West who are doing significant social science and humanities research. But their Western equivalents who study China can’t do that kind of work in China, so it becomes very unbalanced.

What about your own experience with the Asia studies and Chinese history students you taught at Oxford and now Harvard?

When it comes to postgraduate students doing PhDs, a significant number are Chinese. We do still have some Westerners, but the problem is that they have to be enthusiastic about China, willing to learn its language and culture, and then be able to find a meaningful project to research.

It’s harder now to assure students that they can find a pathway to doing really good social science research inside China, so a lot of students just leave China out. If people say China is a very difficult place to do research and South Korea is easy, maybe people just decide they want to learn Korean instead.

The sense among Western researchers that it’s hard to do serious research in China has transmitted to students finding it difficult to find a career path if they do study China. I think that’s a great shame.

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

金融表象与资本实质：香港股市波动的政治经济学透视

1. 现象描述
香港股市受区域科技股波动、中东地缘冲突及内地政策调整影响，呈现震荡分化格局，科技板块逆势上涨，但整体市场承压。

2. 政治经济学定位
从资本运动维度看，香港股市的波动是虚拟资本与实体资本脱钩的典型表现。股票价格（如智谱AI、中芯国际的暴涨暴跌）并非由现实生产过程中的价值创造直接决定，而是受资本化预期、流动性套利与地缘风险溢价驱动。马克思在《资本论》第三卷中指出：“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 此处科技股的剧烈波动，本质上是金融资本对剩余价值预期进行贴现与再定价的过程，而非对实体生产力的直接反映。

3. 深层结构分析
表面上，市场波动源于地缘冲突或政策调整，但深层是全球资本循环中不同利益集团的博弈。一方面，国际金融资本通过“资金空头”（funding shorts）将港股作为套利工具，为配置韩日等市场融资，这体现了金融资本全球流动中对区域市场的工具化利用；另一方面，内地政策扩容南向通及央行外储增配港股，实则通过制度性安排引导资本回流，试图稳定本土金融话语权。这种资本流动背后，是金融资产阶级内部（国际投行与本土政策资本）对资本积累主导权的争夺。

4. 阶级/利益分析
受益者：一是科技垄断资本（如AI、半导体企业），通过资本市场波动实现股权价值重估与套现；二是金融中介与投行，通过做空回补、套利交易获取手续费与价差收益；三是政策资本（如央行体系），通过制度性资本配置强化对香港金融市场的控制权。
受损者：一是中小投资者，其财富随虚拟资本波动被侵蚀；二是实体产业部门，尤其是文中提及的“生产商价格指数上涨挤压工厂利润”，反映产业资本在通胀与金融化挤压下的生存困境；三是全球无产阶级，其工资与社会保障被通胀进一步侵蚀。
权力流动体现为：金融资本通过定价权与流动性配置，实质性地影响生产资源分配，甚至绑架宏观经济政策。

5. 主流叙事批判
主流媒体（如文章引用分析师言论）将波动归因于“技术性反弹”“情绪修复”，掩盖了三个实质矛盾：其一，将金融波动简化为市场自发调节，掩盖了虚拟资本积累制度本身内嵌的危机倾向；其二，强调“政策利好”，却未触及资本分配中阶级权力的不对等——政策红利更多流向金融资产持有者而非劳动者；其三，回避地缘冲突背后的帝国主义资源争夺逻辑，将其简化为“市场情绪扰动”。马克思在《资本论》第一卷中揭示的资本主义生产关系的隐蔽性，在此处体现为金融话语对剥削关系的彻底遮蔽。

6. 解放前景
从政治经济学视角看，出路需直面三个层面：
第一，限制虚拟资本自我增殖的狂奔：如对金融交易征税、遏制高频套利，使资本重新锚定实体价值创造——正如马克思所言，工场手工业分工“发展了新的社会劳动生产力”，未来需构建以社会需求为导向、劳动者参与民主管理的生产组织形式。
第二，打破金融资本的全球霸权循环：区域资本市场应逐步摆脱对美元体系与投机性资本的依赖，通过多边结算机制与生产性投资，重塑“资本服务于生产”的关系。
第三，重建分配正义：当前“生产商价格指数上涨挤压利润”的危机，本质是剩余价值在产业资本与金融资本间分配失衡。需通过劳资集体谈判、超额利润税等工具，将积累的财富导向社会保障与公共生产。

虚拟资本的每一次剧烈波动，都是资本主义基本矛盾——生产的社会化与资本的私人占有——在金融领域的尖锐投射。唯有将金融权力纳入民主监督与社会规划，方能打破“虚拟资本暴增而现实财富匮乏”的困局，走向“每个人的自由发展是一切人的自由发展的条件”的生产关系。

（字数：758）

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Who cares for Singapore’s carers? Recent deaths highlight urgent need to tackle burnout
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1231

AI Summary:

* Recent high-profile cases, including a carer who killed his brother and another who killed his autistic sons, have highlighted severe carer burnout in Singapore. * Carers, often family members, face immense stress, isolation, and "invisible burdens" while managing complex care needs, especially with a rapidly ageing population. * The cases reflect a pattern where extreme distress from chronic stress may only surface at a crisis point due to a lack of timely support. * Social welfare experts urge early identification and improved accessibility of support systems as Singapore transitions to a "super-aged society."

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
When Karasi Chandramogan’s father died four years ago, the 35-year-old Singaporean was left with the overwhelming responsibility of being the sole carer for her brother, who has autism, and mother, an amputee.

“Me and my dad were a tag team,” the freelance behavioural therapist said. “When he passed on, I couldn’t mourn too much because I had to handle the funeral situation as well as my mum and brother.”

She recalled an incident in April, when her brother, Bala, suffered seizures during lunch. “It was very scary for me to handle because I was terrified that he would choke. So I try not to be away for too long, check the CCTV regularly, I’m always on the lookout,” she said.

The recent sentencing of a man who had been the long-term carer for his 56-year-old brother before strangling him to death last year hit close to home for Chandramogan and other carers in Singapore, where families are taking on increasingly complex care needs amid a rapidly ageing population.

Abdul Rani Md Ariffin, 59, had been looking after his brother, who suffered from chronic illnesses and depression. A psychiatric assessment produced in court revealed that Abdul Rani was diagnosed with adjustment disorder, a depressed mood state and irritability, as well as carer stress. He also had difficulties regulating and expressing emotions.

Initially charged with murder, Abdul Rani was sentenced to eight years in jail after he pleaded guilty to a reduced charge of culpable homicide not amounting to murder.

Karasi said such cases affected her greatly. “I think most carers would resonate with such tragic cases, because we know it can get very isolating, and they must have been stretched way beyond the point of burnout.”

She used to blame herself if Bala, who has a history of epilepsy, suffered seizures. “I would always think that it was because I didn’t take care of him properly,” she said. “It’s taken me a few years to remind myself that these things are not in my control.”

Cases where carer burnout leads to death are, unfortunately, uncommon, and highlight the immense toll and demands placed on them.

In 2022, a father killed his 11-year-old twin sons who were diagnosed with autism as he believed it would take away his wife’s burdens. He made an attempt on his own life after strangling his sons.

Such situations are often a result of stress, isolation and a lack of timely support, and highlight the “invisible burden” as carers navigate complex or long-term conditions, said Tim Oei, chief executive of Mindfull Community, a non-profit which supports carers.

“Many carers continue to cope quietly, and distress may only surface at a crisis point.”

The cases underscore the importance of carers’ well-being as the city state transitions to a super-aged society, according to social welfare agencies and experts. They urged early identification and support for carers, and noted that while Singapore had strengthened support in recent years, lack of accessibility and awareness posed challenges.

A 2022 study by the National Council of Social Service, which surveyed over 4,500 carers in Singapore, found that more than half of the respondents were “burdened” or “barely coping” with their caregiving responsibilities and felt they had less control over their lives.

Increased financial assistance, community care services and respite options demonstrated “growing recognition of the critical role” of carers, said Steven Yeo, executive director of Caregiving Welfare Association.

Under Singapore’s Home Caregiving Grant, eligible carers can receive monthly payouts of up to S$600 (US$465) – depending on per capita household income – which can be used to hire a helper or buy healthcare items. Carers can also apply for a grant which subsidises training courses.

Millie Su, a senior lecturer at the Singapore University of Social Sciences’ SR Nathan School of Human Development, said though help was available, many carers were not aware of the policies or found the services inaccessible, she said.

She pointed to the study, which revealed that fewer than three in 10 carers used support services.

Many had difficulties navigating paperwork and applications when applying for subsidies, grants and nursing homes, she said, urging the streamlining of such processes. “Some are being ping-ponged between different agencies, and making the system more integrated and connected will make care more accessible.”

Carers should be identified early for timely support to be given, she added. “As long as someone is taking care of someone who has a chronic condition, they are vulnerable, and we’ve been ignoring this because there’s so much focus on patient care.”

As Singapore becomes a super-aged society, carer support is even more critical, experts say. The city state crossed the threshold of being super-aged this year, with more than 21 per cent of its population now aged 65 or above.

“We are seeing more and more aged couples living on their own and looking after each other, both as frail as the other,” said Oei, urging holistic and sustained support, including expanded access to respite care, and strengthened coordination across healthcare and community services.

Healthcare professionals should also be trained to identify informal carers to provide relevant information, resources and support, she added.

Yeo urged employers to foster carer-friendly workplaces through flexible work arrangements and greater understanding. “Ultimately, caregiving should never rest solely on one individual’s shoulders. Preventing such situations requires a whole-of-society approach,” he said.

A decade ago, Edgar Tang had to step into the role as a carer for his parents after his father’s Parkinson’s disease worsened rapidly, leaving him wheelchair-bound.

“There were times I was breaking down due to work and these duties, and I felt like both ends of the candle were burning out on me,” said Tang, 50, whose mother is diagnosed with a mental condition.

He was eventually forced to move his father into a nursing home, a decision which tore him up with guilt. “It was really tough because I felt like such a horrible child,” he said. “How could I have put my father in a nursing home?”

Filial piety and a stigma associated with seeking help to care for family members – values prevalent in Asian societies such as Singapore – may also prevent carers from seeking timely support.

“We tend to not talk about it and think that caregiving is a family matter, a show of filial piety, a duty and a responsibility, and this cultural norm may prevent people from seeking help,” Su said.

Many didn’t recognise themselves as a carer in need of support. “They simply see themselves as a spouse, son, daughter or sibling doing what they should, and therefore delay seeking help until they are already overwhelmed,” Yeo said.

Tang managed to rationalise his emotions after attending a get-together session for carers organised by a welfare group. “I realised that many are feeling these emotions and it’s OK to feel this way,” he said.

His father also started to walk again in the nursing home – a development which Tang hailed as a “miracle”.

As Tang’s physical and mental health improved, he was able to be fully present for his father, allowing him to shed the guilt. “Now I’m not turning up exhausted. I’m present and we chat, walk, draw our memories, do some writing and listen to music,” he said.

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

金融表象与资本实质：香港股市波动的政治经济学透视

1. 现象描述
香港股市受区域科技股波动、中东地缘冲突及内地政策调整影响，呈现震荡分化格局，科技板块逆势上涨，但整体市场承压。

2. 政治经济学定位
从资本运动维度看，香港股市的波动是虚拟资本与实体资本脱钩的典型表现。股票价格（如智谱AI、中芯国际的暴涨暴跌）并非由现实生产过程中的价值创造直接决定，而是受资本化预期、流动性套利与地缘风险溢价驱动。马克思在《资本论》第三卷中指出：“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 此处科技股的剧烈波动，本质上是金融资本对剩余价值预期进行贴现与再定价的过程，而非对实体生产力的直接反映。

3. 深层结构分析
表面上，市场波动源于地缘冲突或政策调整，但深层是全球资本循环中不同利益集团的博弈。一方面，国际金融资本通过“资金空头”（funding shorts）将港股作为套利工具，为配置韩日等市场融资，这体现了金融资本全球流动中对区域市场的工具化利用；另一方面，内地政策扩容南向通及央行外储增配港股，实则通过制度性安排引导资本回流，试图稳定本土金融话语权。这种资本流动背后，是金融资产阶级内部（国际投行与本土政策资本）对资本积累主导权的争夺。

4. 阶级/利益分析
受益者：一是科技垄断资本（如AI、半导体企业），通过资本市场波动实现股权价值重估与套现；二是金融中介与投行，通过做空回补、套利交易获取手续费与价差收益；三是政策资本（如央行体系），通过制度性资本配置强化对香港金融市场的控制权。
受损者：一是中小投资者，其财富随虚拟资本波动被侵蚀；二是实体产业部门，尤其是文中提及的“生产商价格指数上涨挤压工厂利润”，反映产业资本在通胀与金融化挤压下的生存困境；三是全球无产阶级，其工资与社会保障被通胀进一步侵蚀。
权力流动体现为：金融资本通过定价权与流动性配置，实质性地影响生产资源分配，甚至绑架宏观经济政策。

5. 主流叙事批判
主流媒体（如文章引用分析师言论）将波动归因于“技术性反弹”“情绪修复”，掩盖了三个实质矛盾：其一，将金融波动简化为市场自发调节，掩盖了虚拟资本积累制度本身内嵌的危机倾向；其二，强调“政策利好”，却未触及资本分配中阶级权力的不对等——政策红利更多流向金融资产持有者而非劳动者；其三，回避地缘冲突背后的帝国主义资源争夺逻辑，将其简化为“市场情绪扰动”。马克思在《资本论》第一卷中揭示的资本主义生产关系的隐蔽性，在此处体现为金融话语对剥削关系的彻底遮蔽。

6. 解放前景
从政治经济学视角看，出路需直面三个层面：
第一，限制虚拟资本自我增殖的狂奔：如对金融交易征税、遏制高频套利，使资本重新锚定实体价值创造——正如马克思所言，工场手工业分工“发展了新的社会劳动生产力”，未来需构建以社会需求为导向、劳动者参与民主管理的生产组织形式。
第二，打破金融资本的全球霸权循环：区域资本市场应逐步摆脱对美元体系与投机性资本的依赖，通过多边结算机制与生产性投资，重塑“资本服务于生产”的关系。
第三，重建分配正义：当前“生产商价格指数上涨挤压利润”的危机，本质是剩余价值在产业资本与金融资本间分配失衡。需通过劳资集体谈判、超额利润税等工具，将积累的财富导向社会保障与公共生产。

虚拟资本的每一次剧烈波动，都是资本主义基本矛盾——生产的社会化与资本的私人占有——在金融领域的尖锐投射。唯有将金融权力纳入民主监督与社会规划，方能打破“虚拟资本暴增而现实财富匮乏”的困局，走向“每个人的自由发展是一切人的自由发展的条件”的生产关系。

（字数：758）

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] Rolex Oyster celebrates 100 years of revolutionising watchmaking
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1062

AI Summary:

* In 1926, Rolex introduced the Oyster, the world's first waterproof wristwatch with a hermetically sealed case, revolutionizing watchmaking by making water resistance and durability standard features. * Its effectiveness was famously proven in 1927 when it survived swimmer Mercedes Gleitze's 10+ hour English Channel crossing, and later with Malcolm Campbell's land-speed record attempts. * The Oyster's practical design helped cement the wristwatch as a mainstream, essential item for men, evolving from a delicate feminine accessory into a tool for adventurers and everyday use. * This year, Rolex celebrates the Oyster's centenary, marking 100 years since its creation established the conceptual blueprint for the modern mechanical wristwatch.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Few timepieces can genuinely claim to have changed the course of watchmaking. As it celebrates its centenary this year, the Oyster stands not merely as Rolex’s longest-lasting creation, but also as the conceptual blueprint for the modern mechanical wristwatch. Its influence is embedded so deeply into contemporary horology that its once-radical ideas – water resistance, everyday durability – are now taken for granted.

Until the late 19th century, wristwatches had been regarded as feminine accessories while men relied on pocket watches that were protected from dust and moisture. Warfare transformed that convention. Soldiers, needing easy access to the time in combat, began strapping pocket watches to their wrists, giving rise to the “wristlet”. That practicality caught on beyond the battlefield, and, by the first world war, the wristwatch had firmly established itself among men, too, even though functional drawbacks still existed.

In 1926, Rolex was still far from the global force it would become. Founded in London in 1905 as Wilsdorf & Davis, by German businessman Hans Wilsdorf and his brother-in-law, Alfred Davis, the company had spent its early decades building credibility in precision timekeeping while harbouring ambitions to compete as an equal with long-established, prestigious Swiss manufacturers such as Vacheron Constantin, Blancpain and Patek Philippe.

In 1910, a Rolex became the first wristwatch to receive a chronometric certificate from the Official Watch Rating Centre in Switzerland. Four years later, another achieved the Class A certificate from Britain’s Kew Observatory, an honour previously reserved for marine chronometers. Precision was no longer the question. Durability was.

Wilsdorf understood that accuracy meant little if a watch could not survive real-life challenges. His answer arrived in 1926, by which time Rolex had relocated its base to Switzerland. The innovation that went into producing the Oyster was not simply water resistance – waterproof watches had existed in experimental forms before – but the creation of a hermetically sealed case designed for daily, long-term wear.

Rolex achieved this by engineering a system in which the bezel, case back and winding crown were screwed tightly against the middle case, forming an airtight architecture that protected the movement from water, dust and debris. The name captured the idea; like an oyster protecting the pearl within, the case became a shell safeguarding the movement. The watch could accompany its wearer through rain, travel, sport and work.

The new watch case’s first serious test came in 1927, when British swimmer Mercedes Gleitze attempted to cross the English Channel wearing a Rolex Oyster. After more than 10 hours in freezing waters, she and the watch emerged, the latter still running.

From there, the Oyster became a companion to other adventurers. On Malcolm Campbell’s land-speed record attempts in the 1920s and 30s, it endured extreme vibration, further proving that a wristwatch could maintain accuracy far beyond the controlled conditions of the watchmaker’s bench.

It accompanied pioneering aviators such as Chuck Yeager, surviving the thin air, freezing temperatures and physical strain of early high-altitude flight. It reached the highest peaks with mountaineers, proving that mechanical precision could endure where human performance itself was pushed to its limits.

Still, one challenge remained. Like all watches of its era, a Rolex required daily winding. That changed in 1931, with the Perpetual rotor. Using a freely oscillating weight powered by the motion of the wrist, Rolex created a self-winding mechanism that continuously replenished the mainspring. It eliminated the need for manual winding, reducing wear on the crown and preserving the integrity of the waterproof case.

The Oyster case and Perpetual rotor transformed the wristwatch into something new: an object that not only endured, but maintained itself, and in the process formed the foundation for an entire ecosystem of timepieces.

The Rolex Explorer, launched in 1953, was born from it. Shaped by high-altitude expeditions, it prioritised reliability and legibility – essential in low-oxygen conditions, freezing temperatures and reduced visibility.

The Submariner (also 1953) extended these principles beneath the waves, combining water resistance – initially rated to 100 metres and later 200 metres – with a rotating bezel that enabled divers to track elapsed immersion time. Its unidirectional 60-minute bezel became a safety feature, preventing accidental adjustment during a dive. High-contrast luminous markers and hands ensured legibility in low-light conditions.

The 1950s also saw the launch of the Rolex GMT-Master. Designed for Pan American World Airways pilots, it displays two time zones simultaneously and has a 24-hour rotating bezel and dedicated GMT hand that transform the watch into a navigation tool.

The Rolex Day-Date was launched in 1956 exclusively in precious metals – gold or platinum – setting it apart from the brand’s tool watches. It would become associated with political and cultural figures, most notably a United States president, Lyndon B. Johnson.

Later came the Rolex Cosmograph Daytona (1963), which was designed for racing drivers, combining a tachymeter bezel for calculating average speed, a precision timing mechanism for lap measurement and high-legibility sub-dials for use under pressure. Named after the Daytona International Speedway in Florida, its identity was amplified by celebrities such as actor and racing-car driver Paul Newman.

Other brands noticed how the watches of the Oyster family had become companions built to travel, endure and participate in life, and rose to the challenge. Omega refined water-resistant sports models and professional timing tools; Blancpain defined early modern diving capability with the Fifty Fathoms collection; IWC developed robust, engineer-led pilot’s watches; and Jaeger-LeCoultre explored durable, utility-driven complications such as those of the Memovox series.

But it had been the ingenuity of the Oyster case, 100 years ago, that opened those doors for others to follow.

To mark the centenary, Rolex has introduced commemorative Oyster Perpetual models that remain faithful to the design’s restrained identity. A 41mm version pairs a slate dial with yellow-gold detailing while a 36mm model references classic dial textures with subtle anniversary markings. Discreet engravings, including a “100” crown detail, acknowledge the milestone without disrupting the Oyster’s defining understatement.

The celebration has extended beyond product launches. In Shanghai, Rolex presented the “Oyster Story”, an immersive exhibition at the West Bund Dome that ran from June 10 to 28. The exhibition traced the Oyster’s evolution through archival pieces, technical components and interactive installations that revealed the engineering behind the design.

One hundred years on, the Oyster’s innovations have become industry standards, absorbed into the very language of watchmaking.

item.title: [SCMP] At Indonesia’s sharia frontier, 21 lashes for a TikTok kiss ‘is our right’
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 1218

AI Summary:

* An unmarried couple in Aceh, Indonesia, received 21 public lashes each for kissing in a car and allegedly live-streaming it on TikTok, under the province's unique Islamic Criminal Code. * Aceh is the only Indonesian province permitted to enforce sharia law, a status established in 2004 as part of a peace agreement granting semi-autonomy following a separatist conflict. * International human rights groups, such as Amnesty International, condemn the practice as torture or cruel punishment and have noted Aceh authorities are expanding enforcement to online activities. * Recent months have seen escalating punishments, including a record 200+ collective lashes in a single session in May and a record individual sentence of 140 lashes in January. * While international outcry often follows such cases, support within Aceh is tied to local identity, religion, and autonomy, though it is not universal.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
Footage of the punishment quickly went viral: an unmarried couple each receiving 21 lashes of the cane in Indonesia’s Aceh province after being accused of kissing in a car and live-streaming it on TikTok.

As each blow landed on their backs, the unnamed man, 22, and woman, 25, visibly grimaced. The woman later burst into tears, wailing in pain as the public punishment continued.

The caning was carried out on July 2 in Banda Aceh, the provincial capital. Aceh is the only Indonesian province to enforce sharia law under its Islamic Criminal Code, known locally as Qanun Jinayat.

It was far from the first such case to gain international attention and the response has become familiar: footage spreads online, rights groups condemn the punishment as inhumane and Aceh’s status as the Muslim-majority country’s sole sharia-enforcing province is thrust back into the spotlight.

But inside Aceh, caning is not viewed solely through a human rights lens. For some residents, it is bound up with religion, local autonomy and Acehnese identity.

Support is neither universal nor uncomplicated – even those who back the punishment often disagree over when and how it should be applied.

Aceh was historically one of the most powerful Islamic sultanates in Southeast Asia and for centuries observed informal Islamic law before it was formally codified in 2004. The law was enacted as part of a peace deal granting Aceh semi-autonomous status following a long and bloody civil conflict that left thousands of Acehnese dead in their struggle for independence from the rest of Indonesia.

Rights groups have long argued the punishment violates international human rights law. In a statement issued the same day as the flogging, Amnesty International said all forms of corporal punishment constituted torture or other “cruel, inhuman or degrading punishment”, while its co-regional director, Montse Ferrer, called the caning of the couple “a horrifying act of discrimination”.

Amnesty also said the case showed Aceh authorities were expanding enforcement of sharia law online, with sharia police appearing to intensify digital monitoring to punish acts deemed to violate Islamic law, including public displays of intimacy outside marriage.

The July case followed several other public canings in Aceh that have drawn attention in recent months.

On May 12, the Southeast Aceh Prosecutor’s Office flogged 15 people, administering more than 200 strokes of the rattan cane collectively – the highest number ever carried out in a single session in the regency. One woman received 100 lashes for adultery, while 14 men received between six and 10 strokes each for gambling.

In January, a couple were caned 140 times each – 100 for having sex outside wedlock and 40 for consuming alcohol – in what was thought to be the most severe caning punishment ever handed down in Aceh. The woman collapsed afterwards and had to be escorted to a waiting ambulance.

Yet the outrage that follows Aceh’s canings often travels further than the views of those who live under the law. In Banda Aceh, residents who spoke to This Week in Asia described the punishment in more practical, personal and sometimes conflicted terms.

Nurjani, a 62-year-old housewife in Banda Aceh, said she supported caning because it “was about religion” and in line with sharia law. Personal responsibility was involved, she said, adding if people were worried about being caned, they should not commit a caning offence.

“If you do not do anything wrong, then there is nothing to fear. I support caning and I believe that if you lead your life in the right way and walk the right path, no one will question you.”

Muhammad Nasir, 42, also a Banda Aceh resident, said he supported caning because of Aceh’s semi-autonomous status.

“It was agreed that Aceh could enact sharia law as part of the peace deal, so it is our right. Daily life in Aceh is closely woven with religion, not just regarding the law, but at all levels. It is part of the identity of Acehnese people.”

It is our right … It is part of the identity of the Acehnese people

Muhammad Nasir, Banda Aceh resident

He considers caning a deterrent that was put in place to protect the province’s people.

“If someone is caned, it will be a lesson for them so that they do not do the same thing again. It has a physical element to it, but it is mostly social and designed to make the person ashamed of their crimes.”

Clear standard operating procedures govern the punishment, he added, with the person administering the flogging, known as an algojo, prohibited from raising their arms above their shoulders when administering each blow.

Caning in Aceh uses a thin rattan cane and is carried out over clothing while the perpetrator stands or kneels before the algojo, who wears a hooded robe to protect their anonymity. All algojo are drawn from the ranks of the Wilayatul Hisbah, Aceh’s religious police.

The Legal Aid Institute in Banda Aceh told This Week in Asia that, broadly, it found caning was supported by most of Acehnese society, though support sometimes depended on the specific case.

“At the Legal Aid Institute, we fundamentally oppose the use of caning as a punishment for perpetrators of sexual violence. In our view, caning is too lenient a penalty for sexual violence and it fails to act as a deterrent and lacks a victim-centred perspective,” said Muhammad Qodrat, Banda Aceh institute’s operational head.

Because caning sentences could be “carried out within days or even hours”, he said perpetrators were often quickly able to return to their communities, potentially encountering their victim again and retraumatising them.

“In some instances, perpetrators have even persecuted or intimidated the victims whom they hold responsible for their punishment.”

Especially in cases involving sexual violence, “imprisonment is considered a wiser and more victim-oriented approach” to enforce physical separation, Qodrat said, noting that the perpetrator was often “someone close to the victim who lives in the same or a nearby neighbourhood”.

While Aceh regularly makes headlines when public canings grab global attention, it is not the only place in Southeast Asia to officially sanction the punishment.

In neighbouring Malaysia, flogging is used as punishment for a range of offences including sexual assault, financial crimes and drug-related crimes. Unlike in Aceh, whipping in Malaysia is administered against bare skin, though women and men over 50 are typically exempt.

On May 6 this year, Singapore’s government announced caning could be meted out to male students found bullying classmates as “a last resort”, potentially affecting children as young as nine. Singapore also prescribes caning for more than 35 offences, including rape, drug trafficking, robbery and financial crimes.

Nasir Husein, a 57-year-old videographer based in Lhokseumawe on Aceh’s east coast, told This Week in Asia that views on caning were subjective: some people supported it unconditionally, while others backed it only for certain crimes.

“There are some people who support caning, but who question why it is used for crimes like gambling and not for other crimes like theft or corruption which some people feel are more serious,” he said.

“Others feel that it is more negative than positive as, when footage goes viral like in the most recent case, there is backlash against both Aceh and Islam in general. Some people feel it causes a lot of stigma.”

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:

金融表象与资本实质：香港股市波动的政治经济学透视

1. 现象描述
香港股市受区域科技股波动、中东地缘冲突及内地政策调整影响，呈现震荡分化格局，科技板块逆势上涨，但整体市场承压。

2. 政治经济学定位
从资本运动维度看，香港股市的波动是虚拟资本与实体资本脱钩的典型表现。股票价格（如智谱AI、中芯国际的暴涨暴跌）并非由现实生产过程中的价值创造直接决定，而是受资本化预期、流动性套利与地缘风险溢价驱动。马克思在《资本论》第三卷中指出：“虚拟资本的增加并不等于现实财富的增加，但虚拟资本的剧烈波动却能深刻影响现实经济。” 此处科技股的剧烈波动，本质上是金融资本对剩余价值预期进行贴现与再定价的过程，而非对实体生产力的直接反映。

3. 深层结构分析
表面上，市场波动源于地缘冲突或政策调整，但深层是全球资本循环中不同利益集团的博弈。一方面，国际金融资本通过“资金空头”（funding shorts）将港股作为套利工具，为配置韩日等市场融资，这体现了金融资本全球流动中对区域市场的工具化利用；另一方面，内地政策扩容南向通及央行外储增配港股，实则通过制度性安排引导资本回流，试图稳定本土金融话语权。这种资本流动背后，是金融资产阶级内部（国际投行与本土政策资本）对资本积累主导权的争夺。

4. 阶级/利益分析
受益者：一是科技垄断资本（如AI、半导体企业），通过资本市场波动实现股权价值重估与套现；二是金融中介与投行，通过做空回补、套利交易获取手续费与价差收益；三是政策资本（如央行体系），通过制度性资本配置强化对香港金融市场的控制权。
受损者：一是中小投资者，其财富随虚拟资本波动被侵蚀；二是实体产业部门，尤其是文中提及的“生产商价格指数上涨挤压工厂利润”，反映产业资本在通胀与金融化挤压下的生存困境；三是全球无产阶级，其工资与社会保障被通胀进一步侵蚀。
权力流动体现为：金融资本通过定价权与流动性配置，实质性地影响生产资源分配，甚至绑架宏观经济政策。

5. 主流叙事批判
主流媒体（如文章引用分析师言论）将波动归因于“技术性反弹”“情绪修复”，掩盖了三个实质矛盾：其一，将金融波动简化为市场自发调节，掩盖了虚拟资本积累制度本身内嵌的危机倾向；其二，强调“政策利好”，却未触及资本分配中阶级权力的不对等——政策红利更多流向金融资产持有者而非劳动者；其三，回避地缘冲突背后的帝国主义资源争夺逻辑，将其简化为“市场情绪扰动”。马克思在《资本论》第一卷中揭示的资本主义生产关系的隐蔽性，在此处体现为金融话语对剥削关系的彻底遮蔽。

6. 解放前景
从政治经济学视角看，出路需直面三个层面：
第一，限制虚拟资本自我增殖的狂奔：如对金融交易征税、遏制高频套利，使资本重新锚定实体价值创造——正如马克思所言，工场手工业分工“发展了新的社会劳动生产力”，未来需构建以社会需求为导向、劳动者参与民主管理的生产组织形式。
第二，打破金融资本的全球霸权循环：区域资本市场应逐步摆脱对美元体系与投机性资本的依赖，通过多边结算机制与生产性投资，重塑“资本服务于生产”的关系。
第三，重建分配正义：当前“生产商价格指数上涨挤压利润”的危机，本质是剩余价值在产业资本与金融资本间分配失衡。需通过劳资集体谈判、超额利润税等工具，将积累的财富导向社会保障与公共生产。

虚拟资本的每一次剧烈波动，都是资本主义基本矛盾——生产的社会化与资本的私人占有——在金融领域的尖锐投射。唯有将金融权力纳入民主监督与社会规划，方能打破“虚拟资本暴增而现实财富匮乏”的困局，走向“每个人的自由发展是一切人的自由发展的条件”的生产关系。

（字数：758）

]]>

item.title: [SCMP] As China’s tech firms adapt to AI era, workers worry they’ll be ‘optimised’ out of a job
item.description:
Source: SCMP | Words: 2082

AI Summary:

* Chinese tech workers are increasingly concerned about job displacement by AI, a fear amplified by recent rumors of massive layoffs at Meituan (which the company denied) and confirmed retrenchment across firms like Baidu and Xiaomi. * The traditional layoff euphemism "youhua" (optimisation) now carries a new implication: jobs being rendered obsolete by AI, not just poor performance. * The industry is shifting from chatbots to autonomous "AI agents" that can perform complex tasks, raising fears that entry-level white-collar jobs, including software engineering, are at risk. * Evidence of AI's capability is emerging, with Anthropic reporting over 80% of its code is now written by its AI model, and a Tencent executive stating most code from his company this year is AI-generated, changing engineers' roles toward supervision and architecture.

]]>
item.ns0:encoded:
When a friend checked in on a Meituan employee late last month to see if he had survived the latest round of corporate culling at the food delivery giant, he responded drily: “I don’t know whether it will be me next.”

Anxieties were running high at the company then, after chat screenshots circulated on Chinese social media claiming Meituan planned to slash up to half of its product roles by the end of June, coupled with deep cuts to other departments.

While Meituan quickly denied the rumours, the viral speculation struck a sensitive nerve across China’s tech industry. Behind the online claims, employees whisper that a quieter, more insidious form of retrenchment has been under way for months.

For years, tech workers in China have harboured a deep-seated dread of the word youhua – “optimisation”. The term serves as corporate shorthand for painful lay-offs, often masked by euphemisms like “organisational restructuring”.

This year, however, the term carries a chilling new undertone. The increasingly urgent question echoing through office corridors is no longer whether a worker is performing, but whether their job can be done by artificial intelligence.

Meituan is far from an isolated case. From Baidu to Xiaomi, China’s technology giants have been trimming their teams, according to internal sources and tech recruiters.

The shift follows prophetic warnings from global tech leaders. Last year, Dario Amodei, CEO of US generative AI pioneer Anthropic, cautioned that the technology’s rapid evolution could wipe out half of all entry-level white-collar jobs within five years.

Today, the tools driving that shift are capable of far more than conversations and data retrieval. The industry has rapidly pivoted from basic chatbots to autonomous “AI agents” – systems capable of writing code, conducting market research, analysing legal documents and executing multi-step business tasks.

Anthropic said that as of last month, more than 80 per cent of the code merged into its codebase had been written by its Claude AI model.

Software engineers, long viewed as possessing one of the most secure and sophisticated white-collar skill sets, have found themselves on the front lines of this automation wave. Coding, it turns out, is a task where AI models excel.

We now require engineers to be full-stack ... even if they are doing it side-by-side with AI

Rose Cao, talent acquisition specialist

“I have barely written a line of code by hand for almost a year,” said a veteran AI developer known to the public as Tuxi, who has worked for several of China’s major technology companies.

At a live-streamed industry event this month, senior Tencent Holdings executive Dowson Tong said most of the tech giant’s code written this year was generated by AI systems.

Tong said human engineers were becoming supervisors, spending more time focused on architecture and system design. They delegated the coding work to AI and only stepped in periodically to guide and correct its output.

Such changes have significantly raised the baseline requirements for landing or keeping a tech job.

“We now require engineers to be full-stack,” said Rose Cao, a talent acquisition specialist at a Chinese AI firm, adding that “even if they are doing it side-by-side with AI”, programmers were now expected to handle everything from front-end to back-end tasks.

AI automation is expanding beyond the engineering department. Specialised tools originally built for programmers – such as OpenAI’s Codex or Anthropic’s Claude Code – are evolving to support broader corporate workflows.

A parallel ecosystem of enterprise AI assistants has sprung up across China: Moonshot AI has pushed out Kimi Work; Tencent has rolled out WorkBuddy; ByteDance has launched Coze; and Alibaba Group Holding has introduced QoderWork CN. Alibaba is the owner of the South China Morning Post.

By embedding such tools into daily operations, businesses are eroding some of the traditional boundaries between various corporate functions, allowing a single employee to fulfil tasks that previously required a team of specialised workers.

Rather than being an optional résumé highlight, a headhunter who specialises in tech talent said that “AI fluency has become a requirement for many employers”, creating a hostile environment for fresh university graduates and inexperienced workers.

“It is hard to persuade an employer to hire a newcomer and spend more time and money training them when AI agents can already do a good job – and won’t quit,” she said.

It is a development that has prompted expressions of deep concern from some of the country’s leading economic minds. Cai Fang, a prominent labour economist and member of the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences, has warned that the initial shock wave of the AI boom will disproportionately hit “the young and the old”.

Cai’s research shows that traditional employment rates by age form an inverted U-shaped curve, where middle-aged workers experience the highest job stability, while younger and older cohorts face greater vulnerability.

He said AI was reinforcing that divide by eroding the economic value of entry-level skills for younger workers and also widening the digital capability gap for older employees who struggled to adapt to rapid technological advancement.

The economic data underlines the stakes.

China’s youth unemployment rate for 16- to 24-year-olds, excluding students, fell to 15.6 per cent in May but remained much higher than that of the whole urban population, according to data released by the National Bureau of Statistics on Monday. The Ministry of Education has estimated a record 12.7 million university graduates are preparing to enter the labour market.

But some insiders argued that blaming AI for the wave of lay-offs misdiagnosed a conventional corporate malaise.

“To say that companies are laying people off just because AI has improved workflows is basically a lie,” said Tuxi, the veteran developer, who argued that many of China’s largest tech conglomerates spent years inflating their headcount. AI had simply provided executives with a convenient justification to trim the fat, he said.

Global tech leaders have also pushed back against the narrative that AI is the primary engine of global job cuts. In an interview last month with Singaporean broadcaster CNA, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang dismissed a direct causal link between AI deployment and tech lay-offs as an explanation that was “too lazy”.

He argued that the implementation timeline of enterprise AI had not matched spikes in unemployment, suggesting that the volatility was driven by a mix of over-hiring and the staggering costs required to develop AI.

That financial strain can be seen among China’s tech titans, where resources are being aggressively diverted to fund the AI arms race.

TikTok owner ByteDance has ramped up investment in AI infrastructure this year, boosting its planned capital expenditure to over 200 billion yuan (US$29.5 billion) – an increase of at least 25 per cent compared with preliminary internal budgets discussed late last year, the SCMP reported last month.

Several current and former employees said ByteDance had been consolidating business lines to free up headcount and computing power for its flagship AI products, including its AI assistant Doubao, its AI coding environment Trae, and the Coze agent platform.

The internal realignment coincided with the rapid expansion of Seed, ByteDance’s foundation-model team. Launched in 2023, the Seed team has swollen to about 2,000 people.

Meituan is playing defence and offence. After weathering a brutal local-services price war with Alibaba that squeezed its profit margins last year, the company has trimmed secondary teams, according to employees.

However, it continues to recruit at the high end. Meituan’s “Beidou” talent programme for top-tier campus technology recruits features openings focused on next-generation frontiers: AI infrastructure, large language models, embodied intelligence, autonomous agents and drones.

Alibaba has also been trimming its headcount and pivoting its sprawling business towards AI. The e-commerce giant shed around 34 per cent of its staff last year, mainly by offloading offline retail operations, according to corporate disclosures.

The macroeconomic impact of AI on employment was expected to be neutral in the long run, GF Securities’ chief economist Guo Lei wrote in a recent report. Still, in the short term, the market might need to navigate a painful “displacement effect” before it could unlock the “reinstatement effect”, he said.

Guo said AI would fundamentally redistribute jobs, reshape the nature of jobs and alter income structures. Workers stuck in less differentiated roles would lose their labour market bargaining power, while those who mastered the use of AI stood to see their productivity soar.

As the transition accelerates, Chinese tech firms are steering clear of the blunt rhetoric used by some of their Silicon Valley counterparts.

In February, Block founder Jack Dorsey said the US payments firm would reduce its workforce from more than 10,000 to under 6,000, pointing explicitly to the deployment of “intelligence tools” and flatter, leaner organisational structures.

We must do everything possible to protect the livelihoods of hundreds of thousands of employees

Richard Liu Qiangdong, JD.com chairman

In contrast, Chinese tech companies rarely, if ever, admit publicly that human workers are being replaced by algorithms.

Richard Liu Qiangdong, the billionaire founder and chairman of e-commerce powerhouse JD.com – which employed 951,420 people across the company and its affiliates at the end of last year – stressed the company’s responsibility to protect jobs during a speech to staff last month.

“Facing the impact of new technologies … we must do everything possible to protect the livelihoods of hundreds of thousands of employees, including the jobs of our blue-collar brothers,” Liu said, making a reference to the company’s extensive logistics network.

At a recent Apec panel discussion, he said JD.com would retrain its roughly 700,000 delivery and other blue-collar workers in robot maintenance as AI and automation reshape last-mile logistics.

Beijing has stepped up regulatory oversight to ensure the AI transition does not trigger widespread social friction.

The State Council’s “AI Plus” action plan, released in August, called for stronger assessment of employment risks from AI applications, more AI skills training and guiding innovation resources towards areas with greater job creation potential, with the aim of reducing the technology’s impact on employment.

More recently, the State Council unveiled its employment blueprint for the five years ending in 2030. It placed a new mandate at the top of its agenda, saying the country must “strengthen the employment-first orientation of macroeconomic regulation … and adapt to the development of AI to promote employment and entrepreneurship”.

Chinese courts and labour arbitration bodies have also begun drawing limits around AI-related dismissals.

In April, a Hangzhou court ruled that a financial technology firm had unlawfully terminated a quality-control supervisor who had been auditing large language model outputs.

The company fired him after citing the cost advantages of replacing his labour with AI. The worker had rejected an internal transfer that would have slashed his monthly salary from 25,000 yuan to 15,000 yuan.

The court ruled that while adopting AI constituted a voluntary business choice and a standard business risk, it did not automatically qualify as a “major change in objective circumstances” – the legal threshold required under Chinese labour law to declare an employment contract impossible to perform.

The court declared that the fundamental purpose of AI should be to “liberate labour, promote employment and improve people’s livelihoods”.

In an essay prepared for this month’s Lujiazui Forum, an annual financial gathering in Shanghai, Cai, the economist, said that while AI offered a chance to lift China’s labour productivity, its disruptive side effects could not be ignored.

He warned of the risk of a widening of the digital divide between tech-forward and legacy industries, urban and rural areas, and individual workers, with short-term job displacement highly probable, creating a broader macroeconomic risk that would see household consumption lag rapid productivity gains.

The solution, Cai said, was to “invest in people and ensure that intelligence is put towards good”.

But worries persist for those toiling away in the cubicles of China’s tech hubs. Amid corporate denials of sweeping lay-offs, the quiet reality of “optimisation” continues to play out one desk at a time.

Confronted by what he viewed as an uncertain employment future, the Meituan employee contacted by a friend said “some outsourced workers have already been cleared out”, though he did not know the exact proportion.

While lay-offs happened every year, “this round feels quite big,” he said.

Additional reporting by Minxiao Chang

Commentary - Hong Kong stocks retreat as regional tech volatility offsets short covering:
